<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Peeling — The Gateway Operation in Post-Harvest Engineering

**Peeling** is defined as the removal of the outer skin (epidermis/pericarp/exocarp) of fruits, vegetables, and tubers using mechanical, thermal, chemical, or manual force, with the objective of producing a clean, edible, and visually appealing product suitable for further processing such as canning, drying, freezing, or juice extraction.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Peeling is classified as a **primary unit operation** in the post-harvest processing chain. It precedes almost every downstream operation including slicing, blanching, pasteurization, and packaging.

### 🔍 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Think of peeling like **removing the wrapper from a chocolate before eating it**. The wrapper (skin/peel) is inedible, may contain dirt, pesticides, microbes, or unwanted bitterness. Similarly, in food processing, the peel is often:

- **Tough and fibrous** (difficult to chew)
- **Bitter or astringent** (contains tannins, alkaloids like solanine in potatoes)
- **Contaminated** (microbes, soil, pesticide residues)
- **Aesthetically unappealing** (discoloration in canned products)

Just as we use fingers, teeth, or scissors to remove a chocolate wrapper, food industry uses **manual, mechanical, chemical, or thermal energy** to remove peels at industrial scale — but with **minimal loss of edible flesh (peeling loss should be < 15–20% for most crops)**.

---

## 1.2 Manual Peeling — Definition

**Manual Peeling** is the removal of fruit/vegetable skin using **hand-held tools operated by human labor**, without any external power source. Tools include knives, hand peelers (rotary type), and vegetable scrapers.

| Parameter | Specification |
|---|---|
| **Capacity** | 20–50 kg/person/hour |
| **Peeling Loss** | 15–30% |
| **Labor Cost** | High |
| **Product Suitability** | Irregular shapes, soft fruits |

> [!NOTE]
> Manual peeling is still preferred for **delicate fruits** like mango, papaya, kiwi, and for **artisanal processing** where surface damage must be minimized.

---

## 1.3 Mechanical Peeling — Definition

**Mechanical Peeling** employs **power-driven abrasive or cutting surfaces** to abrade, scrape, or cut away the peel. Common devices include abrasive peelers (rotating carborundum rollers), knife peelers, and roller peelers.

**Key Concept:** The peeling action depends on the **differential strength** between the thin peel and the firm underlying flesh — abrasive force removes the weaker peel while the firmer flesh resists removal.

---

## 1.4 Chemical Peeling — Definition

**Chemical Peeling** (also called **Lye Peeling** or **Caustic Peeling**) involves immersing produce in a hot solution of **sodium hydroxide (NaOH)** or **potassium hydroxide (KOH)** to dissolve the peel through **saponification of the waxy cuticle** and **hydrolysis of pectin** in the middle lamella.

**Typical Lye Concentration:** 1–10% NaOH  
**Temperature:** 60–100 °C  
**Contact Time:** 30 seconds – 10 minutes

---

## 1.5 Thermal Peeling — Definition

**Thermal Peeling** uses **heat energy** (steam, hot water, flame, infrared radiation) to rupture the peel through rapid expansion of internal moisture and dissolution of the pectin layer beneath the skin.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Steam peeling is the **most widely used industrial method** for tomatoes, potatoes, and root crops — adopted by brands like **Haldiram's, MTR, and global canned-tomato producers**.

---

## 1.6 Slicing, Shredding, Crushing, Chopping & Juice Extraction

**Size reduction** is the operation of breaking down a bulk solid food material into smaller pieces using mechanical force. In horticultural processing, this serves to:

- Increase **surface area** for drying, blanching, or extraction
- Improve **heat and mass transfer** rates
- Enable **uniform product geometry** for packaging

| Operation | Typical Size of Output |
|---|---|
| **Slicing** | 2–10 mm thick discs/strips |
| **Shredding** | 1–5 mm width strips |
| **Crushing** | Coarse pulp (5–15 mm) |
| **Chopping** | 5–25 mm chunks |
| **Juice Extraction** | Liquid + residual pulp |

---

## 1.7 Blanching — Definition

**Blanching** is a **preliminary thermal treatment** of fruits/vegetables by exposing them to **hot water (85–100 °C) or steam (100 °C)** for a **short, defined duration (1–10 minutes)**, followed by **rapid cooling** to inactivate enzymes and preserve quality.

**Latin root:** *"Blanchir"* — French for "to whiten" (originally referred to whitening of almonds by skin removal).

> [!NOTE]
> Blanching is **NOT a cooking process**. It is a **quality-preservation step** that prepares the product for freezing, drying, or canning.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Peeling Methods — Engineering Taxonomy

Peeling methods are classified based on the **form of energy applied** to break the peel–flesh interface bond:

```
                    PEELING METHODS
                          │
   ┌──────────┬───────────┼──────────┬─────────────┐
   │          │           │          │             │
 MANUAL   MECHANICAL   CHEMICAL   THERMAL     EMERGING
(kinetic) (kinetic+   (chemical  (thermal    (enzymatic,
          abrasive)    energy)    energy)    vacuum)
```

---

## 2.2 MANUAL PEELING — Engineering Analysis

### Tools and Devices

1. **Straight Knife (French Chef Knife)** — Used for apples, potatoes, carrots
2. **Rotary Hand Peeler (Y-peeler / Speed peeler)** — Most common household device
3. **Vegetable Scraper** — For carrots, cucumber, radish
4. **Thumb Peeler** — For soft fruits like tomato, peach

### Operating Principle
The blade is held at a **shallow angle (5–15°)** to the fruit surface. A **shearing force** is applied parallel to the surface, cutting through the peel layer without penetrating deeply into the flesh.

### Mathematical Representation of Peeling Loss

$$
\text{Peeling Loss (\%)} = \frac{W_1 - W_2}{W_1} \times 100
$$

where $W_1$ = weight before peeling (kg), $W_2$ = weight after peeling (kg).

### Engineering Limitations

- **Low throughput** (20–50 kg/hr/person)
- **High variability** in peel thickness → **non-uniform appearance**
- **Operator fatigue** and **ergonomic injuries**
- **Hygiene risk** due to direct hand contact

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In India, **manual peeling labor cost** typically constitutes **20–30% of total processing cost** in small-scale fruit processing units.

---

## 2.3 MECHANICAL PEELING — Engineering Analysis

### 2.3.1 Abrasive Peeler (Carborundum Roller Type)

**Construction:**
- Rotating **carborundum (silicon carbide) coated roller** mounted in a cylindrical chamber
- Produce is fed through a **rotating disc** with abrasive surface
- Water spray continuously washes away removed peel

**Operating Variables:**
- **Roller speed:** 200–600 rpm
- **Residence time:** 30 sec – 3 min
- **Abrasive grit size:** 30–80 mesh
- **Throughput:** 500–3000 kg/hr

**Peeling Mechanism:** Three-body abrasion — the rotating abrasive surface, the wet peel, and water droplets act together to abrade the skin.

**Suitability:** Potatoes, carrots, sweet potatoes, beetroot, tapioca.

### 2.3.2 Knife Peeler

**Construction:**
- A set of **stationary or rotating knives** mounted around a rotating carrier
- The fruit is impaled on a **centering device** and rotated against the knives
- Adjustable **depth of cut** controls peeling thickness

**Suitability:** Apples, pears, oranges (citrus), peaches.

### 2.3.3 Roller Peeler

Two counter-rotating rollers with differential speeds grip and peel the produce. Mainly used for **soft fruits** and **onions**.

### Engineering Performance Metrics

$$
\text{Peeling Efficiency (\%)} = \frac{\text{Mass of peel removed}}{\text{Total peel mass}} \times 100
$$

$$
\text{Product Recovery (\%)} = \frac{W_2}{W_1} \times 100 = 100 - \text{Peeling Loss}
$$

### KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Parameter | Formula | Unit | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| Peeling Loss | $(W_1 - W_2)/W_1 \times 100$ | % | Yield calculation |
| Peeling Efficiency | Mass removed / Total peel mass × 100 | % | Equipment comparison |
| Product Recovery | $(W_2 / W_1) \times 100$ | % | Process economics |
| Throughput | Mass processed / Time | kg/hr | Capacity planning |
| Specific Energy | Power consumed / Mass processed | kWh/ton | Energy audit |
| Abrasive Index | Function of grit size + contact pressure | dimensionless | Peeler design |
| Lye Concentration | Mass of NaOH / Volume of solution × 100 | % w/v | Chemical peeling |
| Heat Transfer (Blanching) | $q = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$ | kJ | Energy required |
| Steam Consumption | $m_s = \frac{Q}{h_{fg}}$ | kg | Thermal peeling |

---

## 2.4 CHEMICAL PEELING — Engineering Analysis

### Process Steps
1. **Pre-heating** (optional, 50–60 °C)
2. **Lye dipping** in hot NaOH solution
3. **Reaction time** (controlled by conveyor speed)
4. **Rinsing** with high-pressure cold water (spray)
5. **Acid neutralization** (optional, dilute citric acid)

### Governing Chemistry

The peel is degraded by two simultaneous reactions:

**(i) Saponification of waxy cuticle:**
$$\text{Triglyceride} + 3\,\text{NaOH} \rightarrow \text{Glycerol} + 3\,\text{RCOONa (soap)}$$

**(ii) Hydrolysis of middle lamella pectin:**
$$\text{Pectin} + \text{NaOH} \rightarrow \text{Sodium pectate (soluble)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The **middle lamella pectin** is the "biological glue" holding plant cells together. When NaOH converts insoluble pectin to soluble sodium pectate, the peel detaches easily.

### Process Parameters Table

| Parameter | Tomato | Potato | Peach | Apple |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| NaOH Conc. (%) | 8–10 | 10–15 | 2–3 | 4–8 |
| Temperature (°C) | 90–100 | 70–85 | 80–95 | 60–80 |
| Time (sec) | 30–60 | 120–300 | 30–60 | 60–180 |

### Engineering Concerns

- **Wastewater disposal** — high BOD/COD effluent
- **Over-peeling risk** — excess NaOH erodes flesh
- **Surface texture** — fruit may become slippery/soft
- **Cost of chemicals** — NaOH @ ₹40–60/kg

---

## 2.5 THERMAL PEELING — Engineering Analysis

### 2.5.1 Steam Peeling

The produce is exposed to **high-pressure steam (100–150 kPa gauge)** for **10–60 seconds**. The condensed steam raises the surface temperature rapidly, causing:

1. **Thermal expansion** of trapped air and moisture under the peel
2. **Pectin degradation** in the sub-peel layer
3. **Cuticle rupture** due to internal pressure build-up

Subsequent **water spray** (cold or warm) causes **shock cooling** and the peel **flakes off** easily.

**Industrial Example:** In tomato canning, steam peeling achieves **>95% peel removal** with only **5–8% product loss**.

### 2.5.2 Hot Water / Dip Peeling

Fruits like peaches, tomatoes, and apricots are dipped in **boiling water (95–100 °C)** for **30–120 seconds**, then rapidly cooled and peeled manually or mechanically.

### 2.5.3 Flame Peeling

Direct exposure to **gas flame (1200 °C)** for **5–15 seconds**. Used for **onions and garlic** in industrial settings.

### 2.5.4 Infrared Peeling

Uses **IR radiation (wavelength 1–5 μm)** for rapid surface heating without deep penetration. Emerging technology.

### Heat Transfer Analysis

The heat required to raise the peel surface from initial temperature $T_i$ to peel-loosening temperature $T_p$:

$$
Q = m_p \cdot c_p \cdot (T_p - T_i)
$$

For steam peeling, the steam condenses and releases **latent heat**:

$$
Q_{steam} = m_s \cdot h_{fg}
$$

where $h_{fg}$ for saturated steam at 100 °C = **2257 kJ/kg**.

Therefore, the **steam requirement per kg of produce:**

$$
m_s = \frac{m_p \cdot c_p \cdot (T_p - T_i)}{h_{fg}}
$$

### Real-World Utility

In a typical **tomato processing plant** processing **50 tonnes/day**:
- Steam peeling consumes ~**0.2 kg steam per kg tomato**
- Energy cost: ~₹8–10 per quintal
- Compared to manual peeling cost: ~₹60–80 per quintal
- **Saving: ~85% in peeling cost**
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Derivation: Steam Requirement for Thermal Peeling

**Problem:** Calculate the steam required to thermally peel **1000 kg of tomatoes** from an initial temperature of $25\,°\text{C}$ to a peel-loosening temperature of $95\,°\text{C}$.

**Given:**
- Mass of produce, $m_p = 1000$ kg
- Specific heat of tomato, $c_p = 3.95$ kJ/(kg·K) (approximate)
- Initial temperature, $T_i = 25\,°\text{C}$
- Final temperature, $T_p = 95\,°\text{C}$
- Latent heat of steam at 100 °C, $h_{fg} = 2257$ kJ/kg
- Saturated steam temperature = 100 °C

### Step 1: State the heat balance equation

The heat required to raise the produce temperature must equal the heat released by condensing steam:

$$
Q_{\text{required}} = Q_{\text{steam released}}
$$

### Step 2: Express the heat required to raise tomato temperature

$$
Q_{\text{required}} = m_p \cdot c_p \cdot (T_p - T_i)
$$

### Step 3: Substitute numerical values

$$
Q_{\text{required}} = 1000 \, \text{kg} \times 3.95 \, \frac{\text{kJ}}{\text{kg·K}} \times (95 - 25) \, \text{K}
$$

$$
Q_{\text{required}} = 1000 \times 3.95 \times 70
$$

$$
Q_{\text{required}} = 276{,}500 \, \text{kJ}
$$

### Step 4: Express the heat released by steam

$$
Q_{\text{steam released}} = m_s \cdot h_{fg}
$$

### Step 5: Equate and solve for steam mass

$$
m_s \cdot h_{fg} = 276{,}500
$$

$$
m_s = \frac{276{,}500}{2257}
$$

$$
m_s \approx 122.5 \, \text{kg of steam}
$$

### Step 6: Compute steam ratio (specific steam consumption)

$$
\text{Specific steam} = \frac{m_s}{m_p} = \frac{122.5}{1000} = 0.1225 \, \frac{\text{kg steam}}{\text{kg produce}}
$$

### Step 7: Realistic Adjustment for Heat Losses

In practice, heat is lost to **walls of vessel, condensate drainage, radiation, and convection losses**. Apply a **loss factor of 30%**:

$$
m_{s,\text{actual}} = m_s \times 1.30 = 122.5 \times 1.30 \approx 159.3 \, \text{kg}
$$

$$
\text{Actual specific steam} = 0.159 \, \text{kg steam / kg tomato}
$$

This matches the industry benchmark of **0.15–0.25 kg steam/kg produce** for steam peeling.

---

## 3.2 Detailed Derivation: Peeling Loss in Mechanical Abrasive Peeling

**Problem:** A batch abrasive peeler processes **500 kg of potatoes** in **30 minutes**. After peeling, the **weight of peeled potatoes is 420 kg**. The **peel removed** (collected separately) weighs **65 kg**. Compute:
1. Peeling Loss (%)
2. Product Recovery (%)
3. Peeling Efficiency (%)

### Step 1: Identify variables

- $W_1$ = 500 kg (before peeling)
- $W_2$ = 420 kg (after peeling)
- $W_{\text{peel}}$ = 65 kg (peel collected)

### Step 2: Verify mass balance

$$
W_1 = W_2 + W_{\text{peel}} + W_{\text{loss to water/spray}}
$$

$$
500 = 420 + 65 + 15
$$

The remaining **15 kg is lost** as dissolved solids in spray water and abrasion dust. ✓

### Step 3: Peeling Loss calculation

$$
\text{Peeling Loss} = \frac{W_1 - W_2}{W_1} \times 100 = \frac{500 - 420}{500} \times 100
$$

$$
\text{Peeling Loss} = \frac{80}{500} \times 100 = 16\%
$$

### Step 4: Product Recovery calculation

$$
\text{Recovery} = \frac{W_2}{W_1} \times 100 = \frac{420}{500} \times 100 = 84\%
$$

### Step 5: Peeling Efficiency calculation

$$
\eta_{\text{peel}} = \frac{W_{\text{peel removed}}}{W_{\text{peel total}}} \times 100
$$

Assuming theoretical total peel = **80 kg** (from mass balance, accounting for 15 kg solid loss):

$$
\eta_{\text{peel}} = \frac{65}{80} \times 100 = 81.25\%
$$

---

## 3.3 Detailed Derivation: Lye Concentration for Chemical Peeling

**Problem:** Prepare **500 liters of 8% NaOH solution** from **commercial NaOH flakes (98% purity)** for peach peeling.

**Density of solution ≈ 1.09 kg/L**

### Step 1: Total solution mass

$$
M_{\text{solution}} = 500 \times 1.09 = 545 \, \text{kg}
$$

### Step 2: Pure NaOH required

$$
\text{NaOH}_{\text{pure}} = 545 \times 0.08 = 43.6 \, \text{kg of pure NaOH}
$$

### Step 3: Commercial NaOH required (98% purity)

$$
\text{NaOH}_{\text{commercial}} = \frac{43.6}{0.98} = 44.49 \, \text{kg}
$$

### Step 4: Water required

$$
M_{\text{water}} = 545 - 44.49 = 500.51 \, \text{kg of water}
$$

### Step 5: Cost calculation (assuming ₹50/kg NaOH)

$$
\text{Cost} = 44.49 \times 50 = ₹2224.5 \text{ per batch}
$$

If batch processes 500 kg peaches, then **NaOH cost per kg peach = ₹4.45**.

---

## 3.4 Detailed Derivation: Blanching Time Calculation

**Problem:** Estimate the blanching time for **potato cubes of 1 cm side** in water at $90\,°\text{C}$, given initial temperature $20\,\text{C}$. Use the lumped capacitance method.

**Given:**
- Cube side, $L = 0.01$ m
- $T_\infty = 90\,°\text{C}$ (water temp)
- $T_i = 20\,°\text{C}$ (cube initial)
- Target center temp, $T = 80\,°\text{C}$
- Thermal diffusivity, $\alpha = 1.4 \times 10^{-7}$ m²/s
- Characteristic length, $L_c = L/6 = 0.00167$ m

### Step 1: Lumped capacitance equation

$$
\frac{T - T_\infty}{T_i - T_\infty} = e^{-(hA/(\rho V c_p)) \cdot t}
$$

For a cube, $L_c = V/A = L/6$:

$$
\frac{T - T_\infty}{T_i - T_\infty} = \exp\left(-\frac{\alpha \, t}{L_c^2}\right)
$$

### Step 2: Substitute values

$$
\frac{80 - 90}{20 - 90} = \exp\left(-\frac{1.4 \times 10^{-7} \cdot t}{(0.00167)^2}\right)
$$

$$
\frac{-10}{-70} = 0.1429 = \exp(-50.2 \, t)
$$

### Step 3: Solve for t

$$
\ln(0.1429) = -50.2 \, t
$$

$$
-1.945 = -50.2 \, t
$$

$$
t = 0.0388 \, \text{s} \approx 0.04 \, \text{s}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> In reality, blanching times are **60–300 seconds** for 1 cm potato cubes because the lumped capacitance method assumes infinite internal conduction, which is unrealistic. In practice, the **process is governed by enzyme inactivation kinetics** (peroxidase, PPO), not just heat transfer.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Peeling Methods

| Parameter | Manual | Mechanical (Abrasive) | Chemical (Lye) | Thermal (Steam) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Energy form** | Human kinetic | Electrical + kinetic | Chemical | Thermal |
| **Capacity (kg/hr)** | 20–50 | 500–3000 | 1000–5000 | 2000–10000 |
| **Peeling Loss (%)** | 15–30 | 10–20 | 5–12 | 5–10 |
| **Capital Cost** | Negligible | ₹2–10 lakh | ₹5–15 lakh | ₹15–50 lakh |
| **Operating Cost** | High (labor) | Low–Medium | Medium | Medium–High |
| **Water Usage** | Low | Medium | Very High | Medium |
| **Wastewater BOD** | Low | Low | Very High | Medium |
| **Product Quality** | Variable | Uniform | Smooth surface | Uniform |
| **Suitable Crops** | All (esp. soft) | Potato, carrot | Tomato, peach | Tomato, potato |
| **Skill Required** | High | Low | Medium | Low |
| **Automation** | None | Easy | Easy | Easy |

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix: Size Reduction Equipment Selection

| Operation | Equipment | Output Size | Power Required | Typical Crops |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Slicing** | Rotary slicer, Guillotine slicer | 2–10 mm thick | 0.5–2 kW | Potato, carrot, cucumber |
| **Shredding** | Hammer shredder, Disc shredder | 1–5 mm wide | 1–5 kW | Cabbage, papaya |
| **Crushing** | Roller crusher, Hammer mill | <5 mm | 2–10 kW | Grapes, berries, apples |
| **Chopping** | Bowl chopper, Dicer | 5–25 mm | 1–5 kW | Onion, garlic, beetroot |
| **Juice Extraction** | Screw press, Basket press, Centrifugal extractor | Liquid + pulp | 1.5–7.5 kW | Orange, mango, pomegranate |

---

## 3.7 Blanching — Importance and Objectives (KTU Critical Topic)

### Engineering Definition
Blanching is a **short-duration thermal pre-treatment** designed to inactivate enzymes that cause quality deterioration in frozen, dried, or stored horticultural products.

### Primary Objectives

**1. Enzyme Inactivation**
- Inactivates **peroxidase (POD), polyphenol oxidase (PPO), catalase, lipoxygenase**
- PPO causes **enzymatic browning** (e.g., cut apple turning brown)
- POD is the **most heat-resistant** — its inactivation confirms adequate blanching

**2. Microbial Load Reduction**
- Reduces surface microbial count by **1–2 log cycles** (90–99% kill)
- Especially important before **freezing** where microbes are not killed

**3. Texture and Color Preservation**
- Destroys cellular gases (O₂, CO₂) trapped in intercellular spaces
- Prevents **oxidative deterioration**
- Helps maintain **vibrant green color** in peas and beans

**4. Pre-cooking (for Canning)**
- Softens tissue → facilitates can filling
- Removes **raw, earthy flavor**
- Improves **rehydration** of dried products

### Secondary Objectives

- **Fixes color** in green vegetables (chlorophyll → chlorophyllin)
- **Removes surface pesticide residues**
- **Expels trapped air** from intercellular spaces (prevents oxidation)
- **Improves drying rate** by rupturing cell membranes

### Type of Blanching

| Type | Medium | Temperature | Time |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Hot Water Blanching** | Water | 85–100 °C | 1–10 min |
| **Steam Blanching** | Steam | 100 °C | 1–5 min |
| **Microwave Blanching** | MW energy | — | 30 sec – 3 min |
| **Blanching in Solution** | Water + salts/sugars | 85–100 °C | 2–10 min |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Peroxidase Test (KTU Important):** Adequacy of blanching is judged by the **peroxidase enzyme test**. If POD is inactivated, all other enzymes are also inactivated (because POD is the most heat-resistant). Residual POD activity = **incomplete blanching** → quality loss.

# 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PEAGT742]`

**"List any four objectives of blanching in post-harvest processing of horticultural crops."**

**Model Answer:**

The four important objectives of blanching are:

1. **Enzyme Inactivation:** Blanching destroys heat-labile enzymes such as peroxidase (POD), polyphenol oxidase (PPO), catalase, and lipoxygenase that cause enzymatic browning, off-flavor development, and nutrient degradation during storage.

2. **Microbial Load Reduction:** Surface microbial count is reduced by 90–99% (1–2 log cycles), improving the keeping quality of the processed product, especially before freezing or drying.

3. **Texture Softening and Gas Expulsion:** Trapped intercellular gases (O₂, CO₂, N₂) are expelled, which prevents oxidative deterioration and improves rehydration behavior in dried products.

4. **Color Fixation and Pesticide Removal:** Green chlorophyll is converted to bright chlorophyllin (more stable green), and surface pesticide residues are partially removed.

*(Self-explanatory, well-structured answer expected. 1 mark for each objective with brief explanation.)*

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, PEAGT742]`

**"Differentiate between mechanical peeling and chemical peeling of fruits."**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Mechanical Peeling | Chemical Peeling |
|---|---|---|
| Principle | Uses abrasive/cutting force | Uses NaOH/KOH to dissolve peel |
| Equipment | Abrasive roller, knife peeler | Lye bath with conveyor |
| Loss (%) | 10–20% | 5–12% |
| Water Use | Moderate | Very High |
| Wastewater | Low BOD | High BOD/COD |
| Surface Quality | Slightly rough | Smooth, glossy |
| Suitable Crops | Potato, carrot, beetroot | Tomato, peach, apricot |
| Capital Cost | ₹2–10 lakh | ₹5–15 lakh |

*(3 marks expected: clearly tabulated distinction with at least 4–5 contrasting points.)*

---

## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A — Option 1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PEAGT742]`

**"Explain in detail the different methods of peeling horticultural crops. Discuss chemical peeling (lye peeling) with process parameters, chemistry, advantages, and limitations."**

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks: Classification and Description of Peeling Methods**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
Peeling is the primary unit operation in post-harvest processing of horticultural crops aimed at removing the inedible outer skin to obtain clean, edible flesh with minimum product loss.

**Classification (6 marks):**

**(i) Manual Peeling (1.5 marks):**
- Done by hand-held knives, rotary peelers, scrapers
- Capacity: 20–50 kg/hr/person
- Loss: 15–30%
- Used for soft fruits (mango, papaya, kiwi), irregular shapes
- Limitations: labor-intensive, inconsistent thickness, hygiene risk

**(ii) Mechanical Peeling (1.5 marks):**
- **Abrasive Peeler:** Carborundum roller rotates at 200–600 rpm; surface abrasion removes peel
- **Knife Peeler:** Rotating knives cut peel at controlled depth
- Capacity: 500–3000 kg/hr
- Loss: 10–20%
- Used for potato, carrot, sweet potato, apple

**(iii) Chemical Peeling (1.5 marks):**
- Lye (NaOH/KOH) solution at 1–10% concentration, 60–100 °C
- Dissolves pectin in middle lamella → peel detaches
- Industry standard for tomato, peach, apricot
- Loss: 5–12%
- Major limitation: wastewater disposal

**(iv) Thermal Peeling (1.5 marks):**
- **Steam Peeling:** Saturated steam at 100–150 kPa for 10–60 sec → moisture under peel expands → peel ruptures
- **Hot Water Peeling:** Dip in 95–100 °C water for 30–120 sec
- **Flame Peeling:** Direct gas flame exposure for onions/garlic
- **Infrared Peeling:** IR radiation for surface heating
- Loss: 5–10%; widely used in tomato and potato industry

---

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks: Chemical (Lye) Peeling — Detailed Treatment**

**Chemistry (2 marks):**

Lye peeling works through two simultaneous chemical reactions:

**(a) Saponification of Cuticle Waxes:**
$$\text{Triglyceride} + 3\text{NaOH} \rightarrow \text{Glycerol} + 3\text{RCOONa}$$

**(b) Hydrolysis of Pectin in Middle Lamella:**
$$\text{Pectin} + \text{NaOH} \rightarrow \text{Sodium Pectate (soluble)}$$

The dissolution of pectin loosens the bond between peel and flesh, allowing peel removal.

**Process Parameters Table (2 marks):**

| Crop | NaOH (%) | Temp (°C) | Time (sec) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Tomato | 8–10 | 90–100 | 30–60 |
| Peach | 2–3 | 80–95 | 30–60 |
| Potato | 10–15 | 70–85 | 120–300 |
| Apple | 4–8 | 60–80 | 60–180 |

**Process Steps (2 marks):**
1. Sorting and washing of raw material
2. Pre-heating (optional) at 50–60 °C
3. Dipping in hot lye bath
4. Controlled residence time (conveyor speed)
5. High-pressure cold water spray rinse
6. Acid neutralization (dilute citric acid)
7. Roller brush scrubbing (optional)
8. Quality inspection

**Advantages and Limitations (1 mark):**

*Advantages:*
- Lowest product loss (5–12%)
- Uniform, smooth, glossy surface
- High capacity (continuous operation)
- Suitable for delicate fruits like peach

*Limitations:*
- High BOD/COD wastewater requires treatment
- Operator safety (NaOH is corrosive)
- Cannot be used for crops with thin or delicate skin

---

### **Question B — Option 2** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, PEAGT742]`

**"Describe in detail the various size reduction equipment used in horticultural crop processing. Explain blanching — its importance, objectives, and types with process parameters."**

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks: Size Reduction Equipment**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
Size reduction is a primary operation in post-harvest processing used to break bulk produce into smaller pieces for ease of handling, increasing surface area, and improving heat/mass transfer in downstream operations.

**Detailed Description (6 marks):**

**(i) Slicers (1.5 marks):**
- **Rotary Slicer:** Centrifugal disc with adjustable blades; output 2–10 mm thick discs
- **Guillotine Slicer:** Reciprocating blade; suitable for hard roots (carrot, potato, radish)
- Capacity: 200–2000 kg/hr

**(ii) Shredders (1.5 marks):**
- **Hammer Shredder:** Rotating hammers shred against perforated screen
- **Disc Shredder:** Rotating disc with grating surface
- Output: 1–5 mm wide strips
- Used for cabbage, papaya, carrot

**(iii) Crushers (1.5 marks):**
- **Roller Crusher:** Two counter-rotating rollers with adjustable gap
- **Hammer Mill:** High-speed hammers impact the material
- Used for grapes, berries, apple (juice making)
- Output: <5 mm coarse pulp

**(iv) Choppers (1 mark):**
- **Bowl Chopper:** Curved blades rotating inside a stationary bowl
- **Dicer:** Cuts material into uniform cubes (5–25 mm)
- Used for onion, garlic, beetroot, mango

**(v) Juice Extractors (0.5 mark):**
- **Screw Press:** Continuous screw compresses pulp against perforated barrel
- **Basket Press:** Batch pressing in perforated basket
- **Centrifugal Extractor:** High-RPM spinning separates juice from pulp
- Used for citrus, mango, pomegranate

---

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks: Blanching — Importance and Objectives**

**Definition (1 mark):**
Blanching is a short-duration thermal pre-treatment of fruits/vegetables using hot water (85–100 °C) or steam (100 °C) for 1–10 minutes, followed by rapid cooling, to inactivate enzymes and preserve quality.

**Importance (2 marks):**
- Essential pre-treatment before **freezing, drying, and canning**
- Without blanching, frozen/dried products develop **off-flavors, browning, and nutrient loss**
- Extends shelf life by 2–5 times
- Industry standard in peas, beans, cauliflower, potato, carrot processing

**Primary Objectives (2 marks):**

1. **Enzyme Inactivation:** POD, PPO, catalase inactivated. POD is most heat-resistant — its inactivation is the quality benchmark.

2. **Microbial Reduction:** 1–2 log cycle reduction in surface microbial load.

3. **Texture and Color Improvement:** Fixes green color (chlorophyllin), expels trapped gases, prevents browning.

4. **Pre-cooking Effect:** Softens tissue for canning; removes raw flavor.

**Types and Process Parameters (2 marks):**

| Type | Temperature | Time | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|
| Hot Water Blanching | 85–100 °C | 1–10 min | Most common; leaching loss |
| Steam Blanching | 100 °C | 1–5 min | Less nutrient loss |
| Microwave Blanching | — | 30 sec–3 min | Energy efficient, uniform |
| Solution Blanching | 85–100 °C | 2–10 min | Water + 1–2% salt/sugar |

**Cooling Step (Important):**
After blanching, produce must be **rapidly cooled** (≤5 °C) to stop cooking and prevent texture softening. This is called **"shock cooling"** using cold water or air blast.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing peeling with blanching:** Many students mix up the two operations. Peeling removes *outer skin*; blanching *inactivates enzymes inside the tissue*. They are different unit operations.
>
> 2. **Wrong equation for chemical peeling:** Writing "NaOH + skin → skin dissolved" is too vague. Always mention **saponification** and **pectin hydrolysis** as the two specific reactions.
>
> 3. **Missing peroxidase (POD) test:** When asked about blanching adequacy, students often forget that **POD inactivation = adequate blanching** because POD is the most heat-resistant enzyme.
>
> 4. **No mention of "shock cooling"** after blanching — KTU examiners often deduct 1 mark for missing this critical step.
>
> 5. **No engineering metrics:** Writing only theoretical concepts without numerical quantification (capacity in kg/hr, loss in %, temperature in °C) loses marks. Always include **quantitative data**.
>
> 6. **Steam peeling vs hot water peeling** confusion — Steam peeling is done under pressure (10–60 sec), hot water peeling is at atmospheric immersion (1–5 min).
>
> 7. **Confusing size reduction terms:** Shredding (long thin strips) ≠ Chopping (small irregular pieces) ≠ Slicing (uniform flat discs).

---

## 🎯 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

### 🔑 Quick Revision Checklist

✅ **Peeling** = Removal of outer skin/cover of horticultural produce  
✅ **Four Methods:** Manual | Mechanical (abrasive/knife) | Chemical (lye) | Thermal (steam/flame)  
✅ **Manual Peeling:** 20–50 kg/hr, 15–30% loss, used for soft fruits  
✅ **Mechanical Peeling:** Abrasive roller (200–600 rpm), knife peeler, 500–3000 kg/hr  
✅ **Chemical Peeling:** NaOH 1–10%, 60–100 °C; saponification + pectin hydrolysis  
✅ **Thermal Peeling:** Steam (100–150 kPa, 10–60 sec); loss 5–10% (lowest)  
✅ **Peeling Loss Formula:** $(W_1 - W_2)/W_1 \times 100$  
✅ **Peeling Efficiency:** Mass peel removed / Total peel mass × 100  
✅ **Slicing:** Rotary slicer, guillotine — 2–10 mm thickness  
✅ **Shredding:** Hammer/disc shredder — 1–5 mm width strips  
✅ **Crushing:** Roller crusher, hammer mill — for juice making  
✅ **Chopping:** Bowl chopper, dicer — 5–25 mm chunks  
✅ **Juice Extraction:** Screw press (continuous), Basket press (batch), Centrifugal  
✅ **Blanching Definition:** Short thermal pre-treatment (1–10 min) at 85–100 °C  
✅ **Enzymes Inactivated:** POD (most heat-resistant), PPO, catalase, lipoxygenase  
✅ **Peroxidase Test:** Standard QC test for blanching adequacy  
✅ **Blanching Types:** Hot water | Steam | Microwave | Solution  
✅ **Shock Cooling:** Mandatory after blanching to stop cooking  

### 📌 Numerical Values to Memorize

| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| NaOH for tomato peeling | 8–10%, 90–100 °C, 30–60 sec |
| NaOH for potato peeling | 10–15%, 70–85 °C, 120–300 sec |
| Steam pressure (thermal peeling) | 100–150 kPa gauge |
| Abrasive roller speed | 200–600 rpm |
| Latent heat of steam (100 °C) | 2257 kJ/kg |
| Specific heat of tomato | ~3.95 kJ/(kg·K) |
| Blanching temperature | 85–100 °C |
| Blanching time | 1–10 minutes |
| Steam requirement for tomato peeling | 0.15–0.25 kg steam/kg produce |
| Ideal peeling loss | <15–20% |

### 🧠 KTU Frequently Tested Concepts

1. **Peeling Loss vs Peeling Efficiency** — these are NOT the same; peeling efficiency refers to removal of total peel, while peeling loss is product mass removed.
2. **Why NaOH peels but not HCl?** Because NaOH dissolves pectin and saponifies waxes; HCl only attacks minerals and is less effective on organic peel.
3. **Why steam peeling preferred over lye for potatoes?** Steam does not generate chemical wastewater; potatoes have hard skin that responds well to thermal shock.
4. **Difference between blanching and pasteurization:** Blanching targets **enzymes** (short time, lower temp in some cases); pasteurization targets **microbes** (specific time-temp combination to achieve log reduction).
5. **Microwave blanching advantage:** Faster, more uniform, less leaching of water-soluble nutrients.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Tip:** When answering 14-mark questions, always use **headings, sub-points, tables, and where possible, a flow diagram or numerical example**. Examiners award marks based on **clarity of structure, technical accuracy, and inclusion of engineering data (capacity, temperature, time, %).** Aim for at least **one equation, one table, and 3–4 sub-headings** in every 14-mark answer.

---

topics.title: Blanching Methods; Effects on Food (Nutrition, Colour, Pigment, Texture)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAGT742 - POST HARVEST ENGINEERING OF HORTICULTURAL CROPS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Blanching Methods; Effects on Food (Nutrition, Colour, Pigment, Texture)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Blanching

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Blanching** is a thermal pre-treatment process in food processing wherein horticultural produce (fruits and vegetables) is subjected to a brief exposure to hot water, steam, hot air, microwave energy, or chemical agents at temperatures typically between **75 °C and 95 °C for 1–10 minutes**, with the primary objectives of **inactivating degradative enzymes, expelling intercellular gases, fixing colour, and softening tissue structure** prior to further processing operations such as freezing, canning, drying, or dehydration.

The process is a critical unit operation in the post-harvest engineering chain because the cellular integrity of fresh produce continues to live metabolically even after harvest. Enzymes such as **Polyphenol Oxidase (PPO)**, **Peroxidase (POD)**, **Catalase (CAT)**, and **Lipoxygenase (LOX)** continue to act on substrates within the cut/harvested tissue, leading to rapid quality deterioration.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor:** This topic belongs to **Module 1: Principles of Post-Harvest Engineering of Horticultural Crops**, with specific emphasis on thermal processing fundamentals. Expect 14-mark analytical questions linking blanching to downstream operations (canning/freezing).

## 1.2 Intuitive Overview — The "Real-World Analogy"

Imagine you have just plucked a fresh **green bean** from your kitchen garden. You are about to freeze it for later use. If you put the raw bean directly into the freezer:

- A few weeks later, when you thaw it, the bean will have turned **olive-brown** (bitter taste).
- Its **bright green colour** will fade to a dull yellow-grey.
- Its texture will be **limp and rubbery**, and it will exude a grassy, "off" odour.

**Why?** The cell's internal enzymes never received the "shutdown signal" before freezing. Freezing only *slows* enzymes, it does *not* destroy them.

**Blanching = Giving the cells a brief, controlled "Hot Water Bath Shock"** to deactivate these rogue enzymes *before* you freeze the food. Think of it as a **"System Reboot"** for the plant tissue — the heat denatures the protein-based enzymes (permanently altering their 3-D shape), the trapped air between cells rushes out (replaced by water), and the cell walls soften slightly so that subsequent drying/freezing becomes faster and more uniform.

> [!TIP]
> **The Three Functional Goals of Blanching (Mnemonic: G-E-C):**
> - **G** – Gas expulsion from intercellular spaces (improves heat transfer in canning).
> - **E** – Enzyme inactivation (PPO, POD, catalase, lipoxygenase).
> - **C** – Colour fixation and texture pre-conditioning.

## 1.3 Engineering Boundaries of the Operation

| Parameter | Typical Range / Value | Engineering Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Blanching Temperature** | **75 °C – 100 °C** | Determines enzyme destruction rate |
| **Residence Time** | **1 – 10 minutes** | Inversely related to temperature |
| **Product Size / Geometry** | 5 – 50 mm pieces | Governs heat penetration depth |
| **Moisture Loss (Steam method)** | 2 – 5 % w/w | Economic loss in dry products |
| **Moisture Gain (Water method)** | 5 – 15 % w/w | Affects drying load downstream |
| **Reference Peroxidase Residual Activity** | < 3 – 5 % | Industry-accepted destruction benchmark |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Misconception:** Blanching is *not* a cooking step. It is a *partial-thermal-pre-treatment* meant to inactivate enzymes *without* fully cooking the product. Over-blanching causes nutritional loss and texture damage — the engineering challenge is to find the **minimum time-temperature combination** that achieves the required enzyme inactivation.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Blanching Methods

The choice of blanching method is a classic **Engineering Trade-Off** problem in post-harvest engineering. Each method has distinct advantages, disadvantages, and process signatures.

### 2.1.1 Hot Water Blanching (Immersion Method)

- **Principle:** Product is immersed in water maintained at **75 °C – 100 °C**.
- **Mechanism:** Heat transfer occurs by **convection** at the surface and **conduction** through the bulk tissue.
- **Mode of Heat Transfer:** Forced convection (with agitation) → high surface heat transfer coefficient (**h ≈ 500 – 2000 W/m²·K**).
- **Advantages:**
  * Simple, low capital cost.
  * Uniform heating.
  * Effective gas removal.
- **Disadvantages:**
  * **Leaching loss** of water-soluble nutrients (Vitamin C, B-complex, sugars, mineral ions).
  * Effluent generation (high BOD/COD load on wastewater).
  * Net moisture gain increases downstream drying cost.
- **Variants:**
  * *Atmospheric Hot Water Blanching* (75 – 100 °C)
  * *High-Temperature Short-Time (HTST) Water Blanching* (continuous, 90 – 100 °C, 1 – 3 min)

### 2.1.2 Steam Blanching

- **Principle:** Product is exposed to **saturated steam at 100 °C (or slightly higher under pressure)** for a defined residence time.
- **Mechanism:** Heat transfer by **condensation of steam** on the product surface — latent heat release gives very high surface heat transfer coefficient (**h ≈ 5000 – 10000 W/m²·K**).
- **Advantages:**
  * Minimal leaching of nutrients (no direct water contact).
  * Lower moisture gain (in fact, slight moisture *loss* occurs).
  * Rapid surface heating.
- **Disadvantages:**
  * Non-uniform heating (especially for large/dense pieces).
  * Higher capital cost.
  * Risk of *case-hardening* on the surface.

### 2.1.3 Microwave Blanching (Dielectric Heating)

- **Principle:** Product is exposed to electromagnetic waves at **915 MHz or 2450 MHz** (industrial/scientific frequencies).
- **Mechanism:** Heat is generated **volumetrically** inside the product by dipolar rotation of water molecules and ionic conduction — heating is rapid and uniform *throughout* the piece.
- **Advantages:**
  * Rapid, uniform internal heating.
  * No leaching, no effluent.
  * Better retention of heat-sensitive nutrients.
- **Disadvantages:**
  * High capital cost.
  * Risk of *thermal runaway* and local burning.
  * Penetration depth limited (~1 – 3 cm in dense tissues).
  * Textural damage (cell wall rupture) if over-processed.

### 2.1.4 Chemical Blanching

- **Principle:** Use of chemical agents to inactivate enzymes or modify tissue.
- **Common Agents:** **Sulphur dioxide (SO₂)**, **Sodium metabisulphite (Na₂S₂O₅)**, **Citric acid**, **Ascorbic acid**.
- **Typical Conditions:** Dipping in 0.1 – 1 % SO₂ solution for 5 – 30 minutes.
- **Use Case:** Primarily for **fruits** where colour and aroma retention are critical (e.g., dried apricots, raisins).
- **KTU Exam Note:** Always mention **GRAS status** (Generally Recognized As Safe) and regulatory limits (e.g., FDA permits up to 2000 ppm SO₂ in finished dried fruit).

### 2.1.5 Other Methods (Brief)

- **Hot Air / Gas Blanching:** Used for onion, garlic; 50 – 70 °C, longer time.
- **Infrared Blanching:** Surface-focused, used for thin pieces.
- **Ohmic Blanching:** Electric current passes *through* the food (like a resistor); volumetric heating.

## 2.2 The Three Classical Effects of Blanching

### A. Effect on Nutrition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Blanching is a **double-edged sword** for nutrition. It both *destroys* certain nutrients (heat-labile) and *preserves* others (by halting enzymatic degradation).

- **Losses:**
  * **Vitamin C (Ascorbic acid):** Most heat-labile vitamin; losses of **10 – 60 %** depending on method (water > steam > microwave).
  * **B-complex vitamins** (Thiamine, Riboflavin, Folic acid) — partly water-soluble, partly heat-sensitive.
  * **Minerals** (K⁺, Mg²⁺, Ca²⁺) — only in water blanching (leaching).
- **Preservation:**
  * **Vitamin A (β-carotene)** and **Lycopene** — blanching actually *increases* their bioavailability by disrupting cell walls and denaturing the protein-carotenoid complexes.
  * Halts enzymatic degradation that would otherwise consume antioxidants.

### B. Effect on Colour and Pigments

The colour changes are mediated by **enzyme inactivation** and **chemical transformations of native pigments**.

#### Pigment 1: Chlorophyll (Green Colour)

- **Chemistry:** Chlorophyll *a* (blue-green) and *b* (yellow-green) → on heating, the **Mg²⁺ ion in the porphyrin centre is replaced by H⁺ ions**, converting chlorophyll to **pheophytin** (olive-brown).
- **Effect of Blanching:**
  * **Detrimental:** Green vegetables (peas, spinach, beans) often turn olive-brown if blanched at pH < 7.
  * **Beneficial — with additives:** Adding **NaHCO₃ or MgO** to the blanching water maintains pH > 7 and preserves the green colour by **forming Mg²⁺-stabilized chlorophyll derivatives (chlorophyllin)**.
  * Blanching also **deactivates PPO**, preventing enzymatic browning that would further darken the colour.

#### Pigment 2: Carotenoids (Yellow/Orange/Red)

- Examples: **β-carotene** (carrot, mango), **Lycopene** (tomato), **Xanthophylls** (maize).
- **Effect of Blanching:** Carotenoids are **heat-stable** but become **more bioavailable** because:
  * Cell wall disruption releases bound pigments.
  * Protein-pigment complexes are denatured.
  * Blanching deactivates **lipoxygenase (LOX)**, which would otherwise oxidize carotenoids to colourless products.

#### Pigment 3: Anthocyanins (Red/Purple/Blue)

- Examples: Anthocyanins in **berry fruits, brinjal (eggplant), red cabbage**.
- **Effect of Blanching:** Highly heat-sensitive; significant losses at > 70 °C.
- **pH Sensitivity:** Stable at low pH (red), fades at neutral/alkaline pH.
- **Engineering Implication:** Fruits rich in anthocyanins should be blanched at **lower temperatures and shorter times**, or with the addition of organic acids (citric acid) to maintain pH < 4.

### C. Effect on Texture

> [!NOTE]
> **Texture change** in blanching is governed by the **Pectic Substances** in the middle lamella of cell walls.

- **Native Pectin:** Present as **Protopectin** (insoluble), which provides rigidity to fresh tissue.
- **On Heating:**
  * **β-elimination reaction** (especially at high pH, high temperature) → pectin chain breakdown → tissue softening.
  * **Demethylation** of pectin by **Pectin Methyl Esterase (PME)** — initially *increases* firmness (Ca²⁺ cross-bridge formation) but then PME is itself inactivated.
  * **Activation of Polygalacturonase (PG)** — hydrolyses pectin → severe softening.
- **Engineering Outcome:**
  * **Controlled blanching** can *firm* tissue (e.g., for sliced apples used in pie filling — pre-firming with CaCl₂).
  * **Over-blanching** causes *mushy* texture, which is detrimental for whole-vegetable products (peas, beans).

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!TIP]
> **Master these 6 equations — they appear frequently in 14-mark analytical questions.**

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| **1** | $q = h \cdot A \cdot (T_s - T_{\infty})$ | Convective heat flux (surface heating) | W |
| **2** | $\alpha = \dfrac{k}{\rho \cdot c_p}$ | Thermal diffusivity (internal heat conduction) | m²/s |
| **3** | $Fo = \dfrac{\alpha \cdot t}{L^2}$ | Fourier number (dimensionless time) | — |
| **4** | $Bi = \dfrac{h \cdot L}{k}$ | Biot number (ratio of internal to external resistance) | — |
| **5** | $\ln \left( \dfrac{C}{C_0} \right) = -k_d \cdot t$ | First-order enzyme inactivation kinetics (D-value basis) | — |
| **6** | $D_T = D_{ref} \cdot 10^{\left( \dfrac{T_{ref} - T}{z} } \right)$ | Decimal reduction time relation (z-value model) | min, °C |

> [!WARNING]
> **Equation 5 & 6 Notation:** $C_0$ = initial enzyme activity, $C$ = activity after time $t$, $k_d$ = inactivation rate constant (min⁻¹), $D_T$ = time for 90 % inactivation at temp $T$, $z$ = temperature change required for a 10-fold change in $D_T$ (typically **5 – 10 °C for POD**).

## 2.4 Heat Transfer Regime and Process Signatures

| Method | Dominant Heat Transfer Mode | $h$ (W/m²·K) | Penetration Pattern |
|---|---|---|---|
| Hot Water (agitated) | Forced convection | 500 – 2000 | Surface to centre |
| Steam (condensing) | Condensation | 5000 – 10000 | Rapid surface heating |
| Microwave | Dielectric volumetric | (internal) | Uniform throughout |
| Hot Air | Natural/forced conv. | 10 – 100 | Slow, drying type |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The principles learned here directly scale up to industrial systems used by companies like **Döhler, Agrana, SVZ, and Tropicana**. The selection of blanching method for a given product is a **multi-criteria optimization** that involves:
- **Nutrient Retention Index (NRI)**
- **Texture Quality Index (TQI)**
- **Energy Efficiency (kWh/tonne)**
- **Water Footprint (m³/tonne)**
- **Effluent Treatment Cost (BOD removal)**

These form the basis of a **Life Cycle Assessment (LCA)** in sustainable food processing — a frequent viva/short-question topic in KTU 2024 exams.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Temperature Profile Inside a Slab During Water Blanching (Lumped Capacitance → Bi-Correction)

### Problem Setup
A **carrot cube of side length 2L = 2 cm** is immersed in hot water at **T∞ = 90 °C**. The initial temperature is T₀ = 25 °C. The thermal conductivity of carrot is **k = 0.55 W/m·K**, density **ρ = 980 kg/m³**, specific heat **cp = 3900 J/kg·K**, surface heat transfer coefficient **h = 1000 W/m²·K**.

### Step 1: Characteristic Length

For a slab (one-dimensional heat flow from both faces), the characteristic length is:
$$L_c = \dfrac{V}{A_s} = \dfrac{2L \cdot 2L \cdot 2L}{2 \cdot (2L \cdot 2L)} = L$$

So with **2L = 0.02 m**, we have **Lc = 0.01 m**.

### Step 2: Check Biot Number Validity

Compute Bi:
$$Bi = \dfrac{h \cdot L_c}{k}$$

Substituting:
$$Bi = \dfrac{1000 \cdot 0.01}{0.55}$$

Performing the multiplication in numerator and division:
$$Bi = \dfrac{10}{0.55} = 18.18$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Bi ≫ 0.1**, so the **Lumped Capacitance Method is NOT valid**. We must use the **One-Term Approximation of the Transient Conduction Series**.

### Step 3: Apply the One-Term Approximation

For a slab, the temperature at the **thermal centre (x = 0)** is:
$$\dfrac{T_0 - T_{\infty}}{T - T_{\infty}} = \dfrac{\theta_0}{\theta} = A_1 \cdot \exp(-\lambda_1^2 \cdot Fo)$$

where:
- $A_1$ and $\lambda_1$ are the first eigenvalues (functions of Bi).
- $Fo$ is the Fourier number.

For $Bi = 18.18$, from the standard slab-eigenvalue table (Heisler charts):
$$A_1 \approx 1.2730, \quad \lambda_1 \approx 1.5995$$

### Step 4: Time Required for Centre to Reach 80 °C

We require T (centre) = 80 °C:
$$\dfrac{25 - 90}{80 - 90} = \dfrac{-65}{-10} = 6.5$$

So the dimensionless temperature ratio is **6.5**. Setting up:
$$6.5 = 1.2730 \cdot \exp(-1.5995^2 \cdot Fo)$$

Computing the square:
$$1.5995^2 = 2.5584$$

So:
$$6.5 = 1.2730 \cdot \exp(-2.5584 \cdot Fo)$$

Dividing both sides:
$$\dfrac{6.5}{1.2730} = \exp(-2.5584 \cdot Fo)$$

$$5.1061 = \exp(-2.5584 \cdot Fo)$$

Taking natural logarithm:
$$\ln(5.1061) = -2.5584 \cdot Fo$$

$$1.6302 = -2.5584 \cdot Fo$$

$$Fo = -\dfrac{1.6302}{2.5584} = -0.6372$$

The negative sign arises because we have defined the temperature ratio in the inverted sense; the **physical Fourier number is |Fo| = 0.6372**.

### Step 5: Convert Fo to Real Time

The Fourier number definition is:
$$Fo = \dfrac{\alpha \cdot t}{L_c^2}$$

Solving for $t$:
$$t = \dfrac{Fo \cdot L_c^2}{\alpha}$$

First, compute thermal diffusivity:
$$\alpha = \dfrac{k}{\rho \cdot c_p} = \dfrac{0.55}{980 \cdot 3900}$$

Computing the denominator:
$$980 \cdot 3900 = 3{,}822{,}000 \, \text{J/m}^3\text{·K}$$

$$\alpha = \dfrac{0.55}{3{,}822{,}000} = 1.439 \times 10^{-7} \, \text{m}^2/\text{s}$$

Now:
$$t = \dfrac{0.6372 \cdot (0.01)^2}{1.439 \times 10^{-7}}$$

$$t = \dfrac{0.6372 \cdot 10^{-4}}{1.439 \times 10^{-7}} = \dfrac{6.372 \times 10^{-5}}{1.439 \times 10^{-7}}$$

$$t = 442.9 \, \text{seconds} \approx 7.4 \, \text{minutes}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Conclusion:** Even a small 2 cm carrot cube requires **~7.4 minutes of hot water blanching** for the *centre* to reach 80 °C — the entire *blanching* (enzyme destruction) time must therefore exceed this value, but the **surface** would have been at 90 °C for almost the entire duration, leading to **over-cooking at the surface**. This is precisely why industrial blanchers use **agitation, slicing, or steam**, to overcome this limitation.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — First-Order Enzyme Inactivation (POD Inactivation Kinetics)

### Governing Equation
The inactivation of an enzyme (e.g., peroxidase) generally follows **first-order kinetics**:
$$-\dfrac{dC}{dt} = k_d \cdot C$$

### Step 1: Rearrange and Integrate
$$\dfrac{dC}{C} = -k_d \, dt$$

Integrating from $C_0$ at $t=0$ to $C$ at $t=t$:
$$\int_{C_0}^{C} \dfrac{dC}{C} = -k_d \int_0^t dt$$

$$\ln(C) - \ln(C_0) = -k_d \cdot t$$

$$\ln\left(\dfrac{C}{C_0}\right) = -k_d \cdot t$$

### Step 2: Convert to Residual Activity
$$R = \dfrac{C}{C_0} = \exp(-k_d \cdot t)$$

### Step 3: Apply Arrhenius Temperature Dependence
$$k_d = k_{d,ref} \cdot \exp\left[\dfrac{E_a}{R_g}\left(\dfrac{1}{T_{ref}} - \dfrac{1}{T}\right)\right]$$

where $E_a$ = activation energy (J/mol), $R_g$ = 8.314 J/mol·K, $T$ in Kelvin.

### Step 4: Worked Numerical Example
**Problem:** A batch of peas is blanched at **85 °C**. The activation energy for POD is **E_a = 80 kJ/mol**, and the reference rate constant at 70 °C is **k_d,ref = 0.04 min⁻¹**. Find the time to achieve **90 % inactivation** (i.e., R = 0.10).

**Solution:**

Step a: Compute the Arrhenius factor.
$$\dfrac{E_a}{R_g}\left(\dfrac{1}{T_{ref}} - \dfrac{1}{T}\right) = \dfrac{80000}{8.314} \left(\dfrac{1}{343.15} - \dfrac{1}{358.15}\right)$$

$$= 9622.6 \cdot (0.002914 - 0.002792)$$

$$= 9622.6 \cdot (0.000122) = 1.174$$

Step b: Compute $k_d$ at 85 °C.
$$k_d = 0.04 \cdot \exp(1.174) = 0.04 \cdot 3.236 = 0.1294 \, \text{min}^{-1}$$

Step c: Solve for time to reach 90 % inactivation.
$$0.10 = \exp(-0.1294 \cdot t)$$

$$\ln(0.10) = -0.1294 \cdot t$$

$$-2.3026 = -0.1294 \cdot t$$

$$t = \dfrac{2.3026}{0.1294} = 17.8 \, \text{minutes}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Why Peroxidase (POD) is the Benchmark Enzyme:** POD is the **most heat-stable** of the common food enzymes. If POD is inactivated, it is generally accepted that all other enzymes (PPO, catalase, LOX) are also inactivated. Industry uses **3 % residual POD activity** as the quality control endpoint for blanching.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Process Selection

> [!NOTE]
> This matrix is a **favourite KTU 14-mark question** — students are often asked to "Compare any 3 blanching methods" or "Select the best method for a given product with justification."

| Criterion | Hot Water | Steam | Microwave | Chemical |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Nutrient Loss** | High (leaching) | Low–Moderate | Very Low | Variable |
| **Energy Efficiency** | Moderate (latent + sensible) | Moderate | High (volumetric) | Low (chemicals) |
| **Water Use** | High (1.5–2 m³/tonne) | Low | Negligible | Moderate |
| **Effluent Generation** | High BOD/COD | Low | None | Chemical-rich |
| **Equipment Cost** | Low | Moderate | High | Low |
| **Texture Impact** | Softens (good for canning) | Slight firming | Variable | Minimal |
| **Colour Preservation** | Good (with additives) | Excellent | Excellent | Good |
| **Throughput** | Continuous possible | Continuous possible | Batch/continuous | Batch |
| **Best Suited For** | Peas, beans, carrots | Leafy greens, broccoli | High-value cut fruits | Fruits for drying |

### Decision Logic Tree for Process Selection
$$\text{Product type} \rightarrow \begin{cases} \text{Vegetable} \rightarrow \text{Steam/Water} \\ \text{Fruit} \rightarrow \text{Chemical/Microwave} \\ \text{Leafy} \rightarrow \text{Steam (water strips leaves)} \end{cases}$$

$$\text{Priority} \rightarrow \begin{cases} \text{Nutrition} \rightarrow \text{Microwave} \\ \text{Colour} \rightarrow \text{Steam + pH control} \\ \text{Throughput} \rightarrow \text{Continuous Steam} \end{cases}$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Generalized Blanching Line

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Horticultural Produce] --> B[Sorting and Grading]
    B --> C[Cleaning and Washing]
    C --> D[Size Reduction<br>Slicing or Dicing]
    D --> E{Blanching Method Selection}
    E --> F1[Hot Water Blancher<br>75 to 100 degree C]
    E --> F2[Steam Blancher<br>Saturated steam 100 degree C]
    E --> F3[Microwave Blancher<br>915 or 2450 MHz]
    E --> F4[Chemical Blancher<br>SO2 or Citric Acid]
    F1 --> G[Residence Time Control<br>1 to 10 minutes]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    F4 --> G
    G --> H[Cooling Stage<br>Cold water or Air blast]
    H --> I[Quality Check<br>Residual POD activity]
    I --> J[Downstream Processing<br>Canning or Freezing or Drying]
    J --> K[Packaged Finished Product]
```

## 4.2 Process Architecture — Effect Cascade of Blanching on Food Quality

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[Input Conditions]
        P1[Fresh Tissue]
        P2[Active Enzymes<br>PPO, POD, LOX]
        P3[Native Pigments<br>Chlorophyll, Carotenoids]
        P4[Intact Pectin Matrix]
    end

    subgraph BLANCH[Blanching Operation 75 to 100 degree C]
        OP1[Heat Application]
        OP2[Protein Denaturation]
        OP3[Gas Expulsion]
        OP4[Pectin Modification]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT[Output Quality Attributes]
        Q1[Nutrition<br>Vitamin C Loss vs Beta Carotene Gain]
        Q2[Colour<br>Pheophytin Formation vs Anthocyanin Loss]
        Q3[Texture<br>Pectin Beta Elimination]
        Q4[Microbial Load Reduction]
    end

    P1 --> OP1
    P2 --> OP2
    P3 --> OP1
    P4 --> OP4
    OP1 --> Q1
    OP2 --> Q2
    OP3 --> Q4
    OP4 --> Q3
```

## 4.3 Decision Flow — Why POD is the Quality Indicator

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Start: Product enters blancher] --> T[Heat treatment begins]
    T --> Q1{Catalase inactivated?}
    Q1 -->|No early stage| R1[Continue heating]
    Q1 -->|Yes| Q2{PPO inactivated?}
    Q2 -->|Yes but POD active| C1[Risk: quality loss in storage]
    C1 --> R2[Continue heating to inactivate POD]
    R2 --> Q3{POD residual activity less than 3 percent}
    Q3 -->|No| R3[Extend blanching time]
    Q3 -->|Yes| E[Blanching complete - move to cooling]
    E --> STOP[Quality benchmark achieved]
```

## 4.4 Heat Transfer Zones Within a Blanched Particle

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ZONE[Cross Section of Carrot Cube During Blanching]
        direction TB
        L1[Outer Surface<br>Temperature near 90 degree C<br>High PPO destruction]
        L2[Middle Layer<br>Temperature 60 to 85 degree C<br>Partial enzyme inactivation]
        L3[Thermal Centre<br>Temperature rises slowly<br>Last zone to reach target]
    end
    L1 --> L2
    L2 --> L3
```

> [!TIP]
> **Mermaid Compilation Tip for Students:** The above diagrams use only alphanumeric node IDs prefixed with letters (e.g., `L1`, `OP2`, `Q3`) and **no markdown formatting inside double-quoted labels**, complying with the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE safety rules.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, May 2024]
**Define blanching. List any four objectives of blanching in post-harvest processing of horticultural crops.**

**Model Answer:**
Blanching is a thermal pre-treatment process in which horticultural produce is exposed to hot water, steam, or other heating media at 75 – 100 °C for 1 – 10 minutes before further processing.

**Four objectives:**
1. Inactivation of degradative enzymes — **Polyphenol Oxidase (PPO), Peroxidase (POD), Lipoxygenase (LOX)**.
2. Expulsion of intercellular gases, improving heat transfer in subsequent canning operations.
3. Fixation of natural colour (chlorophyll, carotenoids).
4. Reduction of initial microbial load and softening of tissue for ease of further processing.

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Explain why Peroxidase (POD) is used as the quality control indicator enzyme for blanching.**

**Model Answer:**
Peroxidase (POD) is the **most heat-resistant** of the common deteriorative enzymes in horticultural produce. The thermal inactivation rate of POD is the slowest among PPO, catalase, LOX, and POD. Therefore, when a blanching process is designed to reduce POD activity to **≤ 3 % of its original value**, it is guaranteed that all other enzymes have been inactivated. This makes POD a **conservative benchmark** ensuring product quality during subsequent storage.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A: [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**A batch of green peas is to be steam-blanched at 100 °C. With neat sketches, describe the steam blanching process. Discuss in detail the effects of blanching on (a) nutritional quality, (b) colour and pigments of green peas. (14 Marks)**

#### Part (a) — Process Description (7 Marks)

**Steam Blanching Process for Green Peas:**

1. **Pre-conditioning:** Freshly harvested peas are cleaned, graded, and washed. Moisture on the surface is removed by gentle vibration.
2. **Loading:** Peas are fed via a vibratory conveyor onto a **perforated, rotating cylindrical screen** (the steam blancher drum), or onto a **belt conveyor** that passes through a steam chamber.
3. **Steam Application:** **Saturated steam at 100 °C (or 105 – 110 °C under slight pressure)** is injected from above and below the product bed. Residence time is controlled to **1.5 – 3 minutes** by varying the conveyor speed.
4. **Heat Transfer Mechanism:** Steam condenses on the cold surface of peas, releasing its **latent heat of vaporization (2257 kJ/kg)**, which is rapidly absorbed by the product. The surface heat transfer coefficient is very high (**h ≈ 5000 – 10000 W/m²·K**).
5. **Enzyme Destruction:** Internal temperature rises rapidly, and POD activity is reduced to ≤ 3 %.
6. **Exit and Cooling:** Peas exit the steam chamber onto a vibrating discharge chute and pass into a **cold-water spray cooler** to halt residual cooking.

**Key Advantages over hot water:** No leaching of Vitamin C or sugars; no effluent load; net moisture *loss* of 2 – 5 % w/w (unlike water blanching, which causes moisture *gain*).

#### Part (b) — Effects on Nutrition and Colour (7 Marks)

**A. Effect on Nutrition of Green Peas:**

- **Vitamin C (Ascorbic acid):** Peas contain 14 – 30 mg/100 g of Vitamin C. Steam blanching losses are **15 – 25 %** (vs. **30 – 50 %** for hot water blanching). The shorter contact time and absence of water leaching are the key reasons.
- **B-complex vitamins:** Thiamine (B1) loss ≈ 10 – 15 %; riboflavin (B2) loss ≈ 5 – 10 %.
- **β-carotene (provitamin A):** No loss; in fact, **bioavailability increases** because the protein-pigment complex is denatured.
- **Proteins:** Partial denaturation (5 – 10 %) but nutritional value largely retained.
- **Minerals:** Negligible loss (no leaching).
- **Net Effect:** Steam blanching is the **preferred method for peas** from a nutritional retention standpoint.

**B. Effect on Colour and Pigments:**

- **Native pigment:** Chlorophyll (a + b), giving the green colour.
- **On heating:** Mg²⁺ ion in the porphyrin ring is replaced by H⁺ → formation of **pheophytin** (olive-brown) → undesirable colour change.
- **Mitigation by Blanching Control:**
  * Maintain **pH 7 – 8** in any residual tissue moisture by adding **NaHCO₃ (0.1 – 0.5 %)** or **MgO** to the steam supply → preserves green colour.
  * **Rapid heating** (steam) minimizes the time during which chlorophyll exists in the transition state.
- **Effect on PPO:** Blanching **destroys PPO**, which would otherwise oxidize phenolic compounds to **o-quinones**, leading to **enzymatic browning (brown/black discoloration)** — prevented by blanching.
- **Result:** Properly steam-blanched peas retain their **bright green colour**, which is fixed in the subsequent freezing step.

---

### Question B: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Alternative Choice)]

**Compare the following blanching methods: (i) Hot water blanching, (ii) Steam blanching, and (iii) Microwave blanching. Discuss their effects on texture and pigments of horticultural produce. (14 Marks)**

#### Part (a) — Comparative Description (7 Marks)

| Feature | Hot Water | Steam | Microwave |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Temperature** | 75 – 100 °C | 100 °C (saturated) | Internal — variable |
| **Heat Transfer Mode** | Forced convection (h ≈ 500–2000) | Condensation (h ≈ 5000–10000) | Dielectric volumetric |
| **Time** | 2 – 8 min | 1.5 – 3 min | 30 s – 3 min |
| **Nutrient Loss** | High (leaching) | Low | Very low |
| **Water Footprint** | High | Low | Negligible |
| **Equipment Cost** | Low | Moderate | High |
| **Uniformity** | High (with agitation) | Moderate | High (volumetric) |
| **Effluent Load** | High (BOD 500–2000 mg/L) | Low | None |
| **Best For** | Carrot, beans, peas | Leafy greens, broccoli | Premium frozen fruits |

#### Part (b) — Effect on Texture and Pigments (7 Marks)

**A. Effect on Texture:**

- **Pectic substance transformation** is the central chemistry.
- **Hot water blanching:** Long contact with hot aqueous medium → extensive **β-elimination of pectin** → tissue softening. The cell wall **middle lamella** is hydrolysed; tissue becomes tender — **advantageous for canning** of whole peas, beans.
- **Steam blanching:** Shorter time, less moisture penetration → milder texture change. Vegetables retain more of their "bite" — preferred for **frozen vegetables** where texture retention is critical.
- **Microwave blanching:** Rapid internal heating; some cell rupture from internal steam pressure → initial softening, but if properly controlled, can give **uniform texture** without over-processing the surface.
- **Pectin Methyl Esterase (PME)** activation: In tomato, controlled blanching *activates* PME → demethylation → Ca²⁺ cross-bridges → **firming** — exploited in diced tomato for pizza toppings.

**B. Effect on Pigments:**

| Pigment | Hot Water | Steam | Microwave |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Chlorophyll** (green veg) | Converts to pheophytin → olive-brown (mitigated by pH control) | Same chemistry, but faster → better retention | Rapid, short time → best retention |
| **Carotenoids** (β-carotene, lycopene) | Stable; bioavailability improved | Stable; bioavailability improved | Stable; bioavailability improved |
| **Anthocyanins** (berry, brinjal) | High loss (60–80 %) | Moderate loss (30–50 %) | Lowest loss (10–30 %) |
| **Betalains** (beetroot) | Leaching (red colour in water) | Better retention | Best retention |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. ❌ **Forgetting the role of POD as the benchmark enzyme** — losing 2 marks.
> 2. ❌ **Confusing chlorophyll conversion (chemical) with enzymatic browning (PPO-mediated)** — these are *two different* mechanisms. Examiners explicitly test this distinction.
> 3. ❌ **Writing "blanching increases Vitamin C"** — Vitamin C is *destroyed* by blanching. Only carotenoid bioavailability increases. This is a common error.
> 4. ❌ **Failing to mention the time-temperature trade-off and Bi-number relevance** in 14-mark analytical questions.
> 5. ❌ **Not drawing a process flow diagram** — at least one sketch/diagram is mandatory in any 14-mark question for full marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU 2024:**

- ✅ Blanching is a **thermal pre-treatment**, **not a cooking step**.
- ✅ Operating window: **75 – 100 °C for 1 – 10 minutes**.
- ✅ **Four primary methods:** Hot water, Steam, Microwave, Chemical (SO₂).
- ✅ **Three goals:** Enzyme inactivation (G), Gas expulsion (E), Colour/texture fixing (C) — mnemonic **G-E-C**.
- ✅ **Peroxidase (POD)** is the **most heat-stable enzyme** → used as quality benchmark (≤ 3 % residual activity).
- ✅ **Chlorophyll → Pheophytin** is the central colour chemistry of green vegetables (Mg²⁺ → H⁺ substitution in porphyrin).
- ✅ **Carotenoids are heat-stable**; blanching *increases* their **bioavailability** by disrupting protein-pigment complexes.
- ✅ **Anthocyanins are heat-labile** → lowest stability among major food pigments.
- ✅ **Texture change** is governed by **pectin transformation** (β-elimination, demethylation by PME).
- ✅ **Lumped capacitance method** is valid **only if Bi < 0.1**; otherwise, use **Heisler chart / one-term approximation**.
- ✅ **Vitamin C is destroyed** by blanching; **Vitamin A/β-carotene bioavailability is improved**.
- ✅ **Hot water blanching = leaching loss**; **Steam = best balance**; **Microwave = highest nutrient retention + highest cost**.
- ✅ Key equations to memorize: $q = hA\Delta T$, $Fo = \alpha t / L^2$, $Bi = hL/k$, $\ln(C/C_0) = -k_d t$, and the Arrhenius form of $k_d$.
- ✅ **SO₂ chemical blanching** is GRAS up to **2000 ppm** in finished dried fruit (FDA standard).
- ✅ **Quality control endpoint:** residual POD activity ≤ 3 % → cooling → downstream processing.
- ✅ For **14-mark answers**, always include: process description, mechanism (chemistry), mathematical analysis (Fo, Bi, or kinetics), comparative table, and a labelled diagram.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Application of Refrigeration in Different Perishable Food Products
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAGT742 - POST HARVEST ENGINEERING OF HORTICULTURAL CROPS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Application of Refrigeration in Different Perishable Food Products

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Refrigeration in Post-Harvest Engineering** is defined as the controlled reduction and maintenance of product temperature to a level above the freezing point of the food tissue (typically **0 °C to 15 °C**) in order to suppress microbial, enzymatic, and biochemical activity, thereby extending the **shelf-life**, preserving the **organoleptic quality** (texture, flavour, colour), and reducing **post-harvest losses** of perishable horticultural produce.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Tone):**
> "Application of refrigeration in different perishable food products involves the use of cold storage, pre-cooling, chilling, and controlled atmosphere techniques to preserve fruits, vegetables, cut flowers, dairy, meat, fish, and bakery items by maintaining their optimum temperature and relative humidity."

The science is governed by the **Second Law of Thermodynamics**, where heat (Q) is extracted from a low-temperature reservoir (the food) and rejected to a high-temperature reservoir (ambient) using a **Refrigeration Cycle** — most commonly the **Vapour Compression Refrigeration Cycle (VCRC)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Perishable Categories in KTU Module 2:**
> 1. **Fruits & Vegetables (F&V)** — Highly perishable; sensitive to chilling injury.
> 2. **Floricultural Products (Cut Flowers)** — High respiration, very short vase life.
> 3. **Meat, Poultry & Fish** — High water activity, rapid microbial spoilage.
> 4. **Dairy Products (Milk, Cheese, Butter)** — Enzymatic and microbial degradation.
> 5. **Eggs** — Quality loss via CO₂ and moisture loss through shell pores.
> 6. **Bakery & Confectionery** — Staling, mould growth.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Biological Clock" Analogy

Think of every fruit, vegetable, or piece of meat as a **biological clock** that is ticking the moment it is harvested or slaughtered. Microorganisms, enzymes, and respiration are the **"hands" of this clock**, all moving faster when the temperature is high.

**Refrigeration acts like a refrigerator-pause button on this clock.** It does not stop decay forever, but it dramatically slows the hands down. Different foods have different clock speeds:

- **Fish** is like a **digital stopwatch** ticking at 50× normal speed at 25 °C. It spoils in hours. We must chill it to **0 °C** almost immediately.
- **Apples** are like a **mechanical wristwatch** — they tick slowly even at room temperature and can last weeks/months if cooled to **0 °C–4 °C**.
- **Tropical fruits like banana** are like a **fridge-sensitive watch** — putting them in a normal refrigerator (below 13 °C) actually damages them (chilling injury). They need **13 °C–15 °C**.

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic to Remember:** *Hot, Cold, Just-Right* — Just like Goldilocks, every food has its **Optimal Storage Temperature (T_opt)**, its **Chilling Injury Threshold (CIT)**, and its **Freezing Point (T_f)**. Good refrigeration design = hitting the "Just-Right" zone.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics Used in Refrigeration of Food

- **Specific Heat of Food (c_p)** — kJ/(kg·K)
- **Respiration Heat (R)** — mW/kg or kJ/(tonne·day)
- **Relative Humidity (RH)** — **85–95 %** for most F&V
- **Coefficient of Performance (COP)** — dimensionless
- **Ton of Refrigeration (TR)** = **3.517 kW** (≈ 50.4 kJ/min or 3024 kcal/hr)
- **Thermal Conductivity (k)** of food — typically **0.1 to 0.6 W/(m·K)**
- **Freezing Point Depression (ΔT_f)** — varies with solute concentration.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic of Cold Storage for Food Products

The refrigeration engineer must follow a strict sequence when designing a cold chain for perishables:

### Step 1 — Identify Product Physiological Class
- **Climacteric fruits** (banana, mango, tomato, apple) — continue to ripen post-harvest; produce **high respiration heat (ethylene)**.
- **Non-climacteric fruits** (citrus, grapes, pomegranate) — do not ripen further; require slower cooling.
- **Vegetables** — leafy vs. root vs. fruit-vegetable have different RH and temperature optima.

### Step 2 — Determine Optimum Storage Parameters
Each product has a published **T–RH–Shelf Life** triad from ASHRAE Handbook (Refrigeration) and FAO codes.

### Step 3 — Select Pre-cooling Method
Because field heat and respiration heat can be 4–7 times higher than steady-state heat, **pre-cooling** is mandatory before loading into the cold room.

### Step 4 — Calculate Total Refrigeration Load
The total load has **four distinct components** (elaborated in §3.1).

### Step 5 — Select Refrigeration Cycle & Capacity
For tropical Kerala conditions, a **VCRC with R-134a / R-404A / R-22** is standard.

## 2.2 Classification of Refrigerated Storage

| Storage Type | Temperature Range | Typical Products |
|---|---|---|
| **Chilled Storage** | -1 °C to +8 °C | Apples, pears, carrots, milk, eggs |
| **Cold Storage (Medium)** | 0 °C to -18 °C | Frozen F&V, ice cream |
| **Frozen Storage** | -18 °C to -25 °C | Meat, fish, long-term frozen produce |
| **Controlled Atmosphere (CA)** | 0 °C to +5 °C, O₂ 2-3%, CO₂ 3-5% | Apples, kiwifruit, pears |
| **Modified Atmosphere Packaging (MAP)** | Chilled | Cut salads, ready-to-eat meals |
| **Floral Cold Storage** | 0 °C to +4 °C (rose), 7-13 °C (tropical) | Cut flowers, orchids |
| **Banana Ripening Room** | 13 °C to 20 °C with ethylene | Banana, mango, tomato |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All these formulas are examinable in KTU 2024 ESE. Memorise with units.

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Sensible Heat of Product** | Q₁ = m · c_p · ΔT | kJ | Cooling field heat |
| 2 | **Latent Heat of Freezing** | Q₂ = m · L_f | kJ | Freezing load |
| 3 | **Respiration Heat Load** | Q₃ = m · R | kJ/day | Live produce (F&V, flowers) |
| 4 | **Transmission Load through Walls** | Q₄ = U · A · ΔT | W | Cold room walls, floor, ceiling |
| 5 | **Infiltration/Door Load** | Q₅ = V̇ · ρ · c_p · ΔT | kJ/hr | Air ingress when door opens |
| 6 | **Equipment/Person Load** | Q₆ = n · q_occ | W | Lights, workers, fans |
| 7 | **Ton of Refrigeration** | 1 TR = 3.517 kW | kW | Capacity rating |
| 8 | **Coefficient of Performance** | COP = Q_L / W_comp | — | Refrigeration efficiency |
| 9 | **Plank's Freezing Time** | t_f = (ρ · L_f / (T_f - T_a)) · (P·D/h + R·D²/k) | hr | Time to freeze a slab |
| 10 | **Specific Heat (Above Freezing)** | c_p = 0.837 + 0.0334 · w | kJ/(kg·K) | For unfrozen food (w = water fraction) |
| 11 | **Specific Heat (Below Freezing)** | c_p = 0.837 + 0.0126 · w | kJ/(kg·K) | For frozen food |
| 12 | **Freezing Point Depression** | ΔT_f = K_f · m | K | Solute effect on T_f |
| 13 | **Heat of Respiration Approx.** | R = 0.10 Q₁₀^((T - T₀)/10) | mW/kg | Van't Hoff rule, Q₁₀ ≈ 2–3 |
| 14 | **Relative Humidity & Dew Point** | Use Psychrometric Chart | — | Designing humidifiers |
| 15 | **Heat of Adsorption (Drying)** | Q_ads = h_fg(1 - W) | kJ/kg | Dehydro-refrigeration |

> [!NOTE]
> In the table above, `\vert` and `\mid` are intentionally avoided inside cells. The variable **w** denotes the mass fraction of water (decimal), not the absolute-value sign.

## 2.4 Engineering Utility in Real-World Kerala Horticulture

- **Kerala Banana Cold Chain** — Banana (cv. Nendran, Robusta) requires **13–15 °C, 90–95 % RH**. Refrigeration below 13 °C causes **chilling injury** (skin blackening). Special "**Banana Ripening Chambers**" use ethylene + temperature-controlled refrigeration.
- **Kerala Pineapple & Mango Export** — Uses **CA (Controlled Atmosphere)** storage at **8–10 °C** for 4–6 weeks.
- **Cut-Flower Industry (Munnar, Wayanad)** — Rose, jasmine, orchids stored at **2–4 °C, 95 % RH** with silver-thiosulphate (STS) pre-treatment.
- **Kerala Marine Fish (Ernakulam, Kollam)** — Iced storage at **0 °C**, frozen storage at **-23 °C** for export.
