The nucleation rate is the bottleneck — this is why *quick* freezing promotes many small crystals (less cellular damage) and *slow* freezing produces fewer, larger crystals (ruptures cell walls → drip loss on thawing). ### Step 3: Ice Crystal Growth (Latent Heat Removal) Once nuclei form, water molecules join the crystal lattice, releasing **334 kJ/kg** of latent heat per kg of water frozen. Temperature remains nearly constant. The food passes through its **eutectic temperature** — the lowest temperature at which any liquid phase still exists. Below the eutectic point, the food is *fully solidified*. ### Step 4: Sub-cooling After complete solidification, the frozen product's temperature drops further toward the storage temperature. Only sensible heat of the *ice* is removed. > [!TIP] > **Why Quick Freezing is Superior (KTU Favourite Question):** > - Slow freezing → ice forms **outside** the cells → osmotic dehydration of cells → cell wall rupture → drip loss upon thawing. > - Quick freezing → ice forms **inside and outside** cells uniformly → smaller crystals → minimal structural damage → superior texture retention. ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula / Equation | Meaning of Each Term | Units (SI) | When to Use | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | $Q_s = m \cdot C_p \cdot \Delta T$ | Sensible heat removed | J (kJ) | Pre-cooling & sub-cooling zones | | 2 | $Q_L = m \cdot \lambda$ | Latent heat removed during phase change | J (kJ) | Phase-change plateau (Zone II) | | 3 | $Q_{total} = m \cdot [C_{p,above}(T_i - T_f) + \lambda + C_{p,below}(T_f - T_{final})]$ | Total heat to be removed to freeze a product | J (kJ) | Designing refrigeration load | | 4 | $\Delta T_f = K_f \cdot m$ | Freezing point depression (Raoult's cryoscopic law) | °C or K | Calculating freezing point of fruit juices/syrups | | 5 | $t_f = \dfrac{\rho \cdot \lambda}{\Delta T} \left[ \dfrac{P \cdot D}{v} + \dfrac{R \cdot E}{h} \right]$ | **Plank's Equation** — Freezing time | seconds | Predicting freezing time of slabs/blocks/sphere | | 6 | $h = \dfrac{Nu \cdot k}{L_c}$ | Convective heat transfer coefficient (Newton's law) | W/(m²·K) | Air-blast / immersion freezer design | | 7 | $\dot{Q} = h \cdot A \cdot \Delta T$ | Rate of heat transfer (Newton's Law of Cooling) | W | Refrigeration load calculation | | 8 | $W_{ref} = \dfrac{Q_{total}}{COP \cdot t}$ | Refrigeration system power | kW | Freezer compressor sizing | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Plank's Equation — Geometric Constants (P, R, E) — HIGH-YIELD for KTU Board Exam!** > > | Shape of Product | P (Slab constant) | R (Cylinder constant) | E (Sphere constant) | > |---|---|---|---| > | **Infinite Slab** | 1/2 | — | — | > | **Infinite Cylinder** | — | 1/4 | — | > | **Sphere / Cube (3D)** | — | — | 1/6 | > > **Variables in Plank's equation:** > - $t_f$ = freezing time (s) > - $\rho$ = density of food (kg/m³) > - $\lambda$ = latent heat of fusion (J/kg) > - $\Delta T = T_f - T_{air}$ (difference between freezing point and cooling medium) > - $D$ = thickness of slab, or diameter (m) > - $v$ = thermal conductivity of frozen food (W/(m·K)) > - $h$ = convective heat transfer coefficient (W/(m²·K)) > - $P, R, E$ = shape constants (see table above) ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Design of Cold Chain Infrastructure** — sizing of compressors and evaporators for the frozen food industry. - **Selection of Freezing Method** — air blast, plate, immersion, or cryogenic (liquid N₂ / CO₂) based on product geometry and Plank's predicted time. - **Quality Assurance** — freezing rate governs ice crystal size, which directly determines drip loss, texture, and nutritional retention on thawing. - **Energy Auditing** — sensible and latent heat load calculations dictate electricity consumption of cold storage warehouses. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Plank's Equation for Freezing Time **Plank's equation (1913)** is the single most important analytical tool in food freezing. It estimates the time required to freeze an object of a given geometry by assuming: 1. Constant surface temperature (or constant heat transfer coefficient $h$). 2. Negligible sensible heat in the frozen zone (latent heat dominates). 3. Constant thermal conductivity $v$ of the frozen layer. 4. Quasi-steady-state heat conduction through the frozen crust. ### Derivation for an Infinite Slab (Thickness = $D$) **Step 1 — Geometry and Boundary Conditions** Consider an infinite slab of thickness $D$ (m) with its initial temperature at the freezing point $T_f$. The slab is suddenly exposed to a cooling medium at temperature $T_\infty < T_f$ with convective coefficient $h$. Ice forms on both faces and grows inward. **Step 2 — Energy Balance at the Moving Freezing Front** The rate at which latent heat is released at the receding freezing front equals the rate of heat conducted through the frozen crust plus the convective heat removed at the surface. **Step 3 — Fourier's Law of Conduction Through the Frozen Layer** The instantaneous heat flux through the frozen layer of thickness $x$ (one side) is: $$ q = v \cdot \dfrac{T_f - T_s}{x} $$ where $T_s$ is the surface temperature and $v$ is the thermal conductivity of frozen food. The total slab half-thickness at any instant is $x$, with $0 < x < D/2$. **Step 4 — Newton's Law of Cooling at the Surface** $$ q = h \cdot (T_s - T_\infty) $$ **Step 5 — Eliminating the Unknown Surface Temperature $T_s$** Eliminating $T_s$ by combining the two flux equations (conduction = convection) gives an effective *overall* driving temperature: $$ q = \dfrac{T_f - T_\infty}{\dfrac{1}{h} + \dfrac{x}{v}} = \dfrac{\Delta T}{\dfrac{1}{h} + \dfrac{x}{v}} $$ **Step 6 — Relating Heat Flux to Latent Heat Release** As the freezing front moves by $dx$ in time $dt$ over a unit area, the mass of water frozen per unit area is $\rho \cdot dx$ (where $\rho$ = density of food). The latent heat released per unit time per unit area is: $$ q = \rho \cdot \lambda \cdot \dfrac{dx}{dt} $$ **Step 7 — Equating the Two Expressions for $q$ and Rearranging** $$ \rho \cdot \lambda \cdot \dfrac{dx}{dt} = \dfrac{\Delta T}{\dfrac{1}{h} + \dfrac{x}{v}} $$ Rearranging: $$ dt = \dfrac{\rho \cdot \lambda}{\Delta T} \left( \dfrac{1}{h} + \dfrac{x}{v} \right) dx $$ **Step 8 — Integrating from $x = 0$ to $x = D/2$** $$ \int_0^{t_f} dt = \dfrac{\rho \cdot \lambda}{\Delta T} \int_0^{D/2} \left( \dfrac{1}{h} + \dfrac{x}{v} \right) dx $$ $$ t_f = \dfrac{\rho \cdot \lambda}{\Delta T} \left[ \dfrac{x}{h} + \dfrac{x^2}{2v} \right]_0^{D/2} $$ **Step 9 — Substituting the Upper Limit $x = D/2$** $$ t_f = \dfrac{\rho \cdot \lambda}{\Delta T} \left[ \dfrac{D/2}{h} + \dfrac{(D/2)^2}{2v} \right] $$ **Step 10 — Simplifying (with $P = 1/2$ for an infinite slab)** $$ t_f = \dfrac{\rho \cdot \lambda}{\Delta T} \left[ \dfrac{D}{2h} + \dfrac{D^2}{8v} \right] $$ **Final Compact Form (Generalized Plank's Equation):** $$ \boxed{\, t_f = \dfrac{\rho \cdot \lambda}{\Delta T} \left[ \dfrac{P \cdot D}{v} + \dfrac{R \cdot E}{h} \right] \,} $$ > [!NOTE] > The constants $P$, $R$, $E$ in the bracketed term absorb the geometric factors ($1/8$, $1/4$, etc.) for slab ($P=1/2$), infinite cylinder ($R=1/4$), and sphere/cube ($E=1/6$) respectively. ## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style 14-mark problem) > **Problem:** A slab of mango pulp of thickness **0.06 m** is to be frozen in an air-blast tunnel. The initial temperature is **25 °C**, the freezing point is **-1.5 °C**, the air temperature is **-30 °C**, and the convective heat transfer coefficient is **$h = 25 \, W/(m^2 \cdot K)$**. Given: $\rho = 1050 \, kg/m^3$, $\lambda = 280 \, kJ/kg$, $v = 1.2 \, W/(m \cdot K)$, $C_p$ (above freezing) = $3.6 \, kJ/(kg \cdot K)$. Calculate: > (a) the total heat to be removed per kg of pulp, and > (b) the freezing time using Plank's equation. ### Part (a) — Total Heat Load per kg $$ Q = C_p (T_i - T_f) + \lambda + C_{p,ice}(T_f - T_{air}) $$ $$ Q = 3.6 \times (25 - (-1.5)) + 280 + 2.1 \times (-1.5 - (-30)) $$ $$ Q = 3.6 \times 26.5 + 280 + 2.1 \times 28.5 $$ $$ Q = 95.4 + 280 + 59.85 $$ $$ Q = 435.25 \, kJ/kg $$ ### Part (b) — Freezing Time by Plank's Equation For an **infinite slab**, $P = 1/2$. The driving temperature difference is: $$ \Delta T = T_f - T_{air} = -1.5 - (-30) = 28.5 \, K $$ Apply Plank's equation: $$ t_f = \dfrac{\rho \cdot \lambda}{\Delta T} \left[ \dfrac{P \cdot D}{v} + \dfrac{R \cdot E}{h} \right] $$ Substitute the values (note: for a slab, only $P$ is non-zero; $R \cdot E$ terms drop out): $$ t_f = \dfrac{1050 \times 280\,000}{28.5} \left[ \dfrac{0.5 \times 0.06}{1.2} + 0 \right] $$ $$ t_f = \dfrac{2.94 \times 10^8}{28.5} \times \left[ \dfrac{0.03}{1.2} \right] $$ $$ t_f = 1.0316 \times 10^7 \times 0.025 $$ $$ t_f = 2.579 \times 10^5 \, \text{seconds} $$ Convert to hours: $$ t_f = \dfrac{2.579 \times 10^5}{3600} \approx 71.6 \, \text{hours} $$ > [!TIP] > **Insight:** Notice that the conduction term $P \cdot D / v$ dominates when $h$ is large (i.e., the surface offers negligible resistance). Conversely, the convection term $R \cdot E / h$ dominates when $h$ is small (e.g., in still air). KTU examiners love testing this trade-off! ## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choice of Freezing Method | Freezing Method | Typical $\Delta T$ (K) | Typical $h$ (W/m²·K) | Best For | Drawback | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Still Air (Home Freezer)** | 15–20 | 5–10 | Household storage | Very slow, large crystals | | **Air-Blast Tunnel** | 30–60 | 20–50 | Bulk fruits, vegetables, packaged goods | Drying of unprotected surfaces | | **Plate (Contact) Freezer** | 30–40 | 100–300 | Regular shapes (fish fillets, peas) | Limited geometry | | **Immersion (Brine/Glycol)** | 50–100 | 500–1500 | High-value IQF products | Salt uptake, contamination | | **Cryogenic (LN₂ / LCO₂)** | 100–200 | 1000–3000 | Premium individual quick freezing | High cost, surface cracking | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Freezing Process Flow — Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Fresh Horticultural Produce Harvested] --> B[Sorting and Grading] B --> C[Washing and Sanitization] C --> D[Pre-cooling to remove field heat] D --> E[Blanching optional for vegetables] E --> F[Packaging in moisture-proof pouches] F --> G[Loading into Freezer Unit] G --> H[Quick Freezing below -18°C] H --> I[Glazing optional for fruits] I --> J[Frozen Storage at -18°C] J --> K[Distribution via Cold Chain] K --> L[Retail Display Cabinet] L --> M[Consumer Freezer Storage] M --> N[Thawing at Point of Use] N --> O[Consumption] style A fill:#e6f7d4,stroke:#333,color:#000 style H fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000 style J fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#806000,color:#000 style N fill:#ffd6d6,stroke:#660000,color:#000 ``` ## 4.2 Freezing Curve — Temperature vs Time Signature ```mermaid graph LR A["Zone I: Sensible Heat Removal
Liquid cools from Ti to Tf"] --> B["Zone II: Latent Heat Removal
Phase change plateau near Tf
Largest energy demand"] B --> C["Zone III: Sub-cooling of Ice
Frozen mass cools to Tstorage"] C --> D["Steady Frozen Storage
Maintained at -18°C"] style A fill:#fff7e0,stroke:#aa8800,color:#000 style B fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000 style C fill:#e0d4ff,stroke:#330099,color:#000 style D fill:#d4f0d4,stroke:#006600,color:#000 ``` ## 4.3 Ice Crystal Morphology — Slow vs Quick Freezing ```mermaid flowchart LR A["Cellular Structure of Fresh Produce"] --> B{Speed of Freezing} B -- "Slow Freezing
Low h, small Delta T" --> C["Large extracellular ice crystals
Water migrates out of cells
Cell wall rupture
High drip loss on thawing"] B -- "Quick Freezing
High h, large Delta T" --> D["Small intra and extracellular ice crystals
Minimal water migration
Cell walls intact
Low drip loss superior texture"] C --> E[Quality Degraded] D --> F[Quality Preserved] style A fill:#e6f7d4,stroke:#333,color:#000 style C fill:#ffd6d6,stroke:#660000,color:#000 style D fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000 style E fill:#ffb3b3,stroke:#990000,color:#000 style F fill:#b3d9b3,stroke:#006600,color:#000 ``` ## 4.4 Classification of Freezing Methods (Functional Architecture) ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Food Freezing Methods] --> B[Mechanical Freezing] A --> C[Cryogenic Freezing] B --> B1[Air-Blast Freezing] B --> B2[Plate Contact Freezing] B --> B3[Immersion Freezing] C --> C1[Liquid Nitrogen LN2 at -196°C] C --> C2[Liquid Carbon Dioxide LCO2 at -78°C] B1 --> B1a[Tunnel type] B1 --> B1b[Spiral type] B1 --> B1c[Fluidized bed for IQF] B2 --> B2a[Horizontal plates] B2 --> B2b[Vertical plates] B3 --> B3a[Brine NaCl or CaCl2] B3 --> B3b[Propylene glycol solutions] style A fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#806000,color:#000 style B fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000 style C fill:#e0d4ff,stroke:#330099,color:#000 style B1 fill:#d4f0d4,stroke:#006600,color:#000 style B2 fill:#d4f0d4,stroke:#006600,color:#000 style B3 fill:#d4f0d4,stroke:#006600,color:#000 style C1 fill:#ffd6d6,stroke:#660000,color:#000 style C2 fill:#ffd6d6,stroke:#660000,color:#000 ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** **Differentiate between "Chilling" and "Freezing" of horticultural produce. Why is -18 °C chosen as the standard storage temperature for frozen foods?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Aspect | Chilling | Freezing | |---|---|---| | Temperature range | 0 °C to +8 °C | Below -1 °C (down to -40 °C) | | Phase change of water | **No** (water remains liquid) | **Yes** (water → ice) | | Microbial activity | Slowed, not arrested | Largely arrested | | Shelf life extension | Days to weeks | Months to years | | Quality impact | Fresh-like | Texture modified by ice crystals | The temperature **-18 °C** is the internationally accepted standard (set by the **International Institute of Refrigeration, IIR** and adopted by **Codex Alimentarius**) because: 1. It ensures that more than **99%** of the freezable water exists as ice, virtually halting microbial and enzymatic activity. 2. It maintains the eutectic state of most food components, ensuring biochemical stability. 3. It provides a **3-mark-worthy** balance between product quality and refrigeration energy cost (any lower temperature would consume disproportionately more energy without a proportional quality gain). **(1 mark)** --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** **Explain the concept of "Freezing Point Depression" of horticultural produce. How does it affect the design of a freezing system?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** Freezing point depression is the phenomenon by which the freezing point of a horticultural product is **lower than that of pure water (0 °C)** due to the presence of dissolved solutes (sugars, salts, organic acids, pectins). It follows the **Raoult's cryoscopic law**: $$ \Delta T_f = K_f \cdot m $$ where $K_f$ is the cryoscopic constant of water (1.86 K·kg/mol) and $m$ is the molality of dissolved solutes. Most fruits and vegetables freeze between **-0.5 °C and -3.0 °C** (e.g., mangoes: -1.5 °C; apples: -2.0 °C; carrots: -1.3 °C). **(2 marks)** **Design implications:** - The refrigerant/cooling air must be colder than this depressed freezing point to ensure the phase change initiates. - The driving temperature difference $\Delta T$ in Plank's equation must be calculated using the *actual* freezing point of the product, **not** 0 °C. **(1 mark)** --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > - **DO NOT confuse** sensible heat and latent heat in your heat load calculations — this is the #1 reason students lose 3–4 marks. > - **Always use SI units** consistently. Mixing kcal, °C, and W in Plank's equation is a guaranteed 2-mark penalty. > - **Freezing point of the food** (not 0 °C) must be used in $\Delta T$ — examiners specifically test this trap. > - In derivations, **state assumptions first**, then proceed — partial marks are awarded for correctly listed assumptions even if the algebra is incomplete. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model Paper]** **(a)** With a neat sketch, describe the **freezing curve of food** and identify the three thermal zones. Explain why the second zone consumes the most energy. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A cylindrical can of **pineapple juice** (diameter **0.08 m**, length **0.12 m**) is to be frozen in a plate freezer. Given: - Initial temperature $T_i = 20 \, °C$ - Freezing point $T_f = -1.8 \, °C$ - Plate surface temperature $T_s = -35 \, °C$ - Density $\rho = 1060 \, kg/m^3$ - Latent heat $\lambda = 290 \, kJ/kg$ - Thermal conductivity of frozen juice $v = 1.4 \, W/(m \cdot K)$ - Convective coefficient $h = 200 \, W/(m^2 \cdot K)$ Calculate the **freezing time** using Plank's equation. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution **(a) Freezing Curve (7 marks)** The freezing curve is the plot of the food's **temperature vs time** when subjected to a sub-zero cooling medium. The three zones are: 1. **Zone I — Sensible Heat Removal (Pre-cooling):** Product cools from $T_i$ to $T_f$ without any phase change. Heat removed = $m \cdot C_p \cdot (T_i - T_f)$. Duration depends on $C_p$. **1.5 marks** 2. **Zone II — Latent Heat Removal (Phase-change plateau):** Temperature remains almost constant. Ice crystals form, releasing the **latent heat of fusion**. Heat removed = $m \cdot \lambda$. **2.5 marks** 3. **Zone III — Sub-cooling of Ice:** Frozen product cools from $T_f$ to the storage temperature. Heat removed = $m \cdot C_{p,ice} \cdot (T_f - T_{storage})$. **1.5 marks** **Sketch (Neat drawing expected):** A typical "S-shaped" curve with a flat plateau in the middle. The plateau (Zone II) consumes the **most energy** because the latent heat of fusion of water ($\lambda = 334 \, kJ/kg$) is **~80 times greater** than the sensible heat per degree. For most horticultural produce, the latent heat accounts for **70–80% of the total refrigeration load**. **1.5 marks** **(b) Freezing Time Calculation (7 marks)** For a **finite cylinder**, both the slab term ($P$) and the cylinder term ($R$) are non-zero. The characteristic dimension is the diameter $D$ for radial freezing and the half-length for axial freezing. Plank's generalized equation for a finite cylinder is: $$ t_f = \dfrac{\rho \cdot \lambda}{\Delta T} \left[ \dfrac{P \cdot D}{v} + \dfrac{R \cdot D}{v} + \dfrac{R \cdot E}{h} \right] $$ For a finite cylinder, the standard form uses the **equivalent diameter** approach, or the more common textbook formulation: $$ t_f = \dfrac{\rho \cdot \lambda}{\Delta T} \left[ \dfrac{P \cdot D}{v} + \dfrac{R \cdot D}{v} \right] + \dfrac{\rho \cdot \lambda \cdot R \cdot E}{\Delta T \cdot h} $$ Using **P = 1/2** (slab, axial contribution, with $D_{axial}/2$ approximated), **R = 1/4** (cylinder, radial contribution), and **E = 1/6** (cube/sphere end correction), and treating the cylinder as primarily radial (diameter-dominated): $$ \Delta T = T_f - T_s = -1.8 - (-35) = 33.2 \, K $$ **Step 1 — Conduction term (slab + cylinder):** $$ \text{Term}_1 = \dfrac{P \cdot D}{v} + \dfrac{R \cdot D}{v} = \dfrac{0.5 \times 0.08}{1.4} + \dfrac{0.25 \times 0.08}{1.4} $$ $$ = \dfrac{0.04}{1.4} + \dfrac{0.02}{1.4} = 0.02857 + 0.01429 = 0.04286 \, m^2 \cdot K / W $$ **Step 2 — Convection term:** $$ \text{Term}_2 = \dfrac{R \cdot E}{h} = \dfrac{0.25 \times (1/6)}{200} = \dfrac{0.04167}{200} = 2.083 \times 10^{-4} \, m^2 \cdot K / W $$ **Step 3 — Total bracket:** $$ [\ldots] = 0.04286 + 2.083 \times 10^{-4} = 0.04307 \, m^2 \cdot K / W $$ **Step 4 — Prefactor:** $$ \dfrac{\rho \cdot \lambda}{\Delta T} = \dfrac{1060 \times 290\,000}{33.2} = \dfrac{3.074 \times 10^8}{33.2} = 9.259 \times 10^6 \, J \cdot s / (m^4 \cdot K) $$ **Step 5 — Freezing time:** $$ t_f = 9.259 \times 10^6 \times 0.04307 \approx 3.988 \times 10^5 \, s $$ $$ t_f = \dfrac{3.988 \times 10^5}{3600} \approx 110.8 \, \text{hours} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Answer:** The freezing time is approximately **111 hours (≈ 4.6 days)**. This long time is typical for large-dimension cans; in practice, the plate freezer operates at lower temperatures, or the can is sliced thinner to reduce $D$ and hence $t_f$ drastically (since $D^2$ dependence in conduction term). --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper, Alternative]** **(a)** Compare the **slow freezing** and **quick freezing** of horticultural produce. Discuss their effects on **ice crystal size, cell wall integrity, and drip loss upon thawing**. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Design a refrigeration system to freeze **500 kg of strawberries per hour** from $25 \, °C$ to a final storage temperature of $-18 \, °C$. Given: - $C_p$ (above freezing) = $3.8 \, kJ/(kg \cdot K)$ - $C_p$ (below freezing) = $1.9 \, kJ/(kg \cdot K)$ - Freezing point = $-1.0 \, °C$ - Latent heat $\lambda = 285 \, kJ/kg$ Compute the **total heat load in kW** that the refrigeration system must remove. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution **(a) Slow vs Quick Freezing (7 marks)** | Parameter | Slow Freezing | Quick Freezing | |---|---|---| | Cooling rate | < 0.5 °C/min | > 5 °C/min | | Ice crystal location | **Predominantly extracellular** | **Both intra- and extracellular** | | Crystal size | **Large** (50–200 µm) | **Small / Micro** (< 50 µm) | | Cell wall integrity | **Ruptured** by growing extracellular crystals | **Intact**, minimal mechanical damage | | Water migration | Extensive (osmotic dehydration of cells) | Minimal | | Texture after thawing | Mushy, soft | Crisp, near-fresh | | Drip loss (thaw exudate) | **High** (10–30%) | **Low** (1–5%) | | Nutrient retention | Lower (cell rupture → oxidation) | Higher | **Mechanism:** In slow freezing, ice forms first in the extracellular spaces (lower solute concentration, higher freezing point). The unfrozen intracellular water then migrates osmotically toward these crystals, causing cells to dehydrate and collapse. In quick freezing, the temperature drops so rapidly that water inside the cells does not have time to migrate — it freezes in situ, forming tiny intracellular crystals that do not rupture cell walls. **(2 marks)** **(b) Total Heat Load Calculation (7 marks)** Given: Mass flow rate $\dot{m} = 500 \, kg/h = 500/3600 = 0.1389 \, kg/s$ **Step 1 — Sensible heat above freezing (cooling from 25 °C to -1.0 °C):** $$ Q_1 = \dot{m} \cdot C_p \cdot (T_i - T_f) = 0.1389 \times 3.8 \times (25 - (-1.0)) $$ $$ Q_1 = 0.1389 \times 3.8 \times 26.0 = 13.72 \, kW $$ **Step 2 — Latent heat (freezing at -1.0 °C):** $$ Q_2 = \dot{m} \cdot \lambda = 0.1389 \times 285 = 39.58 \, kW $$ **Step 3 — Sensible heat below freezing (cooling ice from -1.0 °C to -18 °C):** $$ Q_3 = \dot{m} \cdot C_{p,ice} \cdot (T_f - T_{final}) = 0.1389 \times 1.9 \times (-1.0 - (-18)) $$ $$ Q_3 = 0.1389 \times 1.9 \times 17.0 = 4.486 \, kW $$ **Step 4 — Total heat load:** $$ Q_{total} = Q_1 + Q_2 + Q_3 = 13.72 + 39.58 + 4.486 = 57.79 \, kW $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Answer:** The refrigeration system must remove approximately **57.8 kW** of heat. Allowing for a 10% safety margin and a typical COP of 2.5, the compressor power required would be $57.8 \times 1.1 / 2.5 \approx 25.4 \, kW$. Note that the latent heat $Q_2$ constitutes **68.5%** of the total load, confirming the dominance of phase-change energy in freezing. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid Revision Checklist (Read this the night before the exam!):** - [x] **Freezing** = preservation by lowering temp *below* the freezing point, causing water → ice. - [x] **Chilling** ≠ Freezing — chilling has *no* phase change. - [x] Most horticultural produce freezes between **-0.5 °C and -3.0 °C** (freezing point depression). - [x] Standard frozen storage temperature = **-18 °C** (IIR / Codex standard). - [x] **Freezing curve** has 3 zones: Sensible (liquid) → Latent (plateau) → Sensible (ice). - [x] **Latent heat of water** = **334 kJ/kg** — accounts for 70–80% of freezing energy. - [x] **Plank's equation** is the primary design tool for freezing time: $$t_f = \dfrac{\rho \cdot \lambda}{\Delta T} \left[ \dfrac{P \cdot D}{v} + \dfrac{R \cdot E}{h} \right]$$ - [x] **Geometric constants:** Slab P=1/2, Cylinder R=1/4, Sphere/Cube E=1/6. - [x] **Quick freezing** → small crystals → intact cells → low drip loss → better quality. - [x] **Slow freezing** → large crystals → cell rupture → high drip loss → poor quality. - [x] **Freezing methods:** Air-blast, Plate (contact), Immersion (brine), Cryogenic (LN₂ / LCO₂). - [x] In Plank's equation, **always use the actual food freezing point**, not 0 °C, in $\Delta T$. - [x] Higher $h$ (better convection) and higher $\Delta T$ (colder coolant) both **reduce** $t_f$. - [x] $t_f \propto D^2$ for thick products — halving the thickness reduces freezing time to one-quarter (a key design insight). - [x] **Raoult's cryoscopic law:** $\Delta T_f = K_f \cdot m$ — used to predict depressed freezing points of juices and syrups. - [x] **Always state assumptions** before applying Plank's equation (constant properties, negligible sensible heat of ice, quasi-steady conduction). - [x] Convert all units to **SI** (kg, m, s, J, W, K) before substituting into Plank's equation. > [!TIP] > **Final Exam Mantra:** "Sensible–Latent–Sensible" — remember this three-word mantra, and the freezing curve, Plank's equation, and heat load calculations will all click into place. Good luck!

topics.title: Freezing Time Calculations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAGT742 - POST HARVEST ENGINEERING OF HORTICULTURAL CROPS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Freezing Time Calculations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Freezing time** is defined as the total time required to lower the temperature of a food (horticultural) product from its initial temperature (usually above the freezing point) to a final temperature (typically at or just below the storage temperature) at the geometric center (thermal center) of the product, such that essentially all the freezable water in the product has been converted to ice.

In food engineering, particularly for **horticultural crops** (fruits, vegetables, flowers), freezing time prediction is a critical design parameter for sizing industrial freezers, blast freezers, plate freezers, cryogenic freezers, and IQF (Individual Quick Freezing) tunnels.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Freezing time is the time elapsed from the moment a food product is placed in a freezing medium until the thermal center reaches the desired final temperature, accounting for sensible heat removal (pre-cooling and sub-cooling) and the latent heat of crystallization (phase change).

Mathematically, the total freezing time $t_f$ can be expressed as the sum of three sequential stages:

$$t_f = t_1 + t_2 + t_3$$

Where:
- $t_1$ = Pre-cooling time (initial sensible heat removal from initial temperature $T_i$ down to freezing point $T_f$)
- $t_2$ = Phase change time (latent heat removal as water turns to ice at $T_f$)
- $t_3$ = Sub-cooling or tempering time (further sensible heat removal from $T_f$ to final temperature $T_{final}$)

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are making an ice cube at home. You take a small plastic tray with water at room temperature (say, **30 °C**) and place it inside a freezer set to **−18 °C**. The water does not freeze instantly. Instead:

1. **First few minutes** — The water cools down from 30 °C to 0 °C. This is the *pre-cooling stage* (sensible heat removal). You can touch the tray and it feels merely cold, not icy.
2. **Next several hours** — Despite the freezer being very cold, the water at the center remains at 0 °C for a long time, slowly turning into ice. The temperature stays "stuck" at 0 °C because all the heat being removed is the **latent heat of fusion**. This is the *phase change stage*, and it dominates the total freezing time.
3. **Final stage** — Once the water has fully crystallized, the solid ice cools further from 0 °C down to −18 °C. This is the *sub-cooling stage*.

> [!NOTE]
> The middle stage (phase change) takes the **longest time** because the latent heat of fusion of water is approximately **334 kJ/kg**, which is roughly 80 times the sensible heat required to cool water by 1 °C. This is why freezing is energy-intensive and why accurate time prediction matters economically.

For a **strawberry** or a **mango piece** placed in a blast freezer, the same three stages occur, but the freezing point is depressed (e.g., −1.5 °C to −2.5 °C) because of dissolved sugars, salts, and other solutes, and the freezable water content is less than 100 %.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Standard Values Used in KTU Examinations:**
> - Latent heat of fusion of pure water: $\lambda = 334 \text{ kJ/kg}$ (or 80 kcal/kg)
> - Latent heat of sublimation of ice: $\lambda_s \approx 2836 \text{ kJ/kg}$
> - Specific heat of water (above freezing): $c_{pw} \approx 4.18 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)}$
> - Specific heat of ice (below freezing): $c_{pi} \approx 2.1 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)}$
> - Thermal conductivity of water: $k_w \approx 0.6 \text{ W/(m·K)}$
> - Thermal conductivity of ice: $k_i \approx 2.2 \text{ W/(m·K)}$

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Stages of Freezing — Logic Breakdown

### Stage 1: Pre-Cooling (Sensible Heat Removal Above Freezing Point)

The product is cooled from its initial temperature $T_i$ to its freezing point $T_f$. The governing equation is based on unsteady-state heat conduction (Lumped or distributed system depending on Biot number).

For a lumped system (small Biot number, $Bi < 0.1$):

$$t_1 = \frac{\rho \, V \, c_{pw}}{h \, A_s} \ln\left(\frac{T_i - T_a}{T_f - T_a}\right)$$

For a distributed system, the solution involves Fourier number and geometry-specific charts (sphere, infinite cylinder, slab).

### Stage 2: Phase Change (Latent Heat Removal) — THE DOMINANT STAGE

This is where the majority of heat is removed. The product temperature remains nearly constant at $T_f$ while ice crystals nucleate and grow. The classical approach is **Plank's Equation (1913)**.

### Stage 3: Sub-Cooling (Sensible Heat Removal Below Freezing Point)

The frozen product is cooled from $T_f$ to the final storage temperature $T_c$. Similar to Stage 1 but using the thermal properties of ice.

$$t_3 = \frac{\rho \, V \, c_{pi}}{h \, A_s} \ln\left(\frac{T_f - T_a}{T_c - T_a}\right)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does Stage 2 dominate?**
> Compare a 1 kg product cooling from 20 °C to −20 °C:
> - Sensible heat (cooling water from 20 to 0 °C): $m \cdot c_{pw} \cdot \Delta T = 1 \times 4.18 \times 20 = 83.6 \text{ kJ}$
> - Latent heat: $m \cdot \lambda = 1 \times 334 = 334 \text{ kJ}$
> - Sensible heat (cooling ice from 0 to −20 °C): $1 \times 2.1 \times 20 = 42 \text{ kJ}$
> 
> **Latent heat = 70 % of total heat load.** This is why freezing time is dominated by phase change.

## 2.2 Plank's Equation (1913) — The Foundational Model

**Egon Plank** of Germany developed the first widely-used analytical freezing time equation in 1913. It assumes:

1. All freezable water freezes at a single, fixed temperature $T_f$ (sharp interface model).
2. The frozen region has uniform, constant thermal properties ($k$, $\rho$).
3. The product has a regular geometry: **infinite slab**, **infinite cylinder**, or **sphere**.
4. Heat transfer occurs in one direction (radial for cylinder/sphere, perpendicular for slab).
5. Convective boundary condition with constant ambient temperature $T_a$ and surface heat transfer coefficient $h$.

**General Plank's Equation:**

$$\boxed{t_f = \frac{\rho \, \lambda}{T_f - T_a}\left(\frac{P \, D}{h} + \frac{R \, D^2}{k}\right)}$$

Where:
- $t_f$ = Freezing time (seconds)
- $\rho$ = Density of the product (kg/m³)
- $\lambda$ = Latent heat of fusion (J/kg)
- $T_f$ = Freezing point of the product (°C)
- $T_a$ = Ambient / refrigerant temperature (°C)
- $h$ = Surface heat transfer coefficient (W/(m²·K))
- $D$ = Characteristic dimension (m) — see table below
- $k$ = Thermal conductivity of frozen product (W/(m·K))
- $P$ and $R$ = Geometry-dependent constants (dimensionless)

### Geometry Constants (P, R, D) for Plank's Equation

| Geometry | D (Characteristic Dimension) | P | R |
|---|---|---|---|
| Infinite Slab (thickness) | $2a$ (full thickness) | $\dfrac{1}{2}$ | $\dfrac{1}{8}$ |
| Infinite Cylinder (radius) | $D = 2R$ (diameter) | $\dfrac{1}{4}$ | $\dfrac{1}{16}$ |
| Sphere (radius) | $D = 2R$ (diameter) | $\dfrac{1}{6}$ | $\dfrac{1}{24}$ |
| Infinite Cylinder (R) | $D = R$ (radius) | $\dfrac{1}{4}$ | $\dfrac{1}{16}$ (using R as D) |
| Brick (2a × 2b × 2c) | Use largest dimension | Modified | Modified |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Remember this ratio for KTU exams:** For a sphere, $P/R = 4$. For an infinite cylinder, $P/R = 4$. For an infinite slab, $P/R = 4$. The **P/R ratio is always 4** because the second term represents the internal conduction resistance and the first term the surface convective resistance, both scaled by the same geometry.

## 2.3 Pham's Equation (1986) — Modern Simplified Model

**Quang T. Pham** developed a much simpler, more accurate empirical equation by statistically fitting experimental data. It is **favored in KTU numerical problems** for its simplicity and accuracy (±15 % for most foods).

**Pham's Equation:**

$$\boxed{t_f = \frac{\rho \, V}{\lambda_s} \left( \frac{\Delta H_{10}}{\Delta T_{c1}} + \frac{\Delta H_1}{h \cdot Bi} \right)}$$

A more practical, widely-cited form for an **infinite slab** of half-thickness $a$:

$$t_f = \frac{\rho \, \lambda \, a^2}{k \, (T_f - T_a)} \left( 1 + \frac{2 \, k}{a \, h} \right) \cdot \frac{1}{2}$$

The simpler 1-D form often seen in textbooks:

$$t_f = \frac{\rho \, \lambda}{T_f - T_a} \left( \frac{a}{h} + \frac{a^2}{2k} \right)$$

Where $a$ = half-thickness for a slab of total thickness $2a$.

## 2.4 Heat Transfer Correlations (for h)

The surface heat transfer coefficient $h$ depends on the freezing medium:

| Medium | Typical $h$ value (W/m²·K) | Velocity |
|---|---|---|
| Still air (cold room) | 5 – 10 | Natural convection |
| Forced air (blast freezer) | 15 – 40 | 2 – 5 m/s |
| Plate freezer (contact) | 100 – 200 | Pressed contact |
| Spray/immersion in brine | 200 – 500 | Agitated liquid |
| Cryogenic (LN₂ spray) | 500 – 1500 | Spray impingement |
| Liquid nitrogen immersion | 1000 – 2000 | Boiling |

> [!NOTE]
> For **sphere geometry in Plank's equation**, the formula uses $D = $ diameter, and the constants are $P = 1/6$, $R = 1/24$. For an **apple** (approximately spherical, ~70 mm diameter), Plank's equation is a very good first approximation.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Application |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $t_f = t_1 + t_2 + t_3$ | Total freezing time = sum of 3 stages |
| 2 | $t_1 = \frac{\rho V c_{pw}}{h A_s} \ln\left(\frac{T_i - T_a}{T_f - T_a}\right)$ | Pre-cooling time (lumped) |
| 3 | $t_3 = \frac{\rho V c_{pi}}{h A_s} \ln\left(\frac{T_f - T_a}{T_c - T_a}\right)$ | Sub-cooling time (lumped) |
| 4 | $t_f = \frac{\rho \lambda}{T_f - T_a}\left(\frac{P D}{h} + \frac{R D^2}{k}\right)$ | **Plank's Equation (general)** |
| 5 | Slab: $P=1/2, R=1/8$ | Plank's for infinite slab |
| 6 | Cylinder: $P=1/4, R=1/16$ | Plank's for infinite cylinder |
| 7 | Sphere: $P=1/6, R=1/24$ | Plank's for sphere |
| 8 | $\lambda_{effective} = \lambda_w \cdot x_w$ | Latent heat with freezable water fraction |
| 9 | $Bi = \frac{h \cdot L_c}{k}$ | Biot number (validity check, $Bi < 0.1$ for lumped) |
| 10 | $Fo = \frac{\alpha \, t}{L_c^2}$ | Fourier number (transient conduction) |
| 11 | $Nu = \frac{h \, D}{k_{air}}$ | Nusselt number for forced convection over shape |
| 12 | $h_{plate} \approx 0.0204 \cdot k_{air} \cdot Re^{0.8}$ (Nu correlation) | External forced convection $h$ |
| 13 | $T_f = -\dfrac{1.86 \, m_{solute}}{1000 / M_w}$ | Freezing point depression (molal) |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

In industrial horticulture (frozen peas, frozen mango pulp, frozen strawberries, frozen okra, frozen cut flowers for export):

- **Freezer Sizing:** Knowing $t_f$ allows the plant engineer to calculate the throughput (kg/h) of a blast freezer, dictating the number of units and tunnel length.
- **Energy Auditing:** Multiplying $t_f$ by the average heat load and the production rate gives the refrigeration capacity (kW) needed.
- **Quality Control:** Slow freezing (large $t_f$) → large extracellular ice crystals → ruptured cell walls → texture loss (e.g., mushy strawberries). Fast freezing (small $t_f$) → small intracellular crystals → better texture retention. Thus, freezing time directly controls product quality.
- **Cold Chain Design:** Predicting the time-temperature profile helps in setting HACCP critical limits and designing packaging.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Plank's Equation — Conceptual Outline

The full analytical derivation of Plank's equation requires solving the **Stefan problem** (moving boundary problem). Below is a detailed, exam-oriented step-by-step development showing the underlying physics and the resulting equation.

### 3.1.1 The Physical Setup

Consider an **infinite slab** of thickness $2a$ (so $-a \le x \le +a$), initially at the freezing point $T_f$ throughout. At time $t = 0$, the slab is plunged into a medium of constant temperature $T_a < T_f$. Heat is removed from both faces ($x = +a$ and $x = -a$).

A frozen layer of thickness $\xi(t)$ advances symmetrically from each face toward the center.

### 3.1.2 The Three Coupled Equations

**Step 1 — Conduction in the frozen layer (Fourier's law):**

The heat flow through the frozen region must equal the rate of latent heat released at the moving interface $x = \xi$:

$$q = \frac{k (T_f - T_a)}{\xi}$$

This is steady-state 1-D conduction through a slab of thickness $\xi$ with surface temperature $T_a$ and interface temperature $T_f$.

**Step 2 — Rate of latent heat release at the interface:**

As the interface moves inward at velocity $d\xi/dt$, the rate of latent heat generation per unit area is:

$$q = \rho \lambda \frac{d\xi}{dt}$$

**Step 3 — Surface convective boundary (Newton's law of cooling):**

The total heat flow at the surface is by convection with coefficient $h$:

$$q = h (T_s - T_a)$$

where $T_s$ is the surface temperature. In Plank's model, the thermal resistance of the **frozen layer** is assumed to dominate, and the surface temperature is taken to be close to $T_a$ (or the convective term is added as a separate resistance — this is why the equation has TWO terms).

### 3.1.3 Combining the Resistances (Electrical Analogy)

The total resistance from the interface to the ambient is the sum of two resistances in series:

$$R_{total} = R_{surface} + R_{conduction} = \frac{1}{h} + \frac{\xi}{k}$$

The driving temperature difference is $T_f - T_a$. So heat flux:

$$q = \frac{T_f - T_a}{\dfrac{1}{h} + \dfrac{\xi}{k}} = \frac{h k (T_f - T_a)}{k + h \xi}$$

### 3.1.4 Energy Balance at the Interface

The heat removed at the surface must equal the latent heat released at the moving interface:

$$\rho \lambda \frac{d\xi}{dt} = \frac{h k (T_f - T_a)}{k + h \xi}$$

### 3.1.5 Separation of Variables and Integration

Rearranging:

$$(k + h \xi) \, d\xi = \frac{h k (T_f - T_a)}{\rho \lambda} \, dt$$

Integrating from $\xi = 0$ at $t = 0$ to $\xi = a$ at $t = t_f$:

$$\int_0^a (k + h \xi) \, d\xi = \frac{h k (T_f - T_a)}{\rho \lambda} \int_0^{t_f} dt$$

Evaluating:

$$k \cdot a + \frac{h a^2}{2} = \frac{h k (T_f - T_a)}{\rho \lambda} \cdot t_f$$

Solving for $t_f$:

$$t_f = \frac{\rho \lambda}{h k (T_f - T_a)} \left( k a + \frac{h a^2}{2} \right)$$

$$\boxed{t_f = \frac{\rho \lambda}{T_f - T_a} \left( \frac{a}{h} + \frac{a^2}{2k} \right)}$$

For an infinite slab of total thickness $D = 2a$, substituting $a = D/2$:

$$t_f = \frac{\rho \lambda}{T_f - T_a} \left( \frac{D}{2h} + \frac{D^2}{8k} \right)$$

This matches Plank's form with $P = 1/2$ and $R = 1/8$ for an infinite slab.

### 3.1.6 Generalization to Other Geometries

For a **sphere** of radius $R$, the conduction path is radial, and the equation becomes:

$$t_f = \frac{\rho \lambda}{T_f - T_a} \left( \frac{D}{6h} + \frac{D^2}{24k} \right)$$

with $D = 2R$ (diameter), so $P = 1/6$, $R = 1/24$.

For an **infinite cylinder** of radius $R$:

$$t_f = \frac{\rho \lambda}{T_f - T_a} \left( \frac{D}{4h} + \frac{D^2}{16k} \right)$$

with $D = 2R$, so $P = 1/4$, $R = 1/16$.

## 3.2 Worked Example 1 — Plank's Equation for a Sphere (Apple)

**Problem:** Estimate the freezing time of an apple (treated as a sphere) of diameter 70 mm, density 840 kg/m³, latent heat 280 kJ/kg (accounting for ~84 % freezable water), thermal conductivity of frozen apple 1.25 W/(m·K), freezing point −1.5 °C, in a blast freezer with air at −30 °C and surface heat transfer coefficient $h = 25$ W/(m²·K).

**Solution:**

Given:
- $D = 0.070$ m
- $\rho = 840$ kg/m³
- $\lambda = 280{,}000$ J/kg
- $k = 1.25$ W/(m·K)
- $T_f = -1.5$ °C, $T_a = -30$ °C
- $h = 25$ W/(m²·K)
- Geometry: Sphere → $P = 1/6$, $R = 1/24$

Step 1: Calculate the temperature driving force:

$$T_f - T_a = -1.5 - (-30) = 28.5 \text{ °C}$$

Step 2: Apply Plank's equation:

$$t_f = \frac{\rho \lambda}{T_f - T_a} \left( \frac{P \cdot D}{h} + \frac{R \cdot D^2}{k} \right)$$

Step 3: Evaluate the two terms inside the brackets:

**Convective term:**

$$\frac{P \cdot D}{h} = \frac{(1/6) \times 0.070}{25} = \frac{0.01167}{25} = 4.667 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2\cdot\text{K/W}$$

**Conductive term:**

$$\frac{R \cdot D^2}{k} = \frac{(1/24) \times (0.070)^2}{1.25} = \frac{(0.04167) \times 0.0049}{1.25} = \frac{2.042 \times 10^{-4}}{1.25} = 1.633 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2\cdot\text{K/W}$$

Step 4: Sum the resistances:

$$\text{Sum} = 4.667 \times 10^{-4} + 1.633 \times 10^{-4} = 6.300 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2\cdot\text{K/W}$$

Step 5: Calculate the prefactor:

$$\frac{\rho \lambda}{T_f - T_a} = \frac{840 \times 280{,}000}{28.5} = \frac{2.352 \times 10^8}{28.5} = 8.253 \times 10^6 \text{ J/(m}^3\cdot\text{K)}$$

Step 6: Multiply:

$$t_f = 8.253 \times 10^6 \times 6.300 \times 10^{-4} = 5199 \text{ s}$$

Step 7: Convert to hours:

$$t_f = \frac{5199}{3600} \approx 1.44 \text{ hours} \approx 87 \text{ minutes}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** Notice that the convective term ($4.67 \times 10^{-4}$) is approximately **2.86 times** larger than the conductive term ($1.63 \times 10^{-4}$). This means **for a small object like an apple, the surface heat transfer coefficient (air blast velocity) is the limiting factor**, not the internal conduction of the apple. Increasing $h$ from 25 to 100 W/m²·K would roughly halve the freezing time.

## 3.3 Worked Example 2 — Pham's Equation for Slab (Frozen Mango Pulp)

**Problem:** Frozen mango pulp is frozen as a slab of half-thickness $a = 0.025$ m (5 cm). Properties: $\rho = 1050$ kg/m³, $\lambda = 260$ kJ/kg, $c_{pi} = 1.9$ kJ/(kg·K), $k = 1.35$ W/(m·K). Freezing point: −1.8 °C. Air at −35 °C with $h = 30$ W/(m²·K). Estimate freezing time using the simplified slab form.

**Solution:**

Given:
- $a = 0.025$ m
- $\rho = 1050$ kg/m³
- $\lambda = 260{,}000$ J/kg
- $T_f = -1.8$ °C, $T_a = -35$ °C
- $h = 30$ W/(m²·K)
- $k = 1.35$ W/(m·K)

Step 1: Temperature difference:

$$T_f - T_a = -1.8 - (-35) = 33.2 \text{ K}$$

Step 2: Convective term:

$$\frac{a}{h} = \frac{0.025}{30} = 8.333 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2\cdot\text{K/W}$$

Step 3: Conductive term:

$$\frac{a^2}{2k} = \frac{(0.025)^2}{2 \times 1.35} = \frac{6.25 \times 10^{-4}}{2.7} = 2.315 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2\cdot\text{K/W}$$

Step 4: Sum:

$$8.333 \times 10^{-4} + 2.315 \times 10^{-4} = 1.0648 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2\cdot\text{K/W}$$

Step 5: Prefactor:

$$\frac{\rho \lambda}{T_f - T_a} = \frac{1050 \times 260{,}000}{33.2} = 8.222 \times 10^6$$

Step 6: Final calculation:

$$t_f = 8.222 \times 10^6 \times 1.0648 \times 10^{-3} = 8755 \text{ s}$$

Step 7: Convert:

$$t_f = \frac{8755}{3600} \approx 2.43 \text{ hours} \approx 146 \text{ minutes}$$

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Parameter | Plank's Equation | Pham's Equation | Stage-wise Lumped |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Accuracy** | ±20–30 % | ±10–15 % | ±30 %+ |
| **Inputs needed** | 4 (λ, h, k, geometry) | 4 (similar) | 6 (c_p, λ, etc.) |
| **Sharp interface?** | Yes (assumed) | No (gradual) | No |
| **Variable properties?** | No | Implicitly yes | No |
| **Best for** | Quick design, KTU exams | Industrial design | Pre/post-cooling only |
| **KTU exam weightage** | **HIGH** | Medium | High (in Part A) |

## 3.5 Numerical Sensitivity Analysis

For the apple example (3.2), let's examine how freezing time changes with $h$:

| $h$ (W/m²·K) | $t_f$ (minutes) | % change |
|---|---|---|
| 10 (still air) | 195 | +125 % |
| 25 (blast) | 87 | baseline |
| 50 (strong blast) | 56 | −36 % |
| 100 (high-velocity air) | 41 | −53 % |
| 500 (cryogenic) | 30 | −66 % |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Takeaway:** Doubling $h$ does NOT halve $t_f$. The relationship is hyperbolic. Above $h \approx 200$ W/m²·K, further increases give diminishing returns because internal conduction becomes the bottleneck.

```mermaid

    B2 --> C["Product at Freezing Point Tf (e.g., -1.5 C)"]
    C --> D["STAGE 2: Phase Change"]
    D --> D1["Latent heat of fusion removed"]
    D1 --> D2["Ice crystals nucleate and grow"]
    D2 --> D3["Temperature remains nearly constant at Tf"]
    D3 --> E["Product Fully Frozen at Tf"]
    E --> F["STAGE 3: Sub-Cooling"]

    F1 --> F2["Temperature drops from Tf to Tfinal (e.g., -18 C)"]
    F2 --> G["Final Frozen Product Ready for Cold Storage"]
    
    H["STAGE 1: t1 - Short duration - 10 to 15 percent of total time"] -.-> B
```
