> 1. Pre-cooling unit (immediate field-heat removal)
> 2. Cold storage / pack-house chamber
> 3. Refrigerated transport vehicle (reefer)
> 4. Wholesale / retail cold room
>
> *(A 5th acceptable link is the consumer refrigerator.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
Distinguish between **sensible heat load** and **latent heat load** in a refrigerated vehicle carrying mangoes.

**Model Answer:**

> | **Sensible Heat ($Q_{2s}$)** | **Latent Heat ($Q_{2l}$)** |
> |---|---|
> | Heat removed to reduce product temperature **without phase change** | Heat removed to **change phase** (water to ice) |
> | $Q_{2s} = m \cdot C_p \cdot \Delta T$ | $Q_{2l} = m \cdot h_{fg}$ |
> | Applicable when cooling above the freezing point | Applicable when cooling below the freezing point |
> | Example: cooling mangoes from 30°C to 13°C | Example: freezing peas from 0°C to –18°C |
> | Smaller magnitude, faster removal | Larger magnitude (~335 kJ/kg), energy-intensive |

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]*

**(a)** A reefer truck is loaded with **5,000 kg of mangoes** at a temperature of **30°C**. The desired transit temperature is **13°C**. The specific heat of mangoes above freezing is **3.5 kJ/(kg·K)**. Calculate the **product sensible heat load** in kJ and the **average cooling rate required in kW** if the pull-down must be completed in **4 hours**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **heat of respiration** of horticultural produce with reference to **climacteric** and **non-climacteric** fruits. Why is respiration load particularly critical when designing reefers for mango or banana transport? **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Step 1 — Identify given data:**
$m = 5000 \; kg$, $T_{load} = 30^\circ C$, $T_{set} = 13^\circ C$, $C_p = 3.5 \; kJ/(kg \cdot K)$, $t = 4 \; h = 14{,}400 \; s$.

**Step 2 — Compute the cooling range:**

$$
\Delta T = T_{load} - T_{set} = 30 - 13 = 17 \; K
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the sensible heat equation:**

$$
Q_{2s} = m \cdot C_p \cdot \Delta T
$$

$$
Q_{2s} = 5000 \times 3.5 \times 17
$$

$$
Q_{2s} = 297{,}500 \; kJ
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to kW by dividing by time in seconds:**

$$
\dot{Q}_{2s} = \frac{297{,}500}{14{,}400} \approx 20.66 \; kW
$$

> ✅ **Final Answer:** $Q_{2s} = 2.975 \times 10^5 \; kJ$ and $\dot{Q}_{2s} \approx 20.66 \; kW$.

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

The **heat of respiration ($Q_5$)** is the metabolic heat released by living produce as it respires — converting stored sugars into $CO_2$, water, and energy.

**Climacteric fruits** (mango, banana, tomato, papaya) exhibit a **respiratory burst** during ripening, accompanied by a sharp spike in ethylene production. The respiration rate can **double or triple** within 24–48 hours of harvest.

**Non-climacteric fruits** (grape, apple, citrus, pomegranate) have a **steady, low respiration rate** with no burst.

**Why critical for mango/banana reefers:**

1. Mango and banana have **very high respiration rates** ($\dot{q}_r \approx 0.10$–$0.20 \; W/kg$ at 13°C), far exceeding that of apples.
2. The respiration burst during ripening **adds a continuous, time-dependent load** ($Q_5 = m \cdot \dot{q}_r \cdot t$).
3. If not extracted by the reefer, this metabolic heat **raises the internal body temperature**, accelerating further respiration — a **runaway thermal feedback loop**.
4. The compressor must be **sized 15–25% larger** than for non-climacteric produce of identical mass.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – May 2023 Model]*

**(a)** With the help of a **neat sketch**, describe the construction and working of a **mechanical refrigerated road transport vehicle**. Label the major components. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An insulated reefer truck has a **total external surface area of 60 m²**, an overall heat transfer coefficient of $U = 0.35 \; W/(m^2 \cdot K)$, and operates with an **outside temperature of 38°C** and an **inside set temperature of 4°C**. The compressor runs **20 hours per day**. Calculate the **daily heat transmission load** in kJ and the **average transmission power** in kW. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

A mechanical refrigerated road transport vehicle (reefer truck) consists of the following major components:

1. **Insulated Cargo Body** — Built with PUF (Polyurethane Foam) sandwiched between galvanized steel (outer) and food-grade aluminium (inner) panels. Wall thickness typically **75–100 mm**.
2. **Refrigeration Unit** — A self-contained vapour-compression system comprising:
   - **Compressor** (hermetic/semi-hermetic, diesel-engine or electric-motor driven)
   - **Condenser** (air-cooled, with axial fans)
   - **Thermostatic Expansion Valve (TXV)** or capillary tube
   - **Evaporator** (forced-convection type with multiple fans)
3. **Air Distribution System** — T-bar ceiling and floor channels ensuring uniform cold-air circulation around the produce.
4. **Control Panel** — Digital thermostat, defrost timer, and data logger.
5. **Door** — Insulated rear door with rubber gasket and PVC strip-curtain to minimize infiltration.
6. **Monitoring System** — GPS + GSM temperature telemetry.

**Working principle:** The compressor circulates refrigerant (R-404A/R-134a) through the loop. The evaporator absorbs heat from the body interior (pulling it from the produce), the condenser rejects this heat to ambient air, and the TXV throttles the liquid refrigerant to repeat the cycle. The thermostat cycles the compressor to maintain the set-point.

*(For the KTU exam, a labelled line diagram showing the box body with a roof-mounted refrigeration unit, evaporator fan, produce load, and insulated walls is expected.)*

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1 — Given data:** $A = 60 \; m^2$, $U = 0.35 \; W/(m^2 \cdot K)$, $T_{out} = 38^\circ C$, $T_{in} = 4^\circ C$, $t = 20 \; h = 72{,}000 \; s$.

**Step 2 — Compute $\Delta T$:**

$$
\Delta T = T_{out} - T_{in} = 38 - 4 = 34 \; K
$$

**Step 3 — Compute instantaneous transmission power:**

$$
\dot{Q}_1 = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T
$$

$$
\dot{Q}_1 = 0.35 \times 60 \times 34
$$

$$
\dot{Q}_1 = 714 \; W = 0.714 \; kW
$$

**Step 4 — Compute daily energy load:**

$$
Q_1 = \dot{Q}_1 \times t
$$

$$
Q_1 = 0.714 \; kW \times 20 \; h
$$

$$
Q_1 = 14.28 \; kWh
$$

**Step 5 — Convert to kJ (1 kWh = 3600 kJ):**

$$
Q_1 = 14.28 \times 3600 = 51{,}408 \; kJ
$$

> ✅ **Final Answer:** $Q_1 \approx 51{,}408 \; kJ/day$ and $\dot{Q}_1 \approx 0.714 \; kW$.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. **Forgetting to include the respiration heat ($Q_5$)** in the total load for climacteric fruits. Up to 4 marks lost.
> 2. **Mixing $kW$ and $kJ$** without proper time conversion. Always write: $Q = \dot{Q} \cdot t$.
> 3. **Using Fahrenheit or wrong unit** for $\Delta T$. KTU strictly expects $K$ (or $^\circ C$ difference).
> 4. **Ignoring the safety factor** of 1.15–1.25 when asked to "size" a compressor. This is a 2-mark deduction.
> 5. **Co-loading ethylene-producer and ethylene-sensitive produce** in design examples. This is an automatic "design failure" in the examiner's eyes.
> 6. **Omitting the latent heat term** when produce temperature crosses the freezing point.
> 7. **Confusing $Q_3$ (infiltration)** with $Q_1$ (transmission). They are separate loads and must be computed separately.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ A **cold chain** is a temperature-controlled, unbroken supply network. A **reefer vehicle** is one link in this chain.
- ✅ The **five heat loads** in a reefer are: $Q_1$ (transmission), $Q_2$ (product sensible + latent), $Q_3$ (infiltration), $Q_4$ (internal sources), and $Q_5$ (respiration).
- ✅ **$Q_1 = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T$** is the most-tested equation. Master its derivation and unit conversions.
- ✅ **Climacteric fruits** (mango, banana, tomato) respire strongly and produce ethylene — they need larger compressors and **must not be co-loaded** with ethylene-sensitive produce.
- ✅ **Respiration heat ($Q_5$) is time-dependent** and continues throughout transit — never treat it as a one-time pull-down load.
- ✅ **Air infiltration $Q_3$** is often underestimated; in multi-stop delivery, it can rival $Q_1$.
- ✅ **PUF insulation** with $k \approx 0.022 \; W/(m \cdot K)$ is the industry standard. Steel skin resistance is negligible.
- ✅ Always apply a **safety factor of 1.15–1.25** when sizing the compressor capacity.
- ✅ The **$Q_{10}$ rule** states that for every 10°C rise in temperature, the spoilage rate of produce roughly doubles. This is the strongest single argument for cold chain integrity.
- ✅ In KTU numerical problems, **always state units explicitly** ($kJ$, $kW$, $kg$, $m^2$, $K$) and **show all five steps** of the calculation.
- ✅ Cold chain **break points** occur most frequently during: (1) loading bay delays, (2) door openings in transit, (3) defrost cycles, and (4) last-mile retail transfer.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Dryers for Fruits and Vegetables: Tray, Drum, Spray, Pneumatic, Fluidized Bed Dryer, and Foam Mat Drying; Osmo-Dehydration; Freeze Drying
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAGT742 - POST HARVEST ENGINEERING OF HORTICULTURAL CROPS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Dryers for Fruits and Vegetables: Tray, Drum, Spray, Pneumatic, Fluidized Bed Dryer, and Foam Mat Drying; Osmo-Dehydration; Freeze Drying

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Drying in Post-Harvest Engineering?

In the context of horticultural crop processing, **drying** is defined as a simultaneous heat and mass transfer operation in which the moisture content of a biological material (fruit or vegetable) is reduced by evaporation of water into a hot, unsaturated gas stream (usually air), to a level that inhibits microbial spoilage and allows safe storage. The objective is to extend shelf life, reduce weight for transport, and preserve nutrients, color, and aroma.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** *Drying is a coupled heat-and-mass-transfer unit operation in which unbound water is removed from a hygroscopic biological solid by vaporization, driven by the vapor pressure gradient between the wet material and the surrounding heated air.*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Sweating Towel on a Hot Stone"

Imagine spreading a wet cotton towel over a heated stone on a sunny day. The stone supplies **sensible heat** to the towel, water inside the cloth turns to vapor, and the surrounding air sweeps the vapor away. The towel becomes bone-dry in a few hours. Replace the towel with apple slices, the stone with a steam coil, and the moving air with a blower — and you have an **industrial tray dryer**. The principle is identical: heat in, vapor out, dry product left behind.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric for the Industry:** The final moisture content for safe storage of most dried fruits and vegetables is between **3 % and 8 % (wet basis)**. For long-term ambient storage, the value must lie below the **water activity (aw) = 0.60** threshold to suppress mold growth.

---

## 1.3 Classification of Dryers — A Bird's-Eye View

Drying equipment used for horticultural crops can be broadly classified by:

1. **Mode of Heat Transfer** — Convection (tray, spray, fluidized bed), Conduction (drum), Radiation (solar, infrared).
2. **Mode of Material Handling** — Batch (tray, cabinet) vs. Continuous (tunnel, belt, spray, fluidized bed).
3. **Physical State of Feed** — Solid pieces (tray, fluidized bed), slurry/liquid (spray, drum), paste (foam mat).
4. **Operating Pressure** — Atmospheric (most dryers) vs. Vacuum/Freeze drying.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Highlight:** Of the dozens of industrial dryer types, the syllabus restricts you to **eight** families — Tray, Drum, Spray, Pneumatic, Fluidized Bed, Foam Mat, Osmo-Dehydration, and Freeze Drying. Master the operating principle, feed state, and a representative diagram of each.

---

## 1.4 One-Paragraph Definition of Each Dryer

| # | Dryer | One-Line Definition |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Tray / Cabinet Dryer** | A batch convective dryer where thin layers of product are placed on perforated trays inside an insulated chamber through which hot air is circulated. |
| 2 | **Drum Dryer** | A conduction dryer in which a liquid/paste film is dried on the surface of a rotating, internally heated metallic drum; the dried sheet is scraped off by a knife. |
| 3 | **Spray Dryer** | A continuous convective dryer that atomizes a liquid feed into a fine spray inside a heated chamber, producing spherical powder particles in seconds. |
| 4 | **Pneumatic Dryer** | A co-current flash dryer in which moist, finely divided particles are conveyed in a hot gas stream and dried in suspension. |
| 5 | **Fluidized Bed Dryer** | A dryer in which hot air is forced upward through a bed of particulate solids at a velocity sufficient to suspend them, giving excellent gas–solid contact. |
| 6 | **Foam Mat Drying** | A technique in which a liquid/puree is whipped into a stable foam with a foaming agent, then dried as a thin mat using hot air. |
| 7 | **Osmo-Dehydration** | A non-thermal water removal process in which fruits/vegetables are immersed in a concentrated osmotic solution (sugar or salt) to drive out water. |
| 8 | **Freeze Drying (Lyophilization)** | A low-temperature dehydration process where water is removed by sublimation from a frozen state under high vacuum. |

---

## 1.5 Why Multiple Dryers? — Matching the Dryer to the Product

| Product State | Best Dryer | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Whole slices / cubes (apple, banana, potato) | Tray, Fluidized Bed, Pneumatic | Gentle handling, uniform drying |
| Puree / juice (tomato, mango) | Spray, Drum, Foam Mat | Liquid cannot be handled on trays |
| Heat-sensitive (color, vitamin C) | Freeze, Foam Mat | Low temperature preserves quality |
| Quick bulk drying of granules (peas, corn) | Fluidized Bed, Pneumatic | Excellent heat transfer coefficient |

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy:** Choosing a dryer is like choosing a cooking method — you would not deep-fry a salad nor steam-fry an onion bhaji. The **product's physical state** and **thermal sensitivity** dictate the dryer.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Fundamental Drying Equation

Drying is governed by a simultaneous heat-and-mass-transfer balance. The driving force is the **vapor pressure deficit** between the wet surface and the bulk air.

**Mass balance on water:**

$$\frac{dW}{dt} = -h_m \, A \, (P_{w,s} - P_{w,a})$$

where:
- $W$ = mass of water in the solid (kg)
- $t$ = time (s)
- $h_m$ = mass-transfer coefficient (kg/m²·s·Pa)
- $A$ = surface area available for evaporation (m²)
- $P_{w,s}$ = vapor pressure of water at the surface (Pa)
- $P_{w,a}$ = partial pressure of water vapor in bulk air (Pa)

**Energy balance (heat supplied to evaporate water):**

$$Q = h_g \, A \, (T_a - T_s) = \lambda \, \frac{dW}{dt}$$

where:
- $h_g$ = convective heat-transfer coefficient (W/m²·K)
- $T_a, T_s$ = air and surface temperatures (K)
- $\lambda$ = latent heat of vaporization of water (≈ **2.26 × 10⁶ J/kg** at 100 °C)

Combining the two:

$$\frac{dW}{dt} = \frac{h_g}{\lambda} \, A \, (T_a - T_s)$$

This is the foundation of **Lewis's relationship**, which says the drying rate is proportional to the air–surface temperature difference.

---

## 2.2 Moisture Content Definitions

### (a) Moisture Content — Wet Basis (wb)

$$M_{wb} = \frac{m_w}{m_w + m_{dm}} \times 100\%$$

### (b) Moisture Content — Dry Basis (db)

$$M_{db} = \frac{m_w}{m_{dm}} \times 100\% = \frac{W - W_d}{W_d}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why two bases?** Wet basis is intuitive for industry ("the apple is 80 % water"). Dry basis is mathematically cleaner because $m_{dm}$ (dry matter mass) is **constant** during drying, whereas total mass is not.

### (c) Interconversion

$$M_{db} = \frac{M_{wb}}{1 - M_{wb}} \quad ; \quad M_{wb} = \frac{M_{db}}{1 + M_{db}}$$

---

## 2.3 The Drying-Rate Curve — Three Critical Periods

A typical drying curve (moisture content vs. time) is non-linear. The corresponding **drying-rate curve** (rate vs. moisture content) shows three distinct periods:

1. **Adjusting (Initial) Period** — Surface warms up; rate rises.
2. **Constant-Rate Period** — Free water at surface evaporates; rate is governed purely by external conditions ($h_g$, $\Delta T$, $\Delta P$).
3. **Falling-Rate Period** — Internal moisture diffusion controls the rate; surface starts to dry out.

Mathematically:

$$N = -\frac{dM_{db}}{dt} \quad \text{(drying rate, kg water/kg dry matter·hr)}$$

In the constant-rate period:

$$N_c = \frac{h_g \, (T_a - T_{wb})}{\lambda \, m_{dm}}$$

where $T_{wb}$ is the **wet-bulb temperature** of the air.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** When asked "which period controls fruit drying?", the correct answer is almost always the **falling-rate period**, because most horticultural products have high sugar/pectin content and bound water — there is essentially no constant-rate phase.

---

## 2.4 Thin-Layer Drying Models (Empirical)

Used to predict moisture ratio vs. time for thin layers of fruits and vegetables:

| Model | Equation | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Newton (Lewis)** | $MR = \exp(-k\,t)$ | First-order approximation |
| **Page** | $MR = \exp(-k\,t^n)$ | Most widely used for foods |
| **Henderson–Pabis** | $MR = a\,\exp(-k\,t)$ | One-parameter fit |
| **Midilli–Kucuk** | $MR = a\,\exp(-k\,t^n) + b\,t$ | Excellent fit for many fruits |

where the **Moisture Ratio (MR)** is:

$$MR = \frac{M_t - M_e}{M_0 - M_e}$$

with:
- $M_t$ = moisture content at time $t$
- $M_0$ = initial moisture content
- $M_e$ = equilibrium moisture content
- $k, n, a, b$ = empirical drying constants

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $M_{wb} = m_w / (m_w + m_{dm}) \cdot 100$ | Wet-basis moisture | % |
| 2 | $M_{db} = m_w / m_{dm} \cdot 100$ | Dry-basis moisture | % |
| 3 | $M_{db} = M_{wb} / (1 - M_{wb})$ | Conversion wb → db | — |
| 4 | $MR = (M_t - M_e)/(M_0 - M_e)$ | Moisture ratio (dimensionless) | — |
| 5 | $MR = \exp(-k\,t)$ | Newton model | — |
| 6 | $MR = \exp(-k\,t^n)$ | Page model | — |
| 7 | $N = -dM_{db}/dt$ | Drying rate | kg/kg·hr |
| 8 | $N_c = h_g (T_a - T_{wb}) / (\lambda\, m_{dm})$ | Constant-rate drying | kg/kg·hr |
| 9 | $Q = \lambda \cdot m_w$ | Heat required to evaporate water | kJ |
| 10 | $\lambda \approx 2400 - 2.4\,T$ | Latent heat (T in °C) | kJ/kg |
| 11 | $WL = (M_0 - M_t)\,m_0 / 100$ | Water loss (wb basis) | kg |
| 12 | $SG = (m_t - m_0)\,m_0 / m_0$ | Solid gain (osmo) | g/100g |
| 13 | $\dot{m}_a = \dot{m}_w / (Y_2 - Y_1)$ | Air mass flow rate | kg dry air/s |
| 14 | $\eta_{th} = (T_1 - T_2)/(T_1 - T_{wb})$ | Dryer thermal efficiency | — |
| 15 | $P_{sat}(T) = 610.78 \cdot \exp\!\left(\frac{17.27\,T}{T + 237.3}\right)$ | Saturation vapor pressure | Pa |
| 16 | $t_{sub} = \frac{\rho_w\, \lambda_{sub}\, L^2}{2\,k_s\,(T_s - T_e)}$ | Freeze-drying time (slab) | s |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Markdown Rule:** Inside the table above, every absolute value / magnitude indicator is written as `\vert` or `\mid` to avoid breaking the Markdown pipe layout. Do **not** type a literal `\vert` symbol — write it as `\vert` in LaTeX.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Drying-rate curves** drive **dryer sizing** — knowing which period controls lets you pick chamber length, belt speed, or air residence time.
- **MR models** are used by R&D teams to design solar, cabinet, and fluidized-bed dryers for mango, banana, and grape products.
- **Latent heat and air-mass-flow equations** form the basis for calculating **specific energy consumption** (kWh per kg water removed), a key economic indicator for dryer selection.
- **Freeze-drying time equation** tells you upfront whether sublimation drying is commercially viable for a given product thickness.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Wet-Basis to Dry-Basis Moisture

**Given:** A sample of freshly cut mango has total mass $m_{total} = 100$ kg and contains 80 % water (wb).

**Step 1 — Identify components.**

$$\text{Water mass: } m_w = 0.80 \times 100 = 80 \text{ kg}$$
$$\text{Dry matter: } m_{dm} = 100 - 80 = 20 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 2 — Apply dry-basis formula.**

$$M_{db} = \frac{m_w}{m_{dm}} \times 100 = \frac{80}{20} \times 100 = 400\%$$

**Step 3 — Verify the interconversion formula.**

$$M_{db} = \frac{M_{wb}}{1 - M_{wb}} = \frac{0.80}{1 - 0.80} = \frac{0.80}{0.20} = 4.0$$

Multiplying by 100 gives 400 %, matching the direct calculation. ✔

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Drying Rate from the Constant-Rate Equation

**Given:** A tray dryer holds $m_{dm} = 5$ kg of apple slices (dry matter). Air enters at $T_a = 80 °C$ with wet-bulb $T_{wb} = 35 °C$. The convective coefficient is $h_g = 25$ W/m²·K. The exposed evaporation surface area is $A = 0.8$ m².

**Step 1 — Write the rate equation.**

$$\frac{dW}{dt} = \frac{h_g\, A\, (T_a - T_{wb})}{\lambda}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values.** (Take $\lambda = 2400$ kJ/kg at the wet-bulb temperature.)

$$\frac{dW}{dt} = \frac{25 \times 0.8 \times (80 - 35)}{2400 \times 10^3}$$

**Step 3 — Numerator evaluation.**

$$\text{Numerator} = 25 \times 0.8 \times 45 = 900 \text{ W}$$

**Step 4 — Final evaluation.**

$$\frac{dW}{dt} = \frac{900}{2{,}400{,}000} = 3.75 \times 10^{-4} \text{ kg/s} = 1.35 \text{ kg/hr}$$

**Step 5 — Drying rate per kg of dry matter.**

$$N_c = \frac{1.35}{5} = 0.27 \text{ kg water/kg dm·hr}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Moisture Ratio Using Page's Model

**Given:** Banana slices dried in a cabinet dryer give $M_0 = 320\%$, $M_e = 12\%$ (db). At $t = 4$ hr, $M_t = 80\%$ (db). The drying constants are $k = 0.45$ /hr, $n = 1.2$.

**Step 1 — Compute the moisture ratio.**

$$MR = \frac{M_t - M_e}{M_0 - M_e} = \frac{80 - 12}{320 - 12} = \frac{68}{308} = 0.2208$$

**Step 2 — Apply Page's model.**

$$MR_{Page} = \exp(-k\,t^n) = \exp(-0.45 \times 4^{1.2})$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the exponent.**

$$4^{1.2} = e^{1.2\,\ln 4} = e^{1.2 \times 1.3863} = e^{1.6636} = 5.275$$

$$k\,t^n = 0.45 \times 5.275 = 2.374$$

**Step 4 — Final MR.**

$$MR_{Page} = e^{-2.374} = 0.0932$$

**Step 5 — Compare with experimental value.**

$$MR_{exp} = 0.2208 \quad \text{vs} \quad MR_{Page} = 0.0932$$

The model under-predicts, indicating that banana may require **higher-order** models (Midilli–Kucuk) for better fit.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Water Loss in Osmo-Dehydration

**Given:** 100 g of carrot cubes immersed in 60 % sucrose solution for 3 hours. Final mass = 88 g, dry matter remains at 9 g.

**Step 1 — Initial water content.**

$$m_{w,0} = 100 - 9 = 91 \text{ g}$$

**Step 2 — Final water content.**

$$m_{w,t} = 88 - 9 = 79 \text{ g}$$

**Step 3 — Water loss.**

$$WL = m_{w,0} - m_{w,t} = 91 - 79 = 12 \text{ g}$$

**Step 4 — Express as % of initial mass.**

$$WL\% = \frac{12}{100} \times 100 = 12\%$$

**Step 5 — Solid gain.** (Assume dry matter was unchanged by leaching.)

Since $m_{dm} = 9$ g both before and after, in a pure-osmosis case where leaching is negligible:

$$SG = 0\%$$

But if solute enters the cubes, dry matter *increases*. Real experimental values for carrot in 60 % sucrose give **SG ≈ 3–5 %** with corresponding **WL ≈ 30–45 %**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Concept:** *Water Loss* (WL) and *Solid Gain* (SG) are the two key performance indices of osmotic dehydration, expressed in **g per 100 g initial mass**.

---

## 3.5 Derivation: Freeze-Drying Time for a Slab

**Given:** A 10 mm-thick slab of frozen strawberry, initial ice density $\rho_{ice} = 917$ kg/m³, latent heat of sublimation $\lambda_{sub} = 2.83 \times 10^6$ J/kg, thermal conductivity of dried layer $k_s = 0.04$ W/m·K, surface temperature $T_s = 30 °C$, sublimation-front temperature $T_e = -10 °C$.

**Step 1 — Apply slab sublimation time equation.**

$$t_{sub} = \frac{\rho_{ice}\, \lambda_{sub}\, L^2}{2\, k_s\, (T_s - T_e)}$$

**Step 2 — Plug numbers.**

$$\rho_{ice} = 917 \text{ kg/m³},\ \lambda = 2.83\times 10^6 \text{ J/kg},\ L = 0.010 \text{ m}$$
$$k_s = 0.04 \text{ W/m·K},\ \Delta T = 30 - (-10) = 40 \text{ K}$$

**Step 3 — Numerator.**

$$\rho_{ice}\,\lambda_{sub}\,L^2 = 917 \times 2.83\times10^6 \times (0.010)^2$$
$$= 917 \times 2.83\times10^6 \times 10^{-4}$$
$$= 917 \times 283 = 259{,}511 \text{ J/m·K}$$

**Step 4 — Denominator.**

$$2\,k_s\,\Delta T = 2 \times 0.04 \times 40 = 3.2 \text{ W/m·K}$$

**Step 5 — Final time.**

$$t_{sub} = \frac{259{,}511}{3.2} = 81{,}097 \text{ s} \approx 22.5 \text{ hours}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This long residence time is exactly why freeze drying is **energy-intensive** and reserved for high-value products (strawberry, coffee, herbs).

---

## 3.6 Analytical Comparison Matrix of All Eight Dryers

| Parameter | Tray | Drum | Spray | Pneumatic | Fluidized Bed | Foam Mat | Osmo | Freeze |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Feed State** | Solid | Liquid/Paste | Liquid | Granular | Granular | Foam | Solid | Frozen |
| **Heat Mode** | Convection | Conduction | Convection | Convection | Convection | Convection | None (mass) | Sublimation |
| **Operating T (°C)** | 50–90 | 120–160 | 150–220 | 100–400 | 40–150 | 50–80 | 20–50 | −30 to +30 |
| **Pressure** | Atm | Atm | Atm | Atm | Atm | Atm | Atm | Vacuum |
| **Batch / Continuous** | Batch | Continuous | Continuous | Continuous | Batch / Cont | Batch | Batch | Batch |
| **Drying Time** | 6–24 hr | sec–min | sec | sec | min | 3–8 hr | 1–5 hr | 10–30 hr |
| **Energy Intensity** | Medium | Medium | High | Medium | Medium | Low | Very Low | Very High |
| **Product Form** | Slice, cube | Sheet/flakes | Powder | Granules | Granules | Sheet/powder | Cut pieces | Porous solid |
| **Capital Cost** | Low | Med | High | Med | Med | Low | Very Low | Very High |
| **Typical Products** | Apple, grape, herbs | Potato flakes, milk | Milk, tomato powder | Starch, MSG | Peas, corn, dice | Mango, papaya, banana | Pineapple, cherry | Strawberry, coffee, herbs |
| **Quality Retention** | Medium | Medium-Low | High | Med-High | Med-High | High | High | Very High |
| **Shrinkage / Case-Hardening** | Yes | Yes | None | Med | Low | Low | Low | None |
| **Rehydration** | Fair | Good | Excellent | Good | Good | Excellent | Excellent | Excellent |

---

## 3.7 Worked Numerical Example for KTU Valuation Practice

**Problem:** A tray dryer processes 50 kg of sliced mango per batch. Initial moisture is 80 % (wb), final is 15 % (wb). The drying takes 8 hours. Calculate (a) initial and final dry-basis moisture, (b) total water removed, (c) average drying rate in kg water/hr.

**Solution:**

**(a) Convert to dry basis.**

$$M_0^{db} = \frac{0.80}{1 - 0.80} = 4.0 = 400\%$$
$$M_f^{db} = \frac{0.15}{1 - 0.15} = 0.1765 = 17.65\%$$

**(b) Initial and final dry-matter mass.**

Total water = 0.80 × 50 = 40 kg initially → Dry matter = 10 kg.
Final total mass = 10 / (1 − 0.15) = 11.76 kg.
Water remaining = 0.15 × 11.76 = 1.76 kg.
Water removed = 40 − 1.76 = **38.24 kg**.

**(c) Average drying rate.**

$$\text{Rate} = \frac{38.24 \text{ kg}}{8 \text{ hr}} = 4.78 \text{ kg water/hr}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Many students compute water removed as $(80\% - 15\%)\times 50 = 32.5$ kg. **This is wrong**, because the total mass changes during drying. Always work in dry-matter mass for correctness.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 System-Level Architecture of a Tray / Cabinet Dryer

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Fresh Air Inlet] --> B1[Pre-Heater / Filter]
    B1 --> C1[Heating Coil / Steam]
    C1 --> D1[Plenum Chamber]
    D1 --> E1[Perforated Trays with Product]
    E1 --> F1[Fan / Blower Recirculation]
    F1 --> G1[Exhaust to Atmosphere via Damper]
    E1 -.Recirculated Air.-> D1
    classDef hw fill:#FDE7A7,stroke:#7A5A00,color:#3A2C00;
    classDef process fill:#CFE6FF,stroke:#003D7A,color:#001F3D;
    class A1,B1,C1,F1,G1 hw;
    class D1,E1 process;
```

**Reading guide:** Fresh ambient air is filtered, heated by steam coils, distributed through a plenum, passes horizontally through stacked trays carrying sliced fruit, picks up moisture, and is partly exhausted / partly recirculated.

---

## 4.2 Drum Dryer Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A2[Feed Tank - Liquid or Paste] --> B2[Applicator Roll]
    B2 --> C2[Heated Drum Surface]
    C2 --> D2[Drying Zone - One Revolution]
    D2 --> E2[Doctor Blade / Knife]
    E2 --> F2[Dried Film / Flakes]
    C2 <-.-> G2[Internal Steam / Hot Oil]
    F2 --> H2[Conveyor to Mill or Packer]
    classDef hw fill:#FFD9B3,stroke:#7A3300,color:#3A1800;
    classDef process fill:#D4F4DD,stroke:#1F5C36,color:#082A1A;
    class A2,G2,B2,H2 hw;
    class C2,D2,E2,F2 process;
```

---

## 4.3 Spray Dryer — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A3[Liquid Feed Tank] --> B3[Feed Pump]
    B3 --> C3[Atomizer - Disc or Nozzle]
    C3 --> D3[Drying Chamber - Hot Air in Co-Current]
    D3 --> E3[Cyclone Separator]
    E3 --> F3[Powder Collection Bin]
    D3 --> G3[Bag Filter - Fines]
    G3 --> F3
    D3 --> H3[Exhaust Blower]
    H3 --> I3[Exhaust to Scrubber]
    A3 --> J3[Air Filter]
    J3 --> K3[Air Heater - Indirect Fired]
    K3 --> D3
    classDef hw fill:#E6D7FF,stroke:#2C0E5C,color:#1A0733;
    classDef process fill:#FFE4E1,stroke:#7A1A1A,color:#3A0A0A;
    class B3,K3,J3,H3,I3 hw;
    class A3,C3,D3,E3,F3,G3 process;
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Note:** In spray drying, atomization is the rate-controlling step. The **mean droplet diameter** (Sauter mean) determines whether the particle dries adequately before hitting the chamber wall.

---

## 4.4 Pneumatic (Flash) Dryer

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A4[Wet Feed - Granular or Paste] --> B4[Disintegrator / Shredder]
    B4 --> C4[Drying Duct - Co-Current Hot Gas]
    C4 --> D4[Cyclone Separator]
    D4 --> E4[Dried Product Outlet]
    D4 --> F4[Bag Filter - Fines]
    F4 --> E4
    C4 <-.-> G4[Fan / Blower]
    G4 --> H4[Air Heater]
    H4 --> C4
    classDef hw fill:#FFF2B3,stroke:#7A5C00,color:#3A2E00;
    classDef process fill:#CFEFFF,stroke:#004A66,color:#002633;
    class B4,G4,H4 hw;
    class A4,C4,D4,E4,F4 process;
```

---

## 4.5 Fluidized Bed Dryer

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A5[Wet Particulate Feed] --> B5[Hopper above Bed]
    B5 --> C5[Perforated Distributor Plate]
    D5[Hot Air Blower] --> E5[Air Heater]
    E5 --> F5[Windbox / Plenum Below Plate]
    F5 --> C5
    C5 --> G5[Fluidized Bed Zone]
    G5 --> H5[Cyclone / Bag Filter]
    H5 --> I5[Dried Product Outlet]
    G5 --> J5[Exhaust to Chimney]
    classDef hw fill:#D4F8E8,stroke:#1B5C3F,color:#0A2E1F;
    classDef process fill:#FFE0F0,stroke:#7A1F4F,color:#3A0F27;
    class A5,D5,E5,F5,J5 hw;
    class B5,C5,G5,H5,I5 process;
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Concept:** Fluidization begins at the **minimum fluidization velocity** $u_{mf}$, typically 0.4–1.0 m/s for food particulates. Above $u_{mf}$, pressure drop across the bed equals weight per unit area.

---

## 4.6 Foam Mat Drying — Process Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A6[Fruit Puree] --> B6[Mixer with Foaming Agent and Stabilizer]
    B6 --> C6[Foam Generator - Mechanical Whipping]
    C6 --> D6[Foam Mat Tray - 2 to 5 mm Thickness]
    D6 --> E6[Hot Air Cabinet / Tunnel]
    E6 --> F6[Dried Foam Mat]
    F6 --> G6[Crushing / Milling]
    G6 --> H6[Free-Flowing Powder]
    classDef hw fill:#E8E8FF,stroke:#1F1F7A,color:#0A0A3D;
    classDef process fill:#FFE8C4,stroke:#7A4F00,color:#3A2700;
    class B6,E6 hw;
    class A6,C6,D6,F6,G6,H6 process;
```

---

## 4.7 Osmo-Dehydration Setup

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A7[Fruits or Vegetables - Cut Pieces] --> B7[Immersion Tank]
    B7 --> C7[Concentrated Osmotic Solution - Sucrose or NaCl]
    C7 --> B7
    B7 --> D7[Agitator - Gentle Stirring]
    B7 -.Equilibrium.-> E7[Osmo-Dehydrated Product]
    E7 --> F7[Rinse and Surface Dry]
    F7 --> G7[Optional Secondary Drying - Tray or Fluidized]
    classDef hw fill:#C4F0FF,stroke:#003D5C,color:#001F2E;
    classDef process fill:#FFE2C4,stroke:#7A3A00,color:#3A1D00;
    class C7,D7,B7 hw;
    class A7,E7,F7,G7 process;
```

---

## 4.8 Freeze Drying — Lyophilization Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A8[Fresh Product] --> B8[Pre-Freezing - minus 30 to minus 40 C]
    B8 --> C8[Vacuum Chamber - Below 100 Pa]
    C8 --> D8[Heated Shelves - Sublimation Front]
    D8 --> E8[Vapor Flow Through Dried Layer]
    E8 --> F8[Cold Trap / Condenser - minus 50 C]
    F8 --> G8[Vacuum Pump]
    C8 --> H8[Dried Porous Product]
    H8 --> I8[Sealed Packaging under Inert Gas]
    classDef hw fill:#D6E8FF,stroke:#003366,color:#001A33;
    classDef process fill:#FFE0D6,stroke:#7A2C0E,color:#3A1607;
    class F8,G8,D8,I8 hw;
    class A8,B8,C8,E8,H8 process;
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Triple Point of Water:** $T_{tp} = 0.01 °C$, $P_{tp} = 611.657$ Pa. Freeze drying must operate below this pressure so that ice sublimates directly to vapor without passing through the liquid phase.

---

## 4.9 Decision Tree — Which Dryer Should You Choose?

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Start - Choose a Dryer] --> Q1{Is the feed a liquid or paste?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Is the product heat-sensitive?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> Q3[Use Foam Mat or Spray]
    Q2 -- No --> Q4[Use Drum Dryer]
    Q1 -- No --> Q5{Is the product frozen and high value?}
    Q5 -- Yes --> Q6[Use Freeze Dryer]
    Q5 -- No --> Q7{Need water removal with minimal heat damage?}
    Q7 -- Yes --> Q8[Use Osmo-Dehydration plus Secondary Drying]
    Q7 -- No --> Q9{Small granules like peas or diced vegetables?}
    Q9 -- Yes --> Q10[Use Fluidized Bed Dryer]
    Q9 -- No --> Q11{Large slices or batches?}
    Q11 -- Yes --> Q12[Use Tray or Cabinet Dryer]
    Q11 -- No --> Q13[Use Pneumatic Flash Dryer]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Distinguish between moisture content expressed on a wet basis and a dry basis. Why is dry-basis moisture preferred for drying-rate calculations?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Wet Basis (wb) | Dry Basis (db) |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | $m_w / (m_w + m_{dm})$ | $m_w / m_{dm}$ |
| Range | 0 to 100 % | 0 to ∞ |
| Total mass changes | Yes | No (denominator fixed) |

**Why preferred for drying calculations:** During drying, water leaves the product so the total mass keeps changing. In a wb expression both numerator and denominator vary, which complicates the derivative $\frac{dM}{dt}$. With dry basis, only the numerator changes because the dry-matter mass $m_{dm}$ is constant. This makes the drying rate $N = -dM_{db}/dt$ directly meaningful.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]
**Explain the principle of freeze drying. Why is it considered the highest-quality drying method for horticultural products?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Freeze drying (lyophilization) removes water by **sublimation** — the direct phase transition from solid ice to vapor — under high vacuum (below the triple-point pressure of water, ~611 Pa). The product is first frozen to about −30 °C, then placed in a vacuum chamber where heat is supplied through shelves. Ice sublimes from the frozen core outward through the already-dried porous layer and condenses on a cold trap.

**Reasons for highest quality:**

1. **Low temperature** preserves heat-labile nutrients (vitamin C, anthocyanins, aroma compounds).
2. **No liquid water phase** prevents shrinkage, case-hardening, and solute migration.
3. **Porous structure** gives excellent rehydration behavior.
4. **Color and shape** are retained almost intact (e.g., freeze-dried strawberry, coffee).

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Many students write "water is removed by evaporation" — that is **wrong**. In freeze drying, water is removed by **sublimation**, which requires the product to remain frozen throughout. Use the correct word!

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A — Tray / Drum / Spray Dryers [14 marks] `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]`

**(a) With a neat sketch, describe the construction and working of a tray (cabinet) dryer. List its advantages and limitations for drying fruits and vegetables.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Construction (3 marks):**
- An **insulated rectangular chamber** (length 2–4 m, height 1.5–2 m) made of galvanized iron or stainless steel.
- A **set of stacked perforated trays** (typically 10–20) loaded with the product at a loading density of 5–10 kg/m².
- **Steam or electric heating coils** placed inside the chamber or in an external plenum.
- **Axial or centrifugal fans** for air circulation, with provisions for partial recirculation.
- **Inlet and outlet dampers** for fresh-air makeup and exhaust of humid air.

**Working (2 marks):**
- Hot air at 50–90 °C is forced by the fan through the tray stack in cross-flow or through-flow mode.
- It transfers sensible heat to the product, evaporates surface moisture, picks up vapor, and exits through the exhaust damper.
- The drying proceeds in batch mode over 6–24 hours, with the product manually turned or agitated mid-cycle for uniformity.

**Advantages (1 mark):**
- Simple construction, low capital cost, easy to clean, flexible across many products, suitable for small and medium processors.

**Limitations (1 mark):**
- Batch operation gives low throughput, non-uniform drying between trays, high labor cost for loading/unloading, longer drying time, and risk of overheating product near the heating coils.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes:** (1) Drawing a tray dryer without showing the heating coil location. (2) Forgetting to mention the **turning/mixing** of product to avoid crust formation. (3) Conflating "tray dryer" with "tunnel dryer" — the tunnel is continuous, the tray/cabinet is **batch**.

---

**(b) Explain the working of a spray dryer with a labeled diagram. What are the key parameters that control the quality of the spray-dried powder?** *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Working (4 marks):**
A spray dryer consists of a feed tank, an **atomizer** (centrifugal disc, pressure nozzle, or two-fluid nozzle), a **co-current drying chamber**, a **cyclone separator**, a **bag filter** for fines, and an exhaust system.

1. **Atomization:** The liquid feed is broken into millions of droplets of 10–500 µm diameter. Surface area increases dramatically (from ~1 m² in the tank to ~100,000 m² in spray form), enabling ultra-rapid drying.
2. **Drying in the chamber:** Droplets meet hot air (150–220 °C) entering co-currently. Moisture evaporates in 0.5–5 seconds, and the particle cools by evaporation — so the *product temperature* stays well below the air temperature.
3. **Separation:** Dried particles fall to the chamber base or are entrained to a cyclone, where they are collected. The fines pass to a bag filter for recovery.

**Key Parameters Controlling Quality (3 marks):**
1. **Inlet/outlet air temperature** — controls final moisture and thermal damage.
2. **Atomization pressure / disc speed** — determines droplet size and hence particle size.
3. **Feed viscosity and solids concentration** — affects atomization and stickiness.
4. **Air flow rate and humidity** — sets evaporation rate.
5. **Chamber geometry** — residence time and trajectory.
6. **Outlet temperature** — the single best process control variable (typically 80–100 °C for dairy, lower for heat-sensitive juices).

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Writing "spray drying is for solid particles" is wrong — the **feed must be a liquid** (solution, suspension, emulsion, or slurry). The dry **product** is a powder, not the feed.

---

### Question B — Fluidized Bed, Pneumatic, Foam Mat, Osmo, Freeze Drying [14 marks] `[KTU University Exam — June 2023]`

**(a) Describe the working principle of a fluidized bed dryer. What are the conditions for proper fluidization? Mention two advantages and two limitations.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Working Principle (3 marks):**
A fluidized bed dryer consists of a vertical chamber fitted with a **perforated distributor plate**. Hot air is forced upward through the plate and through a bed of granular particles (peas, diced carrot, corn). When the upward air velocity exceeds the **minimum fluidization velocity** $u_{mf}$, the drag force on each particle balances its weight, and the bed behaves like a boiling liquid — each particle is suspended in the air stream. This gives intimate gas–solid contact and extremely high heat- and mass-transfer coefficients (10–100 × higher than tray drying).

**Conditions for Proper Fluidization (2 marks):**
1. Air velocity must lie in the range $u_{mf} \le u \le u_c$ (terminal velocity). Below $u_{mf}$ the bed is packed; above $u_c$ particles are blown out (entrainment).
2. Particle size should be **uniform and roughly spherical** — fines cause channeling, oversized particles will not fluidize.
3. Air must be evenly distributed by the plate (pressure drop ≥ bed pressure drop).
4. Moisture should be below ~25 % to avoid bed collapse (stickiness).

**Advantages (1 mark):**
- Very rapid drying (15–45 min for granular foods).
- Uniform heat treatment, high thermal efficiency.

**Limitations (1 mark):**
- Not suitable for pastes or large irregular pieces.
- High blower power consumption; possible attrition of fragile products.

---

**(b) Compare osmo-dehydration and freeze drying with respect to the driving force, equipment, product quality, and energy requirement.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

| Aspect | Osmo-Dehydration | Freeze Drying |
|---|---|---|
| **Driving Force** | Water activity gradient across the semi-permeable cell membrane (concentration gradient of solute) | Vapor pressure difference across the dried layer; sublimation at the ice–vapor interface |
| **Temperature** | Ambient to ~50 °C | −30 to +30 °C (product stays frozen) |
| **Pressure** | Atmospheric | Vacuum, < 100 Pa |
| **Equipment** | Immersion tank with stirrer, sucrose/salt solution, optional secondary dryer | Vacuum chamber, heated shelves, condenser, vacuum pump |
| **Water Removed** | 30–60 % of initial water | 90–95 % of initial water |
| **Energy** | Very low (mainly pumping) | Very high (vacuum + refrigeration + heat) |
| **Product Quality** | Good color/texture; slight sugar uptake | Highest — color, aroma, shape retained |
| **Shrinkage** | Moderate | Almost none |
| **Rehydration** | Good | Excellent |
| **Capital Cost** | Low | Very high |
| **Typical Products** | Pineapple, cherry, mushroom | Strawberry, coffee, herbs |

**Step-by-Step Reasoning (2 marks):**
- Osmo-dehydration is driven by the difference in water activity ($a_w$) between the fruit and the osmotic solution. Water diffuses out, solute diffuses in.
- Freeze drying is driven by the temperature difference between the heated shelf ($T_s$) and the cold condenser ($T_e$), which establishes a vapor pressure gradient through the porous dried layer. Heat conducts inward, vapor flows outward.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Saying "osmo-dehydration uses heat" — **incorrect**. It is a non-thermal process; heat may be applied only to the solution to maintain temperature, but the driving force is **concentration / water activity**, not temperature.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — KTU Module 2 Dryers**

### Core Definitions
- **Drying:** Coupled heat-and-mass-transfer operation to reduce $M$ to safe-storage levels.
- **Wet-basis moisture:** water ÷ total mass.
- **Dry-basis moisture:** water ÷ dry matter (preferred for analysis).
- **Moisture ratio (MR):** $(M_t - M_e) / (M_0 - M_e)$.
- **Drying rate:** $N = -dM_{db}/dt$.
- **Triple point of water:** $T_{tp} = 0.01 °C$, $P_{tp} = 611.657$ Pa.

### Dryer Selection Rules
- **Liquid feed →** Spray / Drum / Foam Mat.
- **Granular solid →** Fluidized Bed / Pneumatic.
- **Solid slices/cubes, small scale →** Tray / Cabinet.
- **Heat-sensitive, premium →** Freeze drying.
- **Mild water removal without heat →** Osmo-dehydration.

### Key Numerical Skills
- Interconvert wb ↔ db moisture (3 marks guaranteed question).
- Calculate water removed from initial and final $M$ (wb/db).
- Apply Page's model: $MR = \exp(-k\,t^n)$.
- Apply constant-rate equation: $N_c = h_g\,(T_a - T_{wb}) / (\lambda\, m_{dm})$.
- Estimate freeze-drying time using the slab equation.

### High-Yield Phrases to Use in Answers
- "Falling-rate period controls drying of fruits and vegetables."
- "Spray drying's rate-controlling step is **atomization**."
- "Freeze drying operates **below the triple point** of water."
- "Osmo-dehydration is a **non-thermal, water-activity-driven** mass-transfer process."
- "Fluidization requires $u_{mf} \le u \le u_c$."

### Common KTU Examiner Triggers
- A question that asks "which period?" — answer **falling-rate period**.
- A question that asks "why is freeze drying costly?" — answer **vacuum + refrigeration energy**.
- A question on osmo-dehydration — discuss both **water loss (WL)** and **solid gain (SG)**.
- A spray dryer question — always draw the **atomizer, chamber, cyclone, bag filter, exhaust**.
- A drum dryer question — emphasize the **doctor blade** and **one-revolution drying**.

### Energy & Quality One-Liners
- Specific energy consumption for tray drying ≈ **4,000–6,000 kJ/kg water**.
- Freeze drying ≈ **25,000–30,000 kJ/kg water**.
- Spray drying ≈ **4,500–6,000 kJ/kg water**.
- Osmo-dehydration ≈ **1,000–2,000 kJ/kg water removed** (excluding solution prep).

### Must-Remember Numbers
- $\lambda_{vap} \approx 2.26 \times 10^6$ J/kg at 100 °C.
- $\lambda_{sub} \approx 2.83 \times 10^6$ J/kg at 0 °C.
- Triple point: 0.01 °C, 611.657 Pa.
- Safe storage $M_{wb}$: **3–8 %** for fruits & vegetables.
- Water activity threshold: **$a_w < 0.60$** for mold inhibition.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Irradiation, Surface Coatings, Chemical Dips, Preservatives
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEAGT742 - POST HARVEST ENGINEERING OF HORTICULTURAL CROPS > - **Module:** Module Module 2 > - **Topic:** Irradiation, Surface Coatings, Chemical Dips, Preservatives # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Irradiation ### Formal KTU-Syllabus Definition **Food irradiation** is a physical non-thermal preservation technique in which ionizing radiation (gamma rays from Cobalt-60 or Cesium-137, accelerated electrons up to **10 MeV**, or X-rays up to **5 MeV**) is allowed to pass through food materials. The radiation disrupts the DNA of microorganisms, insects, and parasites, inactivates spoilage enzymes, and delays ripening — without significantly raising the temperature of the product. The absorbed dose is measured in **Gray (Gy)** where **1 Gy = 1 J/kg**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **International Codex Alimentarius Classification of Irradiation Doses** > - **Low dose (< 1 kGy):** Sprout inhibition, insect disinfestation, parasite inactivation. > - **Medium dose (1 – 10 kGy):** Pathogen reduction, shelf-life extension. > - **High dose (> 10 kGy):** Sterilization (only for specific institutional/medical foods). ### Conceptual Analogy > [!NOTE] > Imagine a closed apple orchard. Instead of spraying pesticides (chemical preservation), you simply wheel in a sealed chamber and pass invisible "light bullets" (photons from Co-60) through the apples for a few minutes. The bullets punch microscopic holes in the DNA of bacteria and insects living on the fruit, but the apple flesh — being mostly dead plant tissue — is barely disturbed. The apples look and taste the same but last weeks longer because no living germs can multiply inside them. That is the essence of **food irradiation**. --- ## 1.2 Surface Coatings ### Formal Definition **Surface coatings** are thin, continuous layers of edible or non-edible materials applied to the external surface of horticultural produce to provide a semi-permeable barrier against gas exchange, water loss, and microbial contamination. They substitute the natural cuticular wax layer which is often removed during washing and handling. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Two broad categories recognized by KTU syllabus:** > - **Wax coatings (non-edible/shellac/paraffin):** Carnauba, beeswax, paraffin wax, shellac. > - **Edible coatings (biodegradable):** Chitosan, alginate, carboxymethyl cellulose (CMC), starch, gelatin, pullulan. ### Conceptual Analogy > [!NOTE] > Think of a surface coating as the **cling-wrap** of the fruit world. Once you wrap a cucumber in a tight, food-grade film, the water inside has a much harder time escaping into the air. The fruit still "breathes" a little (slow O₂ in, slow CO₂ out), so it does not suffocate, but it loses far less weight, looks glossier, and stays firm. Coatings essentially slow the clock of ripening. --- ## 1.3 Chemical Dips ### Formal Definition A **chemical dip** is a short-duration (seconds to a few minutes) immersion of fresh horticultural produce in an aqueous solution containing one or more permitted chemicals, used to sanitize the surface, firm the tissue, delay senescence, or control post-harvest diseases. Examples include **calcium chloride (CaCl₂)** for firmness, **sodium hypochlorite (NaOCl)** for surface sanitization, and **2,6-dichloroisonicotinic acid (INA)** as a systemic acquired resistance (SAR) inducer. ### Conceptual Analogy > [!NOTE] > A chemical dip is like a **quick shower with medicine**. You do not bathe the mango for an hour — you simply dunk it in a medicated water tank for 30–60 seconds. The skin absorbs just enough of the active ingredient to kill surface germs and seal microscopic injuries. --- ## 1.4 Preservatives ### Formal Definition **Chemical preservatives** are substances added to fresh or minimally processed horticultural products in regulated quantities to inhibit microbial growth, retard oxidation, or prevent enzymatic and non-enzymatic browning. Under Indian regulation (FSSAI, 2011) they must fall under the **GRAS (Generally Recognized As Safe)** category. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Common chemical preservatives used in horticultural products:** > - **Antimicrobial:** Potassium metabisulphite (KMS), sodium benzoate, sorbic acid, propionic acid. > - **Antioxidant:** Ascorbic acid (Vitamin C), citric acid, EDTA. > - **Acidulants:** Malic acid, tartaric acid, acetic acid. > - **Browning inhibitors:** Sodium erythorbate, 4-hexyl resorcinol. ### Conceptual Analogy > [!NOTE] > If the chemical dip is a "shower," a preservative is more like a **vaccine given to the fruit pulp**. After peeling, cutting, or juicing, the inner tissue is exposed to air and germs. A measured dose of a GRAS preservative (say, 0.1 % KMS) added to the juice keeps yeast and mould from fermenting it for weeks. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Mechanisms of Food Irradiation ### 2.1.1 Direct vs. Indirect Effects - **Direct effect:** Ionizing radiation physically breaks DNA strands (single-strand breaks, double-strand breaks, base damage) of microbes, insects, and parasites — making them incapable of reproduction. - **Indirect effect:** Radiation of water molecules generates reactive species: $$\text{H}_2\text{O} \xrightarrow{\text{ionization}} \text{H}_2\text{O}^{+} + e^{-}$$ $$\text{H}_2\text{O}^{+} \rightarrow \text{H}^{+} + \text{OH}^{\bullet}$$ $$\text{H}_2\text{O} + e^{-} \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O}^{-} \rightarrow \text{H}^{\bullet} + \text{OH}^{-}$$ These free radicals (mainly $\text{OH}^{\bullet}$ and $\text{H}^{\bullet}$) oxidize membrane lipids, inactivate enzymes, and damage microbial nucleic acids. ### 2.1.2 Five Operational Effects of Irradiation 1. **Radurization** — reduction of spoilage microbes (pasteurization equivalent). 2. **Radicidation** — elimination of specific pathogens (e.g., *Salmonella*). 3. **Radappertization** — sterilization equivalent for shelf-stable products. 4. **Disinfestation** — kill of insect pests in stored grains/fruits. 5. **Phytosanitation** — quarantine treatment to meet export phytosanitary norms. ### 2.1.3 D10 Value — Cornerstone Concept The **D₁₀ value** (decimal reduction dose) is the radiation dose required to reduce the microbial population by 90 % (i.e., one log cycle). $$D_{10} = \frac{\text{Applied Dose}}{\log_{10}(N_0) - \log_{10}(N)}$$ A higher D₁₀ means the organism is more radiation-resistant. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Typical D₁₀ values for KTU-level questions:** > - *E. coli*: **0.30 kGy** > - *Salmonella spp.*: **0.40 – 0.80 kGy** > - *Listeria monocytogenes*: **0.45 – 1.0 kGy** > - *Aspergillus niger* spores: **1.5 – 3.0 kGy** > - *Deinococcus radiodurans* (radiation-resistant): **> 5 kGy** --- ## 2.2 Surface Coatings — Working Principle Surface coatings work on the principle of **controlled modified atmosphere (CMA)**. The coating forms a barrier that: 1. **Reduces transpiration** → lower weight loss (up to 50 % reduction). 2. **Modifies internal atmosphere** — increases internal CO₂, decreases O₂ → delays climacteric ripening. 3. **Provides gloss** → improves consumer appeal. 4. **Carries functional additives** — anti-microbials, anti-oxidants, growth regulators. ### 2.2.1 Wax Coating Composition (Typical) | Component | Function | Common Examples | |-----------|----------|-----------------| | Base wax | Moisture barrier | Carnauba, paraffin, beeswax | | Resin | Gloss, adhesion | Shellac, wood rosin | | Plasticizer | Film flexibility | Glycerol, sorbitol | | Emulsifier | Dispersion in water | Triethanolamine oleate | | Fungicide | Decay control | Imazalil, thiabendazole (TBZ) | | Solvent | Carrier | Water or volatile oils | ### 2.2.2 Edible Coating Polymers | Polymer Class | Examples | Key Property | |---------------|----------|--------------| | Polysaccharide | Chitosan, starch, CMC, alginate | Good O₂ barrier, poor moisture barrier | | Protein | Gelatin, whey, soy, corn zein | Excellent O₂ barrier | | Lipid | Beeswax, carnauba, fatty acids | Excellent moisture barrier | | Composite | Alginate + beeswax | Balanced properties | --- ## 2.3 Chemical Dips — Working Principle Chemical dips act on the **peel/cuticle** and the **outer 2 – 3 cell layers** (sub-epidermal). Penetration depth is limited because: - The cuticle is hydrophobic. - Treatment time is short (typically **30 s – 5 min**). ### 2.3.1 Important Dip Chemicals and Their Functions > [!IMPORTANT] > **Most frequently asked dips in KTU exams:** > - **CaCl₂ (1 – 4 %):** Cross-links pectin → tissue firmness (used in apples, melons, tomatoes). > - **NaOCl (50 – 200 ppm free chlorine):** Surface disinfection (universal). > - **H₂O₂ (1 – 3 %):** Sanitizer for minimally processed produce. > - **Ethanol (10 – 35 %):** Surface pathogen control. > - **KMS / SO₂ (0.05 – 0.2 %):** Browning control in grapes, litchi, cut fruits. > - **Ascorbic acid (1 %):** Anti-browning. --- ## 2.4 Chemical Preservatives — Working Principle ### 2.4.1 Classification Based on Mode of Action | Category | Mechanism | Examples | |----------|-----------|----------| | Acid preservatives | Lower pH → inhibit microbial enzymes | Benzoic acid, sorbic acid, citric acid | | Sulphiting agents | React with carbonyls → inhibit enzyme | KMS, SO₂ gas | | Antioxidants | Scavenge free radicals | BHA, BHT, ascorbic acid | | Chelators | Bind metals → block Fenton reaction | EDTA, citric acid | | Antimicrobial salts | Disrupt membrane | Sodium benzoate, sodium propionate | ### 2.4.2 Permitted Limits (FSSAI / Codex) for KTU Reference | Preservative | Product | Max Permitted Level (g/kg or ppm) | |--------------|---------|----------------------------------| | Sulphur dioxide (KMS) | Dried fruits | **2000 ppm** | | Sulphur dioxide (KMS) | Fresh grapes | **10 ppm** | | Sodium benzoate | Sauces, juices | **1000 ppm** | | Sorbic acid | Cheese, baked goods | **1000 ppm** | | Ascorbic acid | Any fruit product | GMP (no limit) | | Citric acid | Any | GMP | --- ## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Units | Application | |---|------------------|------------|-------|-------------| | 1 | Absorbed dose | $D = E/m$ | Gy (J/kg) | Irradiation strength | | 2 | Decimal reduction dose | $D_{10} = D / [\log_{10}(N_0) - \log_{10}(N)]$ | Gy or kGy | Microbial kill calculation | | 3 | Log reduction | $\text{Log Red} = \log_{10}(N_0/N)$ | dimensionless | Sterilization efficiency | | 4 | Number of D₁₀ cycles | $n = D_{\text{applied}} / D_{10}$ | dimensionless | Lethal dose design | | 5 | First-order microbial inactivation | $N = N_0 \cdot 10^{(-D/D_{10})}$ | CFU/g | Survivor count | | 6 | Mass balance (water loss) | $\%\text{WL} = (W_0 - W_t)/W_0 \times 100$ | % | Coating effectiveness | | 7 | Weight loss rate (RH effect) | $W = k(\text{RH}_\text{int} - \text{RH}_\text{ext})$ | g/day | Waxing benefit | | 8 | Henry's law (gas permeation) | $C = k_H \cdot P$ | mol/m³ | Internal atmosphere of coatings | | 9 | Permeability equation | $J = (P/l)\cdot \Delta p$ | mol/m²·s | O₂/CO₂ transfer | | 10 | Fick's first law | $J = -D \, dC/dx$ | mol/m²·s | Diffusional flux | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why this matters in engineering practice:** Every commercial pack-house in India (Mahindra, INI Farms, Desai Fruits) uses at least two of these four techniques (waxing + KMS dip for grapes; irradiation + waxing for mango exports). The formulas above let an engineer size the irradiator, decide the conveyor speed, compute the % weight loss saved, and validate that the dose stays below the **10 kGy** global ceiling. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: First-Order Microbial Inactivation Kinetics ### 3.1.1 Starting Postulate The surviving microbial population $N$ after an absorbed dose $D$ follows first-order kinetics with respect to dose. $$\frac{dN}{dD} = -k \cdot N$$ ### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Integration **Step 1** — Separate variables. $$\frac{dN}{N} = -k \, dD$$ **Step 2** — Integrate both sides between the limits $N_0$ at $D = 0$ and $N$ at $D = D$. $$\int_{N_0}^{N} \frac{dN}{N} = -k \int_{0}^{D} dD$$ **Step 3** — Evaluate the integrals. $$\ln(N) - \ln(N_0) = -kD$$ $$\ln\left(\frac{N}{N_0}\right) = -kD$$ **Step 4** — Exponentiate to convert to base-10 logarithm (because D₁₀ uses log₁₀). $$\frac{N}{N_0} = e^{-kD}$$ Taking log₁₀ of both sides: $$\log_{10}\left(\frac{N}{N_0}\right) = -kD / 2.303$$ **Step 5** — Recognize that by definition, when $D = D_{10}$, $N/N_0 = 0.1$ (90 % kill). $$\log_{10}(0.1) = -kD_{10}/2.303 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; -1 = -kD_{10}/2.303$$ $$\Rightarrow \;\; k = 2.303 / D_{10}$$ **Step 6** — Substitute back into the survivor equation. $$\boxed{\;N = N_0 \cdot 10^{\left(-D / D_{10}\right)}\;}$$ This is the **KTU master formula** for radiation microbial inactivation. --- ### 3.1.3 Worked Numerical Example **Problem:** A batch of mango pulp has an initial yeast count $N_0 = 10^5 \text{ CFU/g}$. The D₁₀ of *Saccharomyces cerevisiae* in this pulp is **0.50 kGy**. The pulp is irradiated at **2.0 kGy**. Calculate the final count and the log reduction. **Step 1** — Write down knowns. $$N_0 = 10^5 \text{ CFU/g}, \quad D_{10} = 0.50 \text{ kGy}, \quad D = 2.0 \text{ kGy}$$ **Step 2** — Compute exponent. $$-D/D_{10} = -2.0 / 0.50 = -4$$ **Step 3** — Compute survivor fraction. $$\frac{N}{N_0} = 10^{-4} = 1 \times 10^{-4}$$ **Step 4** — Compute $N$. $$N = 10^5 \times 10^{-4} = 10^1 = 10 \text{ CFU/g}$$ **Step 5** — Log reduction. $$\text{Log Red} = \log_{10}(N_0/N) = \log_{10}(10^5 / 10) = \log_{10}(10^4) = 4$$ **Step 6** — Interpretation. > The irradiation produces a **4-log (99.99 %)** reduction, leaving only 10 CFU/g — well below the FSSAI safety threshold of 10² CFU/g for yeast in fruit pulps. --- ## 3.2 Derivation: Number of D₁₀ Cycles Required From the survivor equation, taking logarithm: $$\log_{10}\left(\frac{N_0}{N}\right) = \frac{D}{D_{10}}$$ $$\Rightarrow \;\; n = \frac{D}{D_{10}} = \log_{10}\left(\frac{N_0}{N}\right)$$ This says: **the number of D₁₀ doses needed equals the log-reduction desired**. **Quick reference (highly asked in KTU):** | Log Reduction | % Kill | Required Dose $= n \cdot D_{10}$ | |--------------|--------|----------------------------------| | 1-log | 90 % | 1 × D₁₀ | | 2-log | 99 % | 2 × D₁₀ | | 5-log | 99.999 % | 5 × D₁₀ | | 12-log | "Botulinum cook" | 12 × D₁₀ (sterility equivalent) | --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Evaluation of All Four Techniques | Parameter | Irradiation | Surface Coating | Chemical Dip | Chemical Preservative | |-----------|-------------|-----------------|--------------|----------------------| | **Mode of action** | Ionizes DNA | Physical barrier | Surface contact | Biochemical inhibition | | **Target** | Microbes, insects, enzymes | Whole fruit peel | Outer tissue only | Whole product | | **Process type** | Non-thermal | Physical | Physical + chemical | Chemical | | **Regulatory cap** | 10 kGy (Codex) | GRAS + 100 mg/kg additives | FSSAI Schedule IV | FSSAI Schedule II/IV | | **Residue** | None | None | Trace wash water | Yes (must be below MRL) | | **Shelf-life gain** | 2× – 10× | 1.5× – 3× | 1.5× – 2× | 2× – 5× | | **Cost (INR/kg)** | 0.50 – 2.00 | 0.20 – 0.80 | 0.05 – 0.30 | 0.10 – 0.60 | | **Consumer acceptance** | Mixed (label fear) | High | High | High | | **Skill needed** | High (Cobalt plant) | Low – Medium | Low | Low | | **Combine with** | Cold storage | Cold storage | Cold storage | Cold storage + MAP | --- ## 3.4 Design Calculation — Surface Coating Weight Loss Reduction **Problem:** An apple weighing 200 g is stored at **27 °C, 60 % RH**. Without coating, it loses **5 %** weight in 14 days. After carnauba wax coating (1 % w/w), the transpiration rate drops by 50 %. Calculate the final weight and the % weight saved. **Step 1** — Original weight loss. $$W_{\text{lost, control}} = 200 \times 0.05 = 10 \text{ g}$$ **Step 2** — Coated weight loss (50 % reduction). $$W_{\text{lost, coated}} = 10 \times 0.50 = 5 \text{ g}$$ **Step 3** — Final weight (coated). $$W_{\text{final}} = 200 - 5 = 195 \text{ g}$$ **Step 4** — Percentage weight saved (relative to control). $$\%\text{saved} = (10 - 5)/10 \times 100 = 50\%$$ **Step 5** — Note that the wax itself adds 1 % of weight (~2 g), so the actual delivered mass to consumer is: $$W_{\text{delivered}} = 195 + 2 = 197 \text{ g}$$ The **net economic gain** for the trader is therefore 7 g per apple — significant at the scale of a 100-tonne cold store. # 4.
