KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks = 6 Marks) ### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Define D₁₀ value in food irradiation. A fruit pulp has an initial microbial load of 10⁶ CFU/g and is irradiated at 1.5 kGy. The D₁₀ of the contaminant is 0.50 kGy. Calculate the final microbial load. **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Definition (1 mark):** D₁₀ is the radiation dose required to kill **90 %** (i.e., reduce by one log cycle) of a microbial population. **Calculation (2 marks):** Apply the survivor equation: $$N = N_0 \cdot 10^{(-D/D_{10})}$$ Substitute: $$N = 10^6 \cdot 10^{(-1.5/0.50)} = 10^6 \cdot 10^{-3} = 10^3 \text{ CFU/g}$$ **Final microbial load = 10³ CFU/g (a 3-log / 99.9 % reduction).** --- ### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** Differentiate between wax coatings and edible coatings with two examples each. **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Feature | Wax Coating | Edible Coating | |---------|-------------|----------------| | Composition | Hydrophobic lipids (paraffin, carnauba) | Biopolymers (chitosan, starch) | | Examples | Carnauba wax, paraffin wax | Chitosan, alginate, CMC | | Edibility | Not necessarily eaten | Eaten with the fruit | | Primary role | Gloss + moisture barrier | Gas barrier + carrier of additives | | Removal | Sometimes peeled off | Eaten, so must be food-grade | --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (Internal Choice: A or B — 14 Marks Each) --- ### Question A (14 Marks) > **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 2]** **(a)** Explain the **principle, types, and applications of food irradiation** in horticultural produce. Discuss the permitted dose limits as per international (Codex Alimentarius) and Indian (FSSAI) regulations. **(7 marks)** **(b)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **components and working of a Gamma irradiation plant using Co-60 source**. List the dose-mapping procedure. **(7 marks)** --- ### Model Solution — Question A #### Part (a) — 7 Marks **Principle (2 marks):** Food irradiation uses **ionizing radiation** — high-energy photons (gamma), accelerated electrons, or X-rays — to disrupt the genetic material (DNA) of microorganisms, insects, and parasites. The dose is expressed in **Gray (Gy)**, where **1 Gy = 1 J/kg** of energy absorbed per kilogram of food. Two mechanisms operate: - **Direct** — radiation breaks DNA bonds. - **Indirect** — radiolysis of water generates free radicals ($\text{OH}^{\bullet}$, $\text{H}^{\bullet}$) that damage cell components. **Types of radiation sources (2 marks):** 1. **Gamma rays from Co-60** (1.17 and 1.33 MeV) — most common. 2. **Gamma rays from Cs-137** (0.66 MeV) — used in some blood irradiation units. 3. **Accelerated electrons** (≤ 10 MeV) — from linear accelerators. 4. **X-rays** (≤ 5 MeV) — generated by bombarding a metal target with electrons. **Applications (2 marks):** - **Sprout inhibition** in potatoes and onions (0.05 – 0.15 kGy). - **Insect disinfestation** in mango, citrus, pulses (0.25 – 1.0 kGy). - **Pathogen reduction** in minimally processed fruits (1 – 3 kGy). - **Shelf-life extension** of mushrooms, strawberries (2 – 5 kGy). - **Phytosanitary treatment** for export compliance (USDA-APHIS, EPPO). **Regulatory dose limits (1 mark):** - **Codex Alimentarius:** Max **10 kGy** for any food. - **FSSAI (Food Safety and Standards Regulations, 2011):** Permitted for specific items — potatoes, onions, spices, mango, rice, semolina. - **Mandatory labelling:** All irradiated foods must display the **Radura logo** (green flower-like symbol) and a written declaration. #### Part (b) — 7 Marks **Block diagram (refer SECTION 4.2 above) (3 marks).** **Components of a Co-60 Gamma Plant (2 marks):** 1. **Source rack** — pencils of Co-60 housed in a stainless-steel assembly. 2. **Source storage pool** — 6 m deep water pool (deionized) that shields the source when not in use. 3. **Biological shield** — 1.5 – 1.8 m thick high-density concrete walls. 4. **Irradiation chamber** — concrete cell where product moves on monorail conveyors. 5. **Product carriers (trolleys/totes)** — stainless steel, double-walled, height up to 1.2 m. 6. **Conveyor system** — pneumatic or hydraulic, with multiple passes to achieve target dose. 7. **Control room** — PLC-based, with safety interlocks. 8. **Dosimetry system** — Fricke, ceric-cerous, or alanine dosimeters. **Working (1 mark):** The source is raised from the pool into the chamber by a winch. Product totes enter through a maze (labyrinth) entry to prevent radiation leakage. They orbit the source in pre-set paths (single-pass, two-pass, or shuffle-dodge) until the required absorbed dose is achieved. After irradiation, the totes exit through a similar maze. **Dose-mapping procedure (1 mark):** - Install dosimeters (alanine pellets or PMMA) in a 3-D grid throughout representative product loads. - Run a routine cycle (full source strength, typical conveyor speed). - Measure dose at each grid point using EPR spectroscopy (alanine) or spectrophotometry (Fricke). - Plot **iso-dose contours** to identify the **minimum dose zone (Dmin)** and **maximum dose zone (Dmax)**. - Adjust conveyor speed and routing so that **Dmin ≥ target dose** and **Dmax ≤ 10 kGy**. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) > **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 2 — Alternate Choice]** **(a)** Classify **surface coatings** used in horticultural produce. Describe the formulation and functions of a **commercial wax coating emulsion**. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Explain in detail the various **chemical dips** used for firmness retention and surface disinfection of fresh fruits and vegetables. State the FSSAI-permitted concentration limits. **(7 marks)** --- ### Model Solution — Question B #### Part (a) — 7 Marks **Classification of surface coatings (3 marks):** **A. Wax coatings (Lipid-based, traditionally non-edible)** - Carnauba wax (from Brazilian palm) - Beeswax - Paraffin wax - Shellac (lac insect resin) - Candelilla wax **B. Edible coatings (Bio-polymeric)** - Polysaccharide-based: chitosan, alginate, starch, CMC, pectin - Protein-based: gelatin, whey protein, soy protein isolate, corn zein - Lipid-based edible: fatty acids, mono/di-glycerides - Composite coatings (multilayer): alginate + beeswax, chitosan + oleic acid **C. Synthetic polymer coatings (limited use)** - Polyvinyl acetate (PVA) - Methylcellulose (MC) and hydroxypropyl methylcellulose (HPMC) **Formulation of a typical commercial fruit-wax emulsion (3 marks):** A standard apples / citrus / mango wax emulsion contains the following: | Component | % by weight | Function | |-----------|-------------|----------| | Carnauba or paraffin wax | 8 – 12 % | Main moisture barrier | | Shellac / wood rosin | 4 – 6 % | Gloss and adhesion | | Oleic acid / morpholine oleate | 1 – 2 % | Emulsifier | | Glycerol / sorbitol | 1 – 2 % | Plasticizer | | Imazalil or TBZ (fungicide) | 0.05 – 0.1 % | Decay control | | Sodium hydroxide | 0.5 – 1 % | Saponification | | Water | Balance (≈ 75 – 85 %) | Continuous phase | **Functions of the wax coating (1 mark):** 1. Reduces **transpiration** → 30 – 60 % weight loss reduction. 2. Adds **gloss** → visual quality, market value. 3. Forms **modified internal atmosphere** → delays ripening. 4. Carries **fungicides and growth regulators** → integrated disease control. 5. Acts as a **carrier of calcium** (when fortified) → firmness retention. **Application methods (1 mark):** - Spray (most common) - Dip and drain - Foam application - Brush application (small-scale) #### Part (b) — 7 Marks **Definition and purpose (1 mark):** Chemical dips involve **brief immersion (30 s to 5 min)** of fresh produce in aqueous chemical solutions. They sanitize surfaces, firm tissues, and control decay without leaving harmful residues when used at permitted levels. **Important chemical dips and their applications (5 marks):** **1. Calcium chloride (CaCl₂) – Firmness Retention** - Concentration: **1 – 4 % w/v**. - Mechanism: Ca²⁺ ions cross-link with the **carboxyl groups of pectin** in the middle lamella → forms calcium pectate → strengthens cell wall → delays softening. - Used in: apples, tomatoes, melons, mango, papaya. - Often combined with **0.5 % ascorbic acid** to prevent oxidation. **2. Sodium hypochlorite (NaOCl) – Surface Disinfection** - Concentration: **50 – 200 ppm free chlorine** (pH 6.5 – 7.0). - Mechanism: HOCl / OCl⁻ oxidizes microbial membrane proteins and nucleic acids. - Used universally for fresh-cut salads, tomatoes, carrots. - Drawback: Forms chlorinated by-products → must be rinsed. **3. Hydrogen peroxide (H₂O₂) – Sanitization** - Concentration: **1 – 3 %**. - Mechanism: Produces reactive oxygen species → kills bacteria, viruses, fungi. - Leaves only water and oxygen as residues → eco-friendly. - Used for capsicum, mushrooms, sprouts. **4. Potassium metabisulphite (KMS) – Browning & Disease Control** - Concentration: **0.05 – 0.2 %** (releases SO₂). - Mechanism: Inhibits **polyphenol oxidase (PPO)** → prevents enzymatic browning; also inhibits spoilage microbes. - Used in: grapes, litchi, longan, cut apple, peeled pear. - FSSAI limit: **2 000 ppm in dried fruits**, **10 ppm in fresh grapes**. **5. Ethanol dip – Pathogen Control** - Concentration: **10 – 35 %** for 30 s – 2 min. - Effective against *E. coli O157:H7*, *Salmonella*, *Listeria*. - Used for organic / residue-free fresh produce. **6. Ascorbic acid (1 %) – Anti-browning** - Mechanism: Reduces quinones back to phenolics → blocks browning chain. - Used in: cut apple, banana, potato, pear. **7. 2,6-Dichloroisonicotinic acid (INA) – SAR Inducer** - Activates **systemic acquired resistance** in mango, citrus. - Used at 50 – 100 ppm before storage. **FSSAI-permitted concentration table (1 mark):** | Chemical | FSSAI / Codex Max | |----------|-------------------| | CaCl₂ (firmness agent) | GMP | | NaOCl (sanitizer, wash water) | **70 ppm** residual chlorine in final wash | | H₂O₂ | GMP, **residue ≤ 0.5 ppm** | | KMS (as SO₂) | 10 – 2 000 ppm depending on product | | Ascorbic acid | GMP (no limit) | | Ethanol | GMP for surface use | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:** > 1. Students often write **D value** when they mean **D₁₀** — both are different (D is time-based for thermal, D₁₀ is dose-based for radiation). > 2. Many confuse the **Codex 10 kGy** limit with the **FSSAI** rules — India follows the 10 kGy cap but also lists **specific items** that may be irradiated. > 3. In wax formulations, students forget to mention the **plasticizer** and **emulsifier** — both are mandatory. > 4. For CaCl₂ dips, students must specify the **concentration in %** and **immersion time** — generic "calcium dip" answers get only 50 % credit. > 5. Many spell KMS wrongly as **"potassium metabisulphite"** — correct spelling is **metabisulphite** (British) or **metabisulfite** (American). > 6. Do **not** recommend irradiation + KMS together for fresh grapes — the legal KMS limit is only **10 ppm**, and KMS with irradiation is redundant for surface microbes. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 2** ### ✅ Irradiation - Definition: ionizes DNA; measured in **Gray (Gy)**; 1 Gy = 1 J/kg. - Sources: **Co-60 gamma**, Cs-137 gamma, **electron beam ≤ 10 MeV**, X-rays ≤ 5 MeV. - Codex cap: **10 kGy**; FSSAI cap also 10 kGy for listed items. - Survivor equation: $N = N_0 \cdot 10^{(-D/D_{10})}$. - Typical D₁₀ of *Salmonella* ≈ 0.4 – 0.8 kGy; *E. coli* ≈ 0.3 kGy; mould spores 1.5 – 3 kGy. - Five effects: **radurization, radicidation, radappertization, disinfestation, phytosanitation**. - Label mandatory with **Radura logo**. ### ✅ Surface Coatings - Two types: **wax coatings** (carnauba, paraffin, shellac, beeswax) and **edible coatings** (chitosan, alginate, starch, CMC, gelatin). - Typical emulsion: 8 – 12 % wax + 4 – 6 % resin + 1 – 2 % plasticizer + 0.05 – 0.1 % fungicide + balance water. - Functions: gloss, transpiration barrier, modified atmosphere, fungicide carrier, calcium fortification. - Application: spray > dip > foam > brush. ### ✅ Chemical Dips - CaCl₂ (1 – 4 %): firmness via calcium pectate. - NaOCl (50 – 200 ppm): surface disinfection. - H₂O₂ (1 – 3 %): residue-free sanitization. - KMS (0.05 – 0.2 % as SO₂): browning control. - Ascorbic acid (1 %): anti-browning. - INA (50 – 100 ppm): SAR inducer. ### ✅ Chemical Preservatives - Antimicrobial: KMS, sodium benzoate, sorbic acid, propionic acid. - Antioxidant: ascorbic acid, citric acid, EDTA, BHA, BHT. - Browning inhibitors: KMS, sodium erythorbate, 4-hexyl resorcinol. - FSSAI limits: **KMS = 10 ppm (fresh grapes) to 2 000 ppm (dried fruits)**; sodium benzoate = 1 000 ppm in juices. - All must be **GRAS** and below MRL. ### ✅ Comparative Highlights - Irradiation = **no residue**, but consumer-label fear. - Wax = **best weight loss control** but no pathogen kill. - Dip = **fastest** unit operation but only surface action. - Preservative = **most economical** for processed products but residue concerns. - **Combined treatments** (irradiation + waxing + cold chain) give the best commercial shelf life.

topics.title: Packaging of Horticultural Commodities
Packaging Requirements (in Terms of Light Transmittance, Heat, Moisture and Gas Proof, Microorganisms)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAGT742 - POST HARVEST ENGINEERING OF HORTICULTURAL CROPS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Packaging Requirements (in Terms of Light Transmittance, Heat, Moisture and Gas Proof, Microorganisms)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Packaging Requirements** for horticultural produce refer to the set of **physical, chemical, and biological barrier properties** that a packaging material must possess to preserve the quality, extend the shelf-life, and prevent physico-chemical and microbiological deterioration of fresh and processed fruits, vegetables, and flowers during storage, transport, and marketing.

In the context of KTU Module 3, packaging requirements are evaluated across **five critical functional dimensions**:

1. **Light Transmittance Control** (Optical Properties)
2. **Heat / Thermal Resistance** (Thermal Properties)
3. **Moisture Proofing** (Water Vapour Barrier)
4. **Gas Proofing** (Oxygen, CO₂, Ethylene Barrier)
5. **Microbial Protection** (Antimicrobial & Sanitary Barrier)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Definition:** A horticultural package is *not* merely a container — it is an **engineered micro-environment** that regulates the mass transfer of gases, water vapour, heat, and light between the produce and its ambient surroundings.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Human Skin Analogy"**
>
> Think of horticultural packaging as the **"artificial skin"** of a fruit or vegetable, much like human skin protects the body:
>
> | Human Skin Function | Packaging Equivalent |
> |---------------------|----------------------|
> | Blocks UV from sun | **Light transmittance control** |
> | Regulates body temperature | **Heat / thermal insulation** |
> | Prevents dehydration | **Moisture vapour barrier** |
> | Controls oxygen–CO₂ exchange in lungs | **Gas permeability control** |
> | Immune system stops pathogens | **Antimicrobial barrier** |
>
> Just as a burn victim needs specialized artificial skin with controlled permeability, fresh tomatoes or cut roses need engineered polymer films (e.g., **LDPE, BOPP, PET, PVC**) that mimic selective barrier properties.

---

## 1.3 Why Packaging Requirements Matter (KTU-Mandated Rationale)

- **Respiration Rate** of fresh produce consumes O₂ and releases **CO₂ + heat + ethylene (C₂H₄)**. Without gas-proofing, accelerated senescence occurs.
- **Transpiration** causes water loss; even **5–10% weight loss** makes leafy vegetables wilt and unmarketable.
- **Light exposure** degrades chlorophyll, vitamin C, and β-carotene, and triggers **photo-bleaching**.
- **Pathogen ingress** (bacteria, fungi, yeast) accelerates spoilage, especially in MAP (Modified Atmosphere Packaging) systems.
- **Thermal abuse** during the cold chain break causes condensation, accelerating microbial growth.

> [!TIP]
> **Key KTU Highlight:** The **Water Vapour Transmission Rate (WVTR)** and **Oxygen Transmission Rate (OTR)** are the two **most frequently tested numerical parameters** in KTU exams for this topic.

---

## 1.4 Standard Reference Metrics (Engineering Constants)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following are **KTU-board standard units** you must memorize:
>
> - **WVTR** (Water Vapour Transmission Rate) → measured in **g·m⁻²·day⁻¹** (at 38 °C, 90% RH)
> - **OTR** (Oxygen Transmission Rate) → measured in **cm³·m⁻²·day⁻¹** (at 23 °C, 0% RH)
> - **CO₂TR** (CO₂ Transmission Rate) → measured in **cm³·m⁻²·day⁻¹**
> - **Light Transmittance** → measured as **% transmittance** in wavelength range **200–800 nm**
> - **Thermal Conductivity (k)** of common packaging films → **0.2–0.5 W·m⁻¹·K⁻¹**
> - **Heat Seal Strength** → **N/15 mm** (Newtons per 15 mm width)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Functional Barriers of Horticultural Packaging

### 2.1.1 Light Transmittance Control (Optical Barrier)

**Definition:** The ability of the packaging film to **block, filter, or transmit** specific wavelengths of visible (400–700 nm) and UV (200–400 nm) light to prevent photo-degradation of pigments, vitamins, and lipids.

**Operational Mechanism:**

- Horticultural produce contains **chlorophyll, anthocyanins, carotenoids, and ascorbic acid** that are photo-sensitive.
- UV-B (280–315 nm) and visible blue light (400–500 nm) catalyze **oxidative rancidity** in cut fruits and **chlorophyll degradation** in leafy vegetables.
- **Light-blocking films** (aluminized pouches, black LDPE, multilayer EVOH) reduce transmittance to **<5%** in the 200–700 nm range.
- **Selective Light Transmitting Films** (used in tomato ripening) transmit **red light (600–700 nm)** to enhance lycopene synthesis.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Rule of Thumb:** For most fresh fruits and vegetables, packaging should transmit **<10% UV-B** and **<30% total visible light** to preserve quality.

**Material Examples:**
- **Aluminium foil laminates** → 0% light transmittance
- **Black LDPE / MDPE** → <5% light transmittance
- **Clear PET** → ~88% transmittance (used only for display purposes)
- **Yellow-tinted films** → selectively block UV while allowing 70%+ visible light

---

### 2.1.2 Heat / Thermal Resistance

**Definition:** The ability of the packaging material to **resist heat flow, withstand temperature abuse, and maintain structural integrity** across the cold chain (typically −2 °C to +30 °C).

**Operational Mechanism:**

- During **pre-cooling and cold storage**, the package must not become brittle, crack, or delaminate.
- During **hot-fill pasteurization (85–95 °C)** or **retort sterilization (121 °C)**, the film must retain seal integrity.
- The package acts as a **thermal buffer**, reducing the rate of heat transfer between the produce and ambient air.

**Governing Equation — Fourier's Law of Heat Conduction:**

$$q = -k \cdot A \cdot \frac{dT}{dx}$$

Where:
- $q$ = rate of heat transfer (W)
- $k$ = thermal conductivity of packaging film (W·m⁻¹·K⁻¹)
- $A$ = cross-sectional area (m²)
- $\frac{dT}{dx}$ = temperature gradient (K·m⁻¹)

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Tip:** A lower $k$ value indicates better **insulation**. Expanded polystyrene (EPS) boxes have $k \approx 0.033$ W·m⁻¹·K⁻¹, making them ideal for thermal-sensitive produce like strawberries and cut flowers.

**Material Examples:**
- **Corrugated Fibreboard (CFB)** → $k \approx 0.065$ W·m⁻¹·K⁻¹
- **EPS (Thermocol)** → $k \approx 0.033$ W·m⁻¹·K⁻¹
- **Polypropylene (PP)** → $k \approx 0.20$ W·m⁻¹·K⁻¹
- **PET** → $k \approx 0.29$ W·m⁻¹·K⁻¹

---

### 2.1.3 Moisture Proofing (Water Vapour Barrier)

**Definition:** The ability of the package to **restrict the transmission of water vapour** from the produce (high RH internal environment) to the external atmosphere (lower RH), thereby preventing **transpiration losses, weight loss, and shrivelling**.

**Operational Mechanism:**

- Fresh produce transpires water at a rate of **0.01–0.5% of fresh weight per hour** (highly variable).
- The package must maintain **internal RH > 90%** to prevent wilting, while preventing condensation.
- The **Water Vapour Transmission Rate (WVTR)** is the governing parameter.

**Governing Equation — Fick's First Law Applied to Water Vapour:**

$$WVTR = \frac{P_{H_2O} \cdot \Delta P_{H_2O}}{L}$$

Where:
- $P_{H_2O}$ = water vapour permeability coefficient of film (g·m·m⁻²·day⁻¹·Pa⁻¹)
- $\Delta P_{H_2O}$ = partial pressure difference of water vapour across film (Pa)
- $L$ = film thickness (m)

**Standard Test Condition:** 38 °C, 90% RH (ASTM E96 / ISO 2528)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Critical Threshold:** Weight loss **>5–10%** in most fruits and vegetables causes **visible shrivelling and market rejection**. Packaging must keep weight loss <5% over the target shelf-life.

**Material Comparison (WVTR values, g·m⁻²·day⁻¹):**

| Material | WVTR | Moisture Barrier Rating |
|----------|------|--------------------------|
| **Aluminium foil (25 µm)** | ~0 | Excellent |
| **LDPE (25 µm)** | 12–20 | Moderate |
| **HDPE (25 µm)** | 5–10 | Good |
| **PP (25 µm)** | 8–12 | Moderate |
| **PVC (25 µm)** | 30–50 | Poor |
| **PET (12 µm)** | 15–20 | Moderate |
| **PVDC-coated films** | 1–3 | Excellent |

---

### 2.1.4 Gas Proofing (Oxygen, CO₂, Ethylene Barrier)

**Definition:** The ability of the package to **selectively control the permeation of respiratory gases** (O₂, CO₂) and **ripening hormone ethylene (C₂H₄)** to create a **Modified Atmosphere (MA)** or **Controlled Atmosphere (CA)** inside the package.

**Operational Mechanism:**

- Respiration equation: $C_6H_{12}O_6 + 6O_2 \rightarrow 6CO_2 + 6H_2O + Energy$ (2820 kJ·mol⁻¹)
- Ethylene ($C_2H_4$) is the **natural plant ripening hormone**; even **0.1–1 ppm** accelerates senescence.
- The package should ideally:
  - Allow **O₂ ingress** at 2–5% internal concentration
  - Allow **CO₂ egress** to maintain 3–8% internal concentration
  - **Block ethylene** to prevent premature ripening

**Governing Equation — Steady-State Permeation:**

$$OTR = \frac{P_{O_2} \cdot \Delta p_{O_2}}{L}$$

Where:
- $P_{O_2}$ = oxygen permeability coefficient (cm³·cm·cm⁻²·s⁻¹·Pa⁻¹)
- $\Delta p_{O_2}$ = oxygen partial pressure difference (Pa)
- $L$ = film thickness (cm)

**Ideal Gas Ratio for Modified Atmosphere Packaging (MAP):**

$$\text{Respiration Quotient } RQ = \frac{CO_2 \text{ produced}}{O_2 \text{ consumed}} = \frac{TR_{CO_2}}{TR_{O_2}}$$

Where $TR$ = Transmission Rate.

For equilibrium MAP: $RQ = \frac{CO_2TR}{O_2TR}$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Concept:** When the **package permeability ratio = respiration ratio**, the system reaches a **steady-state modified atmosphere (SSMA)** — this is the engineering design goal of MAP.

**Material Examples (OTR in cm³·m⁻²·day⁻¹ at 23 °C, 0% RH):**

| Material | OTR | Gas Barrier Rating |
|----------|-----|--------------------|
| **EVOH (ethylene vinyl alcohol)** | 0.5–3 | Excellent (used as inner layer) |
| **PVDC (polyvinylidene chloride)** | 1–5 | Excellent |
| **PET (oriented)** | 50–100 | Good |
| **LDPE** | 5000–8000 | Poor |
| **PP** | 2000–3000 | Moderate |
| **Aluminium foil** | ~0 | Impermeable |

---

### 2.1.5 Microbial Protection (Antimicrobial Barrier)

**Definition:** The ability of the packaging to **prevent ingress of microorganisms** (bacteria, moulds, yeasts, viruses) and **inhibit microbial growth** on the produce surface.

**Operational Mechanism:**

- The package forms a **physical barrier** against airborne and contact-borne microbes.
- Modern **active packaging** incorporates **antimicrobial agents** (e.g., silver nanoparticles, chitosan, essential oils, sorbic acid) that migrate to the produce surface.
- **MAP with high CO₂ (10–20%)** also suppresses aerobic microbial growth (especially *Pseudomonas*, *Botrytis*).
- The packaging surface must be **sanitizable** (e.g., washable crates, sterilizable pouches).

**Key Microbial Targets in Horticultural Packaging:**

| Pathogen | Produce Affected | Packaging Mitigation |
|----------|------------------|----------------------|
| *Botrytis cinerea* (grey mould) | Strawberries, grapes | MAP + SO₂-generating pads |
| *Erwinia carotovora* (soft rot) | Tomatoes, potatoes | Ventilated CFB boxes |
| *Penicillium* spp. | Citrus, apples | Wax coating + low O₂ MAP |
| *Escherichia coli*, *Salmonella* | Cut fruits, sprouts | Antimicrobial films |
| *Aspergillus* (aflatoxins) | Nuts, dried fruits | Vacuum packaging |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Note:** Microbial protection is **integrated** with moisture and gas control — high internal humidity in MAP can paradoxically **promote fungal growth**, so the design must balance both.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units | KTU Application |
|---|---------|---------|-------|------------------|
| 1 | $WVTR = \dfrac{P_{H_2O} \cdot \Delta P_{H_2O}}{L}$ | Water vapour transmission | g·m⁻²·day⁻¹ | Moisture loss calculation |
| 2 | $OTR = \dfrac{P_{O_2} \cdot \Delta p_{O_2}}{L}$ | Oxygen transmission | cm³·m⁻²·day⁻¹ | MAP design |
| 3 | $q = -k \cdot A \cdot \dfrac{dT}{dx}$ | Fourier heat conduction | W (J·s⁻¹) | Thermal insulation |
| 4 | $R_{CO_2/O_2} = \dfrac{CO_2TR}{O_2TR}$ | Permeability ratio | dimensionless | MAP equilibrium |
| 5 | $R = \dfrac{\Delta C}{\Delta p}$ | Permeance | mol·m⁻²·s⁻¹·Pa⁻¹ | Material selection |
| 6 | $Q_{10} = \left(\dfrac{r_2}{r_1}\right)^{\frac{10}{T_2 - T_1}}$ | Temperature acceleration of respiration | dimensionless | Cold chain design |
| 7 | $W = \dfrac{(W_0 - W_t)}{W_0} \times 100$ | Percentage weight loss | % | Shelf-life assessment |
| 8 | $\tau_{shelf} = \dfrac{M_{crit} \cdot A}{WVTR}$ | Estimated shelf-life | days | Packaging thickness design |

Where:
- $M_{crit}$ = critical mass loss for visible wilting (typically **5% of initial weight**)
- $A$ = surface area of the package (m²)

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Applications

| Application | Critical Barrier Required | KTU-Relevant Industry Context |
|-------------|---------------------------|-------------------------------|
| **Cut rose packaging** | High gas + ethylene barrier, moisture control | Kerala floriculture export |
| **Mango export (Alphonso, Kesar)** | MAP + corrugate CFB + foam net | KTU case study |
| **Banana ripening boxes** | Ventilated + ethylene-permeable LDPE | Kerala banana value chain |
| **Strawberry punnets** | MAP (high CO₂) + anti-fog film | Polyhouse produce |
| **Coconut water packaging** | Light barrier + oxygen barrier (Tetra Pak) | Kerala coconut industry |
| **Spice packaging (Kerala)** | Moisture + gas + light barrier (aluminium laminate) | Spice Board of India |
| **Cashew kernels** | Vacuum + nitrogen flush + moisture barrier | KAU cashew research |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Steady-State Modified Atmosphere Packaging (SSMA) Equation

> [!NOTE]
> **Pedagogical Goal:** Derive the equation that relates the **respiration rate of produce** to the **permeability of packaging film** at equilibrium. This is a KTU-favourite derivation.

### Given:
- A horticultural produce of mass $m$ (kg) is sealed in a package of surface area $A$ (m²).
- The produce respires at a rate $r_{O_2}$ (mL O₂·kg⁻¹·h⁻¹) and produces $r_{CO_2}$ (mL CO₂·kg⁻¹·h⁻¹).
- The package film has oxygen permeability $P_{O_2}$ and CO₂ permeability $P_{CO_2}$.

### Step 1 — Respiration Oxygen Consumption

Total O₂ consumed by produce per unit time:

$$R_{O_2} = m \cdot r_{O_2}$$

Where $R_{O_2}$ is in mL·h⁻¹.

### Step 2 — O₂ Influx Through Film

At steady state, the rate of O₂ entering the package equals the rate of O₂ consumed. By Fick's Law:

$$R_{O_2} = \frac{P_{O_2} \cdot A \cdot (p_{O_2}^{ext} - p_{O_2}^{int})}{L}$$

Where $p_{O_2}^{ext}$ = external (atmospheric) O₂ partial pressure = **21.2 kPa**, and $p_{O_2}^{int}$ = internal steady-state O₂ partial pressure.

### Step 3 — Solve for Internal O₂ Partial Pressure

Rearranging:

$$p_{O_2}^{int} = p_{O_2}^{ext} - \frac{R_{O_2} \cdot L}{P_{O_2} \cdot A}$$

Expressing in **% O₂** by dividing by total pressure (101.325 kPa) and multiplying by 100:

$$[\%O_2]_{int} = 21 \times \left(1 - \frac{R_{O_2} \cdot L}{P_{O_2} \cdot A \cdot p_{atm}}\right)$$

### Step 4 — Apply Same Logic to CO₂

$$[\%CO_2]_{int} = \frac{R_{CO_2} \cdot L}{P_{CO_2} \cdot A \cdot p_{atm}} \times 100$$

### Step 5 — Combine to Get Respiration Quotient (RQ)

At steady state:

$$RQ = \frac{R_{CO_2}}{R_{O_2}} = \frac{[\%CO_2]_{int} \cdot P_{CO_2}}{[\%O_2]_{ext} - [\%O_2]_{int} \cdot P_{O_2}}$$

### Final Result (SSMA Equation):

$$RQ = \frac{[CO_2]_{int} \cdot P_{CO_2}}{(21 - [O_2]_{int}) \cdot P_{O_2}}$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Interpretation:** This is the **master equation** for MAP design. The student must be able to compute the required $[O_2]_{int}$ and $[CO_2]_{int}$ for a given produce RQ, or select the correct film permeability ratio.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Estimated Shelf-Life from WVTR

### Given:
- Initial mass of produce: $W_0$ (kg)
- Critical mass loss: $M_{crit}$ = 5% of $W_0$ (for most leafy vegetables)
- Film area: $A$ (m²)
- Water vapour transmission rate: $WVTR$ (g·m⁻²·day⁻¹)

### Step 1 — Express Critical Mass Loss in Grams

$$M_{crit} = 0.05 \times W_0 \times 1000 \text{ grams}$$

### Step 2 — Total Water Vapour Transmitted Over Time $t$

$$M(t) = WVTR \cdot A \cdot t$$

### Step 3 — Set $M(t) = M_{crit}$ and Solve

$$WVTR \cdot A \cdot t = 0.05 \times W_0 \times 1000$$

$$\boxed{t = \frac{0.05 \times W_0 \times 1000}{WVTR \cdot A}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This equation assumes **isothermal conditions** and **linear WVTR**. In real systems, Arrhenius correction is applied for temperature variation.

### Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style):

**Problem:** A bunch of spinach has $W_0 = 250$ g. The package area $A = 0.04$ m² and the LDPE film WVTR = 15 g·m⁻²·day⁻¹. Calculate the maximum safe storage time before 5% wilting.

**Solution:**

$$t = \frac{0.05 \times 250 \times 1000}{15 \times 0.04}$$

$$t = \frac{12500}{0.6}$$

$$\boxed{t = 20{,}833 \text{ hours} = 868 \text{ days}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Students forget to convert $W_0$ from kg to grams when $WVTR$ is in g·m⁻²·day⁻¹. **Always check unit consistency**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Temperature Acceleration of Respiration (Q₁₀ Concept)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Module 3 High-Yield Topic** — Cold chain engineers use $Q_{10}$ to model how respiration increases with temperature.

### Derivation:

The Arrhenius-type temperature dependence of respiration rate is given by:

$$r(T) = r_0 \cdot e^{\frac{E_a}{R \cdot T}}$$

For a 10 °C interval, the $Q_{10}$ factor is defined as:

$$Q_{10} = \frac{r(T + 10)}{r(T)}$$

**Simplified form:**

$$Q_{10} = \left(\frac{r_2}{r_1}\right)^{\frac{10}{T_2 - T_1}}$$

Where $T$ is in °C.

**Typical Q₁₀ values for horticultural produce:**

| Produce | Q₁₀ Range | Storage Implication |
|---------|-----------|---------------------|
| Apples | 2.0–2.5 | Cold storage (0–4 °C) adequate |
| Bananas | 3.0–4.0 | Highly temperature sensitive |
| Strawberries | 3.0–4.5 | Strict cold chain mandatory |
| Leafy vegetables | 2.5–3.0 | Hydrocooled + refrigerated |
| Mangoes | 2.0–3.0 | Mature-green stage best |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Tip:** For every **10 °C rise** in storage temperature, the respiration rate of fresh produce **roughly doubles to quadruples** ($Q_{10} \approx 2-4$).

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Selection of Packaging Material

The following decision matrix is used in KTU design problems:

| Property | LDPE | HDPE | PP | PET | PVC | PVDC | EVOH | Al-foil | CFB |
|----------|------|------|-----|-----|-----|------|------|---------|-----|
| **Light barrier** | P | M | M | P | P | G | G | E | E |
| **Heat resistance** | P | M | G | G | P | G | M | E | P |
| **Moisture barrier** | M | G | M | M | P | E | P | E | P |
| **Gas barrier (O₂)** | P | M | M | G | P | E | E | E | E |
| **CO₂ barrier** | P | M | M | G | P | E | E | E | E |
| **Microbial barrier** | M | M | M | M | M | G | G | E | M |
| **Cost (₹/kg)** | L | L | L | M | M | H | H | H | L |
| **Recyclability** | G | G | M | G | P | P | P | P | E |

*Legend:* E = Excellent | G = Good | M = Moderate | P = Poor | L = Low | H = High | CFB = Corrugated Fibreboard

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Key:** No single material meets all five barrier requirements perfectly. **Multilayer laminates** (e.g., PET/Al-foil/LDPE) are engineered to combine complementary properties.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Five Functional Barriers of Horticultural Packaging (Block Architecture Flow)

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid diagram maps the **interactions between the five barrier functions** of packaging and the **physiological processes** of horticultural produce.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    produce1[Fresh Horticultural Produce] --> resp1[Respiration Process]
    produce1 --> tran1[Transpiration Process]
    produce1 --> ethy1[Ethylene Emission]
    produce1 --> phot1[Photo Degradation]
    produce1 --> micr1[Microbial Load]

    resp1 --> o2c[O2 Consumption]
    resp1 --> co2p[CO2 Generation]
    resp1 --> heat1[Heat Liberation]

    tran1 --> wv1[Water Vapour Loss]
    phot1 --> uv1[UV and Visible Light Damage]
    ethy1 --> c2h41[Ethylene C2H4 Senescence Trigger]
    micr1 --> bac1[Bacteria and Fungi and Yeast]

    pkg1[ENGINEERED PACKAGING SYSTEM] --> barr1[Light Barrier Layer]
    pkg1 --> barr2[Thermal Insulation Layer]
    pkg1 --> barr3[Moisture Proof Layer]
    pkg1 --> barr4[Gas Proof Layer O2 and CO2 and C2H4]
    pkg1 --> barr5[Antimicrobial Barrier]

    barr1 --> block1[Blocks UV and Visible Light]
    barr2 --> block2[Insulates against Heat Transfer]
    barr3 --> block3[Restricts Water Vapour Transmission]
    barr4 --> block4[Controls O2 and CO2 and Ethylene Permeation]
    barr5 --> block5[Inhibits Microbial Growth]

    block1 --> stable1[Extended Shelf Life]
    block2 --> stable1
    block3 --> stable1
    block4 --> stable1
    block5 --> stable1
```

## 4.2 Mass Transfer Pathways Across a Packaging Film

> [!NOTE]
> The following diagram illustrates the **simultaneous mass transfer pathways** through a multilayer packaging film. Each arrow represents a distinct engineering transport process governed by a specific physical law.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    interior[Horticultural Produce Interior] -->|Respiration| o2in[O2 Outflux]
    interior -->|Transpiration| wvin[H2O Vapour Outflux]
    interior -->|Ethylene| ethin[C2H4 Outflux]
    interior -->|CO2| co2out[CO2 Outflux]

    o2in --> film1[Packaging Film Multilayer]
    wvin --> film1
    ethin --> film1
    co2out --> film1

    film1 -->|Ficks Law| o2out[O2 Influx from Air]
    film1 -->|Ficks Law| co2in[CO2 Influx from Air]
    film1 -->|Fourier Law| hx[Heat Exchange]
    film1 -->|Beers Law| lt[Light Transmission]

    o2out --> exterior[External Atmosphere]
    co2in --> exterior
    hx --> exterior
    lt --> exterior
```

## 4.3 Decision Tree for Selecting Packaging Material

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start1[Start: Select Packaging for Horticultural Produce] --> q1{Respiration Rate of Produce?}
    q1 -->|High strawberries mangoes| high1[Choose MAP with High Gas Barrier]
    q1 -->|Low apples citrus| low1[Choose Ventilated Packaging]
    q1 -->|Minimal dried fruits nuts| min1[Choose Moisture and Light Barrier]

    high1 --> q2{Heat Treatment Required?}
    low1 --> q2
    min1 --> q2

    q2 -->|Yes retort pasteurization| heat1[Select PP or PET with Heat Seal]
    q2 -->|No cold chain only| noheat1[Select LDPE or CFB]

    heat1 --> q3{Antimicrobial Protection Needed?}
    noheat1 --> q3

    q3 -->|Yes fresh cut| anti1[Add Active Packaging Silver or Chitosan]
    q3 -->|No whole produce| noanti1[Standard Barrier Sufficient]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define the term "Modified Atmosphere Packaging" (MAP) and explain its significance in post-harvest handling of horticultural crops.**

**Model Answer:**

> Modified Atmosphere Packaging (MAP) is a preservation technique in which the **gaseous environment (O₂, CO₂, N₂)** surrounding a horticultural produce inside a sealed package is altered from the normal atmospheric composition (21% O₂, 0.04% CO₂, 78% N₂) to a modified composition (typically 2–5% O₂, 3–10% CO₂, balance N₂) in order to **suppress respiration rate, delay senescence, and extend shelf-life**.
>
> **Significance:**
> - Reduces respiration rate by 30–60%, extending shelf-life of fruits and vegetables.
> - Suppresses ethylene action, delaying ripening in climacteric fruits (banana, mango, tomato).
> - Inhibits aerobic microbial growth (especially *Botrytis*, *Penicillium*).
> - Maintains firmness, colour, vitamin C, and organoleptic quality during transport and storage.

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**List the five major packaging requirements for horticultural produce and explain any two in brief.**

**Model Answer:**

The five major packaging requirements for horticultural produce are:

1. **Light Transmittance Control** — to prevent photo-degradation of pigments and vitamins.
2. **Heat / Thermal Resistance** — to insulate the produce during the cold chain and resist heat during pasteurization.
3. **Moisture Proofing** — to prevent transpiration losses and weight loss >5%.
4. **Gas Proofing** — to control O₂, CO₂, and ethylene permeation for MAP/CA systems.
5. **Microbial Protection** — to act as a physical and chemical barrier against pathogens.

**Brief Explanation of Two:**

- **Moisture Proofing:** Packaging films must have a low **WVTR** (Water Vapour Transmission Rate). Materials like **aluminium foil, HDPE, and PVDC** offer excellent moisture barriers. Critical threshold: weight loss must remain below **5%** to avoid visible shrivelling.

- **Gas Proofing:** Packaging films must be selected based on their **Oxygen Transmission Rate (OTR)** and **CO₂ Transmission Rate (CO₂TR)**. For MAP of strawberries, films with OTR of 1000–3000 cm³·m⁻²·day⁻¹ are preferred, while for cut salads, films with OTR of 8000–12000 are used.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q. (a)** Explain in detail the **packaging requirements of horticultural crops** with respect to **light transmittance, heat, moisture, gas, and microorganisms**. Discuss the role of **multilayer films** in achieving these combined barrier properties. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

Horticultural produce is **highly perishable** due to its biological activity (respiration, transpiration) and vulnerability to environmental stresses. Therefore, packaging must simultaneously address **five functional barriers**:

**(i) Light Transmittance Control:**
Light, especially UV-B (280–315 nm) and blue light (400–500 nm), causes **chlorophyll degradation, β-carotene oxidation, and loss of vitamin C** in fresh produce. Packaging films should transmit **<10% UV-B** and **<30% total visible light**. **Aluminized pouches** and **black LDPE** are commonly used for light-sensitive items like dried spices, cashew kernels, and cut flowers.

**(ii) Heat / Thermal Resistance:**
The package must insulate the produce during the cold chain (0–4 °C) and resist temperature abuse. **Fourier's Law of heat conduction** governs this: $q = -k \cdot A \cdot \frac{dT}{dx}$. Materials with low thermal conductivity (**EPS, CFB**) are preferred for temperature-sensitive items like strawberries and roses.

**(iii) Moisture Proofing:**
Transpiration causes weight loss; **>5% loss** makes leafy vegetables wilt. The **WVTR** (g·m⁻²·day⁻¹) must be minimized. **HDPE, PVDC, and aluminium foil** provide excellent moisture barriers.

**(iv) Gas Proofing:**
Respiration consumes O₂ and releases CO₂ + ethylene. Films must have controlled **OTR** and **CO₂TR** to establish an equilibrium MA. **EVOH and PVDC** are the best gas-barrier materials. The SSMA equation $RQ = \dfrac{[CO_2] \cdot P_{CO_2}}{(21 - [O_2]) \cdot P_{O_2}}$ is used for MAP design.

**(v) Microbial Protection:**
The package acts as a physical barrier against pathogens. **Active packaging** with **silver nanoparticles, chitosan, or essential oils** provides antimicrobial action. High CO₂ in MAP also suppresses aerobic microbial growth.

**Role of Multilayer Films:**
No single material meets all five requirements. Therefore, **multilayer laminates** like **PET/Al-foil/LDPE** (typical Tetra Pak structure) are engineered to combine:
- **Outer PET layer** → mechanical strength, printability, moderate gas barrier.
- **Middle Al-foil layer** → 100% light + gas + moisture barrier.
- **Inner LDPE layer** → heat sealability, food contact safety.

This combination provides a **complete barrier system** for processed horticultural products (juices, concentrates, dried fruits).

---

**Q. (b)** With a neat sketch, explain the **Modified Atmosphere Packaging (MAP) system**. Derive the equation for **steady-state equilibrium** of O₂ inside the package. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**MAP System Description:**

A typical MAP system consists of:
1. **Produce** placed in a tray or pouch.
2. **Permeable film** sealed over the tray (LDPE, BOPP, or multilayer).
3. **Gas-flushed environment** (initial gas mix: low O₂, high CO₂, balance N₂).
4. **Time-dependent equilibrium** achieved as produce respires and gases permeate.

*(Sketch: A horizontal cross-section of a sealed tray with produce in the centre, arrows showing O₂ diffusing in and CO₂ diffusing out through the film.)*

**Derivation of Steady-State O₂:**

At steady state, **O₂ entering the package = O₂ consumed by respiration**:

$$\frac{P_{O_2} \cdot A \cdot (p_{O_2}^{ext} - p_{O_2}^{int})}{L} = m \cdot r_{O_2}$$

Solving for internal O₂ partial pressure:

$$p_{O_2}^{int} = p_{O_2}^{ext} - \frac{m \cdot r_{O_2} \cdot L}{P_{O_2} \cdot A}$$

Converting to **% O₂**:

$$[\%O_2]_{int} = 21 \times \left(1 - \frac{m \cdot r_{O_2} \cdot L}{P_{O_2} \cdot A \cdot p_{atm}}\right)$$

**Interpretation:**
- Higher produce mass $m$ → more O₂ consumption → lower internal O₂.
- Higher film permeability $P_{O_2}$ → more O₂ ingress → higher internal O₂.
- Higher respiration rate $r_{O_2}$ → lower internal O₂.

This equation allows the **designer to choose film thickness $L$** for a desired steady-state $[O_2]_{int}$ that maximizes shelf-life for a given produce.

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative): `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q. (a)** Discuss the role of **moisture-proof** and **gas-proof packaging** in extending the shelf-life of fresh horticultural produce. Explain the concepts of **WVTR, OTR, and Respiration Quotient (RQ)** with suitable examples. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

Fresh horticultural produce continuously undergoes **transpiration** (water loss) and **respiration** (O₂ consumption, CO₂ release). Without proper packaging, these processes cause **wilting, shrivelling, and senescence** within hours to days.

**Role of Moisture-Proof Packaging:**
- Transpiration causes **weight loss**; exceeding 5% leads to visible wilting in leafy vegetables.
- Moisture-proof films (HDPE, PVDC, Al-foil) restrict the **Water Vapour Transmission Rate (WVTR)**, maintaining high internal RH (>90%) while preventing condensation.
- **WVTR Definition:** The mass of water vapour transmitted through a film of unit area per unit time under specified temperature and RH conditions, measured in **g·m⁻²·day⁻¹** (standard: 38 °C, 90% RH, ASTM E96).
- **Example:** Wrapping cucumbers in **PVC stretch film** (WVTR = 30 g·m⁻²·day⁻¹) extends shelf-life from 3 days (unwrapped) to 10 days (wrapped) at 10 °C.

**Role of Gas-Proof Packaging:**
- Respiration equation: $C_6H_{12}O_6 + 6O_2 \rightarrow 6CO_2 + 6H_2O + 2818 \text{ kJ}$
- Gas-proof films control the **Oxygen Transmission Rate (OTR)** and **CO₂ Transmission Rate (CO₂TR)**, establishing a beneficial modified atmosphere.
- **OTR Definition:** The volume of O₂ transmitted per unit area per unit time at standard conditions (23 °C, 0% RH), measured in **cm³·m⁻²·day⁻¹**.
- **Respiration Quotient (RQ):** The molar ratio of CO₂ produced to O₂ consumed: $RQ = \dfrac{CO_2 \text{ produced}}{O_2 \text{ consumed}}$.
  - For aerobic respiration, **RQ ≈ 1.0**.
  - For anaerobic respiration (fermentation), **RQ > 1.0** (unfavourable, produces off-flavours).
- **Example:** MAP of strawberries using **LDPE film** (OTR ≈ 6000 cm³·m⁻²·day⁻¹) achieves steady-state O₂ of 3–5% and CO₂ of 10–15%, extending shelf-life from 5 days to 14 days at 2 °C.

---

**Q. (b)** A batch of fresh **spinach** weighing 250 g is packed in a **polyethylene pouch** of area 0.04 m². The WVTR of the film is **15 g·m⁻²·day⁻¹**. Calculate the **maximum safe storage time** if the **critical weight loss is 5%**. What packaging modification would you suggest to **double the shelf-life**? **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Given Data:**
- Initial mass: $W_0 = 250$ g
- Package area: $A = 0.04$ m²
- WVTR = 15 g·m⁻²·day⁻¹
- Critical weight loss: 5% of $W_0$

**Step 1 — Critical Mass Loss in Grams:**

$$M_{crit} = 0.05 \times 250 = 12.5 \text{ g}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Shelf-Life Equation:**

$$t = \frac{M_{crit}}{WVTR \cdot A}$$

$$t = \frac{12.5}{15 \times 0.04}$$

$$t = \frac{12.5}{0.6}$$

$$\boxed{t = 20.83 \text{ days} \approx 21 \text{ days}}$$

**Step 3 — Packaging Modification to Double Shelf-Life:**

To double the shelf-life to **42 days**, the new effective WVTR must be **7.5 g·m⁻²·day⁻¹**. Suggested modifications:

1. **Switch to a multilayer film** with a higher moisture barrier, e.g., **PET/LDPE laminate** (WVTR ≈ 5–7 g·m⁻²·day⁻¹).
2. **Increase film thickness** from 25 µm to 50 µm (WVTR scales inversely with thickness, so doubling thickness halves WVTR).
3. **Add an inner pouch of micro-perforated PE** combined with an outer aluminium laminate to combine moisture barrier with controlled gas exchange (MAP).
4. **Incorporate a moisture-absorbing sachet** (silica gel or molecular sieve) inside the package to scavenge transpired water and lower the effective RH.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
>
> **Common Mistakes in This Type of Question:**
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency:** WVTR is in g·m⁻²·day⁻¹ but students often mix it with kg·m⁻²·day⁻¹ or forget to convert area correctly. **Always show unit cancellation.**
> 2. **Forgetting the 5% threshold:** Critical weight loss is **not 10%**; it is **5%** for most leafy vegetables. Forget this and the entire calculation collapses.
> 3. **Ignoring temperature:** The 15 g·m⁻²·day⁻¹ value is at 38 °C (ASTM E96). At lower temperatures (2–5 °C cold storage), the actual WVTR is 2–3 times lower, so real shelf-life is longer.
> 4. **Not specifying modified atmosphere:** Simply stating "use better packaging" is **not acceptable** in a 7-mark KTU question. Always specify the **material, thickness, WVTR/OTR value, and engineering reason**.
> 5. **Confusing WVTR with OTR:** Moisture barrier (WVTR) and gas barrier (OTR) are different. A film with low WVTR is not necessarily a good O₂ barrier (e.g., LDPE is good for moisture but poor for O₂).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Exam**

- [ ] **Packaging is an engineered micro-environment**, not just a container — it regulates mass transfer.
- [ ] **Five critical barriers:** Light, Heat, Moisture, Gas, Microbial.
- [ ] **Light Transmittance** should be **<10% UV-B** and **<30% visible light** for most fresh produce.
- [ ] **Fourier's Law** governs heat transfer: $q = -k \cdot A \cdot \frac{dT}{dx}$.
- [ ] **WVTR** is measured in **g·m⁻²·day⁻¹** at 38 °C, 90% RH (ASTM E96).
- [ ] **OTR** is measured in **cm³·m⁻²·day⁻¹** at 23 °C, 0% RH.
- [ ] **Critical weight loss for wilting:** **5% of initial mass** for leafy vegetables.
- [ ] **Respiration Quotient (RQ):** $RQ = \dfrac{CO_2 \text{ produced}}{O_2 \text{ consumed}}$ — aerobic RQ ≈ 1.0.
- [ ] **SSMA Equation:** $RQ = \dfrac{[CO_2]_{int} \cdot P_{CO_2}}{(21 - [O_2]_{int}) \cdot P_{O_2}}$ — use for MAP design.
- [ ] **$Q_{10}$** ≈ 2–4 for most produce; every 10 °C rise roughly doubles–quadruples respiration.
- [ ] **Aluminium foil** offers **100% barrier** to light, gas, and moisture.
- [ ] **EVOH and PVDC** are the **best gas-barrier polymers**; **LDPE and HDPE** are best for moisture.
- [ ] **No single material** meets all five barriers — **multilayer laminates** (PET/Al/LDPE) are the industry standard.
- [ ] **High CO₂ (10–20%)** in MAP suppresses *Botrytis* and *Pseudomonas*.
- [ ] **Ethylene (C₂H₄)** at **0.1–1 ppm** accelerates ripening — must be scavenged.
- [ ] **Active packaging** uses **silver nanoparticles, chitosan, essential oils** for antimicrobial action.
- [ ] **Common KTU materials to memorize:** LDPE, HDPE, PP, PET, PVC, PVDC, EVOH, EPS, CFB, Al-foil.
- [ ] **Common KTU produce case studies:** Mango, Banana, Strawberry, Cut Rose, Coconut Water, Cashew, Spices.
- [ ] **Kerala-specific KTU context:** Banana ripening boxes, Alphonso mango export, coconut water Tetra Pak, cardamom and pepper moisture-proof packs.
- [ ] **Always check unit consistency** in numerical problems — KTU loses easy marks on this.
- [ ] **Equation to derive in exam:** SSMA equilibrium for O₂ and CO₂.
- [ ] **Equation to apply in exam:** Shelf-life from WVTR: $t = \dfrac{0.05 \cdot W_0 \cdot 1000}{WVTR \cdot A}$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Different Types of Packaging Materials Commonly Used for Raw and Processed Fruits and Vegetables Products
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAGT742 - POST HARVEST ENGINEERING OF HORTICULTURAL CROPS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Different Types of Packaging Materials Commonly Used for Raw and Processed Fruits and Vegetables Products

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Packaging** in the context of post-harvest engineering of horticultural crops is defined as a *coordinated system* of preparing goods for transport, distribution, storage, retailing, and end-use, such that the product reaches the consumer in optimum condition at minimum overall cost, while simultaneously conveying essential information and ensuring regulatory compliance.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Packaging of horticultural products is a **multi-functional technology** that envelops and protects the food product from the point of harvest to the point of consumption. It is classified into three hierarchical levels:
> - **Primary Packaging:** The package in **direct contact** with the product (e.g., cling film on a fruit tray, polyethylene pouch around lettuce).
> - **Secondary Packaging:** The outer wrapper that **groups** several primary packages (e.g., corrugated fiberboard carton containing punnets of strawberries).
> - **Tertiary Packaging:** The bulk-handling unit designed for **transport and warehousing** (e.g., wooden pallets, shrink-wrapped shipping containers).

For **raw (fresh)** fruits and vegetables, the principal engineering requirement is to maintain *respiration balance* (manage O₂ and CO₂ exchange) and to retard *transpiration* (moisture loss). For **processed** fruits and vegetables (canned, frozen, dehydrated, fermented, minimally processed), the dominant requirement is to provide an *impermeable barrier* against oxygen, light, water vapor, and microbial ingress, while also providing *mechanical* and *thermal* stability.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Engineering Function** — Packaging must perform the "**4 P + 2 C**" functions:
> **P**rotect, **P**reserve, **P**resent, **P**ortability + **C**onvenience, **C**ommunication.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine the **human body**: your **skin** is *primary packaging* (direct contact, regulates what enters and leaves — oxygen, water, sweat), your **shirt and trousers** are *secondary packaging* (groups and protects the skin and inner body, carries labels and brand), and the **delivery truck** that brings the clothes from the factory to your wardrobe is *tertiary packaging* (bulk handling, logistics).

In the same way, a tomato in a supermarket is wrapped in a **cling film (primary)**, placed in a **plastic tray (also primary)**, packed with several other trays in a **corrugated carton (secondary)**, and stacked on a **wooden pallet (tertiary)** for transport from the farm.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory hook:** *Primary = Phone case. Secondary = Amazon box. Tertiary = Shipping container on a ship.*

## 1.3 Why This Topic is Critical for KTU

- **7–12 marks** in typical KTU ESE papers.
- Direct question on **"Compare glass vs. metal vs. plastic packaging"** is a Module 3 favorite.
- Frequently asked as a **3-mark short answer** on specific materials (e.g., *properties of PET, OTR/WVTR of HDPE, advantages of glass for jams*).

## 1.4 Standard Metrics Used in Packaging Engineering

The following quantitative descriptors are universally used and must be memorised:

- **WVTR (Water Vapor Transmission Rate)** — measured in **g·m⁻²·day⁻¹** at a specified temperature (usually **23 °C**) and relative humidity gradient (usually **0 → 90 % RH**).
- **OTR (Oxygen Transmission Rate)** — measured in **cm³·m⁻²·day⁻¹·atm⁻¹** (or **cc/m²/day** in older units).
- **CO₂TR (Carbon Dioxide Transmission Rate)** — measured analogously.
- **Tensile Strength** — **MPa** (ASTM D882 for films).
- **Heat Seal Strength** — **N/15 mm** (ASTM F88).
- **Burst Strength (Mullen)** — **kPa** (TAPPI T810 for paperboard).
- **Shelf Life** — typically **days to years** depending on product class.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students frequently report WVTR units as "g/day". Always include the **per m² and per day** components. The unit is fundamentally a *flux density*.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Packaging Materials

Packaging materials for horticultural products are classified by **chemical origin**, **physical structure**, and **functional role**. The complete taxonomy is as follows:

### A. Rigid Packaging Materials
1. **Glass** — soda-lime-silica composition, inert, transparent, hermetic.
2. **Metals** — tinplate (tin-coated steel), aluminium (foil, sheet, cans), tin-free steel (TFS / ECCS).
3. **Rigid Plastics** — PET bottles, HDPE jugs, PP tubs, PS cups, PVC blister packs.

### B. Flexible Packaging Materials
1. **Plastic Films** — LDPE, LLDPE, HDPE, PP, PVC, PVDC, EVOH, PET, PA (Nylon), cellophane, oriented polypropylene (OPP/CPP).
2. **Aluminium Foil** — typically 6–30 µm gauges.
3. **Paper & Paperboard** — kraft paper, glassine, parchment, greaseproof, SBS (Solid Bleached Sulphate), CRB (Coated Recycled Board), FBB (Folding Box Board).
4. **Multilayer Laminates** — combinations of two or more of the above bonded by adhesives or extrusion (e.g., PET/Al/PE).

### C. Bio-based / Sustainable Materials
1. **PLA (Polylactic Acid)** — derived from corn starch.
2. **PHB / PHBV (Polyhydroxyalkanoates)** — bacterially produced.
3. **Edible Films & Coatings** — chitosan, alginate, starch, pullulan, carnauba wax, beeswax.
4. **Moulded Pulp** — recycled paperboard trays (egg-carton style).
5. **Bagasse / Palm Leaf / Bamboo** — single-use rigid trays.
6. **Mycelium (Fungal)** — mushroom-root packaging for shipping.

### D. Wood, Textile, and Miscellaneous
1. **Wooden Crates, Baskets, and Pallets** — traditional for raw produce.
2. **Jute / HDPE Woven Sacks** — for onions, potatoes, citrus.
3. **Expanded Polystyrene (EPS) / EPS Trays** — for apples, eggs, fragile produce.

## 2.2 Properties Required of a Packaging Material (The "Decision Matrix")

| Property | Reason it Matters for F&V |
|---|---|
| **Gas Permeability (O₂, CO₂, N₂)** | Determines whether the package is suitable for **MAP** of respiring produce. |
| **WVTR** | Prevents desiccation (wilting) or condensation (rot). |
| **Optical Clarity** | Influences consumer purchase decision for fresh produce. |
| **Mechanical Strength** | Withstands stacking loads, vibration, impact, puncture. |
| **Chemical Inertness** | No migration of toxicants, no flavour scalping. |
| **Heat Sealability** | Enables hermetic closure on form-fill-seal lines. |
| **Printability** | Carries mandatory FSSAI / barcode / batch / MRP information. |
| **Microwave / Oven Compatibility** | Critical for *ready-to-eat* frozen/processed meals. |
| **Recyclability / Sustainability** | Mandatory under Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) — **India PWM Rules 2016 (amended 2022)**. |
| **Thermal Resistance** | Must survive retort (121 °C), pasteurisation (90 °C), or blast freezing (−40 °C). |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Equations for KTU Numerical Problems** — Memorise these in symbol form, value, and units.

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $J = \dfrac{D \cdot (P_1 - P_2)}{L}$ | **Fick's First Law** — Steady-state permeant flux (J) through a film of thickness L. | mol·m⁻²·s⁻¹ |
| 2 | $P = D \cdot S$ | **Permeability coefficient (P)** = Diffusivity (D) × Solubility (S) | mol·m·m⁻²·s⁻¹·Pa⁻¹ |
| 3 | $\text{WVTR} = \dfrac{\Delta m}{A \cdot t}$ | Mass change Δm over area A in time t | g·m⁻²·day⁻¹ |
| 4 | $\theta_s = \dfrac{M_0 - M_f}{r_w}$ | **Shelf life** (θₛ) from initial to final moisture content M₀ → M_f at desiccation rate r_w | days |
| 5 | $R = \dfrac{P_{\text{O}_2}^{\text{film}}}{P_{\text{CO}_2}^{\text{film}}}$ | **Selectivity ratio** for MAP design | dimensionless |
| 6 | $\sigma_t = \dfrac{F}{A}$ | **Tensile stress** = Force / Cross-sectional area | MPa |
| 7 | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{\Delta L}{L_0} \times 100$ | **Elongation at break** | % |
| 8 | $\tau = \dfrac{F_{\text{seal}}}{w}$ | **Heat seal strength** per unit width | N / 15 mm |

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary conditions for Fick's Law (Eq. 1):** Valid only for **steady state**, **isothermal**, **single-component permeation**, and **no chemical reaction** between permeant and film. For non-steady state (transient sorption), **Fick's Second Law** applies:
> $$\dfrac{\partial C}{\partial t} = D \cdot \dfrac{\partial^2 C}{\partial x^2}$$

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **MAP (Modified Atmosphere Packaging)** of fresh-cut lettuce uses **perforated PP / PE films** tuned to O₂ ≈ **1–3 %** and CO₂ ≈ **5–10 %** to slow respiration while preventing anaerobic fermentation.
- **Retort pouches** (3-ply: **PET / Al foil / CPP**) allow thermal processing of fruit purées at 121 °C with **30 % less energy** than metal cans, and survive 18-month ambient shelf life.
- **Shelf-stable fruit juices** rely on **Tetra Pak** (paperboard / PE / Al / PE) for > 6 months unrefrigerated storage.
- **Active packaging sachets** (O₂ scavengers, ethylene absorbers like **KMnO₄-impregnated zeolite**) extend banana shelf life by **3–5 days** when inserted inside LDPE-lined cartons.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Steady-State Permeation Through a Packaging Film

We derive the governing equation for **gas transmission through a polymeric film**, which is the foundation for *barrier property* calculations.

### Given
- A flat polymer film of **thickness L (m)**, area **A (m²)**.
- Permeant gas at partial pressure **P₁ (Pa)** on the *upstream* side and **P₂ (Pa)** on the *downstream* side.
- Temperature **T** assumed constant.

### Step 1 — State Fick's First Law
Fick's First Law states that the diffusive flux **J** (mol·m⁻²·s⁻¹) is proportional to the concentration gradient:

$$J = -D \cdot \dfrac{dC}{dx}$$

where **D** is the diffusion coefficient (m²·s⁻¹) and **dC/dx** is the concentration gradient across the film thickness.

### Step 2 — Apply Henry's Law at the Two Interfaces
At the upstream face (x = 0), equilibrium gives:

$$C_1 = S \cdot P_1$$

At the downstream face (x = L), equilibrium gives:

$$C_2 = S \cdot P_2$$

where **S** is the solubility coefficient (mol·m⁻³·Pa⁻¹).

### Step 3 — Assume Steady State
Under steady state, **dC/dx** is linear in x. Substituting the boundary values:

$$\dfrac{dC}{dx} = \dfrac{C_2 - C_1}{L - 0} = \dfrac{S \cdot (P_2 - P_1)}{L}$$

### Step 4 — Insert into Fick's Law

$$J = -D \cdot \dfrac{S \cdot (P_2 - P_1)}{L} = D \cdot S \cdot \dfrac{P_1 - P_2}{L}$$

### Step 5 — Define Permeability Coefficient
Defining the **permeability coefficient** $P = D \cdot S$:

$$\boxed{\,J = \dfrac{P \cdot (P_1 - P_2)}{L}\,}$$

### Step 6 — Convert to Practical OTR / WVTR
Multiplying by the molar volume (RT) and film area A, the **volume of gas** transmitted per day is:

$$\text{OTR} = \dfrac{P \cdot A \cdot (P_1 - P_2)}{L \cdot RT} \quad \left[\text{cm}^3 \cdot \text{m}^{-2} \cdot \text{day}^{-1} \cdot \text{atm}^{-1}\right]$$

**Engineering interpretation:** To *reduce* permeation by a factor of 10, you may either (a) reduce L by 10× (i.e., use a *thinner* but better film), (b) reduce P by 10× (i.e., switch to a *higher-barrier* polymer such as EVOH), or (c) reduce (P₁ − P₂) by using vacuum or nitrogen flushing.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Shelf-Life Equation for Moisture-Sensitive Produce

For dried fruits (e.g., raisins, dried mango) or fresh leafy vegetables losing water, **moisture-driven shelf life** is given by:

### Given
- Initial moisture content **M₀ (% wb)**
- Critical (maximum) moisture content **M_c** at which product is unacceptable
- Critical moisture content at the package surface **M_e** (equilibrium with internal RH)
- Product mass **W_s (kg)** of solids
- Package surface area **A (m²)**
- Constant drying/desiccation rate **N (kg water / m² · s)**

### Step 1 — Mass Balance
The water lost by the product must equal the water transmitted across the package:

$$W_s \cdot (M_0 - M_c) = N \cdot A \cdot \theta_s$$

### Step 2 — Solve for Shelf Life θₛ

$$\boxed{\,\theta_s = \dfrac{W_s \cdot (M_0 - M_c)}{N \cdot A}\,}$$

**Engineering interpretation:** To double the shelf life, you may (a) halve N (use a higher-barrier film with lower WVTR), (b) halve A (smaller package), or (c) increase M_c (accept a slightly higher final moisture content — but this is product-driven, not packaging-driven).

### Step 3 — Worked Numerical Example

> **Problem (KTU-style):** A pack of dried mango slices has **W_s = 0.25 kg**, **M₀ = 22 % wb**, and the critical moisture is **M_c = 14 % wb**. The film area is **A = 0.04 m²** and the desiccation rate is **N = 4.5 × 10⁻⁶ kg/m²·day**. Calculate the shelf life.

**Solution:**

$$\theta_s = \dfrac{0.25 \times (0.22 - 0.14)}{4.5 \times 10^{-6} \times 0.04}$$

$$\theta_s = \dfrac{0.25 \times 0.08}{1.8 \times 10^{-7}}$$

$$\theta_s = \dfrac{0.02}{1.8 \times 10^{-7}}$$

$$\boxed{\,\theta_s \approx 1.11 \times 10^{5} \text{ days}\,}$$

> [!NOTE]
> While this number looks large, in practice other spoilage modes (oxidation, microbial) limit shelf life to **6–12 months**. This equation only computes the *moisture-driven* limit.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Raw vs. Processed F&V

This is the single most important comparison table for KTU — practice reproducing it from memory.

| Packaging Material | Best Suited for **Raw F&V** | Best Suited for **Processed F&V** | Key Limitation |
|---|---|---|---|
| **HDPE Crates** | Apples, mangoes, citrus, tomatoes | — | High cost, must be returned |
| **Corrugated Fibreboard Carton** | Apples, pomegranate, kiwi, banana | — | Sensitive to moisture (wax-coated variant needed) |
| **LDPE / PP Pouches (perforated)** | Grapes, leafy greens, okra, chillies | — | Poor barrier; high WVTR |
| **PET Trays + Cling Film** | Strawberries, blueberries, mushrooms | — | Not hermetic |
| **Glass Jars / Bottles** | — | Jams, pickles, sauces, juice, whole fruit in syrup | Heavy, breakable |
| **Tinplate Cans** | — | Canned pineapple, peaches, peas, tomato paste | Corrosion if uncoated; heavier than Al |
| **Aluminium Cans / Foil Pouches** | — | Juice concentrates, retorted vegetables, ready-meals | Cannot be microwaved (sparks) |
| **Aseptic Cartons (Tetra Pak)** | — | UHT juice, coconut water, tomato ketchup | Non-recyclable without specialised plant |
| **EVOH / PVDC Multilayer Pouches** | Vacuum-packed fresh-cut salad | Soups, sauces, retort pouches | Cost, recycling complexity |
| **Edible Coatings (chitosan, wax)** | Apples, cucumbers, citrus gloss | — | Sensitive to RH; not a complete barrier |
| **EPS Trays** | Apples, pears, eggs | Frozen peas, frozen sweet corn | Not biodegradable |
| **Moulded Pulp / Bagasse** | Apples, stone fruits, eggs | Ready-meal trays | Limited moisture resistance |
| **Jute / HDPE Laminated Sacks** | Onions, potatoes, garlic, citrus | — | Poor moisture barrier unless laminated |
| **Wooden Crates / Baskets** | Mangoes, melons (traditional) | — | Phytosanitation issues; ISPM-15 compliance |

---

## 3.4 Decision Logic for Selecting a Packaging Material (Engineering Methodology)

The standard *stepwise* selection procedure used in industry:

**Step 1 — Identify the product class:** Fresh (respiring) vs. processed (non-respiring, stable).

**Step 2 — Identify the dominant spoilage mode:**
- Fresh: respiration, transpiration, ethylene sensitivity, microbial decay.
- Processed: oxidation, moisture migration, microbial recontamination, light-induced degradation.

**Step 3 — Set the target barrier properties (WVTR, OTR, CO₂TR):** e.g., for fresh-cut lettuce, OTR target ≈ **5,000–10,000 cm³·m⁻²·day⁻¹** (highly permeable). For retort pouches, OTR target ≈ **< 1 cm³·m⁻²·day⁻¹** (near-impermeable).

**Step 4 — Choose material chemistry:** match the required barrier profile to a polymer family (EVOH for O₂, HDPE for water vapour, PVDC for both, glass/metal for near-zero).

**Step 5 — Verify mechanical and thermal performance:** stacking, vibration, retort temperature, freezing temperature.

**Step 6 — Check regulatory and sustainability compliance:** FSSAI (India), FDA / EFSA (exports), and the **Plastic Waste Management (PWM) Rules 2016 / 2022 amendment** for EPR.

**Step 7 — Cost analysis:** per-unit cost vs. savings from extended shelf life.

---

> **Engineering notes on Figure 4.4:**

> - **CPP inner layer:** provides **heat sealability** to itself and **food-contact safety** (no aluminium contact with acidic sauces).

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Similar Style]**
**List any three properties required of a packaging material for fresh-cut fruits and vegetables and explain why each is important.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **High gas permeability (selective O₂/CO₂ ratio):** Fresh-cut produce is still respiring. The film must allow O₂ entry and CO₂ escape to prevent anaerobic fermentation while slowing respiration. *— 1 mark*
2. **Moderate WVTR:** Must permit some transpiration to avoid condensation (which promotes microbial growth) but not so much that the product wilts. *— 1 mark*
3. **Optical clarity and food-contact safety:** Consumers prefer transparent packaging, and the material must comply with **FSSAI** and **FDA** food-contact regulations (no migration of toxicants). *— 1 mark*

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Similar Style]**
**Differentiate between rigid and flexible packaging. Give two examples of each for horticultural products.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Rigid | Flexible |
|---|---|---|
| Shape | Self-supporting | Deformable |
| Examples | **(i) Glass jar** (jam, pickle), **(ii) Tinplate can** (canned pineapple) | **(i) LDPE pouch** (grapes), **(ii) Multilayer retort pouch** (curry) |

*— 1 mark for the correct distinction, 1 mark for two rigid examples, 1 mark for two flexible examples.*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option 1

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Adapted]**
**(a)** Classify the different types of packaging materials commonly used for **raw fruits and vegetables**, with **specific examples** for each class. Discuss the **functions** of primary, secondary, and tertiary packaging in the supply chain of fresh horticultural produce. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare the **barrier properties, mechanical strength, and environmental impact** of **glass, tinplate, and aluminium** as packaging materials for **processed fruits and vegetables** (jams, canned fruit, fruit juice). **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Q.A (a)

**Classification of packaging materials for raw F&V (4 marks):**

1. **Rigid plastic containers:** HDPE crates for tomatoes/mangoes; PET clamshells for strawberries; PP tubs for grapes.
2. **Flexible plastic films:** Perforated LDPE/PP pouches for leafy greens; PVC stretch film over palletised citrus.
3. **Paper and paperboard:** Corrugated fibreboard cartons (CFB) for apples and kiwi; SBS folding cartons for pomegranates; moulded pulp trays for stone fruits.
4. **Bio-based / sustainable:** Bagasse trays, palm-leaf baskets, PLA clamshells, chitosan-coated wraps.
5. **Wood / jute / EPS:** Wooden crates for mangoes and melons; jute sacks for onions and potatoes; EPS trays for apples and pears.

**Functions of primary, secondary, tertiary packaging (3 marks):**

- **Primary packaging** (e.g., cling film on a fruit tray): *Direct contact*, prevents mechanical damage, controls micro-atmosphere, carries unit-level traceability (QR code, MRP, FSSAI logo).
- **Secondary packaging** (e.g., CFB carton grouping 12 trays): *Aggregates* primary units for ease of handling, provides stacking strength, displays brand, carries carton-level batch and shipping information.
- **Tertiary packaging** (e.g., wooden pallet + stretch wrap): *Optimises logistics* — enables forklift handling, protects against transit vibration, secures the load for long-haul refrigerated (reefer) transport.

---

#### Model Solution — Q.A (b)

**Comparison table (5 marks):**

| Property | **Glass** | **Tinplate** | **Aluminium** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **O₂ barrier** | Perfect (hermetic) | Perfect (after sealing) | Perfect (after sealing) |
| **WVTR** | Perfect | Perfect (if lacquer intact) | Perfect |
| **Light barrier** | Amber = full; Clear = partial | Full (opaque metal) | Full (opaque metal) |
| **Mechanical strength** | Brittle, low impact | High (steel substrate) | Moderate, dent-prone |
| **Weight** | Heavy (≈ 2.5 g/cm³) | Heavy (≈ 7.8 g/cm³) | Very light (≈ 2.7 g/cm³) |
| **Thermal resistance** | Excellent (retortable) | Excellent (retortable) | Excellent |
| **Recyclability** | 100 % infinite loop | High (steel loop) | 100 % infinite loop, energy-intensive |
| **Cost** | Moderate | Low–Moderate | Moderate–High |
| **Best use for F&V** | Jams, pickles, juice, whole-fruit in syrup | Canned pineapple, peas, tomato paste | Beverage cans, retort pouches, foil lids |
| **Limitations** | Breakage, weight | Corrosion if inner lacquer fails | Cannot be used in microwave, denting |

**Environmental impact discussion (2 marks):**
- **Glass** has the *lowest production carbon footprint per kg* but is heavy → high transport emissions.
- **Tinplate (steel)** is widely recycled in India (> 80 % recycling rate) → strong circular economy.
- **Aluminium** has *very high* primary-production emissions (≈ 12 kg CO₂/kg metal) but **95 % energy saving** when recycled; ideal for closed-loop systems.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option 2

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Adapted]**
**(a)** Explain the **characteristics, advantages, and limitations** of the following packaging materials, with **specific horticultural applications** for each: **(i) Polyethylene (PE), (ii) Polypropylene (PP), (iii) Polyethylene Terephthalate (PET), (iv) Polyvinyl Chloride (PVC).** **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write short notes on: **(i) Tetra Pak / Aseptic Cartons, (ii) Edible Films and Coatings, (iii) Modified Atmosphere Packaging (MAP) for fresh produce.** **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Q.B (a)

**(i) Polyethylene (PE):** *Low density* (LDPE) is soft, transparent, high WVTR, good sealability — used as **clover film / overwrap** for apples, tomatoes, cucumbers, mushrooms. *High density* (HDPE) is rigid, opaque, high chemical resistance — used as **crates and jugs** for juices and pickles. WVTR (LDPE) ≈ **15–20 g·m⁻²·day⁻¹**; OTR ≈ **6,000–8,000 cm³·m⁻²·day⁻¹**. *Limitation:* poor oxygen barrier.

**(ii) Polypropylene (PP):** Higher melting point (≈ 165 °C) and tensile strength than PE. **Oriented PP (OPP/CPP)** is widely used in **BOPP / MET-PET / PE laminates** for snack packaging and as the heat-seal layer in retort pouches. PP is also used for **microwaveable trays** of frozen vegetables. *Limitation:* brittle at low temperatures.

**(iii) PET (Polyethylene Terephthalate):** Excellent **clarity, gloss, tensile strength**, and *moderate gas barrier* (OTR ≈ **100–150 cm³·m⁻²·day⁻¹**). Used for **carbonated beverage bottles, edible-oil bottles, clamshell fruit punnets, and as the outer layer of retort pouches**. Recyclable as **rPET** (food-grade recycling). *Limitation:* not heat-sealable by itself; needs a coextruded sealing layer.

**(iv) PVC (Polyvinyl Chloride):** Excellent **clarity, good cling, and moderate gas barrier**. Used as **stretch cling film** for fresh produce trays and as **blister packs** for tomato punnets. *PVDC-coated PVC* is the standard **high-barrier film** for **MAP of cheese and minimally processed fruits**. *Limitation:* chlorine content raises environmental and incineration concerns; restricted in several countries for fresh produce contact.

---

#### Model Solution — Q.B (b)

**(i) Tetra Pak / Aseptic Cartons (2 marks):**
Multilayer structure: **Paperboard (75 %) / LDPE / Aluminium foil (6 %) / LDPE (inner)**. The paperboard provides stiffness and printability; LDPE layers provide moisture sealing and food contact; aluminium foil provides **absolute light, oxygen, and microbial barrier**. Aseptic processing (UHT + sterile packaging) gives **6–12 months** ambient shelf life for **fruit juices, coconut water, and tomato ketchup**. Advantages: lightweight, no refrigeration required, brick-shape efficient for transport. Limitations: **non-recyclable in most Indian municipal systems** without specialised plants; consumer perception of "plastic-lined paper".

**(ii) Edible Films and Coatings (2.5 marks):**
Thin layers (typically 0.05–0.5 mm) of **biopolymers (chitosan, alginate, starch, pullulan, gelatin)** or **waxes (carnauba, beeswax, paraffin)** applied directly on the fruit surface. Functions: create a *modified internal atmosphere* (suppress transpiration and respiration), carry *antimicrobials* (e.g., chitosan itself has antifungal action), and improve *gloss and appearance* (waxed apples, cucumbers). Examples: **wax-coated apples and citrus**, **chitosan-coated strawberries** (extending shelf life by 4–6 days), **pullulan-coated fresh-cut mango**. Limitation: highly sensitive to high RH; not a complete barrier.

**(iii) Modified Atmosphere Packaging — MAP (2.5 marks):**
MAP replaces the in-package atmosphere with a *designed gas mix* — typically **3–5 % O₂, 3–10 % CO₂, balance N₂** for respiring produce — and relies on the *selective permeability* of the film to *maintain* that mix. For **fresh-cut lettuce, strawberries, broccoli**, the film is **perforated PP / PE** with high OTR. For **low-respiration produce like apples**, a *low-permeability* PE / PVC film is used. MAP extends shelf life by **2× to 4×** compared to air storage. *Cap (Controlled Atmosphere)* is the warehouse-scale version used during long-term apple storage (O₂ ≈ **1 %, CO₂ ≈ **2 %**). Limitations: temperature-fluctuation sensitivity, risk of anaerobic respiration if O₂ drops below tolerance.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students typically lose marks on this topic:**
> 1. **Units and Symbols:** Writing WVTR as "g/day" instead of "g·m⁻²·day⁻¹" → lose **1 mark** instantly.
> 2. **Confusing LDPE/HDPE:** LDPE is *flexible film*; HDPE is *rigid crate/bottle*. Examiners will deduct marks if you swap these.
> 3. **Tinplate ≠ Tin:** Tinplate is *steel sheet coated with a thin layer of tin* (≈ 0.5–2 µm). It is not "made of tin". Writing "tin can" is acceptable in lay terms but is technically *tinplate*.
> 4. **MAP vs. CAP confusion:** MAP is *package-level*, CAP is *warehouse-level*. Mixing them up loses **2 marks** in long answers.
> 5. **Forgetting FSSAI/PWM compliance:** If asked about *modern* packaging, the answer must mention the **Plastic Waste Management Rules 2016 / 2022** and **EPR obligations** to score full marks.
> 6. **Calculation blunders in shelf-life equation:** Sign error between M₀ and M_c → negative shelf life → lose all marks. Always ensure M₀ > M_c.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Packaging hierarchy:** Primary → Secondary → Tertiary (think: phone case → Amazon box → shipping container).
- **Three most-tested materials:** Glass, tinplate, aluminium for *processed*; HDPE, LDPE, PP for *raw*.
- **Barrier metrics to memorise:** WVTR, OTR, CO₂TR — units and one typical value for LDPE vs. EVOH vs. glass.
- **Key equation:** $J = \dfrac{P \cdot (P_1 - P_2)}{L}$ — Fick's First Law applied to packaging film.
- **Shelf-life equation:** $\theta_s = \dfrac{W_s \cdot (M_0 - M_c)}{N \cdot A}$ — used for moisture-driven spoilage.
- **MAP target atmospheres:** O₂ 1–5 %, CO₂ 3–10 %, balance N₂ for fresh produce.
- **Tetra Pak structure:** Paperboard / LDPE / Al foil / LDPE.
- **Retort pouch structure:** PET / Al foil / CPP.
- **Bio-based alternatives:** PLA, PHB, chitosan, alginate, moulded pulp, bagasse, mycelium.
- **Regulatory framework:** FSSAI (food safety), Plastic Waste Management Rules 2016/2022 (EPR), ISPM-15 (wood packaging for exports).
- **High-barrier polymers to remember:** EVOH, PVDC, aluminium foil — in order of OTR performance.
- **Memory trick for WVTR ranking (high to low):** *PE > PP > PET > PVC > PVDC > EVOH > Al foil > Glass* (remember: **"P³ Pet Pvc Pdc Evoh AG"**).
- **Always** state **units** explicitly; **always** cite a **specific horticultural product** when discussing a material; **always** mention **sustainability/EPR** in a modern exam answer.
- **Comparison table for processed packaging:** Glass, tinplate, aluminium — memorise the full 10-row matrix.
- **MAP vs. CAP:** MAP = package; CAP = cold storage room; same principle, different scale.

> **Final KTU Mantra for Module 3:**
> *"A KTU answer on packaging must name the material, give one barrier property with units, cite one F&V product, and end with one sustainability or regulatory line. Four bullets = full marks."*

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
