topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAGT742 - POST HARVEST ENGINEERING OF HORTICULTURAL CROPS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Bulk and Retail Packages and Packaging Machines

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Packaging** in the context of post-harvest engineering of horticultural crops is defined as the science, art, and technology of enclosing or protecting horticultural produce (fruits, vegetables, flowers, and processed products) in suitable containers to preserve quality, extend shelf life, facilitate storage and transport, enhance marketability, and provide essential information to consumers. The **Codex Alimentarius Commission** and **Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS)** classify packaging into functional tiers, with **bulk packaging** designed for the safe transport and storage of large quantities of produce, and **retail packaging** intended for direct consumer purchase at point-of-sale.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Highlight:** Packaging is **not merely a container**; it is an integrated **engineered system** that interacts with the biological, chemical, and physical environment of the produce throughout the supply chain.

### Classification of Packaging

| Parameter | Bulk Packaging | Retail Packaging |
|-----------|---------------|------------------|
| **Quantity Handled** | 5 kg to 1000+ kg | 50 g to 5 kg |
| **Primary User** | Producer, Wholesaler, Exporter | End Consumer |
| **Design Priority** | Mechanical strength, stackability | Aesthetics, convenience, information |
| **Material Thickness** | Higher (rigid) | Lower (flexible often) |
| **Cost per Unit Produce** | Lower | Higher |
| **Examples** | Wooden crates, corrugated fiberboard boxes, jute bags, bins | Polyethylene pouches, clamshells, punnets, tetra packs |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Suitcase" Principle:**
> Imagine you are sending a fragile glass ornament to a friend across the country.
> - **Bulk packaging** is like the **shipping company's wooden crate** — its job is to survive trucks, forklifts, and warehouses, protecting hundreds of ornaments together. It does not need to look pretty.
> - **Retail packaging** is like the **gift box inside the crate** that the friend opens at home — it is small, attractive, tells you what is inside, and keeps the ornament fresh.
>
> In horticulture, apples in a corrugated fiberboard carton for transport = **bulk**. The same apples in a 1 kg branded shrink-wrapped tray in a supermarket = **retail**.

## 1.3 Key Functions of Packaging (The 5 P's + 1)

A horticultural package must simultaneously perform the following six functions, often referred to as the **"Six Functions Model"**:

1. **Protection** — from mechanical damage, dust, microorganisms, light, and pests.
2. **Preservation** — control of respiration, transpiration, and ethylene accumulation.
3. **Presentation** — visual appeal, branding, label information.
4. **Portioning** — standardized net weight for trade and consumer convenience.
5. **Provision of Information** — ingredients, nutrition, FSSAI/MRL marks, traceability codes.
6. **Permeability Management** — controlled exchange of O₂, CO₂, and water vapor (critical for MAP).

## 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Standard atmospheric pressure:** $P_{atm} = 101.325 \text{ kPa}$
> - **Standard temperature for permeability tests:** $T = 23 \pm 1\,°\text{C}$ (ASTM D3985)
> - **Relative humidity for WVTR tests:** $\text{RH} = 50 \pm 2\%$
> - **Target oxygen level inside MAP of fresh produce:** $O_2 = 2\text{–}5\%$
> - **Target carbon dioxide level inside MAP of fresh produce:** $CO_2 = 3\text{–}10\%$
> - **Respiration rate of apples at 0 °C:** $R_{O_2} \approx 3\text{–}6 \text{ mL O}_2 \cdot \text{kg}^{-1} \cdot \text{h}^{-1}$
> - **Permeability unit (SI):** $\text{mol} \cdot \text{m} \cdot \text{m}^{-2} \cdot \text{s}^{-1} \cdot \text{Pa}^{-1}$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Bulk Packaging — Engineering Analysis

Bulk packaging in horticulture is engineered around three load scenarios:

1. **Static compression load** — from stacking in warehouses.
2. **Dynamic (impact/vibration) load** — during road/rail transport.
3. **Climatic load** — humidity and temperature variations.

### 2.1.1 McKee Formula for Box Compression Strength (BCT)

The most important equation in bulk packaging design for horticultural crates and corrugated boxes is the **McKee, Gander & Wachuta (1963) formula**:

$$BCT = k \cdot ECT \cdot \sqrt{h \cdot Z}$$

Where:
- $BCT$ = Box Compression Test strength, in **N** or **kgf**
- $k$ = Constant (typically $k = 5.87$ for ECT in kN/m, or $k = 0.0284$ for ECT in lb/in)
- $ECT$ = Edge Crush Test value of the corrugated board (kN/m)
- $h$ = Thickness of the corrugated board (mm)
- $Z$ = Box perimeter (mm)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters:** KTU frequently asks students to determine the safe stacking height of horticultural cartons using the BCT equation. Bulk packaging must withstand $BCT \geq 5\times$ the dynamic load factor.

### 2.1.2 Static Load Equation for Stacked Crates

$$P_{stack} = \frac{BCT}{n \cdot SF}$$

Where:
- $P_{stack}$ = Allowable load per box
- $n$ = Number of boxes stacked
- $SF$ = Safety factor (typically **5 to 8** for horticultural produce)

## 2.2 Retail Packaging — Engineering Analysis

Retail packaging is dominated by **flexible films**, **trays**, and **clamshells**. The governing physics is **mass transfer through polymer films**.

### 2.2.1 Fick's First Law of Diffusion (Steady-State Permeation)

The molar flux of a gas through a packaging film is given by:

$$J = \frac{P \cdot (p_1 - p_2)}{L}$$

Where:
- $J$ = Permeation flux (mol·m⁻²·s⁻¹)
- $P$ = Permeability coefficient of the film (mol·m·m⁻²·s⁻¹·Pa⁻¹)
- $p_1$ = Partial pressure of the gas on the high-concentration side (Pa)
- $p_2$ = Partial pressure on the low-concentration side (Pa)
- $L$ = Film thickness (m)

### 2.2.2 Water Vapor Transmission Rate (WVTR)

For moisture-sensitive produce, the shelf life of the package is governed by:

$$WVTR = \frac{\dot{m}}{A} = \frac{P_{H_2O} \cdot \Delta p_{H_2O}}{L}$$

Where $\dot{m}$ is the mass flow rate of water vapor and $A$ is the film area.

### 2.2.3 Shelf-Life Equation for Desiccation-Limited Produce

$$t_s = \frac{m_0 - m_c}{WVTR \cdot A}$$

Where:
- $t_s$ = Shelf life (seconds/days)
- $m_0$ = Initial mass of produce (g)
- $m_c$ = Critical mass at wilting/shrivelling threshold (typically 5% weight loss for most fruits)
- $WVTR$ = Water vapor transmission rate (g·m⁻²·day⁻¹)
- $A$ = Effective film area (m²)

### 2.2.4 Steady-State Modified Atmosphere Packaging (MAP) — Mass Balance

The steady-state O₂ concentration inside a MAP package is found by equating produce respiration with film permeation:

$$R_{O_2} \cdot W = \frac{P_{O_2} \cdot A \cdot (0.21 - y_{O_2}^{ss})}{L}$$

Solving for the steady-state oxygen mole fraction:

$$y_{O_2}^{ss} = 0.21 - \frac{R_{O_2} \cdot W \cdot L}{P_{O_2} \cdot A}$$

Where:
- $R_{O_2}$ = Respiration rate of produce (mol O₂·kg⁻¹·s⁻¹)
- $W$ = Mass of produce inside package (kg)
- $P_{O_2}$ = O₂ permeability of film (mol·m·m⁻²·s⁻¹·Pa⁻¹)
- $y_{O_2}^{ss}$ = Steady-state O₂ mole fraction inside package

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** To create an effective MAP, the designer must match the **respiration rate of the produce** with the **permeability of the chosen film**. If the film is too impermeable → anaerobic fermentation. If too permeable → no MAP effect.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Symbol Legend | Unit | Application |
|---|----------|---------------|------|-------------|
| 1 | $BCT = k \cdot ECT \cdot \sqrt{h \cdot Z}$ | $BCT$: Box Compression, $ECT$: Edge Crush, $h$: thickness, $Z$: perimeter | N | Corrugated bulk carton design |
| 2 | $P_{stack} = BCT / (n \cdot SF)$ | $P_{stack}$: allowable load, $n$: number of boxes, $SF$: safety factor | N | Safe stacking height |
| 3 | $J = P \cdot (p_1 - p_2)/L$ | $J$: flux, $P$: permeability, $L$: thickness | mol·m⁻²·s⁻¹ | Gas permeation through film |
| 4 | $WVTR = P_{H_2O} \cdot \Delta p_{H_2O} / L$ | $WVTR$: water vapor transmission | g·m⁻²·day⁻¹ | Moisture loss calculation |
| 5 | $t_s = (m_0 - m_c)/(WVTR \cdot A)$ | $t_s$: shelf life, $m_0, m_c$: mass | days | Shelf-life prediction |
| 6 | $y_{O_2}^{ss} = 0.21 - (R_{O_2} W L)/(P_{O_2} A)$ | $R_{O_2}$: respiration rate | mol fraction | MAP design |
| 7 | $Q = R \cdot T$ (Ideal Gas) | $Q$: heat, $R$: gas constant, $T$: temperature | J | Headspace gas analysis |
| 8 | $\eta = \frac{\dot{m}_{in} - \dot{m}_{out}}{\dot{m}_{in}}$ | $\eta$: package efficiency | dimensionless | Packaging line performance |
| 9 | $C_{cost} = \frac{C_{mat} + C_{mach} + C_{labor}}{N_{units}}$ | $C_{cost}$: per-unit cost | ₹/unit | Cost-benefit analysis |
| 10 | $\sigma_{max} = \frac{F}{A} \leq \sigma_{allow}$ | $\sigma$: stress, $F$: force | MPa | Structural integrity check |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Exam Pitfall:** Students often forget to convert units. The permeability coefficient $P$ is **pressure-dependent** — always use **Pa** for partial pressure, **not atm** or **bar**, unless the coefficient is given in those units.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Export Industry:** Bulk packaging of mangoes (India → Gulf) uses CFB boxes with BCT ≥ 250 kgf to survive 30-day shipping.
- **Supermarket Retail:** Pre-cut salads use **anti-fog BOPP film** with $WVTR \approx 12 \text{ g/m}^2/\text{day}$ to prevent condensation.
- **Cold Chain:** Vacuum-precooled capsicum is packed in **perforated LDPE liners** to balance transpiration.
- **Pharma-Horticulture:** Probiotic edible coatings on fresh-cut papaya are an emerging KTU research area.

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Shelf Life from Water Loss

**Problem Statement:** A batch of grapes (initial mass $m_0 = 1.20$ kg) is packed in a polyethylene pouch of effective film area $A = 0.05$ m² and thickness $L = 50 \text{ μm}$. The film has water vapor permeability $P_{H_2O} = 6.5 \times 10^{-13} \text{ mol·m·m}^{-2}\text{·s}^{-1}\text{·Pa}^{-1}$. The relative humidity inside the package is 95% and outside is 40% at 25 °C. Critical mass loss $m_c$ for grapes is 5% of initial mass. Calculate the shelf life in days.

**Step 1: Compute the water vapor partial pressure difference.**

The saturation vapor pressure of water at 25 °C is $p_{sat} = 3.17 \text{ kPa} = 3170 \text{ Pa}$.

$$p_{H_2O}^{in} = 0.95 \times 3170 = 3011.5 \text{ Pa}$$

$$p_{H_2O}^{out} = 0.40 \times 3170 = 1268.0 \text{ Pa}$$

$$\Delta p_{H_2O} = 3011.5 - 1268.0 = 1743.5 \text{ Pa}$$

**Step 2: Compute the molar flux of water vapor through the film.**

Using $L = 50 \text{ μm} = 50 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}$:

$$J = \frac{(6.5 \times 10^{-13}) \times 1743.5}{50 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
J &= \frac{1.1333 \times 10^{-9}}{5.0 \times 10^{-5}} \\
J &= 2.267 \times 10^{-5} \text{ mol·m}^{-2}\text{·s}^{-1}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Convert molar flux to mass flux (WVTR).**

The molar mass of water is $M_{H_2O} = 18.015 \text{ g/mol}$:

$$\begin{aligned}
WVTR &= J \times M_{H_2O} \\
&= 2.267 \times 10^{-5} \times 18.015 \\
&= 4.084 \times 10^{-4} \text{ g·m}^{-2}\text{·s}^{-1}
\end{aligned}$$

Convert to g·m⁻²·day⁻¹ (1 day = 86,400 s):

$$\begin{aligned}
WVTR &= 4.084 \times 10^{-4} \times 86400 \\
&= 35.29 \text{ g·m}^{-2}\text{·day}^{-1}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4: Determine the allowable mass loss.**

$$m_c = 0.05 \times 1200 = 60 \text{ g}$$

$$\Delta m_{allow} = m_0 - m_c = 1200 - 60 = 1140 \text{ g}$$

**Step 5: Apply the shelf life equation.**

$$t_s = \frac{m_0 - m_c}{WVTR \cdot A} = \frac{1140}{35.29 \times 0.05}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
t_s &= \frac{1140}{1.7645} \\
t_s &= 646.1 \text{ days}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** The theoretical shelf life of 646 days is much longer than the actual biological shelf life of grapes (~14 days at 0 °C). This indicates that water loss is **not** the limiting factor — the **biological decay (microbial + senescence)** is the actual limiter. The McKee-type analysis gives the *packaging-limited* shelf life.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Steady-State O₂ Level in a MAP Pouch

**Problem Statement:** 2 kg of strawberries ($R_{O_2} = 12 \text{ mL·kg}^{-1}\text{·h}^{-1}$ at 5 °C) are packed in a pouch with $A = 0.04$ m², $L = 40 \text{ μm}$, and $P_{O_2} = 5.0 \times 10^{-15} \text{ mol·m·m}^{-2}\text{·s}^{-1}\text{·Pa}^{-1}$. Find the steady-state O₂ mole fraction inside the package. (Strawberry spoilage threshold O₂ ≈ 2%).

**Step 1: Convert respiration rate to SI units.**

$12 \text{ mL/kg/h}$ of O₂ at STP (1 mol = 22.4 L = 22,400 mL):

$$R_{O_2} = \frac{12}{22400} \times \frac{1}{3600} = 1.488 \times 10^{-7} \text{ mol·kg}^{-1}\text{·s}^{-1}$$

**Step 2: Substitute into the steady-state MAP mass balance.**

$$y_{O_2}^{ss} = 0.21 - \frac{R_{O_2} \cdot W \cdot L}{P_{O_2} \cdot A}$$

Compute numerator:

$$R_{O_2} \cdot W \cdot L = 1.488 \times 10^{-7} \times 2 \times (40 \times 10^{-6})$$

$$= 1.488 \times 10^{-7} \times 80 \times 10^{-6} = 1.190 \times 10^{-11} \text{ mol·m}^{-1}\text{·s}^{-1}$$

Compute denominator:

$$P_{O_2} \cdot A = 5.0 \times 10^{-15} \times 0.04 = 2.0 \times 10^{-16} \text{ mol·m}^{-1}\text{·s}^{-1}\text{·Pa}^{-1}$$

Ratio:

$$\frac{1.190 \times 10^{-11}}{2.0 \times 10^{-16}} = 5.95 \times 10^{4} \text{ Pa}$$

**Step 3: Convert pressure ratio to mole fraction.**

Using ideal gas, $P_{atm} = 101325 \text{ Pa}$:

$$\frac{5.95 \times 10^{4}}{101325} = 0.587$$

This is impossible (cannot be > 0.21). Conclusion: **The chosen film is too impermeable** — it will create anaerobic conditions.

**Step 4: Select a micro-perforated film.**

For strawberries, the recommended $P_{O_2} \cdot A / L$ ratio should give $y_{O_2}^{ss} \approx 0.05$:

$$y_{O_2}^{ss} = 0.21 - 0.05 = 0.16$$

$$\frac{R_{O_2} \cdot W \cdot L}{P_{O_2} \cdot A} = 0.16 \times 101325 = 16212 \text{ Pa}$$

Required $P_{O_2} \cdot A / L$:

$$\frac{P_{O_2} \cdot A}{L} = \frac{1.190 \times 10^{-11}}{16212} = 7.34 \times 10^{-16} \text{ mol·m}^{-1}\text{·s}^{-1}\text{·Pa}^{-1}$$

**Conclusion:** Micro-perforations (1–2 holes of 100 μm diameter) are needed to achieve the right O₂ transmission.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Packaging Selection Decision Chart

| Produce Type | Recommended Bulk Package | Recommended Retail Package | Critical Film Property | Target Shelf Life |
|--------------|--------------------------|----------------------------|------------------------|-------------------|
| Apples | CFB box with PE liner | Clamshell + flow-wrap | Low $WVTR$ | 4–6 months (cold) |
| Mangoes | Wooden crate (5 kg) | CFB tray + mesh shrink film | Medium OTR, anti-fungal liner | 2–3 weeks |
| Tomatoes | Telescopic CFB | Truss-wrap with macro-perf | High O₂ permeation | 7–14 days |
| Leafy greens | Corrugated plastic crate | Anti-fog BOPP flow-pack | $WVTR > 12 \text{ g/m}^2/\text{day}$ | 7–10 days |
| Cut flowers | Corrugated sleeves with water tube | Cellophane wrap + carton | High humidity retention | 5–7 days |
| Mushrooms | Perforated plastic crate | Tray + PE film with micro-perf | Very high OTR | 4–6 days |
| Fresh-cut papaya | Rigid PET clamshell | Multilayer PA/PE vacuum pouch | Low OTR + active scavenger | 7–10 days |

## 3.4 KTU Problem-Solving Walkthrough — McKee BCT

**Problem:** A corrugated fiberboard carton has ECT = 7.5 kN/m, board thickness $h = 4 \text{ mm}$, and perimeter $Z = 2000 \text{ mm}$. Use $k = 5.87$. Each carton is to be stacked 6 high. Find the maximum allowable load per box using SF = 6.

**Step 1: Compute BCT.**

$$BCT = 5.87 \times 7.5 \times \sqrt{4 \times 2000}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\sqrt{8000} &= 89.44 \\
BCT &= 5.87 \times 7.5 \times 89.44 \\
BCT &= 3937.5 \text{ N} \approx 401.5 \text{ kgf}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2: Apply stacking safety factor.**

$$P_{stack} = \frac{3937.5}{6 \times 6} = 109.4 \text{ N} \approx 11.15 \text{ kgf per box}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Each box can hold at most 11 kg of produce when stacked 6 high. This is why tomatoes are typically packed in 5 kg CFB boxes — leaves a 6 kg safety margin.

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — 3 Mark Questions

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question]** Define **bulk packaging** and **retail packaging**. Give two examples of each used in horticultural produce handling.

**Model Answer:**

**Bulk packaging** refers to packaging designed to hold large quantities (5 kg to over 1000 kg) of horticultural produce for storage, transport, and wholesale distribution. Its primary objective is to protect the produce from mechanical damage during handling and to optimize logistics efficiency.

*Examples:* (i) Wooden crates used for mango and apple transport, (ii) Corrugated Fiberboard (CFB) telescopic boxes used for tomato and grape export.

**Retail packaging** refers to packaging designed for direct consumer purchase at the point of sale, typically containing 50 g to 5 kg of produce. Its primary objectives are consumer convenience, branding, information display, and product protection at the household level.

*Examples:* (i) PET clamshells for strawberries, (ii) Anti-fog BOPP flow-wrap for spinach.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question]** What is the **McKee formula**? Mention its significance in corrugated fiberboard box design.

**Model Answer:**

The McKee formula relates the **Box Compression Test (BCT)** strength of a corrugated fiberboard carton to the **Edge Crush Test (ECT)** of the board, the **thickness (h)**, and the **box perimeter (Z)**:

$$BCT = k \cdot ECT \cdot \sqrt{h \cdot Z}$$

**Significance:**
- Predicts the maximum compressive load a carton can withstand before buckling.
- Enables designers to optimize board grade selection (single wall, double wall) for a given stacking height.
- Reduces trial-and-error in packaging line design, saving material cost and ensuring safe transport of horticultural produce.

---

## Part B — 14 Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### Question A

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question | Module 3 | 14 Marks]**

**(a)** Explain the **principle of Modified Atmosphere Packaging (MAP)** for fresh horticultural produce. Derive the steady-state oxygen mole fraction equation. **\[7 Marks\]**

**(b)** A batch of litchi (1.5 kg) is to be packed in a polypropylene pouch of area 0.035 m² and thickness 60 μm. The O₂ permeability is $4.0 \times 10^{-15} \text{ mol·m·m}^{-2}\text{·s}^{-1}\text{·Pa}^{-1}$ and the respiration rate of litchi at 5 °C is $8 \text{ mL O}_2 \cdot \text{kg}^{-1}\text{·h}^{-1}$. Calculate the steady-state O₂ mole fraction inside the package. Comment on whether the chosen film is suitable. **\[7 Marks\]**

#### Model Solution to (a)

**Principle of MAP:**
Modified Atmosphere Packaging works on the principle of **passive equilibration** between the **respiration of the produce** (which consumes O₂ and releases CO₂) and the **permeation of gases through the packaging film**. After sealing, the O₂ level inside the package drops and CO₂ rises until a **steady-state** is reached where the rate of O₂ consumed by respiration equals the rate of O₂ entering through the film. This altered atmosphere (typically 2–5% O₂ and 3–10% CO₂) slows down respiration, delays senescence, inhibits microbial growth, and extends shelf life.

**Derivation:**

The respiration rate of the produce is $R_{O_2}$ in mol O₂ per kg per second. For a package containing mass $W$ kg, the rate of O₂ consumption is:

$$\dot{n}_{resp} = R_{O_2} \cdot W$$

The rate of O₂ permeating into the package through the film of area $A$, thickness $L$, and permeability $P_{O_2}$ is given by Fick's law:

$$\dot{n}_{perm} = \frac{P_{O_2} \cdot A \cdot (p_{O_2}^{out} - p_{O_2}^{in})}{L}$$

At steady state, $\dot{n}_{resp} = \dot{n}_{perm}$:

$$R_{O_2} \cdot W = \frac{P_{O_2} \cdot A \cdot (p_{O_2}^{out} - p_{O_2}^{in})}{L}$$

Using ideal gas law $p_{O_2} = y_{O_2} \cdot P_{atm}$:

$$R_{O_2} \cdot W = \frac{P_{O_2} \cdot A \cdot P_{atm} \cdot (0.21 - y_{O_2}^{ss})}{L}$$

Solving for $y_{O_2}^{ss}$:

$$y_{O_2}^{ss} = 0.21 - \frac{R_{O_2} \cdot W \cdot L}{P_{O_2} \cdot A \cdot P_{atm}}$$

#### Model Solution to (b)

**Step 1: Convert respiration rate to SI units.**

$$R_{O_2} = \frac{8}{22400 \times 3600} = 9.92 \times 10^{-8} \text{ mol·kg}^{-1}\text{·s}^{-1}$$

**Step 2: Substitute into the steady-state equation.**

With $L = 60 \text{ μm} = 6.0 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}$, $W = 1.5$ kg, $A = 0.035$ m², $P_{atm} = 101325$ Pa:

$$y_{O_2}^{ss} = 0.21 - \frac{(9.92 \times 10^{-8})(1.5)(6.0 \times 10^{-5})}{(4.0 \times 10^{-15})(0.035)(101325)}$$

Compute numerator:

$$9.92 \times 10^{-8} \times 1.5 \times 6.0 \times 10^{-5} = 8.928 \times 10^{-12}$$

Compute denominator:

$$4.0 \times 10^{-15} \times 0.035 \times 101325 = 1.4186 \times 10^{-11}$$

Ratio:

$$\frac{8.928 \times 10^{-12}}{1.4186 \times 10^{-11}} = 0.6293$$

So:

$$y_{O_2}^{ss} = 0.21 - 0.6293 = -0.4193$$

**Step 3: Interpretation.**

A negative $y_{O_2}^{ss}$ is physically impossible. This means the chosen polypropylene film is **far too impermeable** for the litchi respiration rate. The package will become **anaerobic**, leading to fermentation, off-flavors, and spoilage. The designer must either (i) use a micro-perforated film, (ii) reduce the package size, or (iii) add oxygen-scavenging sachets to balance the system.

---

### Question B (Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question | Module 3 | 14 Marks]**

**(a)** Discuss the **different types of packaging machines** used in horticultural processing industries. Classify them based on the degree of automation and product form. **\[7 Marks\]**

**(b)** With a neat block diagram, describe the working of a **Form-Fill-Seal (FFS) machine** for fresh-cut vegetable packaging. Mention the typical throughput and packaging materials used. **\[7 Marks\]**

#### Model Solution to (a)

**Classification of Packaging Machines for Horticultural Produce:**

**1. Based on Degree of Automation:**

- **Manual/Semi-automatic machines:** Require operator intervention for film loading, product filling, and sealing. Examples: Hand-operated heat sealers for small retail pouches of dry fruits, manual tray sealers.
- **Fully automatic machines:** Perform all operations (forming, filling, sealing, labeling, coding) without manual intervention. Examples: Vertical Form-Fill-Seal (VFFS) for frozen peas, Horizontal Form-Fill-Seal (HFFS) for cut salad.

**2. Based on Product Form:**

- **For solid produce (whole fruits, vegetables):** Crate erectors, bin fillers, net-bag filling machines, flow-wrap machines (e.g., apple tray wrapping).
- **For cut/minimally processed produce:** HFFS with MAP gas flushing (e.g., pre-cut lettuce), vacuum skin packaging (e.g., mango cubes).
- **For liquid/puree produce (juices, sauces):** Aseptic fill machines, tetra-pak fillers, bottle rinser-capper-filler monoblocks.
- **For dry produce (powders, spices, dried fruits):** VFFS with multi-head weighers, pouch packing machines.

**3. Special-Purpose Machines:**

- **Vacuum packaging machines** for dehydrated products.
- **Modified Atmosphere Packaging (MAP) machines** with gas flushing and leak testing.
- **Shrink-wrapping machines** for tray packs.
- **Stretch-wrapping machines** for palletized bulk crates.

#### Model Solution to (b)

**Working of Form-Fill-Seal (FFS) Machine for Fresh-Cut Vegetables:**

**Block Diagram:**

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Film Reel Unwinding] --> B[Film Forming Collar]
    B --> C[Vertical Sealing Jaws]
    C --> D[Product Dosing Belt]
    D --> E[Gas Flushing N2 CO2 for MAP]
    E --> F[Top Sealing Jaws]
    F --> G[Cooling and Cutting Station]
    G --> H[Discharge Conveyor]
    H --> I[Weight Check]
    I --> J[Cartoning]
```

**Working Principle:**

1. A roll of flexible packaging film (typically LDPE/PA/PE multilayer) is unwound and passed through a **forming collar** that shapes it into a tube.
2. The **vertical sealing jaws** seal the back seam of the pouch, creating a continuous tube.
3. The **dosing system** (multi-head weigher or auger filler) drops a metered quantity of fresh-cut vegetables into the open pouch.
4. **Gas flushing** is performed by injecting a precise mixture of N₂ and CO₂ (typically 70:30 or 80:20) to displace O₂ and create MAP conditions.
5. The **top sealing jaws** seal the upper end of the pouch.
6. The **cutting station** separates individual pouches, which are discharged via conveyor.
7. A **check-weigher** verifies net weight, and rejected pouches are sent to a recirculation line.

**Typical Throughput:** 30–120 pouches per minute depending on pouch size and product type.

**Packaging Materials Used:**
- Multilayer co-extruded films: **LDPE/PA/EVOH/PE** for high O₂ barrier.
- Anti-fog BOPP laminate for visibility of fresh-cut salads.
- Micro-perforated PP/PE films for high-respiration produce like mushrooms and strawberries.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Unit conversion errors** in permeability and respiration rate calculations (most common).
> 2. **Forgetting the safety factor** in BCT problems — KTU explicitly tests this.
> 3. **Confusing WVTR with OTR** — they are different quantities with different units.
> 4. **Missing the Fick's Law statement** in MAP derivations — examiners deduct 1–2 marks if you skip the law statement.
> 5. **Not commenting on the physical feasibility** of negative $y_{O_2}^{ss}$ — students must say "this is impossible, hence the film is unsuitable."
> 6. **Writing block diagrams without labeled arrows** — KTU mandates that each arrow carry a function label.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Bulk vs Retail:** Bulk = transport/wholesale (CFB boxes, crates, bins, 5–1000+ kg). Retail = consumer point-of-sale (pouches, clamshells, MAP, 50 g–5 kg).
- **Six Functions of Packaging:** Protection, Preservation, Presentation, Portioning, Provision of Information, Permeability Management.
- **McKee Formula:** $BCT = k \cdot ECT \cdot \sqrt{h \cdot Z}$ — predicts box compression strength from board properties.
- **BCT Stacking Equation:** $P_{stack} = BCT / (n \cdot SF)$ — always include safety factor $SF = 5\text{–}8$.
- **Fick's First Law:** $J = P \cdot (p_1 - p_2) / L$ — the basis of all film permeation calculations.
- **WVTR Equation:** $WVTR = P_{H_2O} \cdot \Delta p_{H_2O} / L$ — units: g·m⁻²·day⁻¹.
- **Shelf Life Equation:** $t_s = (m_0 - m_c) / (WVTR \cdot A)$ — but note that **biological decay is often the true limiter**, not water loss.
- **MAP Steady-State O₂:** $y_{O_2}^{ss} = 0.21 - (R_{O_2} \cdot W \cdot L) / (P_{O_2} \cdot A \cdot P_{atm})$.
- **MAP Target Atmospheres:** O₂ = 2–5%, CO₂ = 3–10% for most fruits/vegetables.
- **Micro-perforation:** Used for high-respiration produce (berries, mushrooms) to avoid anaerobic conditions.
- **Active Packaging Components:** Ethylene scavengers (KMnO₄), O₂ scavengers (iron-based), time-temperature indicators.
- **Packaging Machines:** Classified as manual, semi-automatic, automatic; and as VFFS, HFFS, MAP, vacuum, shrink-wrap, stretch-wrap.
- **Material Selection Criteria:** Produce respiration rate, ethylene sensitivity, moisture sensitivity, supply chain length, target shelf life.
- **Common Exam Materials:** CFB (corrugated fiberboard), LDPE/HDPE, PP, PET, PA (nylon), EVOH (barrier), BOPP (anti-fog).
- **KTU Frequently Tested Topics:** BCT calculation, shelf-life from water loss, MAP steady-state derivation, packaging machine classification.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Handling and Transportation of Fruits and Vegetables
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAGT742 - POST HARVEST ENGINEERING OF HORTICULTURAL CROPS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Handling and Transportation of Fruits and Vegetables

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Post-Harvest Handling** refers to the integrated sequence of operations performed on horticultural produce *immediately after harvest* and prior to final consumption, encompassing **pre-cooling, sorting, grading, washing, waxing, packaging, and transportation**, with the primary objective of preserving physiological, biochemical, microbiological, and visual quality while minimizing quantitative and qualitative losses.

**Transportation of Fruits and Vegetables** is the sub-system of post-harvest engineering that physically relocates the harvested produce from the farm gate to the processing unit or consumer, using optimized **cold-chain logistics** that maintain the required **temperature (0–13 °C depending on commodity), relative humidity (85–95 %), and atmospheric composition** throughout the transit period.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** This topic directly maps to **CO3** — *"Apply engineering principles to design post-harvest handling and cold-chain systems for horticultural produce."* Expect application-level questions in the End Semester Examination (ESE).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Fragile Glassware Courier"**
> Imagine fruits and vegetables as **hand-blown glass bottles** — alive, breathing, fragile, and filled with pressurized water. Once picked, they begin "sweating" (respiration), lose water (transpiration), get bruised (mechanical injury), and eventually "explode" (senescence/rot). Your job as a post-harvest engineer is to be the **premium logistics courier** for this glassware: pack them in cushioned boxes, refrigerate the truck, ventilate the cabin, drive gently, and deliver them to the customer intact.

**Key Engineering Reality:** Fruits and vegetables are **living biological systems (O₂ in, CO₂ + heat + ethylene out)**. The moment they are detached from the plant, the clock starts. Every handling step must respect this biology.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

| Parameter | Standard Value | KTU Significance |
|---|---|---|
| Respiration Rate (R) | **5–20 mL CO₂ kg⁻¹ h⁻¹** | Climacteric vs Non-climacteric classification |
| Latent Heat of Respiration | **10.5 kJ per mL O₂ consumed** | Used in heat-load calculations |
| Specific Heat of Produce | **3.35–3.78 kJ kg⁻¹ °C⁻¹** | Pre-cooling load design |
| Water Activity (a_w) | **> 0.95** for most FV | Microbial spoilage threshold |
| Recommended Transit RH | **85–95 %** | Prevents shriveling |
| Acceptable Transit Time | **< 6 hours** for perishables | Cold-chain integrity rule |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Phases of Post-Harvest Handling — Structured Logic

### Phase I — Pre-Harvest to Pre-Cooling (Field Operations)
1. **Harvesting** at optimum maturity (physiological vs commercial).
2. **Field Curing** (e.g., onion, garlic) for skin hardening.
3. **Field Packing** in standardized crates to reduce handling steps.
4. **Rapid Removal of Field Heat** — every 1-hour delay reduces shelf life by ~1 day (Rule of Thumb).

### Phase II — Packing House Operations
1. **Pre-Cooling** to remove 7/8th (87.5 %) of field heat within the first few hours.
2. **Cleaning / Washing** with chlorinated water (**50–100 ppm Cl⁻**).
3. **Sorting & Grading** by size, weight, color, firmness.
4. **Waxing / Surface Coating** to reduce transpiration by **30–50 %**.
5. **Packaging** in vented, cushioned, humidity-controlled containers.

### Phase III — Storage & Transportation
1. **Cold Storage** at commodity-specific temperature.
2. **Transportation** in refrigerated vehicles (reefers) or ventilated open trucks.
3. **Ripening Chambers** for climacteric fruits (banana, mango, tomato).
4. **Distribution & Retailing** maintaining cold-chain integrity.

## 2.2 Pre-Cooling — The 'Why' and 'How'

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Pre-Cool?** Field temperature (30–35 °C) accelerates respiration exponentially. A fruit entering a cold store without pre-cooling acts as an **internal heater**, raising the store's ambient temperature and spoiling surrounding produce.

### Methods of Pre-Ccooling (KTU High-Yield)
- **Room Cooling** — produce placed in cold room; slow (takes 24–48 h).
- **Forced-Air Cooling** — fans push cold air through pallet vents; fast (4–6 h).
- **Hydrocooling** — ice-cold water shower; ideal for stone fruits, asparagus.
- **Vacuum Cooling** — evaporative cooling under vacuum; best for leafy vegetables (lettuce).
- **Package Icing** — crushed ice over the produce; suitable for broccoli, sweet corn.
- **Cryogenic Cooling** — liquid N₂ or CO₂; ultra-fast, used for high-value items.

## 2.3 Packaging — Engineering Function

A packaging system must satisfy the **"5 P's"**: **Protect, Preserve, Present, Portion, Provide Information**.

### KTU-Mandated Packaging Functions
- **Mechanical Protection** against impact, vibration, compression.
- **Environmental Barrier** against moisture, gases, light.
- **Containment & Standardization** for handling automation.
- **Communication** via labels (PLU codes, traceability).
- **Ventilation** — at least **5 % open area** on side walls for produce.

## 2.4 Transportation Modes — Comparative Analysis

| Mode | Capacity | Speed | Cold-Chain Feasibility | Cost | Best For |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Road (Refrigerated Truck / Reefer)** | 5–20 t | Moderate | Excellent | High | Short–medium haul, retail |
| **Rail (Reefer Container)** | 20–40 t | Slow | Good | Moderate | Long-haul, bulk transit |
| **Sea (Reefer Container / CA)** | 20–40 t per TEU | Very Slow | Excellent (CA) | Low | Export, long-distance trade |
| **Air Freight** | < 50 t | Fastest | Excellent (ULD) | Very High | High-value, low-weight produce (mango, strawberry) |
| **Open Truck (Ventilated)** | 5–15 t | Moderate | Poor | Low | Local mandi, short distance |

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Use `\vert` or `\mid` for absolute values in math (not `\|`).** All formulas below are examiner-recommended for ESE.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{1. Respiration Heat Load} \quad & Q_{resp} = R \cdot m \cdot L_{resp} \\
& \text{where } R = \text{respiration rate (mL CO}_2 \text{ kg}^{-1}\text{h}^{-1}\text{)} \\
& m = \text{mass of produce (kg)}, \quad L_{resp} = 10.5\ \text{kJ per mL O}_2
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{2. Half-Cooling Time} \quad & t_{1/2} = \frac{\ln(2)}{k} = \frac{0.693}{k} \\
& \text{where } k = \text{cooling rate constant (h}^{-1}\text{)}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{3. Seven-Eighths Cooling Time (Practical Pre-Cooling End-Point)} \quad & t_{7/8} = 3 \cdot t_{1/2} = \frac{2.079}{k}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{4. Newton s Law of Cooling} \quad & \frac{T(t) - T_{\infty}}{T_i - T_{\infty}} = e^{-k t}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{5. Sensible Heat Load of Produce} \quad & Q_{sensible} = m \cdot C_p \cdot \Delta T \\
& C_p = 3.35 \text{ to } 3.78\ \text{kJ kg}^{-1}\text{K}^{-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{6. Transpiration / Moisture Loss} \quad & ML = m_i - m_f \quad (\%) \\
& \text{Acceptable: } \vert ML \vert \leq 5\% \text{ for most FV}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{7. Ventilation Air Requirement} \quad & V_{air} = \frac{Q_{resp}}{\rho_{air} \cdot C_{p,air} \cdot \Delta T_{allowable}}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{8. Refrigeration Tonnage} \quad & 1\ \text{TR} = 3.517\ \text{kW} = 3024\ \text{kcal h}^{-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 2.6 Engineering Utility & Real-World Application

These equations form the **backbone of cold-chain design** in India — used by **CONCOR (Container Corporation of India), FCI, APEDA, and private reefer fleet operators** for sizing:
- Pre-cooling chamber tonnage
- Reefer container set-point temperature
- Ventilation fan capacity
- Trip-time energy budgeting

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Respiration Heat Load for a 1-Tonne Mango Consignment

> **Given:** Mangoes ($m = 1000\ \text{kg}$), respiration rate $R = 12\ \text{mL CO}_2\ \text{kg}^{-1}\text{h}^{-1}$ at 13 °C, latent heat of respiration $L_{resp} = 10.5\ \text{kJ per mL O}_2$.

**Step 1 — Compute total respiration rate for the lot.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{total} &= R \times m \\
R_{total} &= 12\ \text{mL CO}_2\ \text{kg}^{-1}\text{h}^{-1} \times 1000\ \text{kg} \\
R_{total} &= 12000\ \text{mL CO}_2\ \text{h}^{-1} \\
R_{total} &= 12\ \text{L CO}_2\ \text{h}^{-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert volumetric respiration to energy equivalent.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{resp} &= R_{total} \times L_{resp} \\
Q_{resp} &= 12000\ \text{mL h}^{-1} \times 10.5\ \text{kJ per mL} \\
Q_{resp} &= 126000\ \text{kJ h}^{-1} \\
Q_{resp} &= 35\ \text{kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert kW to Refrigeration Tonnage (TR) for design selection.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{resp,TR} &= \frac{35\ \text{kW}}{3.517\ \text{kW TR}^{-1}} \\
Q_{resp,TR} &\approx 9.95\ \text{TR}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Engineering Interpretation.**
> A 1-tonne mango lot during transit at 13 °C releases **~10 TR of heat continuously**. This means the reefer unit of the truck must remove **at least 10 TR** of respiration heat *plus* conduction/infiltration load. Under-designing the reefer will result in temperature rise and accelerated spoilage.

## 3.2 Derivation: Half-Cooling Time for Forced-Air Cooled Lettuce

> **Given:** Lettuce initially at 25 °C, cooled in air at 1 °C, surface temperature after 1 hour = 13 °C. Find the cooling rate constant $k$ and the seven-eighths cooling time.

**Step 1 — Apply Newton's Law of Cooling.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{T(t) - T_{\infty}}{T_i - T_{\infty}} &= e^{-k t} \\
T(1) &= 13\ ^\circ\text{C}, \quad T_i = 25\ ^\circ\text{C}, \quad T_{\infty} = 1\ ^\circ\text{C}, \quad t = 1\ \text{h}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{13 - 1}{25 - 1} &= e^{-k \cdot 1} \\
\frac{12}{24} &= e^{-k} \\
0.5 &= e^{-k} \\
\ln(0.5) &= -k \\
-0.693 &= -k \\
k &= 0.693\ \text{h}^{-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute half-cooling time.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{1/2} &= \frac{\ln(2)}{k} = \frac{0.693}{0.693} = 1\ \text{h}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute seven-eighths cooling time (pre-cooling design end-point).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{7/8} &= 3 \times t_{1/2} = 3 \times 1\ \text{h} = 3\ \text{h}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Verification via direct equation.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T(3) &= T_{\infty} + (T_i - T_{\infty}) \cdot e^{-k t} \\
T(3) &= 1 + (25 - 1) \cdot e^{-0.693 \times 3} \\
T(3) &= 1 + 24 \cdot e^{-2.079} \\
T(3) &= 1 + 24 \times 0.125 \\
T(3) &= 1 + 3 = 4\ ^\circ\text{C}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Note:** At $t = t_{7/8}$, the produce temperature is $T_{\infty} + \frac{1}{8}(T_i - T_{\infty}) = 4\ ^\circ\text{C}$, confirming the standard engineering definition.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Commodity-Specific Transit Conditions

| Commodity | Transit Temp (°C) | RH (%) | Approx. Shelf Life | Climacteric? | Ethylene Sensitivity |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Mango | 13 | 85–90 | 14–28 d | Yes | High |
| Banana | 13–15 | 85–90 | 7–14 d | Yes | High |
| Apple | 0–4 | 90–95 | 60–180 d | Yes (slow) | High |
| Tomato | 12–15 | 85–90 | 7–14 d | Yes | High |
| Capsicum | 7–10 | 90–95 | 14–21 d | No | Moderate |
| Onion | 0–5 | 65–70 | 90–210 d | No | Low |
| Potato | 4–10 | 90–95 | 60–120 d | No | Low |
| Strawberry | 0–2 | 90–95 | 5–7 d | No | Low |
| Grape | 0–2 | 90–95 | 30–90 d | No | Low |
| Lettuce | 0–2 | 95–100 | 14–21 d | No | High |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flow Diagram — Post-Harvest Handling & Transport Chain

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Field Harvesting at Optimum Maturity] --> B[Field Collection in Crates]
    B --> C[Transport to Packing House]
    C --> D[Pre-Cooling: Hydro / Forced-Air / Vacuum]
    D --> E[Washing with Chlorinated Water 50-100 ppm]
    E --> F[Sorting and Grading]
    F --> G[Waxing and Surface Coating]
    G --> H[Packaging in Vented Corrugated Boxes]
    H --> I[Cold Storage Holding at Commodity-Specific Temp]
    I --> J[Loading into Reefer Container or Truck]
    J --> K[Refrigerated Transportation by Road Rail or Sea]
    K --> L[Distribution Center]
    L --> M[Retail Display Cabinet]
    M --> N[End Consumer]
    I --> O[Ripening Chamber for Climacteric Fruits]
    O --> K
    style A fill:#f9e79f,stroke:#7d6608,stroke-width:2px
    style D fill:#aed6f1,stroke:#1b4f72,stroke-width:2px
    style I fill:#a9dfbf,stroke:#196f3d,stroke-width:2px
    style K fill:#f5b7b1,stroke:#922b21,stroke-width:2px

```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Components of a Refrigerated Transit System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Produce Load 5 to 20 t] --> P2[Sensible Heat m Cp dT]
    P2 --> P3[Respiration Heat Qresp]
    P3 --> P4[Total Cooling Load kW]
    H1[Infiltration Heat from Door Openings] --> P4
    H2[Conduction through Walls] --> P4
    P4 --> R1[Compressor Unit]
    R1 --> R2[Condenser]
    R2 --> R3[Expansion Valve]
    R3 --> R4[Evaporator Coil inside Container]
    R4 --> C1[Internal Air Circulation Fans]

    T1 --> R1
    style P4 fill:#f5b7b1,stroke:#922b21,stroke-width:2px

    style R4 fill:#a9dfbf,stroke:#196f3d,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.3 Decision Tree — Selection of Pre-Cooling Method

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1{Is the produce leafy or thin-skinned?}
    S1 -- Yes --> M1[Vacuum Cooling Recommended]
    S1 -- No --> S2{Is the produce dense with thick skin?}
    S2 -- Yes --> M2[Forced-Air Cooling Recommended]
    S2 -- No --> S3{Does the produce tolerate water contact?}
    S3 -- Yes --> M3[Hydrocooling Recommended]
    S3 -- No --> S4[Room Cooling or Package Icing]
    M1 --> X[Cooling Time 0.5 to 1 h]
    M2 --> Y[Cooling Time 4 to 6 h]
    M3 --> Z[Cooling Time 0.5 to 1 h]
    S4 --> W[Cooling Time 24 to 48 h]
    style S1 fill:#f9e79f,stroke:#7d6608,stroke-width:2px
    style S2 fill:#f9e79f,stroke:#7d6608,stroke-width:2px
    style S3 fill:#f9e79f,stroke:#7d6608,stroke-width:2px
    style M1 fill:#a9dfbf,stroke:#196f3d,stroke-width:2px
    style M2 fill:#aed6f1,stroke:#1b4f72,stroke-width:2px
    style M3 fill:#d7bde2,stroke:#512e5f,stroke-width:2px
    style S4 fill:#f5b7b1,stroke:#922b21,stroke-width:2px
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define pre-cooling. List any four methods of pre-cooling used for horticultural produce. **[3 Marks]** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**
**Pre-cooling** is the rapid removal of field heat from freshly harvested produce before it is stored, transported, or processed. It is one of the most critical operations in the cold chain as it slows down respiration, transpiration, and microbial activity, thereby extending shelf life.

**Four Methods of Pre-Cooling:**

1. **Room Cooling** — Slow cooling by placing produce in a cold room at 0–2 °C.
2. **Forced-Air Cooling** — Cold air is forced through the produce using fans, reducing cooling time drastically.
3. **Hydrocooling** — Produce is drenched or flooded with ice-cold water; very fast and effective for stone fruits.
4. **Vacuum Cooling** — Used for leafy vegetables; the vacuum causes rapid evaporation of surface moisture, removing heat.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often write "cooling" instead of "pre-cooling" and forget to specify the temperature range. Always mention **temperature, time, and produce suitability** for full marks.

---

### Question 2: What is the significance of the 7/8ths cooling time in pre-cooling design? **[3 Marks]** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**
The **seven-eighths (7/8) cooling time** ($t_{7/8}$) is defined as the time required to remove **7/8 = 87.5 %** of the temperature difference between the initial produce temperature and the cooling medium temperature. It is mathematically equal to **three half-cooling times**, i.e., $t_{7/8} = 3 t_{1/2}$.

**Significance:**
1. It is the **practical end-point of pre-cooling** in the industry — complete removal of 100 % temperature difference is thermodynamically impossible in finite time.
2. It serves as the **design standard** for sizing forced-air coolers and hydrocoolers.
3. It ensures produce is **cooled enough** to enter cold storage without raising the ambient room temperature.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1)

**a)** Explain in detail the various methods of pre-cooling used for horticultural produce. Compare their relative advantages and limitations. **[7 Marks]** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**b)** A consignment of 1500 kg of bananas at 28 °C is to be pre-cooled using a forced-air cooler operating at 4 °C. The cooling rate constant is $k = 0.5\ \text{h}^{-1}$. Calculate the half-cooling time, the 7/8th cooling time, and the temperature of the bananas after 5 hours of cooling. **[7 Marks]**

#### Part (a) — Model Answer

**Methods of Pre-Cooling (Detailed):**

**1. Room Cooling:**
Produce is placed in a cold room at the recommended storage temperature. Air circulates naturally around the packages. Cooling is **slow (24–48 h)** and is suitable for low-respiring, low-value produce. Limitation: uneven cooling due to stagnant air.

**2. Forced-Air Cooling:**
Fans create a pressure differential across the produce stack, pulling cold air through the pallet vents. Cooling time is **4–6 h**, much faster than room cooling. Energy-efficient and widely used for apples, stone fruits, and citrus. Limitation: requires properly vented containers.

**3. Hydrocooling:**
Produce is sprayed with or immersed in ice-cold water (0–1 °C). Cooling is **very fast (0.5–1 h)**. The high specific heat of water (4.18 kJ kg⁻¹ K⁻¹) makes it highly effective. Ideal for peaches, mangoes, sweet corn, asparagus. Limitation: not suitable for produce sensitive to water (e.g., strawberries, mushrooms).

**4. Vacuum Cooling:**
Produce is placed in a sealed chamber, and pressure is reduced to ~4.6 mmHg. Water evaporates rapidly, and each 1 % of moisture evaporated removes ~2.4 °C of heat. Cooling time is **15–30 min**. Best for lettuce, spinach, and other leafy vegetables. Limitation: only feasible for high surface-to-volume ratio produce.

**5. Package Icing:**
Crushed or flaked ice is placed on top of the packed produce. Ice melts, and the cold water flows down. Ideal for **broccoli, sweet corn, and leafy greens**.

**6. Cryogenic Cooling:**
Uses **liquid nitrogen (–196 °C)** or **liquid CO₂ (–78 °C)** to flash-cool produce. Used for premium, high-value items. Limitation: very high cost.

**Comparison Table:**

| Method | Time | Cost | Best For | Limitation |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Room | 24–48 h | Low | Onion, Potato | Slow |
| Forced-Air | 4–6 h | Moderate | Apple, Mango | Needs vented packaging |
| Hydro | 0.5–1 h | Moderate | Stone fruits | Water-sensitive produce |
| Vacuum | 15–30 min | High | Leafy vegetables | High surface area needed |
| Icing | Variable | Low | Broccoli, Corn | Melting water handling |

#### Part (b) — Model Solution

**Given:** $m = 1500\ \text{kg}$, $T_i = 28\ ^\circ\text{C}$, $T_{\infty} = 4\ ^\circ\text{C}$, $k = 0.5\ \text{h}^{-1}$.

**Step 1 — Half-cooling time.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{1/2} &= \frac{\ln(2)}{k} = \frac{0.693}{0.5} = 1.386\ \text{h}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Seven-eighths cooling time.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{7/8} &= 3 \times t_{1/2} = 3 \times 1.386 = 4.158\ \text{h}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Temperature after 5 hours.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T(5) &= T_{\infty} + (T_i - T_{\infty}) \cdot e^{-k t} \\
T(5) &= 4 + (28 - 4) \cdot e^{-0.5 \times 5} \\
T(5) &= 4 + 24 \cdot e^{-2.5} \\
T(5) &= 4 + 24 \times 0.0821 \\
T(5) &= 4 + 1.97 = 5.97\ ^\circ\text{C} \approx 6\ ^\circ\text{C}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:** $t_{1/2} = 1.386\ \text{h}$; $t_{7/8} = 4.158\ \text{h}$; $T(5\ \text{h}) = 5.97\ ^\circ\text{C}$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students forget to convert $T_i - T_{\infty}$ in Kelvin-equivalent or use $\ln$ base confusion. Always verify: $\ln(2) = 0.693$, NOT 2.303. Use **natural log (ln)** for cooling equations.

---

### Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative)

**a)** Describe the various modes of transportation used for fruits and vegetables. Discuss the importance of maintaining the cold chain. **[7 Marks]** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**b)** A refrigerated truck carries 2000 kg of apples at an initial temperature of 25 °C. The respiration rate of apples at the storage temperature of 2 °C is 4 mL CO₂ kg⁻¹ h⁻¹. Calculate the total respiration heat generated per hour in kW and the equivalent refrigeration tonnage required. **[7 Marks]**

#### Part (a) — Model Answer

**Modes of Transportation for Fruits & Vegetables:**

**1. Road Transport (Refrigerated Trucks / Reefer Vans):**
Most widely used mode in India. Reefer trucks are insulated containers fitted with refrigeration units. Suitable for distances up to 1000–1500 km. Advantages: door-to-door delivery, flexible routing. Limitation: limited capacity (5–20 t).

**2. Rail Transport (Reefer Rakes / Container Trains):**
Used for long-distance bulk movement (e.g., apple from Kashmir to Delhi). Indian Railways operates special **"Banana, Mango, and Orange" reefer rakes**. Cost-effective for high-volume shipments.

**3. Sea Transport (Reefer Containers / CA Containers):**
Used for export of fresh produce. Standard 20-ft or 40-ft reefer containers (capacity ~20–25 t) maintain –30 °C to +30 °C. **Controlled Atmosphere (CA) containers** also regulate O₂ and CO₂ levels to extend shelf life during multi-week voyages.

**4. Air Freight:**
Fastest mode (24–48 h global reach). Used for high-value, low-weight, highly perishable items (strawberry, fresh fig, cut flowers). Cost: 5–10× higher than sea freight.

**5. Open Trucks / Ventilated Carts:**
Used in local mandis and short-haul (< 100 km) transport. No mechanical refrigeration; relies on natural ventilation and night-time travel. Loss percentage can be **15–25 %** if not managed.

**Importance of Cold Chain Maintenance:**

1. **Respiration Control:** Lower temperature = lower respiration = longer shelf life.
2. **Microbial Inhibition:** Pathogenic bacteria and fungi are suppressed at 0–4 °C.
3. **Moisture Retention:** High RH (90–95 %) prevents shriveling and weight loss.
4. **Quality Preservation:** Color, firmness, flavor, and nutritional value are retained.
5. **Loss Reduction:** A robust cold chain reduces post-harvest losses from **30–40 % (India average)** to less than **5–10 %** (developed nations).

#### Part (b) — Model Solution

**Given:** $m = 2000\ \text{kg}$, $R = 4\ \text{mL CO}_2\ \text{kg}^{-1}\text{h}^{-1}$, $L_{resp} = 10.5\ \text{kJ per mL}$.

**Step 1 — Total respiration rate.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{total} &= R \times m = 4 \times 2000 = 8000\ \text{mL CO}_2\ \text{h}^{-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Heat generated per hour.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{resp} &= R_{total} \times L_{resp} \\
Q_{resp} &= 8000 \times 10.5 = 84000\ \text{kJ h}^{-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert kJ/h to kW.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{resp,kW} &= \frac{84000\ \text{kJ h}^{-1}}{3600\ \text{s h}^{-1}} \\
Q_{resp,kW} &= 23.33\ \text{kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert kW to TR.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{resp,TR} &= \frac{23.33}{3.517} = 6.63\ \text{TR}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:** Heat generated = **23.33 kW**, equivalent to **6.63 TR** of refrigeration capacity. The truck's reefer unit must be sized to handle this load plus infiltration and conduction losses (typically 1.3× design factor).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often forget to convert kJ/h to kW by dividing by 3600. Marks are lost in the unit conversion step. Always write units clearly: kJ h⁻¹ → W → kW → TR.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Must-Memorize for KTU ESE:**

- **Pre-cooling** removes 7/8 = **87.5 %** of field heat. $t_{7/8} = 3 t_{1/2}$.
- **Newton's Law of Cooling:** $T(t) = T_{\infty} + (T_i - T_{\infty}) e^{-k t}$.
- **Respiration heat:** $Q = R \cdot m \cdot 10.5\ \text{kJ mL}^{-1}$ (latent heat constant).
- **1 TR = 3.517 kW = 3024 kcal h⁻¹** — must memorize the conversion.
- **Six pre-cooling methods:** Room, Forced-Air, Hydro, Vacuum, Icing, Cryogenic.
- **Optimal Transit RH:** **85–95 %** for most fruits and vegetables.
- **Ventilation area on crates:** minimum **5 %** of side wall.
- **Climacteric fruits** (mango, banana, tomato) require **ripening chambers**; non-climacteric do not.
- **Waxing** reduces transpiration loss by **30–50 %**.
- **Indian post-harvest loss rate:** **30–40 %** — a key ESE discussion point.
- **Air transport:** highest cost, lowest transit time, used for high-value/low-weight produce.
- **Sea reefer containers:** standard 20-ft and 40-ft sizes; CA containers regulate O₂/CO₂.
- **Specific heat of produce:** $C_p \approx 3.35\text{–}3.78\ \text{kJ kg}^{-1}\text{K}^{-1}$ — used in sensible heat load.
- **Respiration rate** increases **2–3× for every 10 °C** rise (Q₁₀ rule).
- **Memorize commodity table** (Section 3.3) — common 14-mark question source.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Packhouse Technology
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAGT742 - POST HARVEST ENGINEERING OF HORTICULTURAL CROPS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Packhouse Technology

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Packhouse (Definition – KTU/ICAR/APEDA Standard):**
> A **packhouse** is a specially designed, mechanized or semi-mechanized integrated facility equipped with a controlled environment, dedicated equipment, and a sequential workflow system intended to receive harvested horticultural produce directly from the field and carry out a series of post-harvest unit operations — namely, **receiving, dumping, pre-cleaning, sorting, grading, washing/drying, pre-cooling, treatments (fungicide/waxing), packaging, palletizing, and cold storage dispatch** — so as to maintain physiological, biochemical, microbiological, and visual quality prior to long-distance transport, export, or retail.

A packhouse is not merely a "shed for packing"; it is a **micro-processing industrial plant** governed by hygiene codes (HACCP, GAP, GHP), engineering standards (FSSAI, APEDA, NABL, ISO 22000), and operational throughput targets (tonnes/hour). It is the critical interface between the **farm gate** and the **cold chain**.

### 1.2 Key Standard Metrics (KTU/ICAR Reference)

- **Packhouse Throughput:** typically **2 – 10 tonnes/day** for small/mid-level facilities, scaling up to **50 – 100 tonnes/day** for export-oriented megapackhouses.
- **Recommended Floor Area:** **0.1 – 0.2 m²/kg of daily throughput** for product flow.
- **Receiving Bay Height:** **6.0 – 8.0 m** to permit bulk bin stacking and forklift maneuverability.
- **Packing Hall Temperature:** **20 – 25 °C** (operational) with **RH 85 – 90 %**.
- **Pre-Cooling Room Temperature:** **0 – 4 °C** for temperate fruits; **10 – 13 °C** for tropical fruits (chilling-sensitive).
- **Floor Loading Capacity:** **50 – 75 kN/m² (≈ 5 – 7.5 tonnes/m²)**.
- **Floor Gradient (drainage):** **1 in 60 to 1 in 100** slope toward floor drains.

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — "Packhouse = Hospital ICU for Fruits"

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy:** Think of a freshly harvested fruit as a patient arriving at the emergency room. It is alive, respiring, losing moisture, and clock is ticking before spoilage. The **packhouse is the hospital** — the **receiving bay is the triage**, the **washing line is the decontamination chamber**, the **sorting/grading unit is the diagnostic wing**, the **pre-cooling room is the operation theatre** (slows metabolism like anesthesia slows the patient), the **packaging line is the recovery ward**, and the **cold storage dispatch bay is the discharge lobby**. Just as a hospital layout separates clean zones from contaminated zones, a packhouse enforces **forward (unidirectional) flow** of produce to prevent cross-contamination — a concept borrowed from food processing engineering and HACCP.

### 1.4 Functions of a Packhouse

1. **Receive and weigh** incoming bulk produce from the field.
2. **Pre-clean** to remove field heat, dust, latex, and surface contaminants.
3. **Sort** to cull damaged, diseased, overripe, or undersized units.
4. **Grade** by size, weight, color, maturity, or quality.
5. **Wash and sanitize** with chlorinated water (typically **50 – 100 ppm free chlorine**).
6. **Treat** with approved fungicides (e.g., *Imazalil*, *Thiabendazole*) and **wax** (carnauba, shellac, beeswax).
7. **Pre-cool** to remove field heat (≈ 30 – 40 % of postharvest life is lost in the first hour if not cooled).
8. **Package** in corrugated fiberboard (CFB) cartons, plastic crates, punnets, or nets.
9. **Palletize and label** with traceability codes (Lot No., Date, Grower, Grade).
10. **Dispatch** into reefer trucks / cold storage.

### 1.5 Categories of Packhouses

| Type | Capacity | Typical Users |
|---|---|---|
| **Farm-level Packhouse** | < 2 t/day | Small/marginal farmers, FPOs |
| **Cluster Packhouse** | 2 – 10 t/day | FPOs, Cooperatives |
| **Commercial Packhouse** | 10 – 50 t/day | Exporters, Aggregators |
| **Mega / Terminal Packhouse** | > 50 t/day | Large exporters, APEDA-accredited |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The APEDA "Center of Excellence" for horticultural exports (e.g., Mahindra & Mahindra Varanasi, Aricha Mahindra Nashik) follows the **Cluster → Commercial → Terminal** escalation. Students must know the **layout plan of an APEDA-Standard Packhouse** for the exam.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Sequence in a Packhouse (Forward Flow Principle)

The **forward flow principle** (also called **one-way flow**) is the cornerstone of packhouse design — produce NEVER moves backward. This prevents re-contamination of cleaned/graded produce.

> [!NOTE]
> **The 10-Step Linear Workflow:**
>
> **Step 1: Receiving Bay →** bulk bins are unloaded from trucks, weighed (electronic platform scale, ± 0.1 % accuracy), and lot-coded.
> **Step 2: Dumping Station →** crates are tipped onto a roller conveyor or hydro-dump tank.
> **Step 3: Pre-cleaning (Dry Brush / Air Blast) →** loose dirt, leaves, and surface debris removed.
> **Step 4: Washing (Bubble/Dunk/Spray) →** chlorinated water bath at **50 – 100 ppm**, pH **6.5 – 7.5**.
> **Step 5: Drying (Sponge Rollers / Air Knife / Centrifugal) →** removes surface moisture to prevent microbial growth.
> **Step 6: Sorting & Grading →** manual/sensor-based segregation (size, color, defect).
> **Step 7: Fungicide & Wax Application →** spray or dip; wax at **60 – 70 °C**.
> **Step 8: Pre-cooling →** forced-air, hydro-cooling, or vacuum cooling.
> **Step 9: Packaging →** weighing, filling, sealing, labeling.
> **Step 10: Palletizing & Dispatch →** strapping, stretch-wrapping, cold storage.

### 2.2 Why Forward Flow? — The 'How' & 'Why'

- **Backward flow causes cross-contamination** (E. coli, *Salmonella*, *Penicillium* spores).
- **Quality degradation compounds**: every re-handling step increases mechanical injury (bruising index rises by **~5 % per drop from 30 cm**).
- **Regulatory compliance**: Codex Alimentarius & HACCP mandate **segregation of raw vs. finished goods**.

### 2.3 Layout Design Principles

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three-Zone Architectural Model (ICAR-CIPHET Standard):**
>
> **Zone A — Unclean Zone (Black Zone):** Receiving, dumping, pre-cleaning, washing, drying.
> **Zone B — Clean Zone (Grey Zone):** Sorting, grading, treatments, pre-cooling.
> **Zone C — Packaging/Dispatch Zone (White Zone):** Packaging, palletizing, cold storage, dispatch.

The three zones are **physically partitioned** with HVAC differentials and **personnel access protocols** (foot-baths, handwash, apron change). This prevents microbial transfer between raw and finished produce.

### 2.4 Equipment Used in a Packhouse

| S.No. | Unit Operation | Equipment | Capacity Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Receiving | Platform scale, bulk bin tipper | 1 – 5 t/h |
| 2 | Dumping | Hydro-dump tank, rotary tipper | 2 – 10 t/h |
| 3 | Pre-cleaning | Dry brush rollers, air-blower | 1 – 8 t/h |
| 4 | Washing | Bubble washer, spray washer, brush washer | 2 – 15 t/h |
| 5 | Drying | Sponge roller, air-knife, spin dryer | 1 – 12 t/h |
| 6 | Sorting/Grading | Roller grader, belt grader, weight sizer, optical sorter, vision system | 0.5 – 20 t/h |
| 7 | Waxing | Wax applicator (spray/dip), roller | 1 – 5 t/h |
| 8 | Pre-cooling | Forced-air cooler, hydro-cooler, vacuum cooler, room cooler | 5 – 50 t/batch |
| 9 | Packaging | Net weighers, tray sealers, flow-wrap, CFB case erector | 0.5 – 6 t/h |
| 10 | Cold Storage | Walk-in cold room, CA/MA storage | 5 – 100 t |

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Packhouse Engineering Calculations)

> [!NOTE]
> **Important Conventions:** All formulas use **SI units**. Use `\vert` instead of `|` to avoid table breakage.

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Floor Area Required | $A = \dfrac{M_d}{D_f}$ | m² | $M_d$ = daily throughput (kg), $D_f$ = design density **100 – 200 kg/m²** |
| 2 | Volume of Packhouse | $V = A \times h$ | m³ | $h$ = ceiling height (**4 – 6 m** operational zone) |
| 3 | Refrigeration Load (Pre-cooling) | $Q = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$ | kJ | $c_p$ = specific heat (**3.43 kJ/kg·K** for fruits), $\Delta T$ = drop in temp |
| 4 | Pre-cooling Time (Forced Air) | $t = \dfrac{m \cdot c_p}{h \cdot A} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{T_i - T_a}{T_f - T_a}\right)$ | hours | $h$ = heat transfer coeff, $A$ = surface area |
| 5 | Half-cooling Time | $t_{1/2} = \dfrac{0.693 \cdot \rho \cdot c_p \cdot r^2}{\pi^2 \cdot k}$ | hours | $r$ = equivalent radius of fruit |
| 6 | Water in Bubble Washer | $V_w = Q \times t$ | L | Replenish at **10 %** of tank vol/h |
| 7 | Wax Consumption | $W_c = M \times w_f$ | kg | $w_f$ = wax film factor (**0.5 – 1.0 %** of fruit wt) |
| 8 | Chlorine Demand | $Cl = C \times V$ | mg | Maintain free Cl **50 – 100 ppm** |
| 9 | Ventilation Air Changes | $N = \dfrac{V}{V_{room}}$ | ACH | **15 – 20 ACH** for packing halls |
| 10 | Lighting (Lux) | $E = \dfrac{\Phi}{A}$ | lx | Sorting table: **1000 – 1500 lx** |

### 2.6 Real-World Utility of These Formulas

- **Refrigeration load** calculation is essential for selecting compressor tonnage.
- **Half-cooling time** determines residence time in the pre-cooler (typically **4 – 8 h** for apples, **1 – 2 h** for strawberries).
- **Area calculation** drives civil cost (≈ 30 – 40 % of total packhouse CAPEX).

### 2.7 Standards & Codes Governing Packhouse Design

- **APEDA** – Standards for Export Inspection & Packhouse Certification.
- **FSSAI** – Food Safety & Standards (Packaging & Labelling) Regulations, **2011** (and subsequent amendments).
- **BIS IS 4013:2014** – Code of practice for packaging & handling of fruits & vegetables (reaffirmed 2019).
- **HACCP** – Hazard Analysis Critical Control Points (Codex Alimentarius, **1993** rev).
- **GlobalG.A.P., BRC, IFS** – voluntary international benchmarks.
- **Cold Chain Standards**: NCCD, NCDC guidelines for **10 – 15 t reefer trucks**.

### 2.8 Environmental & Energy Considerations

- **HVAC load**: typically **80 – 120 W/m²** of cold room.
- **Solar pre-cooling** (evaporative cooling pads) can reduce HVAC energy by **40 – 60 %** in arid regions.
- **Water recycling**: wash water after filtration (sand + activated carbon + UV) can be reused, reducing consumption by **60 – 70 %**.
- **Waste management**: culled produce → composting / biogas / animal feed (circular economy).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Packhouse Floor Area Calculation

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement:** A commercial packhouse is designed for a daily throughput of **$M_d = 8000 \text{ kg/day}$** of mangoes. Determine the floor area required if the design density is **$D_f = 150 \text{ kg/m}^2$** and the ceiling height is **$h = 5.5 \text{ m}$**. Also compute the volume.

**Step 1: Identify the governing equation.**

$$
A = \frac{M_d}{D_f}
$$

**Step 2: Substitute the values (with unit cancellation check).**

$$
A = \frac{8000 \;\text{kg/day}}{150 \;\text{kg/m}^2}
$$

**Step 3: Compute the numerical value.**

$$
A = 53.33 \;\text{m}^2
$$

**Step 4: Round to engineering tolerance.**

$$
A \approx 54 \;\text{m}^2 \;\;(\text{rounded up for safety margin})
$$

**Step 5: Compute the volume.**

$$
V = A \times h = 54 \;\text{m}^2 \times 5.5 \;\text{m}
$$

$$
V = 297 \;\text{m}^3
$$

**Step 6: Add service corridors (≈ 25 % extra).**

$$
A_{\text{total}} = 54 \times 1.25 = 67.5 \;\text{m}^2
$$

> **Result:** **Floor area ≈ 67.5 m²**, **Volume ≈ 371.25 m³**.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Refrigeration Load for Pre-cooling

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement:** A batch of **$m = 5000 \text{ kg}$** mangoes at field temperature **$T_i = 32 \,°\text{C}$** must be pre-cooled to **$T_f = 12 \,°\text{C}$** in a forced-air cooler maintained at **$T_a = 8 \,°\text{C}$**. The specific heat of mango is **$c_p = 3.6 \;\text{kJ/kg·K}$**, the convective heat transfer coefficient is **$h = 25 \;\text{W/m}^2\text{·K}$**, and the exposed surface area is **$A_s = 35 \;\text{m}^2$**. Compute (a) the refrigeration load, and (b) the pre-cooling time.

#### Part (a): Refrigeration Load

**Step 1: Define the equation.**

$$
Q = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T
$$

**Step 2: Compute the temperature difference.**

$$
\Delta T = T_i - T_f = 32 - 12 = 20 \;\text{K}
$$

**Step 3: Substitute and solve.**

$$
Q = 5000 \;\text{kg} \times 3.6 \;\frac{\text{kJ}}{\text{kg·K}} \times 20 \;\text{K}
$$

$$
Q = 360{,}000 \;\text{kJ} = 100 \;\text{kWh}
$$

**Step 4: Convert to tonnes of refrigeration (1 TR = 3.517 kW = 12,661 kJ/h).**

$$
Q = \frac{360{,}000}{12{,}661} \approx 28.4 \;\text{TR·h}
$$

#### Part (b): Pre-cooling Time (Newton's Cooling Law for Field Heat Removal)

**Step 1: Use the integrated form of Newton's law for batch cooling.**

$$
t = \frac{m \cdot c_p}{h \cdot A_s} \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{T_i - T_a}{T_f - T_a}\right)
$$

**Step 2: Compute the numerator (mass × specific heat).**

$$
m \cdot c_p = 5000 \times 3.6 = 18{,}000 \;\text{kJ/K} = 5 \times 10^6 \;\text{J/K}
$$

**Step 3: Compute the denominator (h × A_s).**

$$
h \cdot A_s = 25 \;\frac{\text{W}}{\text{m}^2\text{·K}} \times 35 \;\text{m}^2 = 875 \;\text{W/K} = 875 \;\text{J/(s·K)}
$$

**Step 4: Compute the logarithmic ratio.**

$$
\frac{T_i - T_a}{T_f - T_a} = \frac{32 - 8}{12 - 8} = \frac{24}{4} = 6
$$

$$
\ln(6) = 1.7918
$$

**Step 5: Assemble and solve.**

$$
t = \frac{5 \times 10^6}{875} \times 1.7918
$$

$$
t = 5714.3 \times 1.7918 \approx 10{,}236 \;\text{seconds}
$$

**Step 6: Convert to hours.**

$$
t = \frac{10{,}236}{3600} \approx 2.84 \;\text{hours}
$$

> **Result:** **Q ≈ 100 kWh (≈ 28.4 TR·h)**; **t ≈ 2.84 h**.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Chlorine Dosing for Wash Tank

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement:** A bubble washer contains **$V = 1500 \;\text{L}$** of water. The desired free chlorine concentration is **$C = 100 \;\text{ppm}$** (= 100 mg/L). Calculate the mass of sodium hypochlorite (12 % active Cl) required.

**Step 1: Compute the mass of pure chlorine needed.**

$$
\text{Cl}_\text{pure} = C \times V = 100 \;\frac{\text{mg}}{\text{L}} \times 1500 \;\text{L}
$$

$$
\text{Cl}_\text{pure} = 150{,}000 \;\text{mg} = 150 \;\text{g}
$$

**Step 2: Compute the commercial-grade bleach required.**

$$
\text{NaOCl}_\text{needed} = \frac{\text{Cl}_\text{pure}}{\text{Purity}} = \frac{150}{0.12}
$$

$$
\text{NaOCl}_\text{needed} = 1250 \;\text{g} = 1.25 \;\text{kg}
$$

**Step 3: Compensate for chlorine demand (organic load ≈ 20 % loss).**

$$
\text{NaOCl}_\text{actual} = 1.25 \times 1.20 = 1.50 \;\text{kg}
$$

> **Result:** **Approximately 1.5 kg of 12 % sodium hypochlorite per 1500 L tank**.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Pre-cooling Methods

| Parameter | Forced-Air Cooling | Hydro-cooling | Vacuum Cooling | Room Cooling |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Suitable Crops** | Most fruits | Stone fruits, mango | Leafy veg, lettuce | All |
| **Cooling Rate (½-cooling time)** | 1 – 4 h | 10 – 30 min | 20 – 30 min | 8 – 24 h |
| **Temperature Range** | 0 – 10 °C | 0 – 5 °C | 0 – 5 °C | 0 – 10 °C |
| **Capital Cost** | Medium | Medium | High | Low |
| **Water Contact** | No | Yes | No | No |
| **Chilling Risk** | Moderate | High (cold water) | Low | Low |
| **Energy Efficiency** | Moderate | High | Very High | Low |

---

### 3.5 Derivation 4 — Half-Cooling Time of a Spherical Fruit

**Step 1: For a sphere of radius $r$, Fourier number $\text{Fo} = 0.318$ gives 50 % cooling (analytical solution of transient conduction).**

$$
\text{Fo} = \frac{\alpha \cdot t_{1/2}}{r^2}
$$

**Step 2: Substitute $\alpha = \dfrac{k}{\rho \cdot c_p}$.**

$$
t_{1/2} = \frac{0.318 \cdot r^2 \cdot \rho \cdot c_p}{k}
$$

**Step 3: For an apple ($r = 0.04 \;\text{m}$, $\rho = 840 \;\text{kg/m}^3$, $c_p = 3.6 \;\text{kJ/kg·K}$, $k = 0.4 \;\text{W/m·K}$):**

$$
t_{1/2} = \frac{0.318 \times (0.04)^2 \times 840 \times 3600}{0.4}
$$

**Step 4: Compute step-wise.**

$$
r^2 = 1.6 \times 10^{-3}
$$

$$
\rho \cdot c_p = 840 \times 3600 = 3.024 \times 10^6 \;\text{J/(m}^3\text{·K)}
$$

$$
t_{1/2} = \frac{0.318 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-3} \times 3.024 \times 10^6}{0.4}
$$

$$
t_{1/2} = \frac{1539.0}{0.4} \approx 3847 \;\text{s} \approx 64 \;\text{min}
$$

> **Result:** **Half-cooling time ≈ 1.07 hours for an apple** in still air. With forced-air, this drops to **10 – 15 min**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow of a Packhouse

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A0[Harvested Produce Arrives at Gate] --> A1[Receiving Bay Weighbridge]
    A1 --> A2[Lot Coding Traceability]
    A2 --> A3[Bin Tipping Hydro Dump]
```
