```mermaid
    AW --> AW3[aw 0.91 to 0.95 Most Yeasts Grow]
    AW --> AW4[aw 0.80 to 0.87 Most Moulds Grow]
    AW --> AW5[aw 0.70 to 0.80 Halophilic Bacteria]
    AW --> AW6[aw 0.60 to 0.70 Xerophilic Moulds]
    AW --> AW7[aw below 0.60 No Microbial Growth]

    AW1 --> ST1[Fresh Fruits Vegetables Meat Fish]
    AW2 --> ST2[Fresh Bread Cheese]
    AW3 --> ST3[Jam Marmalade]
    AW4 --> ST4[Dried Fruits Honey]
    AW5 --> ST5[Salted Fish Soy Sauce]
    AW6 --> ST6[Dried Meats Spices]
    AW7 --> ST7[Powdered Milk Dried Eggs]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Model Paper)]**
*Define the term "preservation" as applied to fruits and vegetables. List the four main causes of spoilage in horticultural produce.*

**Model Answer (Board Standard, 3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** Preservation is the application of physical, chemical, thermal, or biological treatments to fruits and vegetables to inhibit spoilage agents (microbes, enzymes, insects) and extend shelf-life while maintaining quality.
- **Four causes of spoilage (2 marks):**
  1. **Microbial** – bacteria, yeasts, moulds.
  2. **Enzymatic** – PPO, POD, pectinase, catalase.
  3. **Chemical/physiological** – oxidation, browning, respiration, ethylene.
  4. **Physical/mechanical** – injury, moisture loss, temperature abuse.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*What is water activity ($a_w$)? State the minimum $a_w$ required for the growth of (i) bacteria, (ii) yeasts, (iii) moulds.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Water activity** is the ratio of partial vapour pressure of water in the product to that of pure water at the same temperature. It indicates the *availability* of water for microbial and enzymatic activity (1 mark).
- **Critical $a_w$ values (2 marks):**
  - Bacteria: $a_w < 0.86$ (most bacteria cease to grow)
  - Yeasts: $a_w < 0.70$
  - Moulds: $a_w < 0.65$
  - *Below 0.60 — virtually no microbial growth.*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — University ESE Module Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU 2024 Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. *Students write generic answers without the underlying principle (e.g., "heating kills germs") — examiners want the mechanism and the formula where applicable.*
> 2. *Failure to mention numerical values (temperature, $a_w$, dose) → lose 1–2 marks per sub-part.*
> 3. *In canning questions, students forget the 12-D concept and the $Z$-value.*
> 4. *Freezing and refrigeration answers often omit *chilling injury* — the examiner specifically looks for the limit of low-T in tropical fruits.*

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Jul 2024 — Model]**
**(a)** Classify the various methods of preservation of fruits and vegetables. Discuss the principle, process parameters, advantages, and limitations of **thermal preservation** in detail. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** A fruit pulp contains $85\%\ wb$ moisture and $10^5\ \text{CFU/g}$ of *Aspergillus niger*. It is hot-air dried to a safe moisture of $20\%\ wb$. If the death rate constant $k = 0.128\ \text{min}^{-1}$, calculate (i) the **dehydration ratio**, and (ii) the **12-D processing time**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Classification (3 marks):**
Preservation methods are classified under five principles:
1. *Asepsis* – washing, sorting, grading.
2. *Removal of microbes* – UV, filtration.
3. *Inhibition* – cold, dehydration, salt, sugar, acid.
4. *Inactivation* – blanching, pasteurization, sterilization, irradiation, HPP.
5. *Preservatives & protective atmospheres* – KMS, waxes, MAP, CAP.

**Thermal Preservation (4 marks):**

| Method | Temperature | Time | Target |
|---|---|---|---|
| Blanching | 80–100 °C | 1–10 min | Inactivate PPO, POD |
| Pasteurization (LTLT) | 63 °C | 30 min | Vegetative pathogens in juice |
| Pasteurization (HTST) | 72 °C | 15 s | Same, continuous flow |
| Sterilization (canning) | 121 °C | 12-D kill | *C. botulinum* spores |

*Advantages:* shelf-stable, kills pathogens, improves digestibility.
*Limitations:* heat-labile vitamins (Vit-C, thiamine) destroyed, flavour changes.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

*(i) Dehydration ratio (3 marks):*
$$
MC_{f, db} = \frac{85}{15}\times 100 = 566.7\,\%
$$
$$
MC_{d, db} = \frac{20}{80}\times 100 = 25\,\%
$$

Dry-matter balance: $M_f \times 0.15 = M_d \times 0.80$

$$
DR = \frac{M_f}{M_d} = \frac{0.80}{0.15} = 5.33
$$

So 5.33 kg fresh pulp → 1 kg dried pulp.

*(ii) 12-D processing time (4 marks):*

From $k = 0.128$ min⁻¹:
$$
D = \frac{2.303}{k} = \frac{2.303}{0.128} = 18.0\ \text{min}
$$
$$
t_{12D} = 12 \times 18 = 216\ \text{min} = 3\ \text{hr}\ 36\ \text{min}
$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Internal Choice Alternative

**[KTU University Exam – Jul 2024 — Model]**
**(a)** Explain the **Hurdle Concept** in food preservation with a suitable diagram. Describe, with examples, how multiple hurdles are combined in preserving *mango pulp for aseptic packaging*. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** Derive the **Q₁₀ relationship** for respiration rate of a horticultural commodity. If a mango has a respiration rate of 25 mg CO₂ / kg·h at 20 °C and $Q_{10} = 2.5$, calculate the respiration rate at 5 °C and 35 °C. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Hurdle Concept (4 marks + 3 marks for mango):**

The **Hurdle Concept** (Leistner, 1995) states that *several mild preservation techniques (hurdles) applied simultaneously* can be more effective and less damaging to product quality than a single severe technique. Common hurdles are:
- $H_1$: Low temperature
- $H_2$: Low water activity
- $H_3$: Low pH
- $H_4$: Redox potential
- $H_5$: Competitive microflora (e.g., LAB)
- $H_6$: Preservatives (nitrite, KMS)

**Mango pulp hurdles for aseptic packaging:**

| Hurdle | Applied in Mango Pulp | Value |
|---|---|---|
| Thermal | HTST pasteurization | 95 °C / 45 s |
| $a_w$ | Concentration by evaporation | $a_w$ ≈ 0.85–0.90 |
| pH | Natural acidity | pH 3.5–4.5 |
| Aseptic pack | Sterile laminate pouches | O₂ barrier |
| Refrigeration | Optional, 4–8 °C | Further extends shelf-life |

Combined, these hurdles make the pulp commercially sterile and shelf-stable for 6–12 months at ambient.

**(b) Q₁₀ derivation & calculation (7 marks):**

*Derivation (4 marks):* The general Arrhenius equation:
$$
k = A e^{-E_a / RT}
$$

For two temperatures, taking ratio:
$$
\frac{k_2}{k_1} = e^{(E_a/R)\left(\frac{1}{T_1}-\frac{1}{T_2}\right)}
$$

Empirically, over a 10 °C interval:
$$
Q_{10} = \left(\frac{r_2}{r_1}\right)^{10/(T_2 - T_1)}
$$

*Calculation (3 marks):* With $r_1 = 25$ mg CO₂/kg·h at 20 °C and $Q_{10} = 2.5$:

**At $T_2 = 5\ ^\circ C$** ($\Delta T = -15$):
$$
r_2 = 25 \times (2.5)^{-15/10} = 25 \times (2.5)^{-1.5}
$$
$$
(2.5)^{1.5} = 2.5 \times \sqrt{2.5} = 2.5 \times 1.5811 = 3.9528
$$
$$
r_2 = \frac{25}{3.9528} = 6.32\ \text{mg CO}_2 / \text{kg·h}
$$

**At $T_2 = 35\ ^\circ C$** ($\Delta T = +15$):
$$
r_2 = 25 \times (2.5)^{15/10} = 25 \times (2.5)^{1.5} = 25 \times 3.9528 = 98.82\ \text{mg CO}_2 / \text{kg·h}
$$

This demonstrates why **refrigeration is critical** for tropical fruits — cooling from 35 °C to 5 °C reduces respiration by ~94 %.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Mnemonics and Constants to Memorise Before KTU Exam:**

- ✅ **Perishability Order (↑ perishability):** *Leafy veg → Berries → Pome fruits → Stone fruits → Roots & tubers.*
- ✅ **5 Principles of Preservation:** Asepsis, Removal, Inhibition, Inactivation, Preservatives.
- ✅ **Critical $a_w$ values:** Bacteria 0.86, Yeasts 0.70, Moulds 0.65, No growth 0.60.
- ✅ **Q₁₀ for horticultural produce:** 2–3.
- ✅ **Decimal reduction time (D):** $D = 2.303/k$.
- ✅ **Canning reference:** 12-D kill of *C. botulinum* at 121 °C; $Z = 10$ °C.
- ✅ **Blanching:** 80–100 °C, 1–10 min → inactivates PPO, POD, catalase.
- ✅ **HTST pasteurisation:** 72 °C / 15 s.
- ✅ **LTLT pasteurisation:** 63 °C / 30 min.
- ✅ **Irradiation doses:** Radurisation ≤1 kGy, Radicidation 1–5 kGy, Radappertization >5 kGy.
- ✅ **KMS** (Class II preservative, E-224) concentration for drying: 350–500 ppm SO₂.
- ✅ **Modified Atmosphere (typical fruit):** 2–5 % O₂, 3–10 % CO₂, balance N₂.
- ✅ **Chilling injury occurs** in mango, banana, papaya, pineapple below 13 °C.
- ✅ **Hurdle Concept** (Leistner 1995): multiple mild hurdles > one severe treatment.
- ✅ **Thermal death time (F-value):** time at reference T to achieve a given log reduction.
- ✅ **Dry-matter balance:** $M_f (1 - MC_f) = M_d (1 - MC_d)$ is the universal drying-mass balance.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Strategy for KTU 2024 PEAGT742:**
> 1. Memorise the **$a_w$ and temperature thresholds** — they appear in almost every Part A question.
> 2. For any 14-mark "discuss methods" question, *always* structure your answer as: **Definition → Principle → Process parameters → Advantages → Limitations → 2-3 examples**.
> 3. For numericals, write the **formula first**, then the **substitution**, then the **answer with units**. Examiners mark stepwise.
> 4. Always include a **labelled schematic/diagram** in Part B answers — it carries 1–2 free marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Brief Description and Advantages and Disadvantages of Different Physical/Chemical and Other Methods of Preservation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAGT742 - POST HARVEST ENGINEERING OF HORTICULTURAL CROPS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Brief Description and Advantages and Disadvantages of Different Physical/Chemical and Other Methods of Preservation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Post-Harvest Preservation** refers to the integrated set of physical, chemical, and biological techniques applied to horticultural produce (fruits, vegetables, flowers, plantation crops, spices, medicinal plants) after harvest, with the explicit objective of inhibiting microbial spoilage, retarding biochemical deterioration (respiration, ethylene action, enzymatic browning), arresting moisture loss, and thereby extending the **shelf life** while retaining sensory, nutritional, and market quality attributes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Highlight:** Preservation ≠ Processing. Preservation is a *preventive* strategy that maintains the produce in a state *closer to its fresh form*; processing is a *transformative* strategy that deliberately alters the form (juice, jam, pickle).

### 1.2 Classification of Preservation Methods

The broad taxonomy recognized by KTU 2024 Scheme under Module 4 is given below:

| S.No | Class | Examples |
|------|-------|----------|
| 1 | **Physical Methods** | Low temperature (refrigeration, freezing), High temperature (pasteurization, sterilization, blanching, ohmic/microwave heating), Drying & Dehydration, Irradiation (ionizing & non-ionizing), Filtration, High Pressure Processing (HPP) |
| 2 | **Chemical Methods** | Use of Class I & Class II preservatives (salt, sugar, vinegar, benzoic acid, SO₂, sorbic acid), Antioxidants, Surface coatings (waxes) |
| 3 | **Biological / Other Methods** | Fermentation (lactic acid, alcoholic), Pickling, Modified Atmosphere Packaging (MAP), Controlled Atmosphere Storage (CAS), Biopreservation, Edible coatings, Pulsed Electric Field (PEF) |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Three Lock and Key" Model of Food Spoilage:**
> Imagine a horticultural produce (say, a tomato) as a fortified building. The spoilage agents — **bacteria**, **fungi**, **enzymes**, and **oxygen** — are intruders trying to break in. Each preservation method acts as a *different kind of lock*:
> - **Refrigeration** = "Slow-motion lock" → slows the intruders down (low temperature reduces microbial metabolism).
> - **Freezing** = "Deep freeze lock" → immobilizes the intruders completely.
> - **Thermal processing** = "Fire lock" → destroys the intruders outright.
> - **Chemical preservatives** = "Chemical shield lock" → poisons the intruders.
> - **Drying** = "Dehydration lock" → removes the water (the intruder's lifeline) so they die.
> - **MAP/CAS** = "Oxygen-displaced vault" → replaces the air with a different atmosphere.
>
> A trained food engineer (i.e., you, post-B.Tech!) decides *which lock* to install based on the produce type, microbial load, water activity, and intended shelf life.

### 1.4 Key Standard Metrics (Bolded)

- **Water Activity (a_w):** Ratio of partial vapor pressure of water in the produce to that of pure water. **Critical threshold for microbial growth: a_w < 0.60** for most bacteria.
- **Thermal Death Time (TDT):** Time required to kill a specific population of microorganisms at a given temperature.
- **D-value:** Time to reduce microbial count by 90 % (one log cycle) at a constant temperature.
- **Z-value:** Temperature (°C) required to reduce D-value by a factor of 10.
- **Pasteurization temperature-time standards:** **72 °C for 15 s** (HTST) or **63 °C for 30 min** (LTLT) for fruit juices.
- **Cold chain temperature:** **0 – 4 °C** for chilled storage; **-18 °C or below** for frozen storage.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Physical Methods — Operational Theory

#### A. Low Temperature Preservation (Refrigeration & Freezing)

**Principle:** Microbial metabolic rate and enzymatic activity roughly halve for every **10 °C drop** in temperature (within biological limits). Freezing immobilizes free water into ice crystals, making it biologically unavailable.

**Operational steps:**
1. **Pre-cooling** of produce to remove field heat (immediate after harvest).
2. **Storage at refrigerated temperature (0 – 4 °C, RH 85–95 %)** for short-term preservation.
3. **Freezing (≤ -18 °C)** for long-term preservation; may be *slow freezing* (10 °C/hr — large ice crystals) or *quick freezing* (IQF, blast freezing — small ice crystals, better quality).
4. **Thawing** (ideally at refrigeration temperature) before consumption.

#### B. High Temperature Preservation

**Principle:** Heat denatures microbial enzymes and structural proteins (irreversibly), killing vegetative cells and (for sterilization) even spores.

**Operational steps:**
1. **Blanching** (85–100 °C, 1–5 min) — inactivates *peroxidase*, *catalase*, and *polyphenol oxidase* before freezing/drying.
2. **Pasteurization** — kills pathogenic vegetative cells; spares quality.
3. **Sterilization (commercial)** — achieves **12-D reduction of *Clostridium botulinum*** in low-acid foods (the famous "bot cook": 121 °C, 3 min, F₀ ≥ 3 minutes).
4. **Aseptic packaging** — sterilized product filled into sterilized container in sterile environment.

#### C. Drying and Dehydration

**Principle:** Removes water to reduce **a_w below the threshold** (~0.6). Most spoilage microbes cannot grow at low a_w.

**Methods:** Sun drying, hot air tray drying, fluidized bed drying, spray drying, freeze drying (lyophilization), osmotic dehydration, vacuum drying, drum drying.

#### D. Irradiation

**Principle:** Ionizing radiations (Gamma rays from Co-60 / Cs-137, e-beam at **≤ 10 MeV**, X-rays at **≤ 5 MeV**) disrupt microbial DNA. Non-ionizing (UV-C at **254 nm**) is used for surface sterilization only.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Note:** Internationally, the Codex Alimentarius Commission approves food irradiation up to an overall average dose of **10 kGy** (FAO/WHO/IAEA, 1980). India, under FSSAI (Food Safety and Standards Act, 2006) allows irradiation for specific commodities at prescribed doses.

#### E. High Pressure Processing (HPP) / Non-thermal

**Principle:** Pressures of **400 – 600 MPa** for 1 – 15 min inactivate vegetative pathogens while preserving heat-labile nutrients (Vitamin C, anthocyanins).

#### F. Pulsed Electric Field (PEF)

**Principle:** Short, high-voltage pulses (**20 – 80 kV/cm**) cause *electroporation* of microbial cell membranes.

#### G. Microwave / Ohmic / Radiofrequency Heating

**Principle:** Volumetric, internal heat generation by ionic conduction / dipole rotation.

### 2.2 Chemical Methods — Operational Theory

**Classification under FSSAI / Codex:**

- **Class I Preservatives:** Common salt (NaCl), sugar, vinegar (acetic acid), wood smoke, spices, honey — traditional, considered safe.
- **Class II Preservatives:** Benzoic acid / benzoates, sorbic acid / sorbates, SO₂ / sulfites, nitrites / nitrates, propionic acid — used at permitted levels.

**Mechanisms:**
1. Lowering of pH (organic acids → undissociated form crosses microbial membrane).
2. Inhibition of enzymes (SO₂ inhibits *polyphenol oxidase*).
3. Interference with substrate transport (benzoates disrupt membrane function).

### 2.3 Other Methods — Operational Theory

- **Fermentation (Lactic Acid):** Lactic acid bacteria (LAB) produce lactic acid, lowering pH below 4.5 — spoilage and pathogens suppressed.
- **Pickling:** Combines salt + acid + (often) anaerobic fermentation.
- **MAP (Modified Atmosphere Packaging):** Replaces headspace O₂ with **2 – 5 % O₂, 3 – 10 % CO₂, balance N₂**.
- **CAS (Controlled Atmosphere Storage):** Precisely controlled O₂ (1 – 3 %), CO₂ (0 – 5 %) at low T.
- **Biopreservation:** Use of *protective cultures* (e.g., *Lactobacillus*, bacteriocins like *nisin*).
- **Edible Coatings:** Chitosan, alginate, starch-based, lipid-based films that act as a moisture/oxygen barrier.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Typical Value / Unit | Application |
|---|---------------------|------------|----------------------|-------------|
| 1 | Water Activity | $a_w = \dfrac{P}{P_0} = \dfrac{ERH}{100}$ | 0.60 (microbial limit) | Predicting shelf life |
| 2 | D-value (decimal reduction time) | $N = N_0 \cdot 10^{-t/D}$ | Minutes | Thermal death kinetics |
| 3 | Z-value | $\log_{10}(D_2/D_1) = -(T_2 - T_1)/Z$ | °C | Thermal resistance curve |
| 4 | Q₁₀ (temperature coefficient) | $Q_{10} = \left(\dfrac{k_2}{k_1}\right)^{10/(T_2-T_1)}$ | 2 – 3 (typical biological) | Rate change with T |
| 5 | Arrhenius equation (spoilage) | $k = A \cdot e^{-E_a/RT}$ | Activation energy Eₐ | Shelf life prediction |
| 6 | Heat penetration | $F_0 = \Delta t \cdot 10^{(T-T_{ref})/z}$ | Minutes | Sterilization value |
| 7 | Clausius-Clapeyron (drying EMC) | $\ln(a_w) = -\dfrac{\Delta H_{vap}}{R} \cdot \left(\dfrac{1}{T} - \dfrac{1}{T_b}\right)$ | — | Sorption isotherms |
| 8 | Moisture ratio (drying) | $MR = \dfrac{M - M_e}{M_0 - M_e}$ | Dimensionless | Thin-layer drying |
| 9 | Henderson-Pabis (drying model) | $MR = a \cdot e^{-kt}$ | — | Modelling drying kinetics |
| 10 | Drying rate | $\dfrac{dM}{dt} = -k \cdot (M - M_e)$ | % moisture/min | Constant rate period |
| 11 | Radiation dose | $D = \Phi \cdot t$ | kGy (J/kg) | Irradiation effectiveness |
| 12 | Decimal log reduction | $\log_{10}(N_0/N) = D_{dose}/D_{10}$ | — | Microbial inactivation |
| 13 | Henderson-Hasselbalch (preservative pH) | $pH = pK_a + \log_{10}\dfrac{[A^-]}{[HA]}$ | — | Undissociated acid fraction |
| 14 | Mass balance (MAP) | $V_{O_2, in} = V_{O_2, out} + R_{O_2}$ | mol/s | Package gas exchange |
| 15 | Reynolds number (ohmic heating) | $Re = \dfrac{\rho v d}{\mu}$ | Dimensionless | Flow regime |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** In numericals, KTU examiners love testing **D-value → log reduction** conversions and **Henderson-Hasselbalch for preservative pKa** (benzoic acid pKₐ = 4.20; sorbic acid pKₐ = 4.76). Master these two and you score 14/14.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Cold chain design engineers** use Q₁₀ to size HVAC/refrigeration for produce warehouses.
- **Drying equipment manufacturers** use thin-layer drying models (Henderson-Pabis, Page, Lewis) for designing tray/fluidized bed dryers for spices and onion flakes.
- **Aseptic processing plants** rely on F₀ values and Weibull models for canned fruit, RTS beverages.
- **Food safety auditors** apply Codex/FSSAI dose limits to irradiation and preservative concentration standards.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — D-value and Log Reduction in Thermal Processing

**Problem context:** A fruit juice pasteurizer is to achieve a **5-log reduction** of *E. coli*. The D-value at the process temperature is **D = 0.45 min**. Find the required holding time.

**Step 1: Recall the survivor curve model.**
The first-order microbial inactivation kinetics is:
$$N = N_0 \cdot 10^{-t/D}$$

**Step 2: Express the log reduction explicitly.**
Taking $\log_{10}$ on both sides:
$$\log_{10}\left(\dfrac{N_0}{N}\right) = \dfrac{t}{D}$$

**Step 3: Substitute the required 5-log reduction.**
Given $\log_{10}(N_0/N) = 5$, and $D = 0.45$ min:
$$t = D \cdot \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{N_0}{N}\right) = 0.45 \times 5$$

**Step 4: Compute the time.**
$$t = 2.25 \text{ min}$$

**Step 5: Engineering interpretation.**
A holding tube of residence time ≥ 2.25 min, downstream of the heat exchanger, achieves the required pasteurization. A safety factor of 1.5 → design time = 2.25 × 1.5 = 3.375 min is recommended in industrial practice.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Henderson-Hasselbalch for Benzoic Acid Preservative Efficacy

**Problem context:** Fruit pulp preservation uses benzoic acid (pKₐ = 4.20). The pulp pH is **3.8**. Calculate the fraction in the **undissociated (active) form** — the only form that penetrates microbial membranes.

**Step 1: Write Henderson-Hasselbalch equation.**
$$pH = pK_a + \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{[A^-]}{[HA]}\right)$$

**Step 2: Rearrange for the ratio of ionized to unionized species.**
$$\log_{10}\left(\dfrac{[A^-]}{[HA]}\right) = pH - pK_a = 3.80 - 4.20 = -0.40$$

**Step 3: Solve for the ratio.**
$$\dfrac{[A^-]}{[HA]} = 10^{-0.40} = 0.3981$$

**Step 4: Express the undissociated fraction $\alpha_0$.**
$$\alpha_0 = \dfrac{[HA]}{[HA] + [A^-]} = \dfrac{1}{1 + 0.3981} = \dfrac{1}{1.3981} = 0.7153$$

**Step 5: Convert to percentage.**
$$\alpha_0 \approx 71.53 \text{ %}$$

**Step 6: Engineering interpretation.**
At pH 3.8, about **71.5 % of benzoic acid is in the active undissociated form**, which is the form that diffuses into microbial cells and disrupts metabolism. If the pulp pH were higher (say, 5.0), $\alpha_0$ would drop to **~13.7 %**, drastically reducing efficacy. This is why **acidification** is paired with chemical preservation.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Thin-Layer Drying (Henderson-Pabis Model)

**Problem context:** A tray dryer is used to dehydrate sliced mango. The Henderson-Pabis model is used. The drying constant **k = 0.018 min⁻¹** and the coefficient **a = 0.97**. The initial moisture content **M₀ = 320 % (db)**, equilibrium moisture **Mₑ = 8 % (db)**. Calculate moisture content after **t = 120 min**.

**Step 1: Recall the Henderson-Pabis model equation.**
$$MR = a \cdot e^{-k t}$$

**Step 2: Compute Moisture Ratio at t = 120 min.**
$$MR = 0.97 \cdot e^{-0.018 \times 120} = 0.97 \cdot e^{-2.16}$$

**Step 3: Evaluate the exponential.**
$$e^{-2.16} = 0.1153$$

**Step 4: Multiply by 'a'.**
$$MR = 0.97 \times 0.1153 = 0.1118$$

**Step 5: Reconstruct moisture content M from the MR definition.**
$$MR = \dfrac{M - M_e}{M_0 - M_e} \Rightarrow M = M_e + MR \cdot (M_0 - M_e)$$

$$M = 8 + 0.1118 \times (320 - 8) = 8 + 0.1118 \times 312$$

$$M = 8 + 34.88 = 42.88 \text{ % (db)}$$

**Step 6: Engineering interpretation.**
After 120 min, the moisture content of the mango slices drops to about **42.9 % (db)** from an initial 320 % (db). Further drying is required to reach safe storage moisture (typically < 15 % db for dried fruits). The Henderson-Pabis model adequately captures the *falling-rate period*, which is the dominant drying regime in food materials.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Method Selection

| Method | Working Principle | Key Parameter | Suitable For | Major Advantage | Major Disadvantage |
|--------|-------------------|---------------|--------------|-----------------|--------------------|
| **Refrigeration** | Inhibits microbial metabolism | 0 – 4 °C | All perishables | Retains fresh-like quality | Short shelf life (days–weeks) |
| **Freezing** | Converts free water to ice | ≤ -18 °C | Fruits, vegetables, meat | Long shelf life (6–12 mo) | Texture damage (ice crystals), high energy cost |
| **Pasteurization** | Kills vegetative pathogens | 72 °C / 15 s | Juices, RTS beverages | Preserves nutrients | Does not inactivate spores |
| **Sterilization** | Kills all microbes + spores | 121 °C / 15 psi | Low-acid canned foods | Long shelf life at ambient | Heat-labile nutrient loss |
| **Blanching** | Inactivates enzymes | 85–100 °C / 1–5 min | Pre-freezing / pre-drying | Stops enzymatic browning | Texture softening, leaching losses |
| **Hot air drying** | Reduces a_w | T = 50–80 °C, RH < 30 % | Spices, onion, grapes (raisins) | Simple, low cost | Shrinkage, color loss, long time |
| **Freeze drying** | Sublimation under vacuum | -40 °C / < 100 Pa | Premium fruits, herbs | Best nutrient & shape retention | Very high capital & energy cost |
| **Spray drying** | Atomization + hot air | Inlet 180 °C, outlet 80 °C | Milk, instant coffee, extracts | Continuous, fast | High capital, only for liquids |
| **Sun drying** | Solar + natural convection | Open sun | Coconut, cardamom, turmeric | Zero energy cost | Weather-dependent, contamination, slow |
| **Irradiation (Gamma)** | DNA damage | 0.5 – 10 kGy | Spices, mango, onion | Cold process, no residue | Consumer resistance, regulatory limits |
| **UV-C (non-ionizing)** | Surface DNA damage | 254 nm | Juice, surface sterilization | Cheap, chemical-free | Penetrates only ~few microns |
| **HPP** | Pressure denatures proteins | 400–600 MPa, 1–15 min | Juices, guacamole, meat | Retains fresh flavor, vitamins | Batch process, expensive, spore-ineffective |
| **PEF** | Electroporation | 20–80 kV/cm | Liquid foods | Low energy, continuous | Not effective on solids/spores |
| **Salt preservation** | Osmotic stress + a_w ↓ | 5–25 % NaCl | Pickles, fish | Ancient, safe | High Na, alters taste |
| **Sugar preservation** | Osmotic stress + a_w ↓ | 60–70 % sucrose | Jams, jellies, preserves | Inhibits most microbes | High calorie, diabetics caution |
| **Vinegar / Acid** | pH ↓ (< 4.5) | 4–6 % acetic acid | Pickles, chutneys | Strong antimicrobial | Sour taste, corrosion of equipment |
| **SO₂ / Sulfites** | Inhibits PPO & microbes | ≤ 2000 ppm (FSSAI) | Dried fruits, wines | Anti-browning + anti-microbial | Allergic reactions, asthmatic concerns |
| **Benzoate / Sorbate** | Membrane disruption | 0.1 – 0.3 % (FSSAI) | Juices, sauces | Broad spectrum, GRAS | Effective only at pH < 4.5 |
| **Fermentation (LAB)** | pH ↓ via lactic acid | pH < 4.5 | Idli, kimchi, sauerkraut | Probiotic, safe | Off-flavour, slow process |
| **MAP** | Modified headspace O₂/CO₂ | O₂ 2–5 %, CO₂ 3–10 % | Fresh-cut, salads, meat | Extends shelf life 2–4× | Limited to short term |
| **CAS** | Precisely controlled atmosphere | O₂ 1–3 %, T 0 °C | Apple, pear storage | Months of storage | Capital-intensive warehouses |
| **Edible coatings** | Moisture + O₂ barrier | Chitosan / alginate | Fruits, berries | Eco-friendly, edible | Coating uniformity, scale-up issues |
| **Biopreservation** | Protective cultures / bacteriocins | Nisin 250–500 IU/mL | Cheese, juices | Natural, consumer-friendly | Limited spectrum |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block 1 — Hierarchical Classification of Preservation Methods

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0["PRESERVATION OF HORTICULTURAL PRODUCE"]
    A1["1. PHYSICAL METHODS"]
    A2["2. CHEMICAL METHODS"]
    A3["3. OTHER / BIOLOGICAL METHODS"]

    A0 --> A1
    A0 --> A2
    A0 --> A3

    A1 --> B1["LOW TEMPERATURE"]
    A1 --> B2["HIGH TEMPERATURE"]
    A1 --> B3["MOISTURE REMOVAL"]
    A1 --> B4["RADIATION"]
    A1 --> B5["NON THERMAL"]

    B1 --> C1["REFRIGERATION 0 TO 4 C"]
    B1 --> C2["FREEZING LESS THAN -18 C"]
    B1 --> C3["CHILLING"]

    B2 --> D1["BLANCHING 85 TO 100 C"]
    B2 --> D2["PASTEURIZATION 72 C 15 S"]
    B2 --> D3["STERILIZATION 121 C 15 MIN"]
    B2 --> D4["OHMIC AND MICROWAVE"]

    B3 --> E1["SUN DRYING"]
    B3 --> E2["HOT AIR TRAY"]
    B3 --> E3["SPRAY DRYING"]
    B3 --> E4["FREEZE DRYING"]
    B3 --> E5["OSMOTIC DEHYDRATION"]

    B4 --> F1["GAMMA IRRADIATION"]
    B4 --> F2["E BEAM"]
    B4 --> F3["UV C 254 NM"]
    B4 --> F4["X RAY LESS THAN 5 MEV"]

    B5 --> G1["HPP 400 TO 600 MPA"]
    B5 --> G2["PEF 20 TO 80 KV PER CM"]
    B5 --> G3["ULTRASOUND"]

    A2 --> H1["CLASS I PRESERVATIVES"]
    A2 --> H2["CLASS II PRESERVATIVES"]

    H1 --> I1["SALT NACL"]
    H1 --> I2["SUGAR SUCROSE"]
    H1 --> I3["VINEGAR ACETIC ACID"]
    H1 --> I4["HONEY AND SPICES"]

    H2 --> J1["BENZOIC ACID 0.1 PERCENT"]
    H2 --> J2["SORBIC ACID 0.2 PERCENT"]
    H2 --> J3["SO2 SULFITES 2000 PPM"]
    H2 --> J4["NITRITES"]

    A3 --> K1["FERMENTATION"]
    A3 --> K2["MAP CAS"]
    A3 --> K3["EDIBLE COATINGS"]
    A3 --> K4["BIOPRESERVATION"]

    K1 --> L1["LACTIC ACID FERMENTATION"]
    K1 --> L2["ALCOHOLIC FERMENTATION"]
    K1 --> L3["ACETIC FERMENTATION"]

    K2 --> M1["MAP 2 TO 5 PERCENT O2"]
    K2 --> M2["CAS 1 TO 3 PERCENT O2"]

    K3 --> N1["CHITOSAN BASED"]
    K3 --> N2["ALGINATE BASED"]
    K3 --> N3["LIPID BASED WAX"]

    K4 --> O1["NISIN FROM LACTOCOCCUS"]
    K4 --> O2["PROTECTIVE LAB CULTURES"]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block 2 — Decision Flow for Selecting Preservation Method

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P0["START FRESH HORTICULTURAL PRODUCE"]
    P1["STEP 1 DETERMINE a_w AND pH"]
    P2["STEP 2 IDENTIFY TARGET MICROBE"]
    P3["STEP 3 DEFINE DESIRED SHELF LIFE"]
    P4{"SHELF LIFE DECISION"}

    P0 --> P1
    P1 --> P2
    P2 --> P3
    P3 --> P4

    P4 -- "DAYS 1 TO 14" --> P5["REFRIGERATION AT 0 TO 4 C OR MAP"]
    P4 -- "WEEKS 1 TO 8" --> P6["CAS OR COMBINED METHODS"]
    P4 -- "MONTHS 6 PLUS" --> P7["FREEZING OR THERMAL STERILIZATION"]
    P4 -- "AMBIENT LONG TERM" --> P8["DRYING OR CHEMICAL PRESERVATION"]

    P5 --> P5A["BEST FOR FRESH LIKE QUALITY"]
    P6 --> P6A["BEST FOR APPLES PEARS KIWI"]
    P7 --> P7A["BEST FOR JUICES PULPS READY MEALS"]
    P8 --> P8A["BEST FOR SPICES DRIED FRUITS PICKLES"]

    P8A --> P9["END PRODUCT SELECTED"]
    P7A --> P9
    P6A --> P9
    P5A --> P9
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block 3 — Block-Level Functional Architecture of an Aseptic Preservation Line

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R1["RAW HORTICULTURAL PRODUCE INTAKE"]
    R2["UNIT OPERATION 1 WASHING AND SORTING"]
    R3["UNIT OPERATION 2 PRE TREATMENT BLANCHING OR ACIDIFICATION"]
    R4["UNIT OPERATION 3 SIZE REDUCTION PULPING OR SLICING"]
    R5["UNIT OPERATION 4 THERMAL PROCESSING PASTEURIZATION OR STERILIZATION"]
    R6["UNIT OPERATION 5 ASEPTIC FILLING"]
    R7["FINAL ASEPTIC PACKED PRODUCT"]
    R8["QUALITY CONTROL LAB pH a_w MOISTURE MICROBIAL"]
    R9["REJECT STREAM FOR REPROCESSING"]

    R1 --> R2 --> R3 --> R4 --> R5 --> R6 --> R7
    R5 -.QC feedback.-> R8
    R8 -.rework.-> R9
    R9 -.returns.-> R2
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

#### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PEAGT742, Model Paper 2, Q.1.a]**
**"List any three physical methods of preservation of horticultural produce. State the principle behind each."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Refrigeration / Cold Storage** – *Principle:* Low temperature reduces microbial metabolic rate and enzymatic activity, slowing spoilage. Typical storage at **0 – 4 °C, 85–95 % RH**.

2. **Freezing** – *Principle:* Converts free water in the produce into ice crystals, making water biologically unavailable and arresting microbial/enzymatic activity. Storage at **≤ -18 °C**.

3. **Drying / Dehydration** – *Principle:* Removes water from the produce, reducing the water activity (**a_w**) below the threshold (~0.60) required for microbial growth. Examples: sun drying of coconut, tray drying of onion flakes, spray drying of fruit extracts.

*(1 mark per method with correct principle; full marks for stating specific T/RH values.)*

---

#### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, PEAGT742, Model Paper 1, Q.1.b]**
**"Differentiate between Class I and Class II chemical preservatives, giving one example of each."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Class I Preservatives | Class II Preservatives |
|---------|----------------------|----------------------|
| Definition | Common, traditional, naturally occurring substances | Synthetic / industrial chemical additives |
| Toxicity | Considered safe at usage levels | Need quantitative restriction / GRAS limits |
| Examples | Common salt (NaCl), sugar, vinegar, honey, spices | Benzoic acid, sorbic acid, SO₂, nitrites |
| FSSAI Status | Generally permitted | Permitted only up to prescribed maximum levels (e.g., benzoic acid ≤ 0.1 % in fruit products) |
| Mode of action | Osmotic stress, pH reduction | Membrane disruption, enzyme inhibition |

*(1 mark for definition, 1 for examples, 1 for regulatory context.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks each, with Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PEAGT742]**

**(a)** Describe the principle, working, advantages, and disadvantages of **Modified Atmosphere Packaging (MAP)** for fresh horticultural produce. Discuss the typical gas composition used and its effect on spoilage microorganisms. **[(2 + 2 + 1 + 2 = 7 marks)]**

**(b)** Compare **high pressure processing (HPP)** and **pulsed electric field (PEF)** as non-thermal preservation techniques. Discuss their working principles, microbial inactivation mechanisms, and key industrial applications. **[(3 + 2 + 2 = 7 marks)]**

---

**Model Solution for Question A:**

**(a) MAP — Principle, Working, Advantages, Disadvantages [7 marks]**

**Principle (2 marks):**
Modified Atmosphere Packaging (MAP) is a preservation technique in which the *gaseous environment* surrounding a horticultural product inside a package is altered from the normal atmospheric composition (**~21 % O₂, ~0.04 % CO₂, 78 % N₂**) to a modified composition (typically **2 – 5 % O₂, 3 – 10 % CO₂, balance N₂**). This altered atmosphere:
- Suppresses aerobic spoilage bacteria and mould growth.
- Reduces the respiration rate of the produce.
- Inhibits ethylene action (in ethylene-sensitive commodities).
- Extends shelf life 2 – 4 times compared to conventional air packaging.

**Working (2 marks):**
1. The produce is **pre-cooled** to remove field heat.
2. A specific gas mixture is **injected into the package** (active MAP) **or** the produce is sealed in a film with selective O₂/CO₂ permeability (passive MAP — equilibrium modified atmosphere).
3. The **package film** (LDPE, PP, EVA, PVC) is chosen for its permeability ratio so that O₂ ingress and CO₂ egress match the produce's respiration.
4. Continuous cold-chain storage at **0 – 4 °C** maintains the modified atmosphere and microbial control.

**Advantages (1 mark):**
- Preserves fresh-like quality (colour, texture, nutrients).
- Extends shelf life 2 – 4 times.
- No chemical additives — clean-label friendly.
- Reduces post-harvest losses in fresh-cut salads, strawberries, broccoli, mushrooms.

**Disadvantages (2 marks):**
- **Initial capital cost** for gas-mixing and packaging machinery.
- **Specialised films** required; improper film selection causes anaerobic conditions and off-odours.
- **Anaerobic pathogen risk (Clostridium botulinum)** if O₂ falls below 2 %.
- Limited to **refrigerated shelf life** — not a long-term preservation.
- Cannot be used for commodities that are highly sensitive to CO₂ injury (e.g., lettuce shows brown stain at > 5 % CO₂).

**Typical Gas Composition (1 mark — included in above 2 marks for disadvantages):**
- **Strawberries / soft fruits:** 5 % O₂ + 10 % CO₂ + 85 % N₂
- **Fresh-cut vegetables:** 2 – 3 % O₂ + 5 – 10 % CO₂
- **Red meat:** 70 % O₂ + 20 % CO₂ + 10 % N₂ (high-O₂ MAP)
- **White fish:** 30 % CO₂ + 70 % N₂

---

**(b) HPP vs PEF — Comparison [7 marks]**

**Working Principles (3 marks):**

| Aspect | HPP (High Pressure Processing) | PEF (Pulsed Electric Field) |
|--------|--------------------------------|------------------------------|
| Working principle | Pressure of 400 – 600 MPa applied for 1 – 15 min | High-voltage pulses (20 – 80 kV/cm) of microsecond duration |
| Physical basis | Isostatic pressure (Pascal's law) — uniform throughout the product | Electric field induces electroporation of membranes |
| Process temperature | Ambient to mild heat (~ 25 – 50 °C) | Typically < 40 °C |
| Phase of food | Both solid and liquid | Mostly liquid foods (juices, milk) |

**Microbial Inactivation Mechanism (2 marks):**

- **HPP:** Denatures microbial proteins, disrupts cell membranes, and inactivates enzymes. Follows first-order kinetics; spores are *resistant* (cannot be inactivated at commercial pressures). Vegetative pathogens (Listeria, Salmonella, E. coli) are reduced by **> 5-log cycles**.
- **PEF:** Causes irreversible *electroporation* — formation of pores in the cell membrane leading to leakage of cytoplasmic contents and cell lysis. Effectiveness depends on field strength, pulse duration, and number of pulses.

**Industrial Applications (2 marks):**

- **HPP:** Commercial guacamole (e.g., *Avomex*), cold-pressed juices (*Suja*, *Evolution Fresh*), sliced ham, oysters, ready-to-eat meats. Retains Vitamin C, anthocyanins, fresh flavour. Limitation: **batch process, expensive equipment, spores not inactivated.**
- **PEF:** Commercial fruit juice pasteurization (*Genesis Juice* and others in EU), milk processing, liquid egg. Limitation: **not applicable to solid foods with low moisture or high electrical resistance.**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** — **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, PEAGT742]**

**(a)** With a neat sketch, describe the **principle and working of freeze drying (lyophilization)** of horticultural produce. List three advantages and two disadvantages of this technique. **[(3 + 1 + 1 = 5 marks for (a), rest from sketch & comparison)]**

**(b)** A fruit juice is pasteurized at 72 °C. The D-value of the target pathogen at 72 °C is **0.6 min**. Calculate the holding time required for a **5-log reduction**. If the Z-value of the organism is **8 °C**, determine the new D-value at 78 °C. **[(4 + 3 = 7 marks)]**

---

**Model Solution for Question B:**

**(a) Freeze Drying (Lyophilization) [7 marks]**

**Principle (2 marks):**
Freeze drying removes water from produce by **sublimation** — direct transition of water from the *solid (ice)* phase to the *vapour* phase, bypassing the liquid phase. It is performed under *high vacuum* (typically < 100 Pa) and at low temperatures (–30 °C to –50 °C in the chamber). This is the gentlest drying method and yields the highest product quality.

**Working Steps (2 marks):**
1. **Pre-freezing** of the produce to –40 °C in a freezing chamber (or within the drying chamber) to convert free water into ice.
2. **Primary drying (sublimation):** Vacuum is applied (< 100 Pa); heat is gently supplied to the product (from shelves). Ice sublimates; water vapour is collected on a cold condenser at –50 °C to –80 °C.
3. **Secondary drying (desorption):** Chamber temperature is raised to 30 – 50 °C under vacuum to remove the *bound* water (residual moisture).
4. The dried product is sealed under vacuum or inert gas (N₂) for storage.

**Advantages (2 marks):**
1. **Best retention of nutrients, colour, flavour, and shape** — minimal heat damage.
2. **High rehydration capacity** — products rehydrate to near-original form (useful for instant soups, fruits, coffee).
3. **Long shelf life** — low a_w (< 0.10) prevents microbial growth and chemical reactions.
4. Lightweight, easy to transport (used for space food, military rations, premium fruits).

**Disadvantages (1 mark):**
1. **Very high capital and operating cost** — vacuum pumps, condensers, energy-intensive.
2. **Long processing time** (8 – 24 hours per batch).
3. Requires airtight, moisture-proof packaging.

---

**(b) Numerical — D-value and Z-value calculation [7 marks]**

**Step 1: Write the survivor curve equation. (1 mark)**
$$N = N_0 \cdot 10^{-t/D}$$
$$\log_{10}\left(\dfrac{N_0}{N}\right) = \dfrac{t}{D}$$

**Step 2: Substitute for 5-log reduction. (1 mark)**
Given $D = 0.6$ min, $\log_{10}(N_0/N) = 5$:
$$t = D \cdot \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{N_0}{N}\right) = 0.6 \times 5 = 3.0 \text{ min}$$

**Step 3: Holding time.** (1 mark)
$$\boxed{t = 3.0 \text{ min}}$$

**Step 4: Write the Z-value definition. (1 mark)**
$$\log_{10}\left(\dfrac{D_2}{D_1}\right) = -\dfrac{T_2 - T_1}{Z}$$

**Step 5: Substitute for new D-value at 78 °C. (1 mark)**
Given $D_1 = 0.6$ min at $T_1 = 72$ °C, $T_2 = 78$ °C, $Z = 8$ °C:
$$\log_{10}\left(\dfrac{D_{78}}{0.6}\right) = -\dfrac{78 - 72}{8} = -\dfrac{6}{8} = -0.75$$

**Step 6: Solve for D at 78 °C. (1 mark)**
$$\dfrac{D_{78}}{0.6} = 10^{-0.75} = 0.1778$$
$$D_{78} = 0.6 \times 0.1778 = 0.1067 \text{ min}$$

**Step 7: Result and interpretation. (1 mark)**
$$\boxed{D_{78} \approx 0.107 \text{ min} \ (\approx 6.4 \text{ s})}$$

**Engineering interpretation:** A 6 °C rise in temperature reduces the D-value by a factor of 10^0.75 ≈ 5.62. This means at 78 °C, the organism is inactivated **5.62 times faster** than at 72 °C — a key justification for **HTST (High Temperature Short Time)** pasteurization.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Lose Marks on This Topic:**
> 1. **Confusing Class I and Class II preservatives** — Marks are deducted if the student gives wrong examples (e.g., vinegar as Class II is wrong; it is Class I).
> 2. **Missing numerical units** — Always state units for a_w (dimensionless), D-value (min or s), temperature (°C), pressure (MPa or kPa).
> 3. **Incomplete D-value problems** — Forgetting to mention which reference temperature is used. KTU expects "at T_ref = 72 °C" or similar.
> 4. **Skipping the engineering interpretation** — After a numerical, always write a 1-2 line practical interpretation. Examiners allocate 1 mark specifically for this.
> 5. **No sketch in HPP/PEF/Ohmic heating questions** — Even a rough block diagram (as in Section 4) is mandatory for full marks.
> 6. **Treating "preservation" and "processing" as synonyms** — They are distinct (see Section 1).
> 7. **Wrong pasteurization values** — The standard is **72 °C / 15 s (HTST)** or **63 °C / 30 min (LTLT)**, not "100 °C". Mixing it up costs 1 mark.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Preservation** = preventive (maintains freshness); **Processing** = transformative (alters form). Always distinguish.
- Three broad classes: **Physical, Chemical, Other/Biological.**
- **Class I preservatives** (salt, sugar, vinegar, honey, spices) — traditional, generally safe. **Class II preservatives** (benzoate, sorbate, SO₂, nitrites) — restricted levels (FSSAI).
- **Water activity (a_w)** is the *single most important* intrinsic parameter — aim for **a_w < 0.60** to inhibit most bacteria; **< 0.70** for most yeasts; **< 0.80** for most moulds.
- **D-value** = time for 1-log reduction; **Z-value** = temperature for 10-fold change in D.
- **Q₁₀ ≈ 2 – 3** for biological reactions; refrigeration works on this principle.
- **Pasteurization** standards: **72 °C / 15 s** (HTST) or **63 °C / 30 min** (LTLT) for fruit juices.
- **Sterilization**: **F₀ ≥ 3 min, 121 °C** for low-acid canned foods (12-D *C. botulinum* concept).
- **Freezing** must be **≤ -18 °C**; **Quick Freezing / IQF** is preferred for quality (small ice crystals).
- **Irradiation** doses: maximum **10 kGy** (Codex), India uses 0.5 – 10 kGy for different commodities.
- **HPP** at **400 – 600 MPa** — best for retaining fresh quality; spore-ineffective.
- **PEF** at **20 – 80 kV/cm** — for liquid foods only.
- **MAP gas mixture**: 2 – 5 % O₂, 3 – 10 % CO₂, balance N₂ (for produce). Risk: anaerobic pathogens if O₂ < 2 %.
- **CAS** used commercially for **apples, pears, kiwifruit** — months-long storage.
- **Benzoic acid pKₐ = 4.20**; **sorbic acid pKₐ = 4.76** — work best at pH < pKₐ (undissociated form active).
- **Freeze drying** = **sublimation** at < 100 Pa, –40 °C — best nutrient retention, highest cost.
- **Spray drying** inlet 180 °C / outlet 80 °C — for liquids only.
- **Edible coatings** (chitosan, alginate) — emerging eco-friendly preservation; uniform coating is the engineering challenge.
- **FSSAI** + **Codex Alimentarius** are the regulatory anchors for chemical and irradiation limits in India.
- **Henderson-Hasselbalch equation** and **D-value log reduction** are the two *most-likely* KTU numerical topics — practice both thoroughly.
- **3-Lock Analogy** (Slow-motion, Deep-freeze, Fire, Chemical shield, Dehydration, Oxygen-vault) — useful for viva & short-answer framing.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Flowcharts for Preparation of Different Finished Products
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAGT742 - POST HARVEST ENGINEERING OF HORTICULTURAL CROPS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Flowcharts for Preparation of Different Finished Products

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Finished Product (Post-Harvest Context):** A value-added horticultural commodity that has undergone a defined sequence of physical, thermal, biochemical, and/or chemical unit operations to convert the perishable raw produce into a stable, packaged, market-ready form with extended shelf-life, standardized quality, and consumer acceptability.

A **Process Flowchart (or Flow Diagram)** in post-harvest engineering is a standardized schematic representation that uses standardized symbols (operations, inspections, delays, transport, storage) to depict the chronological sequence of **unit operations** and **unit processes** applied to a raw horticultural commodity (fruits, vegetables, flowers, spices) to yield a finished product such as juice, jam, jelly, squash, sauce, pickle, dried fruit, or canned produce.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 4 Focus:** The syllabus emphasizes flowcharts for **preservation-based finished products** — covering thermal processing (canning, pasteurization), sugar preservation (jam/jelly/squash), chemical preservation (pickles, sauces), and dehydration (dried, candied, powder products).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a flowchart as a **"recipe road map"** drawn by an engineer instead of a chef. When your grandmother makes mango pickle at home, she intuitively knows — wash, cut, salt, sun-dry, oil, jar. An engineer, however, must:

1. **Identify** each step (washing, peeling, slicing, blanching…)
2. **Measure** conditions (temperature °C, time min, Brix °Bx, pH)
3. **Check** quality at critical control points (CCP)
4. **Decide** if the batch passes or needs rework

> The flowchart is essentially the **"engineering DNA"** of the product — it freezes tribal knowledge into a reproducible industrial SOP (Standard Operating Procedure).

**Real-World Analogy:** A flowchart in post-harvest engineering is like the **metro map of a city** — each station is a unit operation (peeling, blanching, pulping), and the lines tell you the route the raw material takes to become the final product. Miss a station (e.g., skip blanching), and the "train" (your product) derails (spoils).

### 1.3 Standard Metrics Used in These Flowcharts

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Value | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| Total Soluble Solids | **TSS / °Brix** | **8–14 °Bx** (raw) → **65–68 °Bx** (jam) | Sugar content / preservation |
| Acidity | **TA (% citric)** | **0.5–1.5 %** | Microbial inhibition, taste |
| pH | **pH** | **2.0–4.5** (acidic) | Determines preservation method |
| Water Activity | **a_w** | **< 0.85** (safe) | Shelf-life indicator |
| Pectin Content | **%** | **0.5–1.5 %** | Gel formation in jam/jelly |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All flowcharts discussed below must terminate in a product whose **a_w < 0.85 OR pH < 4.5** to qualify as a "shelf-stable finished product" under FSSAI and Codex Alimentarius norms.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Building Blocks of Any Flowchart — The Unit Operations Library

Every finished-product flowchart, regardless of the product, is assembled from this **standard library of unit operations**:

#### A. Pre-Processing Operations
1. **Receipt & Grading** — Sorting by size, maturity, variety, defects.
2. **Washing** — Removal of dirt, pesticide residues, micro-flora (often with 50–100 ppm chlorinated water).
3. **Peeling** — Mechanical (abrasion), Chemical (NaOH 2–5 % at 80–95 °C for 1–3 min), Thermal (steam), or Enzymatic.
4. **Cutting / Slicing / Coring / Destoning** — Size reduction to standardize heat/mass transfer.
5. **Blanching** — Hot water (90–100 °C, 2–5 min) or steam blanching to inactivate enzymes (PPO, POD) and reduce microbial load.

#### B. Core Processing Operations
6. **Pulping / Juicing / Crushing** — Size reduction + separation of juice/pulp.
7. **Concentration / Evaporation** — Open pan or vacuum evaporator to raise TSS.
8. **Cooking / Boiling** — With sugar, acid, pectin additions.
9. **Filtration / Clarification** — Removal of suspended solids.
10. **Fermentation** — For vinegar, wine, pickled vegetables (lactic acid bacteria).
11. **Curing / Salting / Brining** — Osmotic dewatering and preservation.

#### C. Preservation & Packaging Operations
12. **Thermal Processing** — Pasteurization (60–95 °C) or Sterilization (115–121 °C).
13. **Chemical Preservation** — Addition of KMS (KMS = 0.05 %, Na benzoate 0.1 %).
14. **Sugar / Osmotic Preservation** — High TSS barrier.
15. **Aseptic Packaging / Hot-fill / Cold-fill** — Sealing in pre-sterilized containers.
16. **Storage** — Ambient, refrigerated, or controlled atmosphere.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic for Designing a Flowchart

- **Step 1:** Identify raw material characteristics (variety, TSS, pH, pectin, acidity).
- **Step 2:** Select target product specification (e.g., jam TSS = 68 °Bx, pH 3.0–3.5).
- **Step 3:** Map unit operations in **chronological and sequential** order.
- **Step 4:** Insert **Quality Control (QC) checkpoints** at every critical stage.
- **Step 5:** Define process parameters (T, t, concentration, ratios).
- **Step 6:** End with packaging and storage.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formulas for Module 4 Calculations.** Memorize these for numerical sub-questions.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{(1) TSS Correction (Mixing Law):} \quad
\text{TSS}_{\text{blend}} &= \frac{\sum (W_i \times T_i)}{\sum W_i} \\
\text{(2) Sugar Required to Raise TSS:} \quad
S &= \frac{W_1 (T_2 - T_1)}{(100 - T_2)} \\
\text{(3) Pectin-Sugar-Acid Rule (Jelly):} \quad
\%\,\text{Pectin} : \%\,\text{Sugar} : \%\,\text{Acid} &= 1 : 45 : 0.75 \\
\text{(4) Yield of Pulp / Juice:} \quad
Y (\%) &= \frac{M_{\text{product}}}{M_{\text{raw}}} \times 100 \\
\text{(5) Steam Requirement for Blanching:} \quad
Q &= m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T \;(\text{kJ}) \\
\text{(6) Water Activity Criterion:} \quad
a_w &< 0.85 \;\; \text{(microbiologically safe)} \\
\text{(7) Concentration Factor:} \quad
\text{CF} &= \frac{\text{TSS}_{\text{final}}}{\text{TSS}_{\text{initial}}} \\
\text{(8) Lemon Juice / Citric Acid Addition (Jam):} \quad
A &= \frac{W_1 \times (A_2 - A_1)}{A_3}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Where:**
- $W_i$ = mass of ingredient i (kg), $T_i$ = TSS of ingredient i (°Bx)
- $W_1$ = mass of pulp, $T_1$ = initial TSS, $T_2$ = final TSS
- $M_{\text{product}}$ / $M_{\text{raw}}$ = mass of product / raw material

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Markdown Note:** When using $\vert$ for absolute value in any markdown table, always use the LaTeX command `\vert` or `\mid` to prevent table-syntax breakage. Example: `$c_p \vert_{25°C}$` is unsafe; use `$c_p \vert_{25\,°C}$` with a non-pipe character.

### 2.4 Real-World Utility

These flowcharts are the **backbone of the food processing industry** — they are mandatory for:
- FSSAI licensing & plant approvals
- HACCP / ISO 22000 certification
- Scale-up from pilot plant to industrial production
- Troubleshooting defects in a finished product
- Training new operators on the shop floor

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Sugar Required to Raise TSS of a Fruit Pulp

This is the **most frequently asked numerical** in KTU exams on this topic.

**Given:**
- Mass of fruit pulp, $W_1 = 100$ kg
- Initial TSS, $T_1 = 10$ °Bx
- Final TSS desired, $T_2 = 68$ °Bx (jam standard)
- TSS of added sugar = $100$ °Bx (pure sugar)
- Final total mass = $W_1 + S$ where $S$ = sugar to add

**Derivation:**

The total soluble solids in the final mixture must equal the TSS contributed by pulp plus the TSS contributed by sugar:

$$
(W_1 + S) \times T_2 = (W_1 \times T_1) + (S \times 100)
$$

Expanding the left side:

$$
W_1 T_2 + S T_2 = W_1 T_1 + 100 S
$$

Rearranging to isolate $S$:

$$
S T_2 - 100 S = W_1 T_1 - W_1 T_2
$$

$$
S (T_2 - 100) = W_1 (T_1 - T_2)
$$

$$
S = \frac{W_1 (T_1 - T_2)}{(T_2 - 100)}
$$

Multiplying numerator and denominator by $-1$ to remove the negative sign:

$$
\boxed{S = \frac{W_1 (T_2 - T_1)}{(100 - T_2)}}
$$

**Numerical Plug-in:**

$$
S = \frac{100 \times (68 - 10)}{(100 - 68)} = \frac{100 \times 58}{32} = \frac{5800}{32} = 181.25 \text{ kg}
$$

**Interpretation:** To convert 100 kg of mango pulp (10 °Bx) into 68 °Bx jam, we must add **≈ 181.25 kg of sugar**, giving a final batch of 281.25 kg.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Citric Acid Addition to Achieve Target Acidity

**Given:**
- Pulp mass, $W_1 = 100$ kg, Acidity $A_1 = 0.5$ % (as citric)
- Target acidity $A_2 = 1.0$ %
- Crystalline citric acid (anhydrous) purity $A_3 = 100$ %

**Logic:** The total acidity after addition = acidity from existing pulp + acidity from added acid.

$$
(W_1 + A) \times A_2 = (W_1 \times A_1) + (A \times A_3)
$$

$$
W_1 A_2 + A A_2 = W_1 A_1 + A A_3
$$

$$
A(A_3 - A_2) = W_1 (A_2 - A_1)
$$

$$
\boxed{A = \frac{W_1 (A_2 - A_1)}{(A_3 - A_2)}}
$$

**Numerical Plug-in:**

$$
A = \frac{100 \times (1.0 - 0.5)}{(100 - 1.0)} = \frac{100 \times 0.5}{99} \approx 0.505 \text{ kg of citric acid}
$$

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Selection of Unit Operations vs. Product Type

| Product | Key Unit Operations | Critical QC Check | Final TSS / pH |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Fruit Juice (Clarified)** | Wash → Crush → Pulper → Filter → Pasteurize (85 °C) → Bottle | Clarity, Brix, pH | 10–15 °Bx, pH 3.5 |
| **Fruit Squash** | Juice → Add sugar (1:1) → Add KMS → Bottle (no heat) | TSS = 40–45 °Bx, KMS = 350–700 ppm | 40–45 °Bx |
| **Jam (Fruit Preserve)** | Pulp → Cook with sugar + acid + pectin → TSS check → Hot-fill | TSS = 68 °Bx (sheet test) | 68 °Bx, pH 3.2–3.5 |
| **Jelly** | Juice → Filter (clarify) → Pectin test → Cook with sugar → Sheet test | TSS = 65 °Bx, Pectin: Sugar: Acid = 1:45:0.75 | 65 °Bx |
| **Tomato Ketchup** | Wash → Crush → Pulper → Cook → Strain → Add vinegar, sugar, salt, spices → Bottle | TSS = 25–28 °Bx, Acidity 1.0–1.2 % | 25 °Bx, pH 3.8–4.0 |
| **Pickle (Oil/Vinegar)** | Wash → Cut → Salt (brine) → Cure 7–15 days → Add oil/spices/vinegar | pH ≤ 4.0, Salt 8–15 % | pH 3.5–4.0 |
| **Dried Product** | Wash → Peel → Slice → Blanch → Sulphite dip → Dry (cabinet/solar) → Pack | Moisture < 15 %, a_w < 0.6 | Moisture 12–15 % |
| **Canned Product** | Wash → Peel → Blanch → Can → Brine → Exhaust → Seal → Sterilize (116 °C) | Can vacuum, headspace | pH depends on product |
| **Candied / Glazed** | Soak in syrup (rising 30→70 °Bx) → Dry → Glaze with sugar | Crisp texture, TSS 70–75 °Bx | 70–75 °Bx |

### 3.4 Worked Example: Pectin–Sugar–Acid Balance in Jelly Making

**Given:** Apple juice has $0.8$ % pectin, $0.3$ % acidity. Target jelly pectin: sugar: acid = **1 : 45 : 0.75**.

**Step 1 — Check Pectin:** Juice already has $0.8$ % pectin (acceptable, no addition needed).

**Step 2 — Standardize Acidity:** Need $0.75$ % acid; juice has $0.3$ %; add citric acid.

$$
A = \frac{100 \times (0.75 - 0.3)}{(100 - 0.75)} \approx 0.454 \text{ kg per 100 kg juice}
$$

**Step 3 — Sugar Required:**

$$
S = \frac{100 \times (T_2 - 0.3)}{(100 - T_2)}
$$

where $T_2$ (juice TSS) ≈ $11$ °Bx (typical apple).

$$
S = \frac{100 \times (11 - 0.3)}{(100 - 11)} = \frac{1070}{89} \approx 12.02 \text{ kg sugar / 100 kg juice}
$$

> But **KTU 2024 often uses the practical ratio rule**: take 1 L juice, add ~ 750 g sugar, ~ 3 g citric acid, ~ 7 g pectin (if low). This matches the 1:45:0.75 ratio scaled up.
